3. Use a high-impedance digital readout (e.g., modern DMM, instrumentation amplifier front-end).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Module carries a compulsory 14-mark question with **internal choice** (two alternative sub-questions). Sub-parts (a) and (b) are typically 7 marks each.

### **PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **linear potentiometric transducer** used for displacement measurement. Discuss its **transfer characteristics** and state the equation relating output voltage to displacement. (7 marks)

**(b)** A linear potentiometer has a total resistance of $2\ \text{k}\Omega$ and is excited by a **10 V DC** source. The wiper is connected to a voltmeter of input impedance **$10\ \text{k}\Omega$**. When the slider is at **60% of full travel**, calculate:
- (i) The ideal output voltage.
- (ii) The actual loaded output voltage.
- (iii) The loading error in percentage.
- (iv) The sensitivity of the transducer.
(7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

- **Construction:** A linear potentiometer consists of a long, straight **resistance element** (uniformly wound resistance wire or conductive plastic film) anchored between two end terminals **A** and **B**. A movable **wiper** slides along this element and is mechanically coupled to the **measuring rod** whose displacement is to be measured.
- **Excitation:** A stable DC (or AC) source $V_s$ is applied across terminals A and B.
- **Working:** As the rod moves by displacement $x$, the wiper slides along the element, changing the tap point and therefore the ratio of the two resistance fragments $R_1$ and $R_2$. The output voltage between the wiper (terminal C) and ground is:

$$V_{out} = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{x_{max}}$$

- **Transfer characteristic:** A **straight line** with slope $V_s / x_{max}$ through the origin. Sensitivity $S = V_s / x_{max}$.
- **Sketch description (verbal):** A horizontal rectangular resistance strip with the wiper shown as a sliding arrow; $V_s$ across the ends; $V_{out}$ measured from the wiper to one end.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $R_p = 2\ \text{k}\Omega = 2000\ \Omega$
- $V_s = 10$ V
- $R_L = 10\ \text{k}\Omega = 10000\ \Omega$
- $k = x / x_{max} = 0.60$

**(i) Ideal output voltage (1.5 marks):**

$$V_{out,\,ideal} = V_s \cdot k = 10 \times 0.60 = 6.00\ \text{V}$$

**(ii) Loaded output voltage (2.5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Find $R_2$:**
$$R_2 = R_p \cdot k = 2000 \times 0.60 = 1200\ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Find $R_1$:**
$$R_1 = R_p - R_2 = 2000 - 1200 = 800\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute the parallel combination:**
$$R_2 \parallel R_L = \frac{1200 \times 10000}{1200 + 10000} = \frac{12{,}000{,}000}{11200} = 1071.43\ \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Apply voltage divider:**
$$V_{out,L} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_2 \parallel R_L}{R_1 + (R_2 \parallel R_L)} = 10 \cdot \frac{1071.43}{800 + 1071.43}$$

$$V_{out,L} = 10 \cdot \frac{1071.43}{1871.43} = 10 \times 0.5724 = 5.724\ \text{V}$$

**(iii) Loading error (1.5 marks):**

$$e_L = \frac{6.00 - 5.724}{6.00} \times 100\% = \frac{0.276}{6.00} \times 100\% = 4.60\%$$

**(iv) Sensitivity (1.5 marks):**

The sensitivity in terms of volts per unit of normalized displacement is:

$$S = \frac{V_s}{x_{max}} \quad [\text{V/m}]$$

If, for example, $x_{max} = 50$ mm, then:

$$S = \frac{10\ \text{V}}{50\ \text{mm}} = 0.20\ \text{V/mm} = 200\ \text{V/m}$$

---

### **PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** Compare **wire-wound** and **conductive-plastic (film) potentiometers** under the following heads: (i) resolution, (ii) life/operating cycles, (iii) noise, (iv) cost, (v) typical applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** A rotary potentiometer with **350°** electrical range is excited by **12 V DC**. The potentiometer total resistance is **$5\ \text{k}\Omega$** and it drives a load of **$25\ \text{k}\Omega$**. The shaft is rotated to **$140°$**. Calculate the actual output voltage and the percentage loading error. If the maximum allowable loading error is **1%**, determine the **minimum required load resistance**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per row + 2 marks for synthesis):**

| **Parameter** | **Wire-Wound Potentiometer** | **Conductive-Plastic / Film Potentiometer** |
|---|---|---|
| **Resolution** | Finite, limited by number of turns ($1/N$). Typically 0.05% to 0.1% | Theoretically infinite (continuous resistance change) |
| **Life / Operating Cycles** | Low, ~1 × 10⁶ to 5 × 10⁶ cycles (mechanical wear) | Very high, ~10 × 10⁶ to 50 × 10⁶ cycles |
| **Noise** | Higher (wiper "bounces" between turns, contact noise) | Very low, smooth, almost noise-free output |
| **Cost** | Low (simple construction) | Higher (precision manufacturing process) |
| **Typical Applications** | Volume controls, joysticks, low-precision position sensing | Precision servo feedback, aerospace flight controls, robotics |

**Synthesis (2 marks):** Wire-wound pots are preferred for **low-cost, low-precision** applications; conductive-plastic pots are preferred where **smooth, low-noise, high-resolution** output is required (e.g., flight-control servos).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\theta_{max} = 350°$
- $V_s = 12$ V
- $R_p = 5\ \text{k}\Omega = 5000\ \Omega$
- $R_L = 25\ \text{k}\Omega = 25000\ \Omega$
- $\theta = 140°$

**Step 1 — Normalize the angle:**
$$k = \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}} = \frac{140}{350} = 0.40$$

**Step 2 — Compute $R_1$ and $R_2$:**
$$R_2 = R_p \cdot k = 5000 \times 0.40 = 2000\ \Omega$$
$$R_1 = R_p - R_2 = 5000 - 2000 = 3000\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute loaded output voltage (1.5 marks):**

**Parallel combination:**
$$R_2 \parallel R_L = \frac{2000 \times 25000}{2000 + 25000} = \frac{50{,}000{,}000}{27000} = 1851.85\ \Omega$$

**Voltage divider:**
$$V_{out,L} = 12 \cdot \frac{1851.85}{3000 + 1851.85} = 12 \cdot \frac{1851.85}{4851.85}$$

$$V_{out,L} = 12 \times 0.3817 = 4.581\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute ideal output (1 mark):**
$$V_{out,\,ideal} = 12 \times 0.40 = 4.80\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute loading error (1 mark):**
$$e_L = \frac{4.80 - 4.581}{4.80} \times 100\% = \frac{0.219}{4.80} \times 100\% = 4.56\%$$

**Step 6 — Find minimum $R_L$ for $e_L \leq 1\%$ at $k = 0.40$ (2.5 marks):**

The general loading error formula (from Section 3.2.6), evaluated at mid-stroke worst case (the smallest error), is:
$$e_L = \frac{100}{1 + \dfrac{m}{k(1-k)}}\%$$

Set $e_L = 1\%$ and solve for $m = R_L / R_p$:

**Step 6a — At $k = 0.40$:**
$$k(1-k) = 0.40 \times 0.60 = 0.24$$

**Step 6b — Substitute:**
$$1 = \frac{100}{1 + \dfrac{m}{0.24}}$$

$$1 + \frac{m}{0.24} = 100$$

$$\frac{m}{0.24} = 99$$

$$m = 23.76$$

**Step 6c — Therefore:**
$$R_{L,\,min} = m \cdot R_p = 23.76 \times 5000 = 118{,}800\ \Omega \approx 118.8\ \text{k}\Omega$$

> [!TIP]
> **Note:** The maximum loading error for a given $R_L$ does **not** occur at mid-stroke — it occurs **at the ends** ($k \to 0$ or $k \to 1$). However, the **conventional** worst-case quoted in KTU problems is at the **mid-stroke**, because that is where the error is **uniformly distributed** across the range. Always state your assumption in the exam.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic — KTU 2024 valuation observations:**

1. **Forgetting to use the parallel combination $R_2 \parallel R_L$.** Students often write the divider equation as $V_{out} = V_s \cdot R_2 / R_p$ and forget the load — losing **3 to 4 marks** on numericals.

2. **Sign convention for loading error.** The error is always **negative** (actual < ideal). Writing "$+4.6\%$" instead of "$-4.6\%$" is a common deduction.

3. **Mixing up $R_1$ and $R_2$.** $R_1$ is the resistance from the **input (V_s) terminal** to the wiper; $R_2$ is from the wiper to **ground**. Reversing them gives the correct $V_{out}$ for the **opposite** half of travel. Marks are lost if the student does not label the figure.

4. **Resolution confusion.** Students sometimes state that all potentiometers have infinite resolution. Only **film-type** potentiometers have (theoretically) infinite resolution; **wire-wound** has a finite resolution of $1/N$.

5. **Forgetting to mention the buffer amplifier** when asked *"How is loading error minimized?"* The op-amp voltage follower is the **industry-standard answer** and carries full marks.

6. **Unit mismatch in sensitivity.** Sensitivity is **V/m** (linear) or **V/rad** (rotary). Writing it as just "V" is incorrect.

7. **Ignoring the $k(1-k)$ dependence.** The loading error is **not constant**; it depends on the wiper position. Maximum error occurs at the ends, minimum at mid-stroke — this is a **favourite viva question**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist (Rapid-Fire):**

- **Definition (must memorize):** Potentiometric transducer = passive, variable-resistance, contact-type transducer; converts displacement to a proportional voltage using the **voltage-divider principle**.

- **Two main types:** **Linear** (translational, mm) and **Rotary** (angular, degrees or turns).

- **Core equation (must memorize):** $V_{out} = V_s \cdot (x / x_{max})$ — **ideal, unloaded** case only.

- **Loaded output (must memorize):** $V_{out,L} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_2 \parallel R_L}{R_1 + (R_2 \parallel R_L)}$

- **Loading error (must memorize):** Always **negative**; minimum at mid-stroke; maximum at ends; minimized by large $R_L$ or by using an **op-amp buffer**.

- **Sensitivity (must memorize):** $S = V_s / x_{max}$ in V/m. Increases linearly with excitation voltage.

- **Resolution:** Wire-wound → $1/N \times 100\%$. Film → theoretically infinite.

- **Resolution-limiting factor:** Discrete resistance steps between adjacent wire turns in wire-wound pots.

- **Power dissipation (must memorize):** $P_{max} = V_s^{\,2} / R_p$. Choose $V_s$ such that $P_{max} \leq P_{rated}$ of the element.

- **Wire-wound materials:** Constantan, Nichrome, Karma alloy — chosen for low temperature coefficient.

- **Film types:** Conductive plastic, cermet, carbon film — chosen for high resolution and low noise.

- **Industrial fix for loading error:** **Unity-gain op-amp buffer** (voltage follower) at the wiper output.

- **Applications (must know at least 3):** Aircraft control surfaces, joystick controllers, hydraulic cylinder position, throttle position sensors, audio faders, machine-tool slide position.

- **Common competitor devices (for comparison answers):** LVDT (inductive, active), strain gauge (resistive, bonded), capacitive transducer, optical encoder.

- **Wiper resistance $R_w$:** Small but nonzero; appears in series with the wiper terminal. Assume $R_w \ll R_p$ unless stated.

- **KTU 14-mark question pattern:** Expect (a) descriptive 7-marker on construction/working/characteristics, and (b) numerical 7-marker on loading error, sensitivity, or resolution.

- **Sign convention:** Output voltage $V_{out} = 0$ at one end, $V_{out} = V_s$ at the other end — single-ended output, not bipolar.

- **Linearity class:** Class A ($\pm 0.1\%$ to $\pm 0.5\%$) for high precision; Class B ($\pm 0.5\%$ to $\pm 1\%$) for general purpose.

- **Viva favourite:** *"Why are conductive-plastic pots preferred in aircraft control surfaces?"* — Answer: **infinite resolution, smooth (low-noise) output, very long life, no contact bounce**.

- **KTU numerical tip:** When the wiper position $x$ is given as a **percentage**, set $k = (\text{percentage})/100$ directly. Do not convert to mm unless $x_{max}$ is given.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LVDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LVDT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is a passive inductive transducer that converts **rectilinear (linear) displacement** of a movable ferromagnetic core into a proportional, alternating electrical voltage signal. It operates on the principle of **mutual induction** between a single primary winding and two symmetrically placed secondary windings, with displacement being inferred from the differential output voltage of the secondaries.

The device is widely classified under the KTU 2024 syllabus as a **contactless (non-contact) passive transducer**, since the core never physically touches the coil assembly. It is an inherently **frictionless** device, which is the cornerstone of its **extremely high resolution** (typically on the order of **$\boldsymbol{\mu m}$** or finer).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a **two-tap water tap (faucet)** connected to a single inlet pipe. The inlet pipe (primary winding) has water (AC excitation) flowing through it constantly. Two outlet pipes (secondary windings) draw water from either side. A sliding sleeve (the ferromagnetic core) is moved left or right by your hand. If the sleeve sits **exactly in the middle**, equal water flows out of both outlets — the net flow (differential) is zero. Slide the sleeve slightly to the right, the right outlet flows more; slide left, the left outlet flows more. By measuring the **difference in flow rate** between the two outlets, you can tell **exactly how far and in which direction** the sleeve moved.
>
> The "water flow rate" here represents the **induced EMF voltage** in each secondary, and the "sleeve" is the **soft-iron core** that concentrates the magnetic flux.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The performance of an LVDT is anchored on the following standardized metrics, mandated in the **KTU OEMET725 syllabus**:

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Excitation Frequency** | **1 kHz to 25 kHz** (typically 5 kHz) | Sets carrier frequency for AC excitation |
| **Operating Range** | **$\pm 0.1$ mm to $\pm 500$ mm** | Displacement span |
| **Linearity** | **$\pm 0.25\%$ of Full Scale (FS)** | Indicates high-grade measurement fidelity |
| **Sensitivity** | **0.1 to 50 V/mm** | Voltage generated per unit displacement |
| **Resolution** | **$< 0.1 \text{ }\mu m$** | Practically infinite, set by the voltmeter |
| **Output at Null Position** | **0 V (theoretically)** | Core centered, EMFs equal in magnitude |

### 1.4 KTU-Style One-Liner Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LVDT = Linear + Variable + Differential + Transformer**, i.e., a transformer whose output is linearly variable with the displacement of a ferromagnetic core, and whose two secondaries are connected in **series-opposition (anti-series)** to produce a **differential** output.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy (Logical Bullet Flow)

The construction of an LVDT follows a strict geometric symmetry, which is essential for its differential behavior:

- **Outer Hollow Cylindrical Former:** Made of a non-magnetic, high-permeability shielding material (typically a nickel-iron alloy or permalloy). This acts as an **electrostatic and electromagnetic shield**, protecting the windings from external stray fields and reducing capacitive coupling.
- **Primary Winding ($P$):** Wound centrally on the former, uniformly distributed across a fixed length. Typically energized by an **AC excitation voltage of 1 V to 24 V RMS at 1–25 kHz**.
- **Two Identical Secondary Windings ($S_1$ and $S_2$):** Placed on either side of the primary winding. They have **equal number of turns ($N_{s1} = N_{s2}$)**, are wound on identical bobbins, and are connected in **series opposition** (differential mode). This means their **EMFs are subtracted** algebraically.
- **Movable Soft-Iron Core:** A high-permeability ($\mu_r \approx 5000$ to $10000$) nickel-iron armature that slides freely inside the hollow former. It is the only moving part.
- **Shielding Cage:** A magnetic shield surrounding the assembly to prevent external field interference.

### 2.2 Operational Working — The "Why" and "How"

The LVDT has **three positional states** that every KTU exam question revolves around:

**State 1 — Null (Zero) Position:**
- The core is perfectly centered within the primary.
- Flux linkage to $S_1$ = Flux linkage to $S_2$.
- $V_{s1} = V_{s2}$ in magnitude, but **opposite in phase** (because of series-opposition).
- Differential output $V_o = V_{s1} - V_{s2} = 0$ V.
- **Why?** Mutual inductance is symmetric; the differential cancels out.

**State 2 — Core Displaced Toward $S_1$:**
- The core couples more flux to $S_1$, less to $S_2$.
- $V_{s1} > V_{s2}$. $V_o$ is **positive and finite**, with phase equal to that of $V_{s1}$.

**State 3 — Core Displaced Toward $S_2$:**
- The core couples more flux to $S_2$, less to $S_1$.
- $V_{s2} > V_{s1}$. $V_o$ is **negative** (or 180° phase-shifted with respect to State 2).
- A **phase-sensitive demodulator** interprets the phase to determine **direction** of motion.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU OEMET725 – Module 2 (LVDT)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$ | Standard transformer EMF equation (RMS) | V |
| 2 | $V_o = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$ | Differential output voltage (series-opposition) | V |
| 3 | $V_o = K \cdot x$ | Linear output-displacement relation (operating region) | V |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{dV_o}{dx} = K$ | Static sensitivity of LVDT | V/mm |
| 5 | $M = \dfrac{N_1 N_2 \, \mu_0 \, \mu_r \, A}{l}$ | Mutual inductance between primary and one secondary | H |
| 6 | $E_s = -M \, \dfrac{di_p}{dt}$ | Faraday's law of mutual induction | V |
| 7 | $V_o \propto \dfrac{\mu_0 \, \mu_r \, N_p \, N_s \, i_p \, f \cdot x}{l}$ | Approximate output voltage for small displacement $x$ | V |
| 8 | $\% \text{ Linearity} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{\max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | Non-linearity error over the rated stroke | % |
| 9 | $V_{s1} = V_{s2} = \dfrac{V_p}{2} \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ (ideal null) | Voltage per secondary at null position (assuming perfect symmetry) | V |

**Where:**
- $f$ = excitation frequency (Hz)
- $N$ = number of turns
- $\Phi_m$ = maximum magnetic flux (Wb)
- $x$ = displacement of core from null (mm)
- $K$ = sensitivity constant (V/mm)
- $A$ = effective cross-sectional area of core ($m^2$)
- $l$ = effective magnetic path length (m)
- $N_p, N_s$ = turns of primary and secondary

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Actuator position feedback in aircraft control surfaces, landing-gear displacement monitoring.
- **Civil / Structural:** Deflection of bridges, beam deflection under load testing.
- **Industrial Automation:** Hydraulic cylinder piston position, CNC machine tool slide position.
- **Process Industry:** Float level measurement in non-conductive liquids, diaphragm pressure sensors (where the core is mechanically linked to the bellows).
- **Automotive:** Throttle position sensing, suspension travel.
- **Nuclear & Heavy Engineering:** Valve stem position in radiation-prone zones (no electronics near the core).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not use a potentiometer?** A wire-wound potentiometer suffers from wear, friction, finite resolution, and contact noise. The LVDT has **no sliding electrical contacts**, giving it **virtually infinite life** — often quoted as **$> 10^8$ cycles** of operation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Output Voltage of an LVDT

**Assumptions for the KTU-level derivation:**
- The two secondary windings $S_1$ and $S_2$ are identical ($N_{s1} = N_{s2} = N_s$).
- They are connected in **series opposition** (anti-series).
- The primary is energized by a sinusoidal voltage $v_p(t) = V_p \sin(\omega t)$.
- The core is displaced by a small distance $x$ from the null position (positive $x$ toward $S_1$).

**Step 1: Express the primary current.**
Assuming the primary is driven by a stiff voltage source at frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$i_p(t) = I_p \sin(\omega t)$$

Here, $I_p$ is determined by the primary inductance and source impedance, but for analysis we treat it as a known amplitude.

**Step 2: Flux linkage to each secondary.**
The flux linking $S_1$ depends on the **mutual inductance** $M_1$ between the primary and $S_1$. Similarly, flux linking $S_2$ depends on $M_2$.

For an air-core transformer (which is what the LVDT essentially is, with a movable core), the mutual inductance is **proportional to the position of the core** within the secondary region:

$$M_1 = M_0 + K \cdot x$$
$$M_2 = M_0 - K \cdot x$$

where $M_0$ is the mutual inductance at null, and $K$ is a constant depending on core geometry, permeability, and winding distribution.

**Step 3: Apply Faraday's Law of Mutual Induction to each secondary.**

$$\begin{aligned}
e_{s1}(t) &= -M_1 \frac{di_p}{dt} \\
&= -M_1 \cdot I_p \omega \cos(\omega t)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
e_{s2}(t) &= -M_2 \frac{di_p}{dt} \\
&= -M_2 \cdot I_p \omega \cos(\omega t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Apply the series-opposition connection.**
Because the secondaries are connected in opposition, the **net output voltage is the difference**:

$$v_o(t) = e_{s1}(t) - e_{s2}(t)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
v_o(t) &= -(M_1 - M_2) \cdot I_p \omega \cos(\omega t) \\
&= -\bigl[(M_0 + Kx) - (M_0 - Kx)\bigr] \cdot I_p \omega \cos(\omega t) \\
&= -2Kx \cdot I_p \omega \cos(\omega t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Take the RMS (or peak) magnitude as the measurable output.**

$$V_o = 2Kx \cdot I_p \omega = 4\pi f K I_p x$$

**Step 6: Identify the sensitivity.**

$$S = \frac{dV_o}{dx} = 2 K I_p \omega \;\; (\text{V/mm})$$

Thus, the **output voltage is linearly proportional to the displacement $x$**, which is the central design objective of the LVDT. This proves the linear characteristic $V_o = K' x$ in the operating region.

**Step 7: Direction sensitivity via phase.**
- If $x > 0$ (core toward $S_1$), $V_o$ is **in-phase** with a reference derived from $v_p$.
- If $x < 0$ (core toward $S_2$), $V_o$ is **180° out of phase**.
- A **phase-sensitive detector (PSD)** exploits this to extract **sign** of displacement.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Null, Positive, and Negative Displacement

| Core Position | $M_1$ | $M_2$ | $V_{s1}$ | $V_{s2}$ | $V_o = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$ | Phase of $V_o$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Null (Centered)** | $M_0$ | $M_0$ | $V_0$ | $V_0$ | **0 V** | Indeterminate |
| **Displaced toward $S_1$** ($x > 0$) | $M_0 + Kx$ | $M_0 - Kx$ | $> V_0$ | $< V_0$ | **$+2KxI_p\omega$** | In-phase with primary |
| **Displaced toward $S_2$** ($x < 0$) | $M_0 - Kx$ | $M_0 + Kx$ | $< V_0$ | $> V_0$ | **$-2KxI_p\omega$** | 180° out of phase |

### 3.3 Sensitivity Multiplication Through Frequency

From the derived expression $V_o = 4 \pi f K I_p x$, note that **sensitivity scales linearly with excitation frequency**. This is why KTU problems often specify that increasing the **excitation frequency from 1 kHz to 10 kHz increases sensitivity tenfold**, assuming the core and electronics can handle the higher frequency. However, **excessive frequency** causes:
- Increased **eddy-current losses** in the core.
- Increased **capacitive coupling** between windings.
- **Phase-shift errors** in the demodulation circuitry.

The optimal excitation is therefore a **compromise** — usually in the **3 kHz to 10 kHz** range.

### 3.4 Linearity Range and End-of-Stroke Distortion

The linear relation $V_o = K' x$ holds **only** while the core remains within the **overlap region** of the primary and both secondaries. Beyond this:
- When the core fully leaves $S_1$ and only couples to $S_2$, the rate of change of $M$ saturates.
- The output characteristic **curves downward** and becomes nonlinear.
- This is the **"end-of-stroke"** region, which must be excluded from the operating stroke.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: LVDT Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXC[Excitation Source]
        OSC["AC Oscillator 1 to 25 kHz"]
        AMP["Power Amplifier"]
    end
    subgraph LVDTCORE[LVDT Transducer Assembly]
        PRI["Primary Winding P"]
        SEC1["Secondary Winding S1"]
        SEC2["Secondary Winding S2"]
        CORE["Movable Soft Iron Core"]
    end
    subgraph SIGCOND[Signal Conditioning Block]
        DIFF["Differential Amplifier"]
        PSD["Phase Sensitive Demodulator"]
        LPF["Low Pass Filter"]
        DC["DC Output Vout DC"]
    end
    subgraph DISP[Display Stage]
        METER["Digital Voltmeter or Recorder"]
    end
    OSC --> AMP
    AMP --> PRI
    CORE -- "Mutual Inductance M1" --> SEC1
    CORE -- "Mutual Inductance M2" --> SEC2
    PRI -- "Flux Coupling" --> SEC1
    PRI -- "Flux Coupling" --> SEC2
    SEC1 -- "Vs1" --> DIFF
    SEC2 -- "Vs2" --> DIFF
    DIFF --> PSD
    PSD --> LPF
    LPF --> DC
    DC --> METER
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: LVDT Operating Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Core Position Check"] --> B{"Is Core Centered?"}
    B -- "Yes Null" --> C["Vs1 equals Vs2 Magnitude Opposite Phase"]
    C --> D["Vo equals 0 Volts Differential Cancels"]
    B -- "No Displaced" --> E{"Direction of Displacement?"}
    E -- "Toward S1" --> F["Vs1 greater than Vs2"]
    F --> G["Vo is Positive and In Phase"]
    E -- "Toward S2" --> H["Vs2 greater than Vs1"]
    H --> I["Vo is Negative 180 Degree Phase Shift"]
    G --> J["Phase Sensitive Detector yields Positive DC"]
    I --> K["Phase Sensitive Detector yields Negative DC"]
    D --> L["Output Read as 0 V on Display"]
    J --> M["Display shows Positive Displacement"]
    K --> N["Display shows Negative Displacement"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Mapping — Why the Mermaid Block was Chosen

> [!NOTE]
> A **physical cross-sectional drawing** of the LVDT (showing the bobbin, windings, and core) is best drawn in a textbook figure. In Mermaid syntax, we instead render a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** that traces the **signal flow** from excitation to display. This mapping is what KTU examiners expect in a 14-mark answer when they ask: *"With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an LVDT system."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define an LVDT. State two of its salient features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) is a passive inductive transducer used to convert **linear displacement into a proportional electrical voltage** based on the principle of mutual induction between a primary and two identical secondaries wound on a hollow cylindrical former, with a movable soft-iron core inside.
>
> **Salient features:**
> 1. It is a **frictionless, non-contact** device — the core never touches the windings, ensuring a long operational life (typically $> 10^8$ cycles).
> 2. It offers **very high resolution** (sub-micrometre) and excellent **linearity** ($\pm 0.25\%$ of FS) over a wide operating range of $\pm 0.1$ mm to $\pm 500$ mm.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of connecting the two secondary windings of an LVDT in series opposition?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Connecting the two secondary windings in **series opposition (anti-series)** means their EMFs are **subtracted algebraically** at the output. The significance is threefold:
>
> 1. **Null Balancing:** When the core is centered, $V_{s1} = V_{s2}$ in magnitude but opposite in phase, so the differential output $V_o = V_{s1} - V_{s2} = 0$. This gives a well-defined electrical zero.
> 2. **Direction Discrimination:** The polarity of $V_o$ reverses when the core crosses the null, enabling a **phase-sensitive detector** to identify the **direction** of displacement.
> 3. **Common-Mode Rejection:** External disturbances (hum, drift, temperature-induced EMFs) appear as a common-mode signal and are cancelled by the differential action, improving accuracy.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE rule: Every Part B question carries an **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, OEMET725, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle** of an LVDT. Discuss its output characteristics for the three positional states of the core. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LVDT has a sensitivity of **0.5 V/mm** and is excited by a **5 kHz AC source**. The output is measured by a voltmeter of full-scale **$\pm 10$ V**. Determine:
1. The maximum measurable displacement on either side of null.
2. The output voltage when the core is displaced **3.5 mm** toward secondary $S_1$.
3. The phase relationship of this output with respect to the primary excitation. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**

An LVDT consists of a hollow cylindrical former on which are wound:
- **One primary winding ($P$):** Placed centrally, energized by an AC source (typically 1–24 V RMS at 1–25 kHz).
- **Two identical secondary windings ($S_1$, $S_2$):** Placed symmetrically on either side of the primary, with equal turns $N_{s1} = N_{s2}$. They are connected in **series opposition**.
- **A movable soft-iron core:** Slides inside the former; connected to the measurand (e.g., a bellows, float, or probe).
- **Magnetic shielding:** Surrounds the assembly to block external interference.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

When the primary is excited, an alternating flux is set up. The core, being highly permeable, channels this flux preferentially into the secondary closest to it. The induced EMFs $E_{s1}$ and $E_{s2}$ in the two secondaries depend on the core position:

- **Null Position:** Core is centered. $\Phi_1 = \Phi_2$, so $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$. Net output $V_o = 0$.
- **Core Toward $S_1$:** $\Phi_1 > \Phi_2$, so $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$. $V_o$ is positive, in-phase with the primary.
- **Core Toward $S_2$:** $\Phi_2 > \Phi_1$, so $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$. $V_o$ is negative, 180° out of phase.

**Output Characteristics (1 mark):**

The output voltage is **linear** with displacement over the operating range, given by $V_o = Kx$, where $K$ is the sensitivity (V/mm). Beyond the linear range, the curve flattens (end-of-stroke saturation).

##### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- Sensitivity $K = 0.5$ V/mm
- Excitation frequency $f = 5$ kHz (not required for this part)
- Voltmeter FS = $\pm 10$ V

**(1) Maximum measurable displacement (3 marks):**

$$V_{o,\max} = 10 \text{ V}$$

$$x_{\max} = \frac{V_{o,\max}}{K} = \frac{10 \text{ V}}{0.5 \text{ V/mm}}$$

$$\boxed{x_{\max} = 20 \text{ mm on either side of null}}$$

**(2) Output voltage at $x = 3.5$ mm (2 marks):**

$$V_o = K \cdot x = 0.5 \text{ V/mm} \times 3.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{V_o = 1.75 \text{ V}}$$

**(3) Phase relationship (2 marks):**

Since the core is displaced **toward $S_1$**, the output voltage is **in phase (0°)** with the primary excitation voltage. (If it were displaced toward $S_2$, the output would be **180° out of phase**.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, OEMET725, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the **mathematical expression for the output voltage of an LVDT** in terms of displacement, sensitivity, and excitation parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A certain LVDT is excited at **2 kHz** with a primary current of **10 mA RMS**. When the core is displaced by **0.5 mm** from the null, the differential output voltage reads **250 mV RMS**. Calculate:
1. The sensitivity of the LVDT in V/mm.
2. The constant $K$ in the mutual inductance model $M_1 = M_0 + Kx$, assuming $I_p$ and $\omega$ are known.
3. Comment on how doubling the excitation frequency to 4 kHz would affect the output. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

Refer to the **complete derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. The key result is:

$$\boxed{V_o = 2 K x I_p \omega = 4 \pi f K I_p x}$$

where $K$ is the constant linking mutual inductance to displacement, $I_p$ is the primary current, $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular excitation frequency, and $x$ is the displacement from null.

##### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $f = 2$ kHz, $I_p = 10$ mA $= 0.01$ A
- $x = 0.5$ mm, $V_o = 250$ mV $= 0.25$ V

**(1) Sensitivity (2 marks):**

$$S = \frac{V_o}{x} = \frac{0.25 \text{ V}}{0.5 \text{ mm}}$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.5 \text{ V/mm}}$$

**(2) Constant $K$ in mutual inductance model (3 marks):**

From the derived expression $V_o = 2 K x I_p \omega$:

$$K = \frac{V_o}{2 x I_p \omega}$$

With $\omega = 2 \pi f = 2 \pi \times 2000 = 4000\pi$ rad/s:

$$K = \frac{0.25}{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.01 \times 4000 \pi}$$

$$K = \frac{0.25}{4 \pi \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5} = \frac{0.25}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{K \approx 397.9 \text{ H/mm}}$$

**(3) Effect of doubling frequency (2 marks):**

Since $V_o \propto f$, doubling the frequency from 2 kHz to 4 kHz would **double the output voltage** (assuming no increase in losses or capacitive effects). The new output would be **500 mV RMS** for the same 0.5 mm displacement, and the sensitivity would become **1.0 V/mm**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LVDT Answers — Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the series-opposition detail:** Many students write "$V_o = V_{s1} + V_{s2}$" instead of $V_{s1} - V_{s2}$. This is an **automatic 1-mark penalty** in most KTU valuations, because it violates the differential principle.
> 2. **Confusing "resolution" with "accuracy":** LVDT has **infinite resolution** in principle (limited only by the voltmeter), but accuracy depends on linearity, temperature drift, and calibration — don't claim "100% accuracy."
> 3. **Phase direction reversal:** When the core crosses the null, the phase changes by **180°**, not by some other angle. Examiners are strict about this.
> 4. **Units in sensitivity:** Always express sensitivity in **V/mm** (or V/m), not just "V." Unit errors cost marks.
> 5. **Assuming the LVDT measures only positive displacement:** LVDTs are inherently **bidirectional**; output is positive OR negative depending on side. Mentioning this shows command of the subject.
> 6. **Skipping the demodulator block:** A 14-mark block diagram question **must** show the **Phase-Sensitive Detector (PSD)** and the **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** after the differential stage. Drawing only the LVDT coil and stopping earns only partial credit.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for LVDT (Module 2 — KTU OEMET725):**
>
> - LVDT = **L**inear **V**ariable **D**ifferential **T**ransformer. It is **passive**, **inductive**, and **non-contact**.
> - It has **one primary** and **two secondaries** in **series opposition**.
> - The output is **$V_o = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$**, which is **zero at null**, **positive in-phase** on one side, and **negative 180°** on the other side.
> - **Sensitivity** is in V/mm, defined as the slope of the $V_o$ vs. $x$ curve in the linear region.
> - Sensitivity **scales linearly with excitation frequency** — increasing $f$ increases output.
> - **Advantages:** Frictionless, infinite resolution, long life, bidirectional, high linearity, robust.
> - **Disadvantages:** Requires AC excitation and signal conditioning (PSD + LPF); sensitive to stray magnetic fields; relatively bulky for small displacements.
> - **Applications:** Displacement, force (via load cell with core), pressure (via bellows), acceleration, vibration, and level sensing.
> - **Key derived equation to memorize:** $V_o = 4\pi f K I_p x$.
> - **Phase-Sensitive Detector (PSD)** is **mandatory** to extract signed DC output from the AC carrier — never omit it in block diagrams.
> - The **end-of-stroke** region is **non-linear** — always specify the **linear operating range** separately from the **maximum stroke**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitance Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capacitance Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacitive Transducer / Capacitance Type Instrument:** A passive electrical transducer that converts a physical measurand (displacement, pressure, force, level, humidity, etc.) into a proportional change in electrical **capacitance** of a capacitor formed as part of the sensing element. The measurand alters one of the three defining parameters of capacitance — plate area ($A$), dielectric permittivity ($\varepsilon$), or separation between plates ($d$) — and this change is detected and conditioned by an appropriate AC bridge, oscillator, or charge-discharge circuit to produce a usable output signal.

The fundamental governing relationship of a parallel-plate capacitive element is given by:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where each variable acts as a **transduction handle** through which the input physical quantity modifies capacitance.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Spring-Loaded Piston in a Water Cylinder:**
> Imagine a sealed cylinder divided into two chambers by a thin movable piston connected to a mechanical spring. When you push the piston inward (analogous to mechanical displacement), the effective space between two conducting plates shrinks. Electrically, this shrinking of the gap stores MORE charge at the same applied voltage, just as a narrow water channel builds higher pressure. The device "feels" the input motion as a change in its electrical "stiffness" against charge storage — this electrical stiffness IS capacitance. Capacitance-type instruments are essentially the electrical equivalents of such variable-geometry chambers.

- **Plate area ($A$):** Visualize a sliding window shutter — opening or closing it changes how much light enters. Likewise, varying the **overlap area** between plates modulates capacitance.
- **Dielectric ($\varepsilon_r$):** Think of swapping dry air inside a sandwich box for a slice of cheese — the **"electrical permittivity"** of the filling changes the storage capacity.
- **Distance ($d$):** Pushing the two plates of a parallel-plate capacitor closer together (like bringing two magnets nearer) dramatically increases the stored charge — an **inverse, highly sensitive** relationship.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **standard constants** are mandatory for solving KTU numerical problems on this topic:
> - **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
> - **Relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of air:** $\varepsilon_r \approx 1.0006$ (often taken as **1** for textbook calculations)
> - **SI unit of capacitance:** Farad (F); practical ranges for transducers are **pF to nF**
> - **Typical excitation frequency for AC bridges:** **1 kHz to 1 MHz**
> - **Sensitivity** of capacitive sensors: extremely high — capable of resolving displacements as small as **$10^{-9}$ m (nanometers)**

## 1.4 Why Capacitive Transducers? — KTU Syllabus Highlight

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Capacitive transducers are among the **highest-sensitivity** displacement/pressure transducers available. They require **extremely low force** to operate, have **negligible moving mass**, and excel in **static (DC) measurements** because a true capacitor has no conduction path. They outperform resistive and inductive types in:
> - **Low-pressure measurements** (down to $\mu$bar)
> - **Miniature MEMS devices** (accelerometers, gyroscopes)
> - **High-temperature environments** (up to $\approx$ 300 °C with suitable dielectrics)
> - **Non-contact liquid level sensing** in harsh chemicals
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Structured Logical Breakdown

A capacitive transducer operates on the principle that any measurand-induced change in geometry or material property manifests as a measurable change in electrical capacitance. The transducer is essentially a **variable capacitor** with three possible modes of operation:

### Mode 1: Variation of Plate Separation ($d$)
- **Why:** Direct, highly sensitive, non-linear response.
- **How:** The moving plate is mechanically linked to the measurand. As the measurand displaces the plate, $d$ changes inversely.
- **Sensitivity (theoretical):** $S_d = \dfrac{dC}{dd} = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$
- **Limitation:** Highly non-linear; small $d$ leads to very high sensitivity but risks dielectric breakdown.
- **Typical applications:** Pressure transducers, microphone diaphragms, micro-displacement sensors.

### Mode 2: Variation of Effective Overlap Area ($A$)
- **Why:** Linear output over a wide range; ideal for measuring **angular** or **larger linear displacements**.
- **How:** One plate slides laterally relative to the other, changing the effective overlap area.
- **Sensitivity:** $S_A = \dfrac{dC}{dA} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d}$ (constant → **linear response**)
- **Typical applications:** Angular position sensors, linear displacement gauges with long strokes.

### Mode 3: Variation of Dielectric Constant ($\varepsilon_r$)
- **Why:** Enables measurement of **material properties** — level of a liquid, humidity, composition.
- **How:** The space between fixed plates is partially or fully filled with a material whose permittivity changes with the measurand.
- **Sensitivity:** $S_\varepsilon = \dfrac{dC}{d\varepsilon_r} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$
- **Typical applications:** Liquid level sensing (since different liquids have different $\varepsilon_r$), moisture/humidity sensors.

## 2.2 Three-Plate (Differential) Capacitive Transducer

A KTU-favorite advanced configuration uses **two fixed outer plates** and **one movable central plate** connected to the measurand. The capacitances $C_1$ and $C_2$ vary in **opposite directions** as the central plate moves. The differential output is:

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2$$

This cancels common-mode errors (temperature drift, supply noise), improves **linearity**, and **doubles the sensitivity** compared to the single-capacitor mode.

## 2.3 Associated Signal Conditioning Circuits

> [!NOTE]
> The capacitor itself does NOT generate a voltage or current — it only **stores charge**. Therefore, capacitance must be converted to a measurable electrical signal via:
> 1. **AC Wheatstone Bridge (with adjacent-arm or opposite-arm configuration)**
> 2. **Transformer Ratio Arm Bridge (preferred for ultra-low capacitance)**
> 3. **Oscillator-based frequency modulation (FM):** A capacitive sensor is made part of an LC/RC oscillator. Frequency change $\Rightarrow$ capacitance change.
> 4. **Charge amplifier / Operational amplifier circuit:** Charge $Q = CV$ is measured using a high-gain op-amp integrator.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU board numericals and derivations on this topic.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Governing Equation | Linear? | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic parallel-plate capacitance | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | — | All capacitive sensors |
| 2 | Capacitance with dielectric of thickness $t$ | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - t + \dfrac{t}{\varepsilon_r}}$ | No | Liquid level / multi-layer dielectric |
| 3 | Variable separation mode (sensitivity) | $S_d = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$ | No (hyperbolic) | Pressure, micro-displacement |
| 4 | Variable area mode (sensitivity) | $S_A = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d}$ | **YES (linear)** | Angular position, long-stroke displacement |
| 5 | Variable dielectric mode (sensitivity) | $S_\varepsilon = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | **YES (linear)** | Level, humidity, composition |
| 6 | Fringe capacitance correction | $C_{fringe} \approx \varepsilon_0 P$ (P = perimeter) | — | Guard-ring electrode design |
| 7 | Frequency of RC oscillator with sensor | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | No (inverse) | Frequency-output capacitive sensor |
| 8 | Charge on capacitor | $Q = CV$ | Linear | Charge amplifier output |
| 9 | Capacitive reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | No (inverse) | AC bridge balancing |
| 10 | Differential output (3-plate) | $\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \dfrac{2\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \Delta d}{d^2}$ | Linear (for small $\Delta d$) | Differential pressure sensor |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Capacitive fuel level sensors in tanks — fuel permittivity ($\varepsilon_r \approx 2$) differs from air.
- **Aerospace:** Capacitive pressure sensors in altimeters and wind-tunnel instrumentation.
- **Industrial process control:** Non-contact level measurement of corrosive acids, molten metals, and cryogenic liquids.
- **Consumer electronics:** Touchscreens (projected capacitive), fingerprint sensors, MEMS accelerometers in smartphones.
- **Biomedical:** Capacitive ECG electrodes, respiration monitoring by chest expansion.
- **Precision metrology:** Sub-nanometer positioning feedback in atomic force microscopes (AFM).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacitance of a Parallel-Plate Capacitor (Foundational)

We start from Gauss's Law applied to a parallel-plate geometry. Consider two plates of equal area $A$, separated by a uniform dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ by distance $d$.

**Step 1 — Surface charge density on the plates:**
$$\sigma = \frac{Q}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Electric field between the plates (uniform, perpendicular to plates):**
$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon A}$$

**Step 3 — Potential difference between the plates** (work done per unit charge over distance $d$):
$$V = E \cdot d = \frac{Q \cdot d}{\varepsilon A}$$

**Step 4 — By the definition of capacitance** ($C = Q/V$):
$$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{Q}{\dfrac{Q d}{\varepsilon A}} = \frac{\varepsilon A}{d}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ (the permittivity of the dielectric material):**
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

This is the canonical KTU formula. Each of $A$, $\varepsilon_r$, and $d$ is a candidate transduction variable.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sensitivity in Variable-Separation Mode

Let the initial separation be $d_0$ and the displacement be $\Delta d$. The new capacitance is:
$$C' = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta d}$$

The change in capacitance is:
$$\Delta C = C' - C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left[ \frac{1}{d_0 - \Delta d} - \frac{1}{d_0} \right]$$

After common-denominator algebra:
$$\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left[ \frac{d_0 - (d_0 - \Delta d)}{d_0 (d_0 - \Delta d)} \right] = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \Delta d}{d_0 (d_0 - \Delta d)}$$

Dividing by $C$ to obtain the **normalized sensitivity**:
$$\frac{\Delta C}{C} = \frac{\Delta d}{d_0 - \Delta d}$$

For $\Delta d \ll d_0$, expanding using $\dfrac{1}{1 - x} \approx 1 + x$ for small $x$:
$$\frac{\Delta C}{C} \approx \frac{\Delta d}{d_0} \left( 1 + \frac{\Delta d}{d_0} + \cdots \right)$$

Therefore, the **small-signal sensitivity** is:
$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta C}{C} \approx \frac{\Delta d}{d_0}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-linearity observation:** The exact expression contains the second-order term $\left(\dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0}\right)^2$. The fractional non-linearity is therefore approximately $\dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0}$. For example, a $5\%$ displacement produces roughly $5\%$ non-linearity. This is why **variable-separation mode is reserved for small displacements** (typically $< 10\%$ of $d_0$).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sensitivity in Variable-Area Mode

For a parallel-plate capacitor where one plate slides laterally by $\Delta x$ (with full overlap $A_0 = L \cdot W$ originally):

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (L - \Delta x) W}{d}$$

Differentiating with respect to $\Delta x$:
$$\frac{dC}{d(\Delta x)} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r W}{d}$$

This is **constant** — therefore the output is **strictly linear** with respect to the input displacement:
$$\Delta C = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r W}{d} \cdot \Delta x$$

The negative sign simply indicates the direction of capacitance change. The **magnitude of sensitivity** is:
$$|S_A| = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r W}{d}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Capacitance with Multi-Layer Dielectric (Level Sensor Application)

Suppose the space between two fixed plates of area $A$ is divided into:
- A layer of air of thickness $(d - t)$
- A layer of liquid of thickness $t$ with permittivity $\varepsilon_r$

The two dielectric regions act as **capacitors in series**:
$$C_{air} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - t}, \quad C_{liquid} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$$

Equivalent series capacitance:
$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{C_{air}} + \frac{1}{C_{liquid}} = \frac{d - t}{\varepsilon_0 A} + \frac{t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

Common denominator:
$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0 A}\left[ d - t + \frac{t}{\varepsilon_r} \right] = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0 A}\left[ d - t\left(1 - \frac{1}{\varepsilon_r}\right) \right]$$

Inverting:
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - t + \dfrac{t}{\varepsilon_r}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $t = 0$ (empty tank), $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ — only air.
> When $t = d$ (full tank), $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ — only liquid. The ratio between full and empty is exactly $\varepsilon_r$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Evaluation of the Three Modes

| Criterion | Variable $d$ | Variable $A$ | Variable $\varepsilon_r$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linearity** | Non-linear (hyperbolic) | **Linear** | Linear (for two-region dielectric) |
| **Sensitivity** | Highest (1/$d^2$) | Moderate (constant) | Moderate (constant) |
| **Range** | Small displacements only | Wide range (cm to m) | Wide range |
| **Measurement type** | Displacement, pressure | Displacement, position | Level, humidity, composition |
| **Fringing effects** | Significant at small $d$ | Negligible | Negligible |
| **KTU exam frequency** | Very High | High | High |

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** A parallel-plate capacitive transducer has plates of area $4 \text{ cm}^2$ separated by 1 mm of air. The plate separation is reduced by 0.1 mm. Find (a) the original capacitance, (b) the new capacitance, (c) the change in capacitance, and (d) the percentage sensitivity.

**Given:** $A = 4 \text{ cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, $d_0 = 1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $\Delta d = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}$, $\varepsilon_r = 1$.

**(a) Original capacitance:**
$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}}$$

$$C_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-1} = 3.5416 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 3.54 \text{ pF}$$

**(b) New capacitance (with $d_{new} = 0.9 \text{ mm} = 9 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$):**
$$C_{new} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{9 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.935 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 3.94 \text{ pF}$$

**(c) Change in capacitance:**
$$\Delta C = C_{new} - C_0 = 3.94 - 3.54 = 0.40 \text{ pF}$$

**(d) Percentage sensitivity** (per unit displacement, normalized):
$$\frac{\Delta C / C_0}{\Delta d / d_0} = \frac{(0.40/3.54)}{(0.1/1.0)} = \frac{0.113}{0.1} = 1.13$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sensitivity factor $> 1$ confirms the **non-linear amplification** of the variable-separation mode. For a $10\%$ displacement, we obtain an $\approx 11.3\%$ capacitance change — a $1.3\%$ non-linearity, consistent with our theoretical $\left(\Delta d/d_0\right) = 10\%$ approximation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Capacitive Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Measurand\nDisplacement, Pressure, Level, Humidity] --> B[Mechanical Coupling\nDiaphragm / Slider / Float]
    B --> C[Variable Capacitor Element\nC = eps0 epsr A / d]
    C --> D{Signal Conditioning Mode}
    D --> E1[AC Wheatstone Bridge]
    D --> E2[Transformer Ratio Arm Bridge]
    D --> E3[RC Oscillator FM]
    D --> E4[Charge Amplifier Op-Amp]
    E1 --> F[Demodulator / Detector]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> G[Frequency Counter]
    E4 --> F
    F --> H[Output Display\nVoltage, Current, Digital Readout]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Decision / Control Loop]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Operating Modes Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Capacitive Transducer] --> M1[Mode 1\nVariable Separation d]
    M --> M2[Mode 2\nVariable Area A]
    M --> M3[Mode 3\nVariable Dielectric epsr]
    M1 --> A1[Application\nPressure Microphone\nDiaphragm Sensor]
    M2 --> A2[Application\nLinear Displacement\nAngular Position]
    M3 --> A3[Application\nLiquid Level\nHumidity Sensor]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Differential Capacitive Sensor Working

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SensorAssembly [Differential Capacitive Pressure Sensor]
        P1[Top Fixed Plate] --- M[Movable Diaphragm\nLinked to Pressure] --- P2[Bottom Fixed Plate]
    end
    M -->|Pressure Up| R1[C1 Increases]
    M -->|Pressure Up| R2[C2 Decreases]
    R1 --> SUM[Differential Output\ndC = C1 minus C2]
    R2 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Amplified Output\nCommon-Mode Noise Cancelled]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Signal Conditioning Sub-Blocks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[Capacitive Sensor\nFew pF] --> OSC[High-Frequency AC Excitation\n1 kHz to 1 MHz]
    OSC --> BRG[AC Bridge or Oscillator Tank]
    BRG --> DET[Detector / Phase-Sensitive Rectifier]
    DET --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP --> FILT[Low-Pass Filter]
    FILT --> ADC[ADC for Digital Readout]
    ADC --> DSP[Microcontroller DSP]
    DSP --> DISP[Display / Transmitter Output]
```

## 4.5 Schematic Description of Physical Layout (Verbal Schematic)

> [!NOTE]
> For a **variable-separation pressure sensor**:
> - One plate is a **rigid fixed electrode** mounted on the sensor housing.
> - The second plate is a **thin metallic diaphragm** clamped at its edges.
> - Applied pressure deflects the diaphragm towards the fixed plate, reducing $d$.
> - A **guard ring** surrounds the active plate to suppress fringing-field errors and stray capacitance.
> - The entire assembly is enclosed in a metallic shield connected to circuit ground.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Define a capacitive transducer. List the three variables on which the capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor depends.**

**Model Answer:**
A capacitive transducer is a passive transducer that converts a physical quantity into a measurable change in capacitance. The capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor is:
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$
The three variables on which it depends are:
1. **Plate separation ($d$)** — Mode 1: variable-separation.
2. **Effective overlapping area ($A$)** — Mode 2: variable-area.
3. **Dielectric permittivity ($\varepsilon_r$)** — Mode 3: variable-dielectric.

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the three variables with correct identification)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**Why is a guard ring used in capacitive transducers? Mention its function.**

**Model Answer:**
A guard ring is a conductive annular ring placed around the active plate and maintained at the **same electric potential** as the plate itself. Its functions are:
1. To **eliminate fringing (edge) electric fields**, ensuring the field between the plates remains uniform and parallel.
2. To **bypass stray shunt capacitance** between the active plate and ground — this stray capacitance is now absorbed into the guard circuit, leaving only the true sensing capacitance in the measurement.
3. To **improve measurement accuracy and linearity** in low-capacitance applications (pF range).

**(3 marks — 1 for stating the device, 2 for any two of the above functions with explanation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the sensitivity of a parallel-plate capacitive transducer operating in the **variable-separation mode**. Discuss its linearity. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **differential capacitive pressure transducer** using the three-plate configuration. Show that the differential output is twice that of a single-capacitor arrangement. (7 marks)

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Solution to (a) — Sensitivity Derivation in Variable-Separation Mode:

We begin with the basic parallel-plate capacitance:
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

Let the initial plate separation be $d_0$ and let a small displacement $\Delta d$ reduce the gap. The new capacitance is:
$$C' = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta d}$$

The change in capacitance:
$$\Delta C = C' - C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left( \frac{1}{d_0 - \Delta d} - \frac{1}{d_0} \right)$$

After common-denominator simplification:
$$\Delta C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \Delta d}{d_0 (d_0 - \Delta d)}$$

Dividing by $C$ to get the normalized (fractional) change:
$$\frac{\Delta C}{C} = \frac{\Delta d}{d_0 - \Delta d}$$

For $\Delta d \ll d_0$, using the binomial expansion:
$$\frac{\Delta C}{C} \approx \frac{\Delta d}{d_0} \left( 1 + \frac{\Delta d}{d_0} + \left( \frac{\Delta d}{d_0} \right)^2 + \cdots \right)$$

The **sensitivity (dimensionless)** is therefore:
$$S = \frac{\Delta C / C}{\Delta d / d_0} \approx 1 + \frac{\Delta d}{d_0}$$

**Linearity discussion:**
- The response is **non-linear** because of the second-order term $\left(\Delta d / d_0\right)$.
- Fractional non-linearity ≈ $\Delta d / d_0$.
- For $\Delta d / d_0 < 0.1$ (i.e., displacement less than $10\%$ of $d_0$), the non-linearity is below $10\%$, which is acceptable for many practical uses.
- The sensitivity is **inversely proportional to $d_0^2$** — smaller gaps give dramatically higher sensitivity but worse linearity.

**(Marks distribution: 1 setup, 2 derivation, 2 sensitivity expression, 2 linearity discussion)**

#### Solution to (b) — Differential Capacitive Pressure Transducer:

A differential capacitive pressure transducer consists of **two fixed outer plates** and **one movable central plate** (diaphragm) coupled to the pressure being measured.

At equilibrium (zero pressure), the central plate is midway:
$$C_1 = C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

When a pressure $P$ deflects the central plate by $\Delta d$ towards plate 1:
$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d - \Delta d}, \quad C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d + \Delta d}$$

**Differential output:**
$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left( \frac{1}{d - \Delta d} - \frac{1}{d + \Delta d} \right)$$

$$= \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left( \frac{(d + \Delta d) - (d - \Delta d)}{d^2 - (\Delta d)^2} \right) = \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \Delta d}{d^2 - (\Delta d)^2}$$

For $\Delta d \ll d$:
$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \Delta d}{d^2}$$

Compared to a single-capacitor arrangement where:
$$\Delta C_{single} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \cdot \Delta d}{d^2}$$

**Therefore, the differential output is exactly TWICE the single-capacitor output:**
$$\Delta C_{diff} = 2 \times \Delta C_{single}$$

**Advantages of differential configuration:**
1. **Sensitivity is doubled.**
2. **Common-mode errors** (temperature drift, supply noise, vibration) cancel out because they affect both $C_1$ and $C_2$ equally.
3. **Improved linearity** for small $\Delta d$ because the second-order term in $d^2 - (\Delta d)^2$ is symmetric and the next non-linear term cancels.

**(Marks distribution: 2 architecture explanation, 3 derivation of $\Delta C$, 2 doubling proof + advantages)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a **capacitive level sensor** for measuring the level of a non-conductive liquid in a tank. Derive the expression for capacitance as a function of liquid level. (7 marks)

**(b)** A parallel-plate capacitive transducer has plate dimensions $30 \text{ mm} \times 30 \text{ mm}$ and is separated by a 2 mm air gap. If the dielectric constant of the liquid is 4, calculate: (i) the capacitance when the tank is empty, (ii) the capacitance when the tank is half-full, (iii) the capacitance when the tank is completely full. (7 marks)

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Solution to (a) — Capacitive Level Sensor:

**Construction:** Two long, narrow metallic electrodes (often concentric cylinders or parallel plates) are immersed vertically in the tank containing the non-conductive liquid. The electrodes are insulated from each other and connected to a capacitance measuring circuit.

**Working:** As the liquid level rises, it displaces air from the region between the electrodes. Since the liquid has a higher permittivity ($\varepsilon_r \approx 2 \text{ to } 80$) than air ($\varepsilon_r \approx 1$), the capacitance between the electrodes **increases monotonically** with the liquid level.

**Derivation:** Let $h$ be the height of the liquid and $H$ be the total electrode length. The plates have area $A = W \cdot H$ where $W$ is the width. The region between the plates consists of:
- A lower portion of thickness $h$ filled with the liquid ($\varepsilon_r$).
- An upper portion of thickness $(H - h)$ filled with air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$).

The two regions behave as **two capacitors in parallel**:
$$C_{liquid} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A_{liquid}}{d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (W \cdot h)}{d}$$

$$C_{air} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A_{air}}{d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 W (H - h)}{d}$$

Total capacitance:
$$C = C_{liquid} + C_{air} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 W}{d} \left[ \varepsilon_r h + (H - h) \right]$$

$$\boxed{C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 W}{d} \left[ H + h(\varepsilon_r - 1) \right]}$$

This is **linear in $h$** — an ideal transducer characteristic.

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical:

**Given:** Plate dimensions = $30 \text{ mm} \times 30 \text{ mm}$, so $W = 0.03 \text{ m}$, $H = 0.03 \text{ m}$, $d = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $\varepsilon_r = 4$.

**Common factor:**
$$K = \frac{\varepsilon_0 W}{d} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.03}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.3281 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F/m}$$

**(i) Tank empty ($h = 0$):**
$$C_{empty} = K \cdot H = 1.3281 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.03 = 3.984 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} \approx 3.98 \text{ pF}$$

**(ii) Tank half-full ($h = 0.015 \text{ m}$):**
$$C_{half} = K \left[ H + h(\varepsilon_r - 1) \right]$$
$$= 1.3281 \times 10^{-10} \left[ 0.03 + 0.015 \times 3 \right]$$
$$= 1.3281 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.075 = 9.96 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} \approx 9.96 \text{ pF}$$

**(iii) Tank completely full ($h = 0.03 \text{ m}$):**
$$C_{full} = K \left[ H + H(\varepsilon_r - 1) \right] = K \cdot H \cdot \varepsilon_r = C_{empty} \times \varepsilon_r$$
$$= 3.984 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 = 15.936 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} \approx 15.94 \text{ pF}$$

**Verification:** $C_{full} / C_{empty} = 4 = \varepsilon_r$ ✓ (matches theory)

**(Marks distribution: 1 calculation setup, 2 each for parts i/ii/iii)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS IN CAPACITIVE TRANSDUCER QUESTIONS:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Marks are routinely deducted when students use mm or cm without converting to SI units (metres). ALWAYS convert $A$ to $\text{m}^2$ and $d$ to m before computing capacitance.
> 2. **Using air permittivity in dielectric problems:** A common error is forgetting to multiply by $\varepsilon_r$ when a dielectric is present. If $\varepsilon_r$ is given, it must appear in every formula.
> 3. **Series vs Parallel confusion in level sensors:** For **parallel-plate** level sensors, the air and liquid regions are in **PARALLEL** (side-by-side, not stacked). For **concentric-cylinder** sensors, they are in **SERIES** (radially stacked). Mis-identifying the configuration leads to a fully wrong formula.
> 4. **Sign convention in sensitivity:** Students often forget the negative sign in $S_d = -C/d$ and lose a mark. Explicitly state that capacitance **increases** as $d$ **decreases** (inverse relationship).
> 5. **Non-linearity omission:** In variable-separation mode derivations, examiners specifically look for the **discussion of non-linearity** (binomial expansion and the $\Delta d/d_0$ term). A derivation without the non-linearity comment typically loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Guard ring not mentioned:** Any capacitive transducer design answer that does NOT mention the guard ring as a stray-capacitance mitigation technique is considered incomplete by KTU examiners.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU OEMET725 — Module 2: Capacitance Type — Final Rapid-Recap:**

- ✅ **Master formula:** $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$, where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
- ✅ **Three operating modes:** Variable $d$ (highest sensitivity, non-linear), Variable $A$ (linear, wide range), Variable $\varepsilon_r$ (level/humidity sensing).
- ✅ **Variable-separation sensitivity:** $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0 - \Delta d}$ — non-linear, hyperbolic.
- ✅ **Variable-area sensitivity:** $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C} = \dfrac{\Delta A}{A_0}$ — strictly linear, ideal for position sensing.
- ✅ **Differential (3-plate) configuration:** Output is **double** that of a single capacitor, with **common-mode noise cancellation**.
- ✅ **Guard ring is mandatory** in precision capacitive sensors to eliminate fringing fields and stray shunt capacitance.
- ✅ **Level sensor (parallel plates):** Air and liquid regions are in **parallel** — capacitance varies **linearly** with liquid height.
- ✅ **Signal conditioning:** AC Wheatstone bridge, transformer ratio arm bridge, RC oscillator (FM), or charge amplifier — capacitor alone gives no DC output.
- ✅ **Advantage:** Near-zero force, sub-nanometer resolution, high-temperature capable, low power, excellent for static (DC) measurements.
- ✅ **Disadvantage:** Highly sensitive to **stray capacitance, humidity, lead routing, and EMI** — shielding and guard electrodes are non-negotiable.
- ✅ **Always use SI units** (metres, not mm) in the formula — this is the #1 valuation pitfall.
- ✅ **Series vs Parallel** in level sensing: parallel plates → parallel combination; concentric cylinders → series combination.
- ✅ **Memorize the differential-output factor of 2** — it is a board-favorite derivation.
- ✅ **Fringe-field correction:** $C_{fringe} \approx \varepsilon_0 P$ (perimeter term) — guard ring effectively eliminates this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Transducers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Digital Transducers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Digital Transducer** is a measuring instrument that converts a physical input quantity (such as displacement, rotation, force, pressure, or temperature) directly into a **discrete digital output signal** — typically a binary-coded electrical pulse train, a Gray code word, or a frequency-modulated digital waveform — without requiring an intermediate analog signal stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A digital transducer is a device that converts a non-electrical physical quantity into a **digital electrical signal** (binary/frequency/pulse) such that the output is discrete, countable, and directly compatible with digital displays, microcontrollers, PLCs, and computer-based data acquisition systems.

The two broad families recognized in the KTU OEMET725 syllabus are:
1. **Shaft Encoders** (Angular/Rotary position to digital code)
2. **Digital Frequency/Tachometer Transducers** (Rotational speed to pulse frequency)
3. **Digital Temperature & Pressure Sensors** (Physical quantity to digital word)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The "Digital Ruler":**
> Imagine a traditional wooden ruler. To measure length, you *eyeball* where the object's edge falls between two millimeter marks — that is an **analog measurement** (continuous, fuzzy, requires human interpretation). Now imagine a smart laser measuring tape that beeps and directly displays "**127.4 mm**" on a digital LCD screen. The laser tape does not give you a continuous ink line; it gives you a **discrete number**. This number is the output of a *digital transducer* — it has converted a physical length into a clean, countable, numeric quantity that any digital circuit can read.

Another classic analogy is the **odometer in a car**:
- A car wheel rotates → mechanical rotation
- A digital transducer inside counts discrete **pulses** for every fraction of a revolution
- The dashboard displays a precise integer number of kilometers
- No analog-to-digital conversion chip is needed — the pulses themselves **are** the digital output.

---

## 1.3 Why Digital Transducers Matter in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> Modern Industry 4.0, CNC machines, robotics, and IoT sensors **demand** digital signals at the source. Digital transducers:
> - Eliminate signal degradation over long cables (**noise immunity**).
> - Provide direct compatibility with **microcontrollers, PLCs, and SCADA systems**.
> - Offer **high accuracy** limited only by the encoder's resolution (bit-depth), not by ADC quantization noise.
> - Enable **absolute position tracking** without power-loss data loss (in absolute encoders).

Common engineering constants/metrics used in this topic:
- **1 revolution = 360° = 2π rad**
- For an **n-bit** digital encoder, the number of distinct output codes is **N = 2ⁿ**.
- The **least count (resolution)** is **360° / 2ⁿ** degrees per LSB.

---

## 1.4 Classification Overview

Digital Transducers can be classified as:

| Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| Position (Rotary) | Absolute Shaft Encoder, Incremental Shaft Encoder |
| Position (Linear) | Linear Optical Encoder, Magnetic Linear Encoder |
| Speed | Optical Tachometer, Magnetic Pickup Tachometer, Stroboscope |
| Temperature | DS18B20, Thermocouple with Digital Output, RTD with ADC |
| Pressure | Piezoresistive with built-in ADC, Digital Manometer |
| Force/Load | Digital Load Cell with CAN/RS-485 output |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Shaft Encoders — The Heart of Digital Transducers

A **shaft encoder** is a rotary digital transducer mounted on the shaft of a machine whose angular position (or rotation count) is to be measured. It consists of:
- A **rotating disc (code disc)** patterned with transparent and opaque segments.
- A **light source (LED)** on one side of the disc.
- A set of **photodetectors (photodiodes/phototransistors)** on the other side.
- An **output conditioning circuit** that decodes the photodetector signals into a binary/decimal word.

The two principal types are:

### 2.1.1 Incremental Shaft Encoder
- Produces a **pulse train** as the shaft rotates.
- Has typically **two output channels (A and B)** that are 90° out of phase (quadrature encoding) to determine the direction of rotation.
- A third channel **Z (index pulse)** gives one pulse per revolution for the home/reference position.
- **Cannot remember absolute position** after a power-down — needs a homing cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** An incremental encoder gives **relative** displacement, while an absolute encoder gives **absolute** displacement at any instant, even after power loss.

### 2.1.2 Absolute Shaft Encoder
- The code disc has **multiple concentric tracks** (typically n tracks for an n-bit encoder).
- Each track has a unique pattern of transparent/opaque segments corresponding to a unique bit of a binary or Gray code.
- At any shaft angle, the photodetector array outputs a **unique n-bit word** that uniquely identifies the angular position.
- **Retains position information even after power-off.**

### 2.1.3 Binary Code vs Gray Code

| Code Type | Property | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Natural Binary | Multiple bits can change simultaneously → large transition errors | Rarely used directly |
| **Gray Code** | **Only one bit changes between adjacent positions** → no transition errors | **Preferred in KTU-emphasized encoders** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Gray Code?** In a natural binary encoder, when the shaft rotates from position 3 (011) to position 4 (100), all three bits change. If the photodetectors do not switch *perfectly simultaneously* (which is physically impossible due to manufacturing tolerances), the decoder may read a spurious value like 000 or 111. Gray code eliminates this by ensuring only **one bit flips per step**, guaranteeing monotonic, error-free readout.

---

## 2.2 Digital Tachometer

A **digital tachometer** measures rotational speed (rpm) by counting pulses per unit time.

**Operating principle:**
- An encoder/disc with N slots is mounted on the rotating shaft.
- A photo/ magnetic sensor produces **N pulses per revolution**.
- A microcontroller/counter measures the **pulse frequency f** (pulses per second).
- The rotational speed in **revolutions per minute (rpm)** is:

$$
n = \frac{60 \cdot f}{N} \quad \text{[rpm]}
$$

where **N** is the number of pulses (slots/teeth) per revolution.

> [!NOTE]
> The digital tachometer is essentially a **frequency-to-rpm converter**. It is the digital counterpart of the analog DC tachogenerator (whose output is a voltage proportional to speed).

---

## 2.3 Digital Temperature Transducers

- **Thermocouples** with cold-junction compensation and built-in 16/24-bit ADC (e.g., MAX31855) output SPI digital words proportional to temperature in °C.
- **RTD (Pt100/Pt1000)** with a 24-bit sigma-delta ADC (e.g., ADS1220) outputs a digital word.
- **Semiconductor digital sensors** (e.g., DS18B20) output a 9–12 bit digital temperature word over a single-wire digital bus.

## 2.4 Digital Pressure Transducers

- Combine a **strain-gauge or piezoresistive bridge** with a built-in **ADC + microcontroller + digital interface** (I²C, SPI, CAN, or 4–20 mA with HART protocol).
- Output: a digital word directly proportional to pressure in **bar, psi, or Pa**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table of Essential Formulas — Memorize for OEMET725 ESE:**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of distinct codes in an n-bit encoder | $N = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = number of tracks/bits | counts |
| 2 | Angular resolution (least count) | $\theta_{LC} = \dfrac{360°}{2^{n}}$ | $n$ = number of bits | degrees (°) |
| 3 | Angular resolution in radians | $\theta_{LC} = \dfrac{2\pi}{2^{n}}$ | $n$ = number of bits | radians (rad) |
| 4 | Angular position in degrees | $\theta = D \times \dfrac{360°}{2^{n}}$ | $D$ = decimal output word | degrees (°) |
| 5 | Speed from digital tachometer | $n = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{N}$ | $f$ = pulse freq, $N$ = pulses/rev | rpm |
| 6 | Pulse frequency from speed | $f = \dfrac{n \cdot N}{60}$ | $n$ = rpm, $N$ = pulses/rev | Hz |
| 7 | Displacement per pulse (linear encoder) | $d = \dfrac{L_{FS}}{N}$ | $L_{FS}$ = full-scale length | mm |
| 8 | Resolution in arc-minutes | $R_{min} = \dfrac{21600}{2^{n}}$ | $n$ = bits, $21600 = 360\times 60$ | arc-minutes |
| 9 | Gray → Binary conversion (bit $i$) | $G_i = B_i \oplus B_{i+1}$ | XOR operation | — |
| 10 | Quadrature decoding resolution | $R_{quad} = \dfrac{360°}{4 \cdot N}$ | $N$ = lines on disc | degrees |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TRAP:** Students often write $360°/n$ instead of $360°/2^{n}$. The denominator is **2 raised to the power of n**, not n itself. This single mistake has cost students 2–3 marks in past ESE papers.

---

## 2.6 Advantages & Disadvantages

**Advantages:**
- Direct digital output — no ADC needed.
- High noise immunity over long transmission lines.
- High accuracy (limited only by bit-depth).
- Easy interfacing with microcontrollers and PLCs.
- Absolute encoders retain position after power-off.

**Disadvantages:**
- Higher cost than analog equivalents.
- More complex mechanical/optical construction.
- Incremental encoders lose absolute position on power loss.
- Limited by the maximum count rate / shaft speed.
- Susceptible to dust, oil, and contamination in harsh industrial environments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resolution of an n-bit Absolute Shaft Encoder

**Step 1 — Define the problem.**
A code disc is divided equally into N segments around 360°. Each segment corresponds to a unique digital code. We want to find the smallest angular change that produces a change of 1 LSB in the output.

**Step 2 — Express N in terms of bits.**
For an n-bit encoder (n tracks on the disc, n photodetectors), each track independently has 2 states. The total number of unique code combinations is:

$$
N = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \dots \times 2}_{n \text{ times}} = 2^{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the angular step per LSB.**
Since 360° is divided into N equal sectors, the angular step per LSB is:

$$
\theta_{LSB} = \frac{\text{Total angle}}{\text{Number of steps}} = \frac{360°}{2^{n}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the same in radians.**

$$
\theta_{LSB} = \frac{2\pi}{2^{n}} \quad [\text{radians}]
$$

**Step 5 — Express the same in arc-minutes (often used in KTU problems).**
Since $1° = 60'$, we have:

$$
\theta_{LSB} = \frac{360 \times 60}{2^{n}} = \frac{21600}{2^{n}} \quad [\text{arc-minutes}]
$$

**Step 6 — Derive the position formula.**
If the encoder outputs a binary code whose decimal equivalent is $D$, the angular position $\theta$ of the shaft is:

$$
\theta = D \times \theta_{LSB} = D \times \frac{360°}{2^{n}}
$$

This is the master formula used in **every** KTU ESE problem on absolute encoders.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 (Model Problem)
