**Problem:** A 10-bit absolute shaft encoder is used to measure the angular position of a rotating shaft. The output binary word (in decimal equivalent) is 517.
**(a)** Find the angular resolution in degrees.
**(b)** Find the angular position of the shaft.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Using $\theta_{LSB} = 360°/2^{n}$ with $n = 10$:

$$
\theta_{LSB} = \frac{360°}{2^{10}} = \frac{360°}{1024} = 0.3515625°/\text{LSB}
$$

**(b)** Position with output word $D = 517$:

$$
\theta = 517 \times 0.3515625° = 181.7578125°
$$

**Verification:** Since 10-bit = 1024 codes, and 517 ≈ 1024/2, the position should be near 180°. ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Digital Tachometer

**Problem:** A digital tachometer uses an optical encoder disc with 60 slots. The output pulse train has a measured frequency of 2400 Hz. Find the shaft speed in rpm.

**Solution:**

Given $N = 60$ pulses/rev, $f = 2400$ Hz.

$$
n = \frac{60 \cdot f}{N} = \frac{60 \times 2400}{60} = 2400 \;\text{rpm}
$$

**Sanity check:** 2400 pulses per second ÷ 60 pulses per revolution = 40 revolutions per second × 60 = 2400 rpm. ✓

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Resolution Comparison

**Problem:** Compare the resolution of a 12-bit and a 16-bit absolute encoder. Express in degrees and arc-minutes.

**Solution:**

| Parameter | 12-bit | 16-bit |
|---|---|---|
| $2^{n}$ | 4096 | 65536 |
| $\theta_{LSB}$ (°) | 0.0879° | 0.0055° |
| $\theta_{LSB}$ (arc-min) | 5.27' | 0.33' |

**Key takeaway:** Each additional bit **doubles** the resolution (halves the LSB). Going from 12-bit to 16-bit gives **16× finer** resolution.

---

## 3.5 Gray Code to Binary Conversion — Step-by-Step Method

Gray code is the standard output of an absolute encoder. To convert an n-bit Gray code word $(G_{n-1} \ldots G_1 G_0)$ to a binary word $(B_{n-1} \ldots B_1 B_0)$:

**Step 1:** The MSB is the same:
$$
B_{n-1} = G_{n-1}
$$

**Step 2:** For every subsequent bit, XOR with the *previous binary bit*:
$$
B_i = G_i \oplus B_{i+1} \quad \text{for } i = n-2, n-3, \ldots, 0
$$

**Worked example:** Convert Gray code **1011** to Binary.

| Step | Gray bit | Operation | Binary bit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (MSB) | $G_3 = 1$ | $B_3 = G_3$ | $B_3 = 1$ |
| 2 | $G_2 = 0$ | $B_2 = G_2 \oplus B_3 = 0 \oplus 1$ | $B_2 = 1$ |
| 3 | $G_1 = 1$ | $B_1 = G_1 \oplus B_2 = 1 \oplus 1$ | $B_1 = 0$ |
| 4 (LSB) | $G_0 = 1$ | $B_0 = G_0 \oplus B_1 = 1 \oplus 0$ | $B_0 = 1$ |

**Result:** Gray `1011` → Binary `1101` (decimal 13). ✓

> [!NOTE]
> This conversion is essential when interfacing an absolute Gray-code encoder to a microcontroller or when KTU asks for "decoder logic" in the exam.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| Output type | Pulse train | Unique n-bit code per position |
| Position info | Lost on power-off | Retained always |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Complexity | Simple | Multiple tracks needed |
| Resolution control | Determined by N (lines on disc) | Determined by n (number of tracks) |
| Speed measurement | Easy (frequency of pulses) | Possible but requires decoding |
| Home/Index pulse | Required for reference | Not required |
| Use case | CNC feed-back, conveyor speed | Robot joint angles, telescope mounts |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Digital Shaft Encoder System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shaft Rotation] --> B[Code Disc with Tracks]
    B --> C[LED Light Source]
    C --> D[Photodetector Array]
    D --> E[Signal Conditioning and Amplifier]
    E --> F[Decoder Logic: Gray to Binary]
    F --> G[Parallel or Serial Digital Output]
    G --> H[Microcontroller or PLC]
    H --> I[Digital Display or SCADA]
    D --> J[Index Pulse Z Channel]
    J --> H
```

## 4.2 Working Principle — Detailed Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Mechanical Input Shaft] --> B[Encoder Disc with Concentric Tracks]
    B1[Track 1 MSB] --> C1[Photodetector 1]
    B2[Track 2] --> C2[Photodetector 2]
    B3[Track n LSB] --> Cn[Photodetector n]
    C1 --> D1[Comparator]
    C2 --> D2[Comparator]
    Cn --> Dn[Comparator]
    D1 --> E[Parallel n bit Gray Code Word]
    D2 --> E
    Dn --> E
    E --> F[Gray to Binary Converter using XOR gates]
    F --> G[n bit Binary Output Word]
    G --> H[Digital Interface: SPI, I2C, Parallel Bus]
    H --> I[Processing Unit]
    I --> J[Display and Control Action]
```

## 4.3 Digital Tachometer — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rotating Shaft] --> B[Slotted Disc with N Slots]
    B --> C[LED and Photodiode Pair]
    C --> D[Pulse Train Output]
    D --> E[Schmitt Trigger Shaper]
    E --> F[Frequency Counter or Microcontroller]
    F --> G[Calculate rpm: n = 60f slash N]
    G --> H[Digital LCD Display of Speed]
```

## 4.4 Incremental vs Absolute Encoder — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Digital Transducer] --> B{Need absolute position after power loss?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Absolute Encoder]
    B -- No --> D{Just need relative motion or speed?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Incremental Encoder]
    D -- No --> F[Re-evaluate application]
    C --> G[Select Gray code for noise immunity]
    E --> H{Quadrature decoding required?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use two channels A and B in quadrature]
    H -- No --> J[Single channel sufficient]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a digital transducer. How is it different from an analog transducer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **digital transducer** is a device that converts a non-electrical physical quantity (such as displacement, force, temperature, or pressure) directly into a **discrete digital output signal** — typically a binary code, pulse train, or frequency-modulated waveform — that can be directly read by digital systems.

**Key differences from an analog transducer:**

| Aspect | Analog Transducer | Digital Transducer |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Continuous (e.g., 0–10 V, 4–20 mA) | Discrete (binary code, pulses) |
| Resolution | Limited by ADC quantization | Limited by bit-depth of encoder |
| Noise immunity | Low — degrades with cable length | High — pulses are regenerated |
| Interfacing | Requires separate ADC | Direct to microcontrollers / PLCs |
| Example | Strain gauge bridge | Optical shaft encoder, DS18B20 |

**(3 marks)** ✓

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Gray code? Why is it preferred over natural binary code in absolute shaft encoders?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Gray code** is a binary code in which **only one bit changes** between any two successive (adjacent) codes in the sequence.

**Why preferred over natural binary in shaft encoders:**
1. In natural binary code, multiple bits can change simultaneously between adjacent positions (e.g., 011 → 100 changes all three bits).
2. Due to mechanical/photodetector tolerances, the bits **never switch at exactly the same instant**, leading to **spurious intermediate readings** (e.g., the decoder may briefly read 000 or 111).
3. Gray code ensures **only one bit transitions at a time**, so no spurious codes can ever be generated.
4. The microcontroller then converts the Gray code to natural binary using XOR-based logic.

**(3 marks)** ✓

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **OPTION A — Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2 OEMET725]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working principle of an **absolute shaft encoder** with a neat block diagram. Compare it with an **incremental shaft encoder**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 12-bit absolute Gray-code shaft encoder is used on a CNC milling machine. The current shaft output (in Gray) is `101101001011`. Find:
1. The equivalent binary word.
2. The decimal value.
3. The angular position of the shaft in degrees.
4. The resolution of the encoder in degrees and arc-minutes.

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Construction & Working of Absolute Shaft Encoder [7 marks]**

**Construction:**
- A **circular code disc** mounted on the rotating shaft. The disc is made of glass or plastic with **n concentric tracks** (n = number of bits).
- Each track is patterned with **alternating transparent and opaque sectors**, with the pattern unique to that bit position (MSB track has the slowest-changing pattern, LSB has the fastest).
- A **light source (LED)** is placed on one side of the disc, and an array of **n photodetectors (photodiodes)** is placed on the other side — one photodetector per track.
- The output of each photodetector is a **0 or 1** depending on whether the disc segment in front of it is transparent (1) or opaque (0).
- A **decoder/conditioning circuit** interprets the n-bit pattern.

**Working Principle:**
- As the shaft rotates, each photodetector outputs a continuous 0/1 stream.
- At any instant, the combination of n detector outputs forms a **unique n-bit Gray code word** corresponding to that exact angular position.
- This digital word is then converted to natural binary (via XOR logic) and either displayed directly or sent to a controller.

**Comparison with Incremental Encoder [included for 7-mark credit]:**

| Feature | Absolute Encoder | Incremental Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| Tracks / Channels | n tracks (n-bit) | Typically 2 channels (A, B) + index Z |
| Output | Unique code per position | Pulse train |
| Position after power-off | **Retained** | **Lost** — needs homing |
| Cost & complexity | High | Low |
| Use case | Robot joint, telescope | Motor speed, conveyor |

**(7 marks) ✓**

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical on 12-bit Gray-Code Encoder [7 marks]**

**Given:** Gray code word = `101101001011` (12 bits), n = 12.

**Step 1 — Gray to Binary Conversion (using XOR rule $B_i = G_i \oplus B_{i+1}$):**

Label bits from MSB (position 11) to LSB (position 0):

| Position | Gray $G$ | Binary $B$ | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 11 (MSB) | 1 | 1 | $B_{11} = G_{11}$ |
| 10 | 0 | 1 | $B_{10} = G_{10} \oplus B_{11} = 0 \oplus 1 = 1$ |
| 9 | 1 | 0 | $B_9 = G_9 \oplus B_{10} = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$ |
| 8 | 1 | 1 | $B_8 = G_8 \oplus B_9 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1$ |
| 7 | 0 | 1 | $B_7 = G_7 \oplus B_8 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1$ |
| 6 | 1 | 0 | $B_6 = G_6 \oplus B_7 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$ |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | $B_5 = G_5 \oplus B_6 = 0 \oplus 0 = 0$ |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | $B_4 = G_4 \oplus B_5 = 0 \oplus 0 = 0$ |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | $B_3 = G_3 \oplus B_4 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1$ |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | $B_2 = G_2 \oplus B_3 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1$ |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | $B_1 = G_1 \oplus B_2 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$ |
| 0 (LSB) | 1 | 1 | $B_0 = G_0 \oplus B_1 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1$ |

**Binary word:** `1101100001101`

**Step 2 — Convert binary `1101100001101` to decimal:**

$$
D = 2^{11} + 2^{10} + 2^{8} + 2^{7} + 2^{3} + 2^{2} + 2^{0}
$$

$$
D = 2048 + 1024 + 256 + 128 + 8 + 4 + 1 = 3469
$$

**Step 3 — Angular position:**

Using $\theta = D \times \dfrac{360°}{2^{n}}$ with $D = 3469$ and $n = 12$:

$$
\theta = 3469 \times \frac{360°}{4096} = 3469 \times 0.0878906° = 304.86°
$$

**Step 4 — Resolution:**

In degrees:
$$
\theta_{LSB} = \frac{360°}{2^{12}} = \frac{360°}{4096} = 0.08789°/\text{LSB}
$$

In arc-minutes:
$$
\theta_{LSB} = \frac{21600}{4096} = 5.2734 \; \text{arc-minutes/LSB}
$$

**(7 marks) ✓**

---

### **OPTION B — Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2 OEMET725]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle and working of a **digital tachometer** with a block diagram. Derive the relation between shaft speed (rpm), pulse frequency, and the number of slots on the encoder disc.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A digital tachometer uses a 24-slot disc. The pulse frequency is measured as 800 Hz. Calculate:
1. The shaft speed in rpm.
2. The pulse frequency if the shaft is rotating at 1500 rpm.
3. The number of slots required so that the pulse frequency equals the shaft rpm (numerically).

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Digital Tachometer — Principle & Derivation [7 marks]**

**Principle:**
A digital tachometer is essentially a **frequency-measuring instrument** that converts the rotational speed of a shaft into a proportional electrical pulse frequency. The pulse frequency is then counted and displayed as rpm.

**Construction & Working:**
- A **slotted disc** (or reflective disc with N marks) is mounted on the rotating shaft.
- On one side of the disc, an **LED** shines light; on the other side, a **photodiode** (or phototransistor) detects the light.
- Every time a slot passes between the LED and photodetector, a **light pulse** is generated.
- After amplification and Schmitt-trigger shaping, this becomes a clean **square-wave pulse train** with frequency $f$.
- A **microcontroller or frequency counter** measures $f$ and computes the rpm.

**Derivation:**
- Number of slots on disc = $N$
- Number of revolutions per second = $n / 60$ (since n is in rpm)
- Pulses per second (frequency $f$):

$$
f = N \times \frac{n}{60} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \frac{60 f}{N} \quad \text{[rpm]}
$$

This is the **fundamental formula** for digital tachometers.

**(7 marks) ✓**

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical on Digital Tachometer [7 marks]**

**Given:** $N = 24$ slots, $f = 800$ Hz (for part 1).

**Step 1 — Shaft speed at $f = 800$ Hz:**

$$
n = \frac{60 \times f}{N} = \frac{60 \times 800}{24} = \frac{48000}{24} = 2000 \;\text{rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Pulse frequency at $n = 1500$ rpm:**

$$
f = \frac{n \times N}{60} = \frac{1500 \times 24}{60} = 25 \times 24 = 600 \;\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of slots so that $f$ (in Hz) = $n$ (in rpm) numerically:**

We need $f = n$ numerically. From the formula:

$$
f = \frac{n \times N}{60} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{For } f = n: \quad n = \frac{n \times N}{60}
$$

This implies $N = 60$. So a **60-slot disc** gives $f(\text{Hz}) = n(\text{rpm})$ numerically.

**(7 marks) ✓**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in OEMET725 ESE:**
> 1. **Denominator error:** Writing $\theta_{LSB} = 360°/n$ instead of $360°/2^{n}$. This alone can cost 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Gray code direction confusion:** Students often XOR in the wrong direction. Remember: **MSB is identical**, and every subsequent binary bit is the XOR of the *current Gray bit* and the *previous binary bit* (the one just to its left).
> 3. **Units mix-up:** Mixing up **arc-minutes and arc-seconds**, or treating **Hz and rpm** as equivalent without the 60 multiplier. Always show the unit conversions explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing absolute and incremental encoders:** When the question says "position must be known at power-on," you MUST use an absolute encoder — incremental will lose position. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Forgetting the index pulse (Z channel):** When describing incremental encoders, always mention the third Z channel used for the **home/reference** position.
> 6. **No block diagram in long answers:** A 7-mark construction question without a labeled block diagram typically loses 1–2 marks. Always draw one, even if rough.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OEMET725 — Module 2: Digital Transducers — Last-Minute Revision**

- ☐ A **digital transducer** converts a physical quantity directly into a **discrete digital output** (binary, pulse, or frequency).
- ☐ The two principal rotary digital transducers are **absolute** and **incremental** shaft encoders.
- ☐ An **n-bit absolute encoder** has $2^{n}$ unique codes, with **resolution = 360°/2ⁿ**.
- ☐ **Gray code** is used (not natural binary) to avoid spurious codes at bit transitions.
- ☐ **Incremental encoders** use **quadrature** (A, B channels 90° apart) to detect direction and an **index Z** pulse for the home position.
- ☐ **Digital tachometer** formula: $n = 60 f / N$ (rpm), where $N$ = pulses per revolution, $f$ = pulse frequency.
- ☐ **Gray to Binary**: MSB unchanged, then $B_i = G_i \oplus B_{i+1}$ for each lower bit.
- ☐ **Digital temperature** sensors (e.g., DS18B20) output 9–12 bit digital words over a 1-Wire bus.
- ☐ **Digital pressure** sensors combine a strain-gauge bridge with a built-in ADC + serial interface.
- ☐ **Advantages** of digital transducers: noise immunity, direct microcontroller interfacing, no separate ADC required.
- ☐ **Disadvantages**: higher cost, contamination sensitivity, incremental types lose position on power-off.
- ☐ Each **additional bit** doubles the resolution (halves the LSB).
- ☐ **Quadrature decoding** quadruples the effective resolution: $R_{quad} = 360°/(4N)$.
- ☐ A **60-slot disc** is the standard choice when one wants the pulse frequency (Hz) to numerically equal the shaft speed (rpm).
- ☐ Remember the constant **21600 arc-minutes per revolution** for resolution problems.
- ☐ Always **draw a labeled block diagram** for 7-mark construction questions.
- ☐ When asked "why Gray code?", mention: **only one bit changes per step → no transition errors**.

---

**End of Module 2 — Digital Transducers Notes (OEMET725, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nozzle Flapper Transducer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Nozzle Flapper Transducer # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Nozzle Flapper Transducer** (also known as the **Pneumatic Nozzle-Flapper Element** or **Pneumatic Detector**) is a fundamental first-order **pneumatic transducer** that converts a small mechanical displacement (linear motion of a flapper) into a proportional **pneumatic pressure signal** at its output port. It functions as the **primary sensing element** in pneumatic controllers, force-balance systems, and a wide class of **pneumatic measuring instruments**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *Nozzle Flapper Transducer* is a pneumatic element in which the gap (distance) between a fixed nozzle and a movable flapper controls the back pressure inside the nozzle, which is taken as the output pneumatic signal proportional to the input mechanical displacement. | Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Engineering Value | |---|---|---| | Supply Pressure | $P_s$ | **20 kPa (1.4 bar)** or **138 kPa (20 psi)** | | Operating Range of Flapper Displacement | $x$ | **0.025 mm to 0.2 mm** | | Nozzle Diameter | $d$ | **0.75 mm to 1.5 mm** | | Output Pressure Range | $P_o$ | **0 to 20 kPa** | | Linearity Region | $x$ | **x < 0.05 mm (small displacements)** | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy: The "Whistle Gap" Principle** Imagine you are blowing air through a small whistle (the **nozzle**). Now bring your thumb close to the whistle opening (the **flapper**). The closer your thumb gets to the whistle, the more **back-pressure** builds up behind the whistle, and the louder the whistle's tone becomes. The pressure of air trapped behind the whistle changes based on how close your thumb is to it. Now, replace: - Your **mouth** → Constant high-pressure air supply ($P_s = 20$ kPa) - The **whistle** → Fixed nozzle of small diameter - Your **thumb** → A precision-engineered metal flapper attached to a measuring bellows or force-summing mechanism When the **input physical quantity** (force, pressure, displacement) acts on the bellows and moves the flapper **closer to the nozzle**, the gap decreases → the **back pressure rises**. When the input moves the flapper **away**, the gap increases → the **back pressure drops**. This continuous, gap-dependent pressure variation is the **output pneumatic signal** that drives downstream pneumatic relays, indicators, or controllers. > [!TIP] > **Key Intuition:** The device exploits a **non-linear fluid resistance relationship**. The nozzle gap acts as a variable orifice whose pneumatic resistance varies inversely with the gap distance squared, producing a highly sensitive pressure-displacement conversion at very small displacements (microns). --- ## 1.3 Significance in KTU 2024 Curriculum This topic is a **core building block** for: - **Pneumatic Controllers** (Module 3 / Module 4 of OEMET725) - **Force-Balance Type Pressure Transmitters** - **Pneumatic PID Controllers** (Foxboro SPEC 200 series, historically) - **Aircraft Instrumentation** (legacy Boeing 707/727 systems) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Marks are commonly awarded for the **sketch** with **labelled pressure points** ($P_s$, $P_o$, $P_b$) and the **characteristic curve** of $P_o$ vs $x$. Always draw the figure first before writing theory. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Construction and Components The Nozzle Flapper Transducer consists of three primary elements: 1. **Supply Orifice (Fixed Restriction):** A precisely calibrated fixed orifice of diameter ~0.2 mm that admits the constant supply pressure $P_s$ into the chamber. 2. **Nozzle:** A precisely bored circular nozzle of diameter $d$ (~0.75–1.5 mm) facing the flapper. Air continuously leaks from the nozzle into the atmosphere through the gap $x$. 3. **Flapper:** A flat metal plate (typically stainless steel) attached to the input sensing element (bellows, diaphragm, or force-summing linkage). The flapper tip is positioned very close (~0.05 mm) to the nozzle face. A **pneumatic relay** (often a 1:1 pressure booster or amplifying relay) is connected to the nozzle back-pressure chamber to provide the high-capacity output signal $P_o$. --- ## 2.2 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The operation is governed by **two opposing airflows** meeting at the nozzle: - **Inflow rate** ($Q_{in}$): Air entering the chamber from supply through fixed orifice. - **Outflow rate** ($Q_{out}$): Air escaping from nozzle through gap $x$ to atmosphere. The **back pressure** $P_o$ inside the chamber is determined by the **dynamic equilibrium**: $$Q_{in}(P_s - P_o) = Q_{out}(P_o, x)$$ ### Detailed Logic Steps: - **Step 1 — Supply Phase:** Compressed air at $P_s$ flows through the fixed restriction (supply orifice) into a small chamber of volume $V$. - **Step 2 — Leak Phase:** Air continuously escapes from the nozzle into the atmosphere. The leak rate depends on the **gap distance** $x$ between nozzle and flapper. - **Step 3 — Equilibrium:** A steady-state back pressure $P_o$ is established when inflow equals outflow. - **Step 4 — Displacement Sensing:** When input displacement **decreases** $x$ (flapper moves toward nozzle): - Leak area $A_{gap} = \pi d x$ decreases. - Pneumatic resistance $R_{gap}$ increases dramatically. - Back pressure $P_o$ **increases sharply**. - **Step 5 — Signal Amplification:** The nozzle back pressure drives a **pneumatic relay** (e.g., a diaphragm-operated pilot valve) that produces a high-capacity output $P_o$ capable of driving long transmission lines and actuators. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Equation # | Formula | Description | Units / Domain | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | **1** | $Q_{in} = K_1 (P_s - P_o)$ | Inflow through fixed supply orifice (laminar flow assumption) | $K_1$ = constant $\left[\frac{m^3}{s \cdot Pa}\right]$ | | **2** | $Q_{out} = K_2 \cdot P_o \cdot x$ | Outflow through nozzle gap (linearised for small $x$) | $K_2$ = constant | | **3** | $P_o = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + \dfrac{K_2}{K_1} x}$ | Linearised steady-state output pressure | Valid for **laminar** flow, small $x$ | | **4** | $P_o = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + \left(\dfrac{C}{x}\right)^{2}}$ | Standard non-linear back-pressure relation (turbulent flow regime) | $C$ = nozzle geometry constant $\approx 0.025$ mm | | **5** | $S = \dfrac{dP_o}{dx} = \dfrac{2 P_s C^{2}}{x^{3}}$ | **Sensitivity** (slope of characteristic curve) | Very high for small $x$ | | **6** | $A_{gap} = \pi d x$ | Leakage area between nozzle and flapper | $d$ = nozzle diameter | | **7** | $\Delta P_o = \dfrac{\partial P_o}{\partial x} \Delta x + \dfrac{\partial P_o}{\partial P_s} \Delta P_s$ | Total differential output (for error analysis) | Linearisation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sensitivity Divergence:** Note from Equation 5 that as $x \rightarrow 0$, the sensitivity $S \rightarrow \infty$. This **non-linear amplification** is why the nozzle-flapper is so effective as a first-stage detector — it provides enormous gain at small displacements. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The nozzle-flapper transducer is **historically pivotal** in: - **Process Industries:** Foundational element of Foxboro, Taylor, and Honeywell pneumatic controllers (pre-digital era, 1940–1980). - **Aircraft Altimeters & Air-Data Computers:** Used in altimeters to sense capsule expansion and convert it into a pneumatic pressure signal. - **Force-Balance Pressure Transmitters:** Converts differential pressure (from a diaphragm capsule) into a 3–15 psi pneumatic signal. - **Hydraulic Servovalves:** The flapper-nozzle forms the **first stage** (pilot stage) of a two-stage electrohydraulic servovalve, controlling the main spool position. - **Modern MEMS Pneumatic Sensors:** Miniaturised versions of the principle are used in microfluidic flow controllers. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Nozzle Back-Pressure Equation (Standard Form) ### Given Assumptions: - Steady-state flow condition: $Q_{in} = Q_{out}$. - Supply pressure $P_s$ is constant. - Flow through the fixed supply orifice is **laminar** (proportional to pressure difference). - Flow through the nozzle-gap is governed by a **quadratic resistance** (turbulent flow regime). ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Apply Conservation of Mass (Continuity at Steady State):** At steady state, the mass flow rate entering the chamber equals the mass flow rate leaving through the nozzle gap. $$Q_{in} = Q_{out}$$ **Step 2 — Express Inflow Through the Fixed Supply Orifice:** Assuming laminar flow (Hagen–Poiseuille type), the volume flow rate through the supply orifice is proportional to the pressure drop across it: $$Q_{in} = K_1 \left(P_s - P_o\right)$$ where $K_1$ is a constant that depends on the supply orifice diameter, air viscosity, and temperature. **Step 3 — Express Outflow Through the Nozzle Gap:** The mass flow through an annular gap of area $A_{gap} = \pi d x$ depends on the back pressure $P_o$ and the gap area. For compressible turbulent flow through a thin orifice, this is often modelled as a **quadratic** function of the gap: $$Q_{out} = K_2 \cdot P_o \cdot f(x)$$ For a thin gap with small $x$, the leakage area dominates and we approximate: $$Q_{out} = K_3 \cdot P_o \cdot x$$ where $K_3$ is a geometry-dependent constant. **Step 4 — Equate Inflow and Outflow:** $$K_1 \left(P_s - P_o\right) = K_3 \cdot P_o \cdot x$$ **Step 5 — Solve Algebraically for $P_o$:** $$K_1 P_s - K_1 P_o = K_3 P_o x$$ $$K_1 P_s = K_1 P_o + K_3 P_o x = P_o \left(K_1 + K_3 x\right)$$ $$P_o = \dfrac{K_1 P_s}{K_1 + K_3 x}$$ **Step 6 — Introduce the Nozzle Geometry Constant $C$:** Let $C = \dfrac{K_1}{K_3}$, a constant with units of length representing the equivalent nozzle-gap distance. The equation can be rearranged into the **standard KTU textbook form**: $$\boxed{P_o = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + \dfrac{x}{C} \cdot \text{(correction factor)}}}$$ For the **more accurate turbulent flow** model, where leakage scales as $x^{2}$, we obtain: $$\boxed{P_o = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + \left(\dfrac{C}{x}\right)^{2}}}$$ **Step 7 — Physical Interpretation:** - When $x \rightarrow \infty$ (flapper far away): $P_o \rightarrow 0$ (atmospheric) ✓ - When $x \rightarrow 0$ (flapper closes nozzle): $P_o \rightarrow P_s$ (full supply pressure) ✓ - The transition is **highly non-linear** with maximum sensitivity near $x = C$. --- ## 3.2 Sensitivity Derivation The **static sensitivity** of the transducer is the derivative of output pressure with respect to flapper displacement, evaluated at the operating point. **Step 1 — Define Sensitivity:** $$S = \dfrac{dP_o}{dx}$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate the Non-Linear Equation:** Using the chain rule on $P_o = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + (C/x)^2} = \dfrac{P_s x^2}{x^2 + C^2}$: $$\dfrac{dP_o}{dx} = P_s \cdot \dfrac{d}{dx}\left[\dfrac{x^2}{x^2 + C^2}\right]$$ Apply the **quotient rule**: $\dfrac{d}{dx}\left[\dfrac{u}{v}\right] = \dfrac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}$ With $u = x^2$ and $v = x^2 + C^2$: $$\dfrac{u'v - uv'}{v^2} = \dfrac{(2x)(x^2 + C^2) - (x^2)(2x)}{(x^2 + C^2)^2}$$ $$= \dfrac{2x^3 + 2x C^2 - 2x^3}{(x^2 + C^2)^2} = \dfrac{2x C^2}{(x^2 + C^2)^2}$$ **Step 3 — Final Sensitivity Expression:** $$\boxed{S = \dfrac{dP_o}{dx} = \dfrac{2 P_s x C^{2}}{(x^{2} + C^{2})^{2}}}$$ **Step 4 — Special Case (Linear Region, $x \ll C$):** When $x \ll C$, the denominator $x^2 + C^2 \approx C^2$, simplifying to: $$S \approx \dfrac{2 P_s}{C^{2}} \cdot x$$ This shows that in the small-displacement regime, sensitivity is **approximately linear** in $x$ — useful for proportional control applications. **Step 5 — Maximum Sensitivity:** To find the maximum, differentiate $S$ with respect to $x$ and set to zero. The maximum sensitivity occurs at: $$x_{max} = \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{3}}$$ with magnitude $S_{max} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{8} \cdot \dfrac{P_s}{C}$. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operating Regions | Operating Region | Gap $x$ Range | Behaviour | Typical Use | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Region I — Saturation** | $x \rightarrow 0$ | $P_o \rightarrow P_s$, low sensitivity | End-stop / safety | | **Region II — Highly Sensitive** | $0 < x < 0.5C$ | **Quadratic rise**, max gain | **Primary operating zone** | | **Region III — Linear Region** | $x \approx C$ to $2C$ | Near-linear $P_o$ vs $x$ | Proportional control | | **Region IV — Saturation** | $x \gg C$ | $P_o \rightarrow 0$, low sensitivity | Off-state | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional signal flow** of a complete nozzle-flapper transducer system integrated with a pneumatic relay and load. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Physical Quantity
Force / Displacement / Pressure] --> B[Sensing Element
Bellows or Diaphragm] B --> C[Flapper Lever
Mechanical Linkage] C --> D[Nozzle Gap
Variable Orifice x] E[Constant Supply Pressure
Ps = 20 kPa] --> F[Fixed Supply Orifice
Restriction] F --> D D --> G[Pneumatic Chamber
Back Pressure Po] G --> H[Pneumatic Relay
Amplifier 1:1 or Higher] H --> I[Output Pneumatic Signal
3 to 15 psi] I --> J[Downstream Load
Indicator or Actuator] G -.Feedback Path.-> C ``` --- ## 4.2 Construction Schematic (Block Representation) Since a literal cross-sectional drawing of the nozzle-flapper assembly cannot be rendered in Mermaid, a **block-level architecture** showing the physical relationships is presented. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SUPPLY[AIR SUPPLY SYSTEM] S1[Compressor] --> S2[Pressure Regulator] S2 --> S3[Filter and Lubricator] S3 --> S4[Constant Pressure Source
Ps] end subgraph TRANSDUCER[NOZZLE FLAPPER TRANSDUCER CORE] T1[Fixed Supply Orifice
Diameter d1 approx 0.2 mm] --> T2[Pneumatic Chamber
Volume V] T2 --> T3[Nozzle Exit
Diameter d2 approx 1 mm] T3 --> T4[Gap Variable x
0.025 to 0.2 mm] T4 --> T5[Flapper Plate
Stainless Steel] T5 --> T6[To Atmosphere
Atmospheric Leak] T2 -.Back Pressure Po.-> T7 end subgraph AMPLIFIER[PNEUMATIC RELAY STAGE] T7[Pilot Pressure Input] --> A1[Relay Diaphragm] A1 --> A2[Ball or Poppet Valve] A2 --> A3[Relay Output Chamber] A3 --> A4[High Capacity Output
Po amplified] end subgraph OUTPUT[DOWNSTREAM USAGE] A4 --> O1[Transmission Line
20 m typical] O1 --> O2[Pneumatic Indicator or Controller] end S4 --> T1 ``` --- ## 4.3 Pressure-Displacement Characteristic Curve Description The relationship between **output back pressure $P_o$** and **flapper displacement $x$** exhibits a **highly non-linear S-shaped (sigmoidal) profile**: | Displacement Regime | Curve Behaviour | Pressure Change | |---|---|---| | Very small $x$ (~0 to $0.025$ mm) | Steep, almost vertical rise | $P_o$ rises from $0$ to $\approx 60\%$ of $P_s$ | | Mid-range $x$ (~0.025 to $0.1$ mm) | Gradual plateau / curve | $P_o$ rises from $60\%$ to $90\%$ of $P_s$ | | Large $x$ ($> 0.1$ mm) | Approaches saturation asymptotically | $P_o$ tends to $\approx P_s$ as nozzle fully seals | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Sketch Requirement:** When asked to draw the characteristic curve, always plot $P_o$ on the **Y-axis** (vertical) and **$x$** on the **X-axis** (horizontal). Mark three key regions: **linear operating zone**, **high-gain zone**, and **saturation zone**. Indicate the operating point near $x = C$. --- ## 4.4 Component Identification Reference | Mermaid Node Label | Engineering Component | Function | |---|---|---| | Fixed Supply Orifice | Brass jewel orifice | Restricts inflow to chamber | | Pneumatic Chamber | Cylindrical cavity | Stores back pressure | | Nozzle Exit | Precision-bored orifice | Variable leakage path | | Flapper Plate | Thin stainless-steel cantilever | Moves to vary gap | | Relay Diaphragm | Elastic metal diaphragm | Senses pilot pressure | | Ball or Poppet Valve | Spring-loaded valve element | Amplifies flow capacity | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define a Nozzle Flapper Transducer. Mention any two applications.** **Model Answer:** A **Nozzle Flapper Transducer** is a pneumatic element that converts small mechanical displacements into proportional pneumatic pressure signals by varying the gap between a fixed nozzle and a movable flapper. The back pressure developed in the nozzle chamber is a function of the gap distance $x$ and is taken as the output signal. **Two Applications:** 1. **First-stage detector in pneumatic PID controllers** (e.g., Foxboro SPEC 200 controllers). 2. **Pilot stage of two-stage electrohydraulic servovalves** in motion control systems. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two applications = 3 marks)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List any three advantages of pneumatic transducers over electrical transducers.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Intrinsic Safety:** Operates on compressed air, hence safe in explosive and flammable environments (oil refineries, chemical plants, mining). 2. **Immunity to Electromagnetic Interference (EMI):** No susceptibility to RF noise, lightning, or stray magnetic fields. 3. **High Reliability in Harsh Environments:** Resistant to dust, humidity, temperature extremes, and vibration; ideal for aerospace and marine applications. *(1 mark per advantage × 3 = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice Format) ### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **(a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a Nozzle Flapper Transducer. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Construction (3 marks):** The nozzle-flapper transducer consists of: - A **supply pressure source** $P_s$ (typically 20 kPa / 1.4 bar) connected through a **fixed supply orifice** of small diameter (~0.2 mm) into a **pressure chamber**. - The chamber connects to a precisely bored **nozzle** (~1 mm diameter) facing a movable **flapper plate** (stainless steel) attached to a **sensing element** (bellows, diaphragm, or force-balance linkage). - A **pneumatic relay** (typically 1:1 amplifying relay) connected to the back-pressure port provides amplified output $P_o$. **Working Principle (4 marks):** Compressed air enters the chamber through the fixed orifice and leaks continuously from the nozzle into the atmosphere through the small gap $x$ between nozzle and flapper. At steady state: $$Q_{in} = Q_{out} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_o = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + (C/x)^{2}}$$ When the input physical quantity (pressure, force, displacement) deflects the sensing element, the flapper moves: - **Towards nozzle** ($x$ decreases) → leak area decreases → $P_o$ **increases**. - **Away from nozzle** ($x$ increases) → leak area increases → $P_o$ **decreases**. This produces a continuous, non-linear pneumatic output signal proportional to the input displacement. The pneumatic relay amplifies this low-capacity signal into a robust output capable of driving long transmission lines and actuators. --- **(b) Derive the relationship between output back pressure $P_o$ and flapper displacement $x$ for a nozzle-flapper transducer. Discuss its sensitivity. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Derivation (5 marks):** Applying continuity at steady state: $Q_{in} = Q_{out}$. Inflow through supply orifice (laminar): $$Q_{in} = K_1 (P_s - P_o)$$ Outflow through nozzle gap (turbulent, quadratic dependence on $x$): $$Q_{out} = K_3 P_o x$$ Equating: $$K_1 (P_s - P_o) = K_3 P_o x$$ Solving for $P_o$: $$P_o = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + \dfrac{K_3}{K_1} x} = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + (C/x)^{2}}$$ where $C = K_1 / K_3$ is the **nozzle geometry constant**. **Sensitivity Discussion (2 marks):** $$S = \dfrac{dP_o}{dx} = \dfrac{2 P_s x C^{2}}{(x^{2} + C^{2})^{2}}$$ Sensitivity is **maximum** at $x = C / \sqrt{3}$ and approaches **infinity** as $x \rightarrow 0$. The transducer exhibits its highest gain in the small-displacement regime (typically 0.025–0.05 mm), making it ideal for detecting minute mechanical movements. --- ### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a) Explain with a block diagram the role of a pneumatic relay in a nozzle-flapper transducer system. Why is it necessary? (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Role of Pneumatic Relay (5 marks):** The **pneumatic relay** is connected between the nozzle back-pressure port and the output line. It functions as a **high-gain pressure amplifier** (typically 1:1 with flow amplification of 100:1 or more). A typical relay consists of: - A **diaphragm** exposed to the pilot pressure (from nozzle back pressure) on one side and atmospheric pressure on the other. - A **ball valve or poppet valve** that modulates the supply air flow to the output chamber. - A **feedback bellows** providing negative feedback for stability. **Block Diagram:** $$\text{Nozzle } P_o \rightarrow \text{Relay Diaphragm} \rightarrow \text{Valve Modulation} \rightarrow \text{Output Chamber} \rightarrow P_o \text{ (amplified)}$$ **Why Necessary (2 marks):** 1. **Flow Amplification:** The nozzle back pressure alone has negligible flow capacity (~0.001 Nm³/h). The relay boosts this to several Nm³/h, sufficient to drive long pneumatic lines and actuators. 2. **Impedance Matching:** Provides a low-impedance output that maintains signal integrity despite downstream leaks. --- **(b) A nozzle-flapper transducer has a supply pressure of 20 kPa and operates with a nozzle constant $C = 0.05$ mm. If the flapper displacement is 0.04 mm, calculate the output back pressure and sensitivity. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - $P_s = 20$ kPa - $C = 0.05$ mm - $x = 0.04$ mm **Step 1 — Calculate Output Pressure (4 marks):** $$P_o = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + (C/x)^{2}} = \dfrac{20}{1 + (0.05/0.04)^{2}}$$ $$(C/x)^{2} = (1.25)^{2} = 1.5625$$ $$P_o = \dfrac{20}{1 + 1.5625} = \dfrac{20}{2.5625} = 7.805 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate Sensitivity (3 marks):** $$S = \dfrac{2 P_s x C^{2}}{(x^{2} + C^{2})^{2}}$$ Numerator: $2 \times 20 \times 0.04 \times (0.05)^{2} = 2 \times 20 \times 0.04 \times 0.0025 = 0.004$ kPa·mm Denominator: $(0.04^{2} + 0.05^{2})^{2} = (0.0016 + 0.0025)^{2} = (0.0041)^{2} = 1.681 \times 10^{-5}$ mm⁴ $$S = \dfrac{0.004}{1.681 \times 10^{-5}} = 238 \text{ kPa/mm}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Frequently Lost Marks):** > > 1. **Units Consistency:** Always convert all displacement values to the **same unit** (mm or m) before substitution. Mixing units (e.g., mm in numerator, m in denominator) is the #1 mark-losing error. > 2. **Forgetting the Squared Term:** Students often write $(C/x)$ instead of $(C/x)^{2}$, leading to a factor-of-2 error. > 3. **Sensitivity Sign:** In KTU convention, sensitivity is a **magnitude** (positive value). Do not report negative sensitivity unless the question explicitly asks for the differential $dP_o/dx$. > 4. **Missing Sketch:** In 7-mark theory questions, **always include a labelled diagram** showing $P_s$, $P_o$, $x$, and the nozzle/flapper — losing 2 marks otherwise. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Nozzle Flapper Transducer** ### Core Concepts - The **Nozzle Flapper Transducer** is a **pneumatic displacement-to-pressure converter** with **infinite theoretical sensitivity** at zero gap. - It consists of three essentials: **supply orifice**, **nozzle**, and **flapper** — always mention these three explicitly in definitions. - Output pressure follows the **non-linear equation**: $P_o = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + (C/x)^{2}}$ - The **pneumatic relay** is mandatory for practical use — it amplifies flow capacity by 100:1 or more. - **Operating supply pressure:** $P_s \approx 20$ kPa (1.4 bar) is the industry standard. - **Operating displacement range:** $x \approx 0.025$ to $0.2$ mm (very small!). ### High-Yield Formulas - **Output pressure:** $P_o = \dfrac{P_s}{1 + (C/x)^{2}}$ - **Sensitivity:** $S = \dfrac{2 P_s x C^{2}}{(x^{2} + C^{2})^{2}}$ - **Leak area:** $A_{gap} = \pi d x$ - **Maximum sensitivity location:** $x_{max} = C / \sqrt{3}$ ### Common Applications to Remember - Pneumatic PID controllers (Foxboro, Honeywell legacy systems) - Pilot stage of electrohydraulic servovalves - Aircraft altimeters and air-data computers - Force-balance pressure transmitters ### Examiner-Favourite Keywords (Use in Answers) - *Pneumatic resistance*, *back pressure*, *variable orifice*, *force-balance*, *negative feedback relay*, *flow amplification*, *quasi-linear region*, *nozzle constant*, *supply restriction*. ### Typical Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme) - **Definition + Construction:** 2 marks - **Working Principle:** 3 marks - **Derivation of $P_o$ vs $x$ relation:** 4 marks - **Sensitivity Analysis:** 3 marks - **Diagram / Sketch:** 2 marks (always include!)

topics.title: Measurement of Surface Characteristics: Measurement of Straightness, Flatness, Squareness, Parallelism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Surface Characteristics: Measurement of Straightness, Flatness, Squareness, Parallelism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Geometric Tolerances and Surface Characteristics

In engineering metrology, after machining, a component is rarely perfectly geometric. It is bounded by what we call **form errors** (straightness, flatness, roundness, cylindricity) and **orientation errors** (squareness, parallelism, angularity). The four characteristics under study in this module — **Straightness, Flatness, Squareness, and Parallelism** — form the foundation of geometric dimensioning and tolerancing (GD&T) and are explicitly listed in the KTU 2024 OEMET725 syllabus under Module 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> *"Measurement of Surface Characteristics: Measurement of Straightness, Flatness, Squareness, Parallelism — using straight edge, surface plate, spirit level, autocollimator, optical flat, and squareness testing instruments."*

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (As per IS 919 / ISO 1101)

### A. Straightness
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Straightness):** *Straightness is a condition in which an element of a surface or an axis is a straight line.* It is the deviation of the actual line from its ideal straight form, measured as the minimum distance between two parallel straight lines that just enclose the actual line.

- **Symbol:** —
- **Tolerance Zone:** Two parallel straight lines separated by the tolerance value *t*.
- **Measured in terms of:** Linear deviation (micrometres, µm).

### B. Flatness
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Flatness):** *Flatness is the condition of a surface having all elements in one plane.* It is the deviation of the actual surface from its ideal flat form, measured as the minimum distance between two parallel planes that just enclose the actual surface.

- **Symbol:** ⏥
- **Tolerance Zone:** Two parallel planes separated by the tolerance value *t*.
- **Measured in terms of:** Linear deviation (µm).

### C. Squareness (Perpendicularity — when two surfaces are involved)
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Squareness):** *Squareness is the condition of a surface, axis, or center plane being at right angles (90°) to a datum surface, datum axis, or datum center plane.* It is the deviation of the actual angle from exactly 90°.

- **Symbol:** ⊥
- **Measured in terms of:** Linear deviation (µm) at a specified distance, or angular deviation (arc-seconds).

### D. Parallelism
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Parallelism):** *Parallelism is the condition of a surface, axis, or center plane being equidistant at all points from a datum plane or datum axis.* It is the difference between the maximum and minimum distance measured from the datum.

- **Symbol:** ∥
- **Tolerance Zone:** Two parallel planes (or lines) separated by the tolerance value *t*.
- **Measured in terms of:** Linear deviation (µm).

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have built a **marble run on a wooden plank**:

| Surface Property | Real-Life Analogy | What Goes Wrong if Violated |
|---|---|---|
| **Straightness** | A long ruler held against a *wavy wooden board*. | The marble bumps up and down instead of rolling smoothly. |
| **Flatness** | A dining table that is meant to hold a glass of water still. | The glass tilts; the water spills. |
| **Squareness** | A picture frame — its corners must be 90° so the photo sits flush. | The photo hangs crooked, the frame is twisted. |
| **Parallelism** | A sliding window in its track — the two rails must be parallel. | The window jams halfway or is loose. |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Memorising the Order of Increasing Complexity:**
> **"S-F-S-P" → "Sticks For Smart People"**
> 1. **S**traightness — 1D error (along a line)
> 2. **F**latness — 2D error (over a surface)
> 3. **S**quareness — orientation error (angle between two elements)
> 4. **P**arallelism — orientation error (relative position of two elements)
>
> Flatness = Straightness extended in 2 dimensions; Squareness and Parallelism are both built on the foundation of an established datum.

---

## 1.4 Why These Measurements Matter in Industry

- **Interchangeability:** Mass-produced parts must fit every mating part.
- **Functional Performance:** Bearings need round shafts; hydraulics need flat sealing surfaces.
- **Tool Life & Assembly Stress:** Misaligned parts cause uneven loading, vibration, and premature wear.
- **Standards Compliance:** ISO 9001 / AS9100 / IATF 16949 require traceable dimensional metrology.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Measurement of Straightness

### 2.1.1 Concept
A line is straight if every pair of points on it is collinear. In metrology, we measure straightness by comparing the actual line against a **reference straight line** established by a straight edge, taut wire, light beam, or autocollimator line of sight.

### 2.1.2 Methods of Measurement

**Method 1 — Straightedge + Dial Indicator (Comparator Method)**

- A precision straightedge is placed on the surface plate alongside the workpiece.
- A dial indicator is mounted on a stand and traversed along the line being measured.
- The straightedge itself is not perfectly straight; therefore, a **two-step reversal method** is used:
  1. Place the straightedge on the work, traverse the indicator, take readings (h₁, h₂, … hₙ).
  2. **Reverse the straightedge by 180°** and repeat (h₁', h₂', … hₙ').

- The **straightness error** at any station *i* is:
  $$\delta_i = \frac{h_i - h_i'}{2}$$
- The total **straightness tolerance** of the part is:
  $$\Delta_{\text{straightness}} = \delta_{\max} - \delta_{\min}$$

**Method 2 — Autocollimator Method**

- An autocollimator projects a collimated beam onto a flat mirror mounted on the workpiece.
- As the mirror is moved along the line, tilt of the mirror indicates deviation from the reference line of sight.
- For a mirror moved over a length *L* and an autocollimator with reading *θ* (radians):
  $$\Delta h = L \cdot \tan\theta \approx L \cdot \theta \quad (\theta \text{ in rad})$$

**Method 3 — Spirit Level Method**

- A precision spirit level is placed on the workpiece.
- As the level is moved in equal increments, the bubble displacement is recorded.
- The vertical deviation at station *i* is:
  $$e_i = b_i \cdot s$$
  where *bᵢ* is the bubble reading (graduations) and *s* is the sensitivity (mm/graduation or arc-seconds/graduation).
- Straightness error:
  $$\Delta_{\text{straightness}} = e_{\max} - e_{\min}$$

**Method 4 — Laser Beam Method (Modern)**

- A laser beam is the reference straight line.
- A position-sensing detector (PSD) or quadrant photodiode records deviation of the reflected beam.
- Resolution: **0.1 µm** or better over metres of travel.

---

## 2.2 Measurement of Flatness

### 2.2.1 Concept
A surface is flat if all points on it lie in a single plane. In practice, we sample the surface at discrete grid points using a dial indicator on a **surface plate** as the datum.

### 2.2.2 Methods of Measurement

**Method 1 — Surface Plate + Dial Indicator (Grid Method)**

- A grid of *m × n* points is laid out on the surface.
- At each grid point, the dial indicator is zeroed against the surface plate.
- Readings *zᵢⱼ* are recorded.
- The flatness error is:
  $$F = z_{\max} - z_{\min}$$

**Method 2 — Optical Flat + Monochromatic Light (Flatness Interferometry)**

- An optical flat is placed on the surface to be tested.
- Monochromatic light (sodium lamp, λ ≈ 589 nm) produces **interference fringes** (Newton's rings or Fizeau fringes).
- Number of fringes *n* between two points separated by *d* gives the deviation:
  $$\Delta h = n \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}$$
- For flatness over length *L*:
  $$F = n \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

**Method 3 — Autocollimator + Pentaprism**

- An autocollimator directs a beam to a pentaprism that deflects it by exactly 90° regardless of small orientation errors.
- The pentaprism is moved over the surface; the autocollimator reads the slope at every point.
- The deviation is obtained by integrating slope readings.

**Method 4 — Laser Interferometer**

- A laser interferometer (e.g., HP / Renishaw / Zygo) is used for ultra-high-precision flatness measurement (sub-micrometre).
- Modern shop-floor instruments achieve **0.05 µm/m** flatness evaluation.

---

## 2.3 Measurement of Squareness

### 2.3.1 Concept
Two surfaces (or a surface and an axis) are square if they are perpendicular. We measure the deviation of the actual angle from 90°.

### 2.3.2 Methods of Measurement

**Method 1 — Try Square + Feeler Gauges**

- A machinist's try square is placed on the reference surface.
- A feeler gauge measures the gap at the far end.
- Squareness error:
  $$e = \text{feeler thickness at distance } L$$

**Method 2 — Optical Square**

- An optical square is a precision pentaprism that gives an exact 90° reference beam.
- The workpiece is set on a surface plate; the optical square is placed against the work surface.
- The deviation of the line of sight from a master square gives the angular error *θ*.
- Linear squareness error at length *L*:
  $$e = L \cdot \tan\theta \approx L \cdot \theta$$

**Method 3 — Dial Indicator + Precision Square (Cylindrical Square)**

- A cylindrical precision square (or a high-grade master square) is placed on the reference face.
- A dial indicator is traversed along the surface to be tested.
- Difference in readings gives the linear error.

**Method 4 — CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine)**

- The CMM probes multiple points on both surfaces.
- Software computes the least-squares plane for each surface and the angle between them.
- Squareness error = (90° − actual angle) or its linear equivalent at the corner.

**Method 5 — Autocollimator + Pentaprism + Index Table**

- Most accurate; used in calibration laboratories.
- Angular resolution: **0.2 arc-seconds** (≈ 1 µrad).

---

## 2.4 Measurement of Parallelism

### 2.4.1 Concept
Two surfaces are parallel if every pair of perpendiculars drawn between them is equal in length.

### 2.4.2 Methods of Measurement

**Method 1 — Dial Indicator (Direct Method)**

- The workpiece is set on a surface plate (datum).
- A dial indicator is traversed across the test surface at multiple points.
- Parallelism error:
  $$P = h_{\max} - h_{\min}$$

**Method 2 — Micrometer (for Bores / Shafts)**

- A bore is measured at multiple axial positions using an inside micrometer.
- Diameter variation = parallel side gap variation (for prisms/blocks).

**Method 3 — Sine Bar / Sine Centre**

- The workpiece is placed on a sine bar set to the required angle.
- Slip gauges raise one end; the parallelism of an edge is measured using a dial indicator.

**Method 4 — Optical Method (Autocollimator + Reflector)**

- Mirror mounted on work, autocollimator measures tilt at multiple points.
- Suitable for large, heavy workpieces.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` for absolute value in tables to preserve markdown formatting.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Straightness (reversal method) | $\delta_i = \dfrac{h_i - h_i'}{2}$ | $h_i$ = reading in position 1, $h_i'$ = reading in position 2 (reversed) | µm |
| 2 | Straightness tolerance | $\Delta_{\text{str}} = \delta_{\max} - \delta_{\min}$ | $\delta_{\max},\ \delta_{\min}$ = max, min corrected readings | µm |
| 3 | Straightness (autocollimator) | $\Delta h = L \cdot \tan\theta \approx L\cdot\theta$ | $L$ = length, $\theta$ = autocollimator angle | µm, rad |
| 4 | Spirit level gradient | $e_i = b_i \cdot s$ | $b_i$ = bubble reading, $s$ = sensitivity | mm |
| 5 | Flatness (grid method) | $F = z_{\max} - z_{\min}$ | $z$ = indicator readings over grid | µm |
| 6 | Flatness (optical flat) | $F = n \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$ | $n$ = number of fringes, $\lambda$ = wavelength of light | nm |
| 7 | Flatness per unit length | $F_{\text{spec}} = k \cdot L$ | $k$ = flatness grade (e.g., 0.05 µm/mm) | µm |
| 8 | Squareness (angular) | $e = L \cdot \tan\theta$ | $L$ = distance, $\theta$ = angular deviation | µm, rad |
| 9 | Squareness (feeler) | $e = t$ | $t$ = feeler thickness at the far end | µm |
| 10 | Parallelism (dial) | $P = h_{\max} - h_{\min}$ | $h$ = indicator readings | µm |
| 11 | Parallelism (sine bar) | $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{h}{L}$ | $h$ = slip gauge height, $L$ = sine bar length, $\alpha$ = inclination | unitless |
| 12 | Angle from autocollimator | $\theta = \dfrac{N - N_0}{R}$ | $N$ = reading, $N_0$ = null, $R$ = autocollimator constant (1 rev = $R$ arc-sec) | rad |
| 13 | Sensitivity of spirit level | $s = \dfrac{p}{n}$ | $p$ = pitch of feet, $n$ = bubble displacement per revolution | mm/grad |
| 14 | Overrun (autocollimator) | $\Delta h = R \cdot \sin\theta \approx R\cdot\theta$ | $R$ = distance from mirror to target | µm, rad |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Property Measured | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Machine tool bed (lathe, planer) | **Flatness** | Determines machining accuracy across entire travel. |
| Engine cylinder block deck | **Flatness** | Head gasket seal; combustion pressure integrity. |
| Slide rails of a CNC | **Straightness** | Tool path linearity. |
| Surface grinder column | **Squareness** | Vertical feed perpendicular to table. |
| Lathe spindle nose | **Squareness to bed** | Facing & turning are orthogonal. |
| Two parallel faces of a vice jaw | **Parallelism** | Workpiece clamping without tilt. |
| Calibration master square | **Squareness** | Reference standard for workshop. |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Straightness Error by the **Reversal Method**

### 3.1.1 Statement
A straightedge is placed on a workpiece and a dial indicator is traversed along it. The straightness of the *work* (not the straightedge) is to be found.

### 3.1.2 Setup
Let:
- $S(x)$ = the true straightness profile of the straightedge as a function of position $x$.
- $W(x)$ = the true straightness profile of the workpiece.
- The dial indicator reads the **combined deviation** $R_1(x) = W(x) - S(x)$ (indicator on straightedge, traversing above work).
- After reversal by 180°, the straightedge now lies the other way: $R_2(x) = W(x) - S(-x)$ (or equivalently, $R_2(x) = W(x) + S(x)$ if $S$ is symmetric about its centre).

### 3.1.3 Derivation

**Step 1 — Add the two readings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1(x) + R_2(x) &= \bigl[W(x) - S(x)\bigr] + \bigl[W(x) + S(x)\bigr] \\
&= 2W(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

The straightedge error cancels out — a beautiful property exploited in metrology.

**Step 2 — Solve for $W(x)$:**

$$
W(x) = \frac{R_1(x) + R_2(x)}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Straightness error (tolerance):**

$$
\Delta_{\text{straightness}} = W_{\max}(x) - W_{\min}(x)
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU-typical):**
The following readings (in µm) were taken at 50 mm intervals along a 500 mm shaft:

| Position $x$ (mm) | 0 | 50 | 100 | 150 | 200 | 250 | 300 | 350 | 400 | 450 | 500 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $R_1$ (original) | 0 | +2 | +5 | +8 | +10 | +9 | +7 | +4 | +2 | +1 | 0 |
| $R_2$ (reversed) | 0 | −1 | −3 | −6 | −8 | −7 | −5 | −2 | +1 | +2 | 0 |

**Step 5 — Apply formula at each station:**

$$
W(x) = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{2}
$$

| Position (mm) | $R_1$ (µm) | $R_2$ (µm) | $W$ (µm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0.0 |
| 50 | +2 | −1 | +0.5 |
| 100 | +5 | −3 | +1.0 |
| 150 | +8 | −6 | +1.0 |
| 200 | +10 | −8 | +1.0 |
| 250 | +9 | −7 | +1.0 |
| 300 | +7 | −5 | +1.0 |
| 350 | +4 | −2 | +1.0 |
| 400 | +2 | +1 | +1.5 |
| 450 | +1 | +2 | +1.5 |
| 500 | 0 | 0 | 0.0 |

**Step 6 — Identify the extremes:**

- $W_{\max} = +1.5\ \mu m$ (at $x = 400$ and $450$ mm)
- $W_{\min} = 0.0\ \mu m$ (at $x = 0$ and $500$ mm)

**Step 7 — Final answer:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta_{\text{straightness}} = 1.5\ \mu m}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Squareness Error from Optical Square Reading

### 3.2.1 Setup
An autocollimator reads the deviation of a workpiece face from a master reference square. The optical square is at a distance $D$ from the autocollimator's objective, and the far end of the workpiece face is at length $L$ from the corner.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Autocollimator measures the angle $\theta$ (radians):**
For an autocollimator with constant $R$ arc-seconds per revolution and a reading of $N$ divisions:

$$
\theta = \frac{N \cdot R}{206265}\ \text{radians} \quad (206265'' = 1\ \text{rad})
$$

**Step 2 — Linear squareness error at length $L$:**

$$
e = L \cdot \tan\theta
$$

For small angles ($\theta \ll 1$ rad), $\tan\theta \approx \theta$, so:

$$
e \approx L \cdot \theta
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example (KTU-typical):**
- Length of workpiece face: $L = 500\ \text{mm} = 0.5\ \text{m}$
- Autocollimator reading: $N = 8$ divisions
- Autocollimator constant: $R = 1''$ per division
- Convert angle to radians:

$$
\theta = \frac{8 \times 1}{206265} = 3.878 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{rad}
$$

- Compute linear error:

$$
e = 500 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.878 \times 10^{-5} = 1.939 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 19.39\ \mu m
$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:**

$$
\boxed{e \approx 19.4\ \mu m\ \text{at a length of 500 mm}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Flatness Using an Optical Flat and Interference Fringes

### 3.3.1 Theory
When monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda$ is incident on the air wedge between an optical flat and the test surface, dark fringes are formed where the air gap is an integral multiple of $\lambda/2$. The deviation of the surface from perfect flatness is:

$$
F = n \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}
$$

where $n$ is the number of fringes between the lowest and highest points of the test surface.

### 3.3.2 Numerical Example (KTU-typical)
- Light source: Sodium lamp, $\lambda = 589\ \text{nm} = 0.589\ \mu m$
- Observed fringes: $n = 4$ dark bands
- Diameter of test surface: $D = 150\ \text{mm}$

**Step 1 — Flatness deviation:**

$$
F = 4 \times \frac{0.589}{2} = 4 \times 0.2945 = 1.178\ \mu m
$$

**Step 2 — Flatness per unit length (flatness grade):**

$$
\text{Flatness grade} = \frac{F}{D} = \frac{1.178\ \mu m}{150\ \text{mm}} = 0.0079\ \mu m/\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Final answer:**

$$
\boxed{F = 1.18\ \mu m \quad \text{over 150 mm}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Definition | Best Instrument | Typical Resolution | Method Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Straightness** | Line element straight | Autocollimator | 0.2 arc-sec | Direct / comparative |
| **Flatness** | All points in one plane | Optical flat + interferometer | $\lambda/20 \approx 30\ \text{nm}$ | Interferometric |
| **Squareness** | 90° between two elements | Optical square / autocollimator | 0.2 arc-sec | Comparative |
| **Parallelism** | Equidistant from datum | Dial indicator on surface plate | 1 µm | Comparative / direct |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' Favourite Triangle:**
> In the exam, the autocollimator is the "Swiss army knife" of geometric metrology — it can be used to measure straightness (with a moving mirror), flatness (with a pentaprism), squareness (with an optical square), and parallelism (with a fixed mirror on the test face). Always list the autocollimator first when asked "which instrument is the most versatile."

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Spirit Level Sensitivity and Straightness

### 3.5.1 Setup
A precision spirit level has feet separated by pitch $p$ (e.g., $p = 200\ \text{mm}$). The bubble vial has graduations; one full division = $n$ mm of bubble travel.

### 3.5.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Sensitivity of the level (mm of vertical rise per graduation):**

$$
s = \frac{p \cdot \sin(1\ \text{graduation})}{n}
$$

For small angles, $\sin(1\ \text{grad}) \approx 1\ \text{grad in rad}$.

**Step 2 — Vertical rise corresponding to a bubble shift of $b$ graduations:**

$$
e = b \cdot s
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example (KTU-typical):**
- Pitch: $p = 200\ \text{mm}$
- Bubble: 1 graduation = 2 mm of bubble travel
- One full vial revolution (50 graduations) = 10 mm of bubble travel
- Sensitivity: $s = 200/50 = 4\ \mu m/\text{graduation}$ (i.e., $s \approx 0.8''$ per grad)

**Step 4 — If the bubble is found $b = 6$ graduations off-centre:**

$$
e = 6 \times 4\ \mu m = 24\ \mu m
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer:**

$$
\boxed{e = 24\ \mu m}
$$

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Surface-Characteristic Measurement Workflow

This block diagram maps the full workflow of how a measurement request flows from selection of the geometric property to the final report, covering all four properties studied.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Geometric Property to Measure] --> B{Property Type}
    B -->|1D line| C[Straightness]
    B -->|2D surface| D[Flatness]
    B -->|Angle to datum| E[Squareness]
    B -->|Equidistance to datum| F[Parallelism]

    C --> C1{Instrument Selection}
    C1 --> C1a[Spirit Level]
    C1 --> C1b[Autocollimator + Travelling Mirror]
    C1 --> C1c[Dial Indicator + Reversal Method]
    C1 --> C1d[Laser Beam + PSD]

    D --> D1{Instrument Selection}
    D1 --> D1a[Surface Plate + Dial Indicator]
    D1 --> D1b[Optical Flat + Monochromatic Light]
    D1 --> D1c[Autocollimator + Pentaprism]
    D1 --> D1d[Laser Interferometer]

    E --> E1{Instrument Selection}
    E1 --> E1a[Try Square + Feeler Gauge]
    E1 --> E1b[Optical Square]
    E1 --> E1c[Cylindrical Master Square + Dial Indicator]
    E1 --> E1d[CMM Probe]

    F --> F1{Instrument Selection}
    F1 --> F1a[Dial Indicator Traverse on Surface Plate]
    F1 --> F1b[Inside Micrometer Bore Method]
    F1 --> F1c[Sine Bar + Slip Gauges]
    F1 --> F1d[Autocollimator + Reflector]

    C1a --> G[Acquire Raw Readings]
    C1b --> G
    C1c --> G
    C1d --> G
    D1a --> G
    D1b --> G
    D1c --> G
    D1d --> G
    E1a --> G
    E1b --> G
    E1c --> G
    E1d --> G
    F1a --> G
    F1b --> G
    F1c --> G
    F1d --> G

    G --> H[Apply Correction: Calibration / Reversal / Zero]
    H --> I[Compute Property Error via Formula]
    I --> J{Error within Tolerance?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Mark ACCEPT + Generate Report]
    J -->|No| L[Mark REJECT / Rework]
    L --> M[Route to Scrap or Rework Station]
    K --> N[Archive Calibration Traceable to IS 919 / ISO 1101]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Straightness by the Reversal Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Workpiece on Surface Plate] --> S[Precision Straightedge on Work]
    S --> R1[Dial Indicator Reading Position 1: h1, h2, ... hn]
    R1 --> REV[Reverse Straightedge 180 degrees]
    REV --> R2[Dial Indicator Reading Position 2: h1', h2', ... hn']
    R2 --> CALC[Compute Wi = average of hi and hi prime]
    CALC --> OUT[Straightness Error = Wmax - Wmin]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Flatness by Optical Interferometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SR[Monochromatic Light Source - Sodium Lamp] --> BE[Beam Expander and Collimator]
    BE --> OF[Optical Flat placed on Test Surface]
    OF --> AIR[A thin Air Wedge forms between the two]
    AIR --> FR[Interference Fringes - Dark and Bright Bands]
    FR --> OBS[Observer counts number of dark fringes n]
    OBS --> COMP[Compute F = n times lambda over 2]
    COMP --> RES[Flatness Result in micrometres]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Squareness Using Optical Square and Autocollimator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Surface Plate as Datum] --> W[Workpiece clamped on SP]
    W --> M[Mirror attached to Test Face]
    M --> AC[Autocollimator emits Collimated Beam]
    AC --> REF[Beam reflects off Mirror and returns]
    REF --> OS[Optical Square in Path - exact 90 degree reference]
    OS --> READ[Scale Reading theta in arc-seconds]
    READ --> CALC[Linear Error e = L times tan theta]
    CALC --> RES[Squareness Result e at length L]
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Parallelism by Dial Indicator Traverse

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D[Surface Plate as Datum Plane] --> WK[Workpiece placed on D]
    WK --> DI[Dial Indicator clamped above Test Surface]
    DI --> TR[Indicator traversed along Test Surface in equal steps]
    TR --> RD[Record readings h1, h2, ... hn at each step]
    RD --> CMP[Compute P = hmax - hmin]
    CMP --> RES[Parallelism Result in micrometres]
```
