# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define straightness and flatness. How does a straightness measurement differ from a flatness measurement?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Straightness** is a *one-dimensional* form tolerance — it constrains the deviation of a *line element* from its true straight form. It is measured along a single direction.
- **Flatness** is a *two-dimensional* form tolerance — it constrains *all* points of a *surface* to lie between two parallel planes separated by the tolerance value.
- **Difference:** Straightness is evaluated along a line; flatness is evaluated over an entire surface (essentially straightness extended in two dimensions). Straightness uses a reference line; flatness uses a reference plane (e.g., surface plate or optical flat).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: What is an autocollimator? Mention any two applications of it in surface measurement.**

**Model Answer:**
An **autocollimator** is an optical instrument that measures small angular displacements of a reflecting surface by projecting a collimated beam onto a mirror and viewing the reflected image through a graticule / eyepiece or photoelectric detector.
- **Application 1:** Measurement of **straightness** of a machine tool slide (by moving a mirror along the slide).
- **Application 2:** Measurement of **squareness** of a workpiece face against a datum (using an optical square / pentaprism).
- *(Other valid applications: flatness with pentaprism, small angle measurements, alignment of lathe axes.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions on Module 2 topics are typically framed with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Below are TWO alternative questions (A and B), so you can answer either.

---

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Modified for Module 2)]

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **optical flat method** for the measurement of flatness of a surface. Mention the role of the monochromatic light source and derive the formula relating the number of fringes to flatness error. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A surface is tested with an optical flat using a sodium lamp ($\lambda = 589\ \text{nm}$). Six dark fringes are observed over a circular surface of diameter 100 mm. Calculate (i) the total flatness error, and (ii) the flatness grade in µm/mm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a)

**Concept:**
The **optical flat** is a highly polished, flat (to within $\lambda/20$) glass disc. When placed on a slightly non-flat test surface, a **thin wedge-shaped air film** forms between them. When illuminated by **monochromatic light**, interference occurs — bright and dark bands (Fizeau fringes) appear.

**Working Principle:**
- The path difference between the two reflected rays (one from the bottom of the optical flat, one from the top of the test surface) is $2t$, where $t$ is the local air gap.
- **Dark fringe condition:** $2t = m\lambda$, i.e., $t = m\lambda/2$.
- Between two dark fringes, $t$ changes by $\lambda/2$.

**Derivation of Flatness Formula:**

Let $h$ be the local deviation of the test surface from the optical flat. The wedge gap $t = h$ at any point. The number of dark fringes between the highest and lowest point gives:

$$
\boxed{F = n \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}}
$$

where:
- $F$ = total flatness deviation (µm)
- $n$ = number of dark fringes counted between the highest and lowest point
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of monochromatic light (typically 589 nm for sodium)

**Sketch (in text form):**
```
        ┌──────────────┐  Optical flat (perfectly flat reference)
        │              │
   ─────┤  air wedge   │  ← interference fringes
        │              │
   ─────┴──────────────┘  Test surface (slightly curved)
```

---

#### Solution to A(b)

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 589\ \text{nm} = 0.589\ \mu m$
- $n = 6$ dark fringes
- $D = 100\ \text{mm}$

**(i) Total flatness error:**

$$
F = n \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} = 6 \times \frac{0.589}{2} = 6 \times 0.2945 = 1.767\ \mu m
$$

$$
\boxed{F = 1.767\ \mu m}
$$

**(ii) Flatness grade (flatness per unit length):**

$$
k = \frac{F}{D} = \frac{1.767\ \mu m}{100\ \text{mm}} = 0.01767\ \mu m/\text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{k \approx 0.0177\ \mu m/\text{mm}}
$$

This is excellent flatness — comparable to a Grade-0 reference flat.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Modified for Module 2)]

**Q: (a)** Describe the **reversal method** for measuring the straightness of a workpiece. Derive the formula used to eliminate the error of the straightedge from the final result. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** The following readings (in µm) were obtained on a 600 mm long shaft using a straightedge and dial indicator, before and after reversing the straightedge by 180°.

| Position (mm) | 0 | 100 | 200 | 300 | 400 | 500 | 600 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $R_1$ (original) | 0 | +3 | +6 | +8 | +7 | +3 | 0 |
| $R_2$ (reversed) | 0 | −1 | −3 | −5 | −4 | −1 | 0 |

Calculate the **straightness error** of the shaft. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a)

**Description:**

In the reversal method (also called the **"Rearrangement Method"** or **"End Reversal Method"**):

1. A precision **straightedge** is placed on the **workpiece** resting on a surface plate.
2. A **dial indicator** mounted on a stand is traversed at equal intervals along the length. Readings $R_1(x)$ are noted.
3. The straightedge is then **rotated 180° about its vertical axis** (end-for-end reversal), so that the previous left end is now on the right.
4. The dial indicator is traversed again; readings $R_2(x)$ are noted.

**Why the reversal?**
The straightedge itself has an unknown straightness profile $S(x)$. By reversing it, $S(x) \to -S(x)$ (with respect to the indicator).

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1(x) &= W(x) - S(x) \\
R_2(x) &= W(x) - S(-x) = W(x) + S(x) \quad \text{(for symmetric profile)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding the two:

$$
R_1(x) + R_2(x) = 2W(x)
$$

The straightedge error cancels out. Hence:

$$
\boxed{W(x) = \frac{R_1(x) + R_2(x)}{2}}
$$

The straightness tolerance of the workpiece is:

$$
\boxed{\Delta_{\text{str}} = W_{\max} - W_{\min}}
$$

---

#### Solution to B(b)

**Given:** $R_1$ and $R_2$ readings at 100 mm intervals.

**Apply formula $W(x) = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{2}$ at each station:**

| Position (mm) | $R_1$ (µm) | $R_2$ (µm) | $W = (R_1 + R_2)/2$ (µm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0.0 |
| 100 | +3 | −1 | +1.0 |
| 200 | +6 | −3 | +1.5 |
| 300 | +8 | −5 | +1.5 |
| 400 | +7 | −4 | +1.5 |
| 500 | +3 | −1 | +1.0 |
| 600 | 0 | 0 | 0.0 |

**Identify extremes:**

- $W_{\max} = +1.5\ \mu m$ (at 200, 300, 400 mm)
- $W_{\min} = 0.0\ \mu m$ (at 0 and 600 mm)

**Final straightness error:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta_{\text{str}} = 1.5 - 0.0 = 1.5\ \mu m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1 — Forgetting the factor of ½:** Many students write the straightness error as the simple difference $R_1 - R_2$. This is **wrong**; the formula is the **average** $(R_1 + R_2)/2$, not the difference. Loss: 3 marks.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2 — Forgetting units:** Always quote the answer in **µm** (not mm). Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3 — Missing the explanation of WHY reversal works:** Examiners award full marks only if the derivation shows that the straightedge error cancels out. A bare formula without derivation loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4 — Using the wavelength wrong in the optical flat method:** Always remember that the gap gives a path difference of $2t$, hence the formula has $\lambda/2$ — not $\lambda$. Loss: 2 marks.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5 — Squareness / parallelism confusion:** Squareness is about an **angle** (90° ± deviation), while parallelism is about **distance** (constant). Examiners often give a 1-mark follow-up: "What is the difference?" Be ready.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Straightness** is a *1D* form tolerance; **flatness** is its *2D* extension over a surface.
- [ ] The **straightedge + dial indicator** with **180° reversal** is the most common shop-floor method for straightness; the formula is $W(x) = (R_1 + R_2)/2$.
- [ ] The **autocollimator** is the most versatile instrument — it can measure straightness, squareness, flatness, and parallelism with the right accessories.
- [ ] The **optical flat + monochromatic light** method uses the formula $F = n\lambda/2$ (remember the **2** in the denominator!).
- [ ] **Squareness** is an **orientation tolerance** (angle to a datum). It is always measured against a master 90° reference (try square, optical square, CMM).
- [ ] **Parallelism** is also an **orientation tolerance**, but the measured quantity is the **difference in distance** $P = h_{\max} - h_{\min}$, not an angle.
- [ ] For **autocollimator** angular calculations: $1\ \text{rad} = 206265''$ (arc-seconds). Always convert.
- [ ] For **spirit level** sensitivity: $s = p/n$ where $p$ = pitch of feet, $n$ = graduations per revolution.
- [ ] For **laser-based** methods, accuracy is sub-micrometre and the wavelength is precisely stabilised (e.g., He-Ne laser, $\lambda = 632.8\ \text{nm}$).
- [ ] In the **reversal method**, the straightedge error is cancelled — **not** the dial indicator error or the workpiece error.
- [ ] When asked for the "**most accurate**" method in the exam, answer **autocollimator / laser interferometer**, not the straightedge.
- [ ] When asked for the "**simplest / shop-floor**" method, answer **dial indicator + straightedge (or surface plate)**.
- [ ] Standards reference: **IS 919** (India) and **ISO 1101** (International) define these geometric tolerances.
- [ ] **Mnemonic for formulas:** *Reverse = Average* → $(R_1 + R_2)/2$. *Fringes = half-wave* → $n\lambda/2$.
- [ ] **Units rule of thumb:** Lab / grade 0 work → µm; shop floor → 0.01 mm (10 µm); rough inspection → 0.1 mm.
- [ ] Always state the **wavelength of the light source** explicitly in the optical flat method (sodium = 589 nm, helium-neon = 632.8 nm, mercury green = 546 nm).
- [ ] **Flatness grade** = (flatness error in µm) / (length in mm). Compare with standard grades: Grade 0 = 0.05 µm/mm, Grade 1 = 0.1 µm/mm, Grade 2 = 0.2 µm/mm.
- [ ] For **parallelism of two bores**, the **inside micrometer** is the classic instrument; for **two faces**, use a **dial indicator** on a surface plate.
- [ ] In KTU valuation: a question carries 14 marks split 7+7. Always show full derivation, not just the final number.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Surface Finish
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Surface Finish

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Finish (Surface Texture)** is the measure of the micro-geometrical irregularities (both repetitive and non-repetitive) present on a manufactured surface. It comprises three fundamental components: **Roughness**, **Waviness**, and **Lay** — as classified under **ISO 4287 / ISO 21920** and **BIS 6328** standards.

Mathematically, surface texture is defined as the deviation of a real surface from its nominal (ideal) geometric form, measured in micrometers (μm) or micro-inches (μin).

**Three Primary Components of Surface Texture:**

| # | Component | Description | Spatial Frequency |
|---|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Roughness** | Fine, closely spaced irregularities (asperities & scratches) produced by the manufacturing process | High |
| 2 | **Waviness** | Widely spaced, periodic irregularities usually caused by tool/ machine vibrations, deflection, or heat treatment | Medium |
| 3 | **Lay** | The dominant direction of the surface pattern produced by the machining operation (e.g., circumferential, longitudinal, cross-hatched) | — |

**Secondary Components (sometimes included):**
- **Flaws** — isolated, random irregularities like cracks, pores, and inclusions.
- **Subsurface alterations** — metallurgical changes below the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Roughness Parameters used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Ra** — Arithmetic Mean Roughness (most universally used, ~70% of industry applications).
> - **Rz** — Mean Peak-to-Valley Height (10-point roughness).
> - **Rq (RMS)** — Root Mean Square Roughness.
> - **Rt** — Maximum Peak-to-Valley Height over evaluation length.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hill and Valley" Picture

Imagine you take a **coin** and look at its face under a microscope. To the naked eye, it looks perfectly smooth. Under magnification, however, you would see a surface that resembles a tiny, jagged **mountain range** with sharp peaks (called *asperities*) and deep valleys (called *grooves*).

**Real-world analogy:** Think of the surface of a freshly ploughed field. The "surface finish" of that field would describe:
- How tall are the soil clods? → **Roughness (Ra, Rz)**
- Are there long rolling swells across the field? → **Waviness**
- Which way are the furrows running? → **Lay**

Just as a farmer cares about field texture for planting, an engineer cares about surface finish because it directly controls:
- **Friction and wear** between mating parts.
- **Fatigue life** under cyclic loading (microscopic notches act as stress concentrators).
- **Sealability** of gaskets and O-rings.
- **Aesthetic appeal** and **paint adhesion**.
- **Electrical contact resistance** in switches and connectors.
- **Lubricant retention** in bearings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why measure surface finish?** A bearing journal machined to **Ra = 0.4 μm** will last 5–10 times longer than the same journal at **Ra = 1.6 μm**, even though both surfaces look identical to the eye. This is the engineering reality behind why we quantify what we cannot see.

---

## 1.3 Surface Roughness Symbols and Indication (ISO 1302)

The standard surface-finish symbol is an **open "V" tick** placed on the surface line in engineering drawings. The symbol carries quantitative information inside and around it.

**Basic Symbol:**
- An equilateral "V" with apex on the surface.

**Meaning of the two legs of the V:**
- The **horizontal leg** is the surface being specified.
- The **vertical leg** represents the cutting tool.

**Modified symbols (per ISO 1302):**

| Symbol Variation | Engineering Meaning |
|------------------|---------------------|
| Basic V | Surface roughness unspecified |
| V with single horizontal dash | Material removal required (machining) |
| V with circle in apex | Material removal prohibited (as-molded, forged) |

**Information placed inside the V symbol:**
- **Top value:** Ra (in μm) — e.g., Ra 0.8
- **Below the horizontal extension line:** Machining method, sampling length, cutoff filter.

**Surrounding markings (advanced):**
- Small circle on extension = **lay direction** (e.g., ⊙ = parallel, ⊥ = perpendicular, M = multidirectional).
- Numerical placement indicates **Ra cutoff** (e.g., λc 0.8).
- Machining allowance in mm.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme, students are expected to interpret and draw basic surface roughness symbols as per **BIS 6328 / ISO 1302**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Hierarchy of Surface Deviations

A real engineering surface deviates from its ideal (nominal) form at **four superimposed levels**:

1. **Form Error** (Macro): Straightness, flatness, roundness, cylindricity — measured in mm.
2. **Waviness** (Meso): Periodic, repetitive undulations — measured in μm to mm.
3. **Roughness** (Micro): High-frequency irregularities from tool feed, chip formation — measured in μm.
4. **Sub-surface** (Nano): Lattice-level imperfections — measured in nm.

Each level has its own measurement standard:
- **Form:** ISO 1101 / ASME Y14.5
- **Waviness:** ISO 4287 / ISO 16610 (filtered)
- **Roughness:** ISO 4287 / ISO 21920 (filtered)
- **Sub-surface:** SEM, AFM, STM (research level)

---

## 2.2 Filtering Concept — Gaussian & 2RC Filters

To isolate **roughness** from **waviness** and **form**, the measured profile is passed through an electronic filter.

**Standardized Filters (ISO 16610):**
- **λc (Cutoff Wavelength):** Separates roughness from waviness.
- **λs (Short-Wavelength Cutoff):** Eliminates electronic noise and surface micro-fuzz.
- **λf (Long-Wavelength Cutoff):** Eliminates form error.

**Standard λc Values (mm):**

| Surface Type | Typical Ra (μm) | λc (mm) | Sampling Length Lr (mm) | Evaluation Length Ln (mm) |
|--------------|-----------------|---------|-------------------------|---------------------------|
| Very fine (seals, gauges) | 0.006 – 0.02 | 0.08 | 0.08 | 0.4 |
| Fine (bearings, precision shafts) | 0.02 – 0.1 | 0.25 | 0.25 | 1.25 |
| Medium (general machined) | 0.1 – 2.0 | 0.8 | 0.8 | 4.0 |
| Coarse (rough turning, grinding) | 2.0 – 10.0 | 2.5 | 2.5 | 12.5 |
| Very coarse (rough cast) | > 10.0 | 8.0 | 8.0 | 40.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard rule:** The evaluation length **Ln = 5 × Lr (sampling length)**. This five-sample averaging is mandatory in ISO 4287 because one single trace is statistically inadequate.

---

## 2.3 The Mean Line (Center Line)

The **mean line (m-line)** is the reference line used to define all roughness parameters. It is defined as:

> *"A line parallel to the general direction of the profile such that the sum of the squares of the deviations of the profile from it is minimized (least-squares line)."*

Equivalently, it is the line about which **Ra = 0**.

For a discrete digitized profile with N ordinates, the mean line is established such that:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \;=\; 0
$$

This becomes the reference for all up/down measurements.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Surface Roughness Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTERY BOX — memorize the boxed equations. These appear in nearly every KTU numerical problem.**

### (a) Ra — Arithmetic Mean (Center Line Average, CLA)

The arithmetic average of the absolute values of all profile ordinates from the mean line, over the sampling length.

$$
R_a \;=\; \frac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} \left\vert y(x) \right\vert \, dx
$$

**Discrete form (used by digital profilometers):**

$$
R_a \;=\; \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert y_i \right\vert
$$

where $n$ = number of ordinates sampled.

---

### (b) Rq (RMS) — Root Mean Square Roughness

$$
R_q \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} y(x)^{2}\, dx} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^{2}}
$$

**Relation:** $R_q \;\approx\; 1.11 \, R_a$ (for a Gaussian profile, statistically).

---

### (c) Rz — 10-Point Mean Roughness (Mean Peak-to-Valley Height)

The average of the **five highest peaks (Rp)** and **five lowest valleys (Rv)** over the sampling length, measured from the mean line.

$$
R_z \;=\; \frac{\left(R_{p1}+R_{p2}+R_{p3}+R_{p4}+R_{p5}\right) \;+\; \left(R_{v1}+R_{v2}+R_{v3}+R_{v4}+R_{v5}\right)}{5}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, this is the **second-most-tested parameter** after Ra. Note that **Rz ≠ 5 × Ra**; it is roughly **4 to 7 × Ra** depending on the profile shape.

---

### (d) Rt — Total Roughness (Maximum Peak-to-Valley Height)

$$
R_t \;=\; R_p(\text{max}) \;+\; R_v(\text{max})
$$

Measured over the entire **evaluation length (Ln)**, not the sampling length.

---

### (e) Rsk (Skewness) — Profile Asymmetry

$$
R_{sk} \;=\; \frac{1}{n\,R_q^{3}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^{3}
$$

- $R_{sk} > 0$ → bearing surfaces (peaks dominate).
- $R_{sk} < 0$ → sealing surfaces (valleys dominate).

---

### (f) Rku (Kurtosis) — Profile Sharpness

$$
R_{ku} \;=\; \frac{1}{n\,R_q^{4}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^{4}
$$

- $R_{ku} > 3$ → sharp, peaked surface (e.g., grit-blasted).
- $R_{ku} < 3$ → bumpy, dull surface (e.g., honed).

---

## 2.5 Master Formula Summary Table (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Parameter | Formula (Continuous) | Formula (Discrete) | Unit | Notes |
|---|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|------|-------|
| 1 | **Ra** (Arithmetic Mean) | $(1/L)\int_0^L \left\vert y(x) \right\vert dx$ | $(1/n)\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\vert y_i \right\vert$ | μm | Most commonly specified |
| 2 | **Rq** (RMS) | $\sqrt{(1/L)\int_0^L y(x)^{2}\,dx}$ | $\sqrt{(1/n)\sum y_i^{2}}$ | μm | Statistical measure |
| 3 | **Rz** (10-point) | — | See Section 2.4(c) | μm | Peak-to-valley |
| 4 | **Rt** (Total) | $R_p^{max} + R_v^{max}$ | — | μm | Worst-case value |
| 5 | **Rsk** (Skewness) | — | $(1/nR_q^{3})\sum y_i^{3}$ | — | Asymmetry |
| 6 | **Rku** (Kurtosis) | — | $(1/nR_q^{4})\sum y_i^{4}$ | — | Peak sharpness |
| 7 | **Cutoff rule** | $\lambda_c$ per ISO | — | mm | Standard values |
| 8 | **Evaluation rule** | $L_n = 5 L_r$ | — | mm | ISO 4287 |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical Ra Required | Consequence if Exceeded |
|----------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Hydraulic cylinder bore | 0.1 – 0.4 μm | Seal leakage, pump failure |
| Ball bearing raceway | 0.01 – 0.05 μm | Noise, premature wear |
| Crankshaft journal | 0.2 – 0.8 μm | Bearing seizure |
| Gear tooth flank | 0.4 – 1.6 μm | Pitting, vibration |
| Optical lens surface | < 0.01 μm | Image scatter |
| Bone-implant (medical) | 0.5 – 2.0 μm | Controlled porosity for osseointegration |

---

## 2.7 Stylus Profilometer — Operational Principle

The **stylus-type surface profilometer** is the workhorse instrument for surface finish measurement. Working principle:

1. A **diamond-tipped stylus** (tip radius typically **2 μm or 10 μm**) is drawn across the surface at a constant, slow speed (≈ 0.5 – 1 mm/s).
2. The stylus rides over peaks and valleys, generating vertical displacement.
3. The displacement is transduced into an electrical signal by an **LVDT** (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) or **piezoelectric** pickup.
4. The signal is electronically filtered (λc filter).
5. The instrument computes Ra, Rz, Rt etc. and displays/prints the profile.

**Specifications of a standard stylus:**
- **Stylus tip radius:** 2 μm (high precision) or 10 μm (general).
- **Static stylus force:** ≤ 0.7 mN (ISO 3274) to avoid scratching soft surfaces.
- **Stylus material:** Diamond or sapphire.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a stylus force of only 0.7 mN?** Any higher and the diamond tip will **plastically deform** soft materials like aluminium, copper, or polymers — destroying the very surface we want to measure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Ra from a Digitized Profile

**Given:** A sampling length $L$ divided into $n$ equal intervals of width $\Delta x$, with profile ordinates $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n$ measured from the mean line.

**Step 1 — Establish the Mean Line.**

The mean line is the line about which the **sum of signed deviations is zero**:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \;=\; 0
$$

This means the reference is chosen so that positive (peak) and negative (valley) areas balance.

**Step 2 — Take the Absolute Value of Each Ordinate.**

Because Ra depends on the **magnitude** of deviation, regardless of direction:

$$
\left\vert y_1 \right\vert,\; \left\vert y_2 \right\vert,\; \ldots,\; \left\vert y_n \right\vert
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Absolute Deviations.**

$$
S \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert y_i \right\vert
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by the Number of Ordinates.**

$$
R_a \;=\; \frac{S}{n} \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\vert y_i \right\vert
$$

**Step 5 — Express in Terms of the Sampling Length.**

Since $n = L / \Delta x$, in the continuous limit:

$$
R_a \;=\; \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L}\left\vert y(x) \right\vert\, dx
$$

This completes the derivation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style 8-mark level)

**Problem Statement:**
A surface profile is sampled digitally over a sampling length of **0.8 mm** with **40 equally spaced ordinates**. The deviations (in μm) from the mean line are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Series A: } +2,\; +5,\; -3,\; +1,\; -4,\; +6,\; -2,\; 0,\; +3,\; -1 \\
&\text{Series B: } +4,\; -5,\; +2,\; -3,\; +7,\; -6,\; +1,\; -2,\; +4,\; -3 \\
&\text{Series C: } +3,\; -2,\; +5,\; -4,\; +1,\; -3,\; +6,\; -5,\; +2,\; -1 \\
&\text{Series D: } +1,\; -4,\; +3,\; -2,\; +5,\; -3,\; +2,\; -4,\; +5,\; -2
\end{aligned}
$$

The evaluation length is **4.0 mm** (i.e., 5 sampling lengths). The **five highest peaks** and **five lowest valleys** in the entire dataset are required for Rz.

**Compute:**
(a) Ra
(b) Rz
(c) Rt
(d) Rq

---

### (a) Solution — Ra

**Step 1:** Verify that the mean line is correct (sum of signed deviations = 0).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= (+2+5-3+1-4+6-2+0+3-1) \\
&\quad +(+4-5+2-3+7-6+1-2+4-3) \\
&\quad +(+3-2+5-4+1-3+6-5+2-1) \\
&\quad +(+1-4+3-2+5-3+2-4+5-2)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Series A sum} &= 2+5-3+1-4+6-2+0+3-1 \;=\; 7 \\
\text{Series B sum} &= 4-5+2-3+7-6+1-2+4-3 \;=\; -1 \\
\text{Series C sum} &= 3-2+5-4+1-3+6-5+2-1 \;=\; 2 \\
\text{Series D sum} &= 1-4+3-2+5-3+2-4+5-2 \;=\; 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Total sum} \;=\; 7 - 1 + 2 + 1 \;=\; 9
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sum is **not zero** (it is 9). This means the reference given is slightly above the true least-squares mean line. For KTU problems, we **assume the given deviations are already referenced to the mean line** and use them as is — a small approximation that is common in exam settings.

**Step 2:** Compute the absolute deviations.

For compactness, list all 40 absolute values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&2, 5, 3, 1, 4, 6, 2, 0, 3, 1,\\
&4, 5, 2, 3, 7, 6, 1, 2, 4, 3,\\
&3, 2, 5, 4, 1, 3, 6, 5, 2, 1,\\
&1, 4, 3, 2, 5, 3, 2, 4, 5, 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum the absolute values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row sums:}\;& 2+5+3+1+4+6+2+0+3+1 \;=\; 27 \\
&4+5+2+3+7+6+1+2+4+3 \;=\; 37 \\
&3+2+5+4+1+3+6+5+2+1 \;=\; 32 \\
&1+4+3+2+5+3+2+4+5+2 \;=\; 31
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Total} \;=\; 27 + 37 + 32 + 31 \;=\; 127
$$

**Step 4:** Divide by number of ordinates $n = 40$.

$$
R_a \;=\; \frac{127}{40} \;=\; 3.175 \,\mu m
$$

---

### (b) Solution — Rz (10-Point Mean Roughness)

**Step 1:** Sort all 40 profile ordinates to find the five largest and five smallest values.

**Five largest values (peaks):**
- $+7$ (Series B)
- $+6$ (×2) — Series A and Series B
- $+6$ (Series C)
- $+5$ (×5) — multiple locations

Wait — we need to count occurrences. Let me carefully list:

All positive ordinates sorted descending:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Peaks (top):}\; 7,\; 6,\; 6,\; 6,\; 5,\; 5,\; 5,\; 5,\; 5,\; 4,\; 4,\; 4,\; 4,\; 3,\; 3,\; 3,\; 3,\; 3,\; 3,\; 2,\; 2,\; 2,\; 2,\; 1,\; 1,\; 1,\; 1,\; 1,\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The **five highest peaks (Rp):**
$$
R_{p1}=7,\quad R_{p2}=6,\quad R_{p3}=6,\quad R_{p4}=6,\quad R_{p5}=5
$$

**Five deepest valleys (Rv):** Look at all negative ordinates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Valleys (descending magnitude):}\; -6,\; -5,\; -5,\; -5,\; -4,\; -4,\; -4,\; -4,\; -3,\; -3,\; -3,\; -3,\; -3,\; -2,\; -2,\; -2,\; -2,\; -1,\; -1
\end{aligned}
$$

The **five deepest valleys (Rv):**
$$
R_{v1}=-6,\quad R_{v2}=-5,\quad R_{v3}=-5,\quad R_{v4}=-5,\quad R_{v5}=-4
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Rz formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_z &= \frac{(R_{p1}+R_{p2}+R_{p3}+R_{p4}+R_{p5}) + (R_{v1}+R_{v2}+R_{v3}+R_{v4}+R_{v5})}{5} \\
&= \frac{(7+6+6+6+5) + (-6-5-5-5-4)}{5} \\
&= \frac{30 + (-25)}{5} \\
&= \frac{5}{5} \\
&= 1.0 \,\mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: Rz in KTU notation uses absolute magnitudes for the valley values (some texts use $\left\vert R_v \right\vert$). We used signed values here for rigor.

---

### (c) Solution — Rt (Total Roughness)

Rt is taken over the **entire evaluation length Ln = 4.0 mm**, not the sampling length. Since our data already covers the full evaluation length:

$$
R_t \;=\; \left\vert R_{p}(\text{max}) \right\vert + \left\vert R_v(\text{max}) \right\vert \;=\; 7 + 6 \;=\; 13 \,\mu m
$$

---

### (d) Solution — Rq (RMS)

**Step 1:** Compute squares of each ordinate.

**Row A squares:**
$$
4, 25, 9, 1, 16, 36, 4, 0, 9, 1 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\text{sum} = 105
$$

**Row B squares:**
$$
16, 25, 4, 9, 49, 36, 1, 4, 16, 9 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\text{sum} = 169
$$

**Row C squares:**
$$
9, 4, 25, 16, 1, 9, 36, 25, 4, 1 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\text{sum} = 130
$$

**Row D squares:**
$$
1, 16, 9, 4, 25, 9, 4, 16, 25, 4 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\text{sum} = 113
$$

**Total sum of squares:**
$$
\sum y_i^{2} \;=\; 105 + 169 + 130 + 113 \;=\; 517
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the RMS formula.

$$
R_q \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{517}{40}} \;=\; \sqrt{12.925} \;\approx\; 3.595 \,\mu m
$$

**Step 3:** Verify the empirical relation.

$$
R_q / R_a \;\approx\; 3.595 / 3.175 \;\approx\; 1.132
$$

This is **very close to the expected 1.11** for a Gaussian profile. ✓

---

## 3.3 Compact Answer Key Table

| Parameter | Computed Value | Unit |
|-----------|----------------|------|
| **Ra** | 3.175 | μm |
| **Rz** | 1.0 | μm |
| **Rt** | 13.0 | μm |
| **Rq** | 3.595 | μm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The part would be classified as a **medium-finish component** (Ra ≈ 3.2 μm) suitable for non-critical sliding surfaces such as machine tool beds, gearbox housings, or general-purpose shafting — **NOT** for bearings, seals, or hydraulic cylinders.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stylus Profilometer — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[WORKPIECE SURFACE] --> B[DIAMOND STYLUS TIP 2 um]
    B --> C[STYLUS ARM LEVER PIVOT]
    C --> D[LVDT CORE DISPLACEMENT PICKUP]
    D --> E[SIGNAL CONDITIONER AND AMPLIFIER]
    E --> F[NOISE FILTER SHORT WAVELENGTH LAMBDA S]
    F --> G[ROUGHNESS FILTER LAMBDA C CUTOFF]
    G --> H[MICROPROCESSOR COMPUTE Ra Rq Rz Rt]
    H --> I[DISPLAY DIGITAL READOUT]
    H --> J[PROFILE PLOTTER GRAPH]
    H --> K[DATA PRINTER REPORT]
    L[REFERENCE STAGE WITH SLIDE] -.-> A
    M[CONSTANT TRAVERSE DRIVE MOTOR] -.-> L
    N[STYLUS FORCE SPRING 0.7 mN] -.-> B

    classDef sensor fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    classDef compute fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#721C24,color:#000

    class B,C,D sensor
    class F,G,H compute
    class I,J,K output
    class A,L,M,N mech
```

**Reading the diagram (left to right):**
1. The **workpiece surface** is moved under the **diamond stylus** by a motor-driven reference stage.
2. Vertical micro-displacement of the stylus is converted to an electrical signal by the **LVDT** (Linear Variable Differential Transformer).
3. The signal is **amplified**, then **filtered** — first to remove high-frequency noise (λs), then to remove waviness (λc).
4. The **microprocessor** computes Ra, Rq, Rz, Rt and displays them on a digital readout.
5. A **graph plotter** traces the filtered profile.

---

## 4.2 Profile Composition — Form, Waviness, Roughness Separation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TOTAL SURFACE PROFILE RAW SIGNAL] --> B[FILTER LAMBDA F LONG WAVELENGTH]
    B --> C[FORM ERROR PROFILE MACRO GEOMETRY]
    A --> D[FILTER LAMBDA C CUTOFF]
    D --> E[WAVINESS PROFILE MESO GEOMETRY]
    A --> F[FILTER LAMBDA S SHORT WAVELENGTH]
    F --> G[ROUGHNESS PROFILE MICRO GEOMETRY]

    H[SAMPLING LENGTH Lr] --> D
    I[EVALUATION LENGTH Ln 5 TIMES Lr] --> H
    J[ASSESSMENT LENGTH Lt] --> I

    classDef input fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    classDef macro fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef meso fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000
    classDef micro fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef meta fill:#F0E6FF,stroke:#6A0DAD,color:#000

    class A input
    class B,C macro
    class D,E,H,I,J meta
    class F,G micro
```

**Interpretation of this diagram:**
- The **raw signal** from the stylus contains all frequencies mixed together.
- **Long-wavelength filter (λf)** separates the **form error** (straightness, flatness).
- **Mid-wavelength filter (λc)** isolates the **waviness** (vibration marks, deflection).
- **Short-wavelength filter (λs)** extracts the true **roughness** (tool feed marks).

---

## 4.3 Stylus Tip Geometry — Contact Mechanics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[STYLUS TIP 2 um RADIUS] --> B[CONTACT POINT PEAK]
    C[WORKPIECE SURFACE] --> D[ASPERITY PEAK HEIGHT hp]
    C --> E[VALLEY DEPTH hv]
    B --> F[STYLUS FOLLOWS PEAK]
    G[STYLUS FORCE 0.7 mN] --> A
    H[FILTERED DISPLACEMENT y of x] --> F

    classDef stylus fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#721C24,color:#000
    classDef surface fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000

    class A,B,G stylus
    class C,D,E surface
    class F,H sig
```

**Key notes on stylus tip geometry:**
- A 2 μm radius stylus **cannot** resolve features sharper than ~5 μm (since the tip itself has finite size).
- For nano-surface work, **non-contact profilometers** (optical interferometers, AFM) are used instead.

---

## 4.4 Standard Surface Roughness Symbols (ISO 1302)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BASE V SYMBOL 60 DEGREE TICK] --> B[WITHOUT HORIZONTAL BAR ANY METHOD]
    A --> C[WITH SINGLE HORIZONTAL BAR MACHINING REQUIRED]
    A --> D[WITH CIRCLE IN APEX MACHINING NOT PERMITTED]

    B --> E[ADD Ra VALUE INSIDE V IN MICROMETER]
    E --> F[ADD LAMBDA C CUTOFF BELOW]
    F --> G[ADD LAY DIRECTION SYMBOL]

    classDef basic fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    classDef add fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef warning fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#721C24,color:#000

    class A,B,C,D basic
    class E,F,G add
```

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme, students should be able to **draw and interpret** the basic V symbol with Ra value, λc cutoff, and lay direction indicator.

---

## 4.5 Classification of Surface Finish Measurement Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SURFACE FINISH MEASUREMENT METHODS] --> B[CONTACT TYPE]
    A --> C[NON-CONTACT TYPE]

    B --> B1[STYLUS PROFILOMETER MOST COMMON]
    B1 --> B1a[TALYSURF BRAND TAYLOR HOBSON]
    B1 --> B1b[PROFILOMETER MITUTOYO]

    C --> C1[OPTICAL METHODS]
    C1 --> C1a[OPTICAL PROFILOMETER WHITE LIGHT INTERFEROMETRY]
    C1 --> C1b[LASER CONFOCAL MICROSCOPE]

    C --> C2[PNEUMATIC METHODS]
    C2 --> C2a[PNEUMATIC GAUGING AIR PRESSURE VARIATION]

    C --> C3[ELECTRICAL METHODS]
    C3 --> C3a[CAPACITIVE PROBE]
    C3 --> C3b[INDUCTIVE PROBE]

    C --> C4[COMPARISON METHODS]
    C4 --> C4a[VISUAL COMPARISON WITH STANDARD SPECIMENS]
    C4 --> C4b[TACTILE COMPARISON NAIL TEST]

    A --> D[OTHER METHODS]
    D --> D1[REPLICA METHOD USING CELLULOSE ACETATE]
    D --> D2[TRACER METHOD WITH THALLIUM SULPHIDE]

    classDef contact fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef noncontact fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef other fill:#F0E6FF,stroke:#6A0DAD,color:#000
    classDef main fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000

    class B,B1,B1a,B1b contact
    class C,C1,C1a,C1b,C2,C2a,C3,C3a,C3b,C4,C4a,C4b noncontact

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the following terms with reference to surface finish measurement:**
(a) **Sampling length**
(b) **Evaluation length**
(c) **Cutoff filter (λc)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition Box:**
> **(a) Sampling Length (lr):** The length of the surface profile over which a single measurement of a roughness parameter (such as Ra) is made. It is the **reference cutoff wavelength** at which the measured profile is evaluated.
>
> **(b) Evaluation Length (ln):** The total length of the surface profile used for the assessment of a roughness parameter. By ISO 4287 convention, **ln = 5 × lr**.
>
> **(c) Cutoff Filter (λc):** An electronic filter that separates roughness from waviness by removing all wavelength components longer than λc. Standard λc values are **0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, and 8.0 mm**.

**[3 Marks = 1 mark each for the three terms with examples]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the difference between Ra and Rz. Why is Ra preferred over Rz in industrial specifications?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Ra (CLA) | Rz (10-Point) |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| **Definition** | Arithmetic average of all absolute deviations from the mean line | Average of 5 highest peaks + 5 deepest valleys, measured peak-to-valley |
| **Computation** | Statistical average of all N ordinates | Only 10 extreme points |
| **Sensitivity to scratches** | Insensitive to isolated defects | Highly sensitive (a single scratch dramatically alters Rz) |
| **Statistical stability** | High — low variability between samples | Lower — depends on extreme values |
| **Industry preference** | Universally preferred (>70% of specifications) | Used for bearings, sealing surfaces |

**Ra is preferred because:**
1. It is **statistically robust** — averaging over many points smooths out local anomalies.
2. It correlates **well with functional performance** for most sliding/rolling applications.
3. It is the **primary international standard parameter** (ISO 4287, ASME B46.1).
4. **Instrumentation** for Ra is widely available and calibrated globally.

**[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **stylus-type surface profilometer**. Discuss the role of the cutoff filter.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A surface profile is sampled at 50 equally spaced ordinates over a sampling length of 0.8 mm. The deviations (in μm) from the mean line are given below. Compute **Ra** and **Rq** for the sampling length.

**Data:** Deviations in μm:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&+3,\; +5,\; -2,\; +4,\; -1,\; +6,\; -3,\; +2,\; 0,\; -4,\\
&+5,\; -2,\; +3,\; -5,\; +1,\; +4,\; -3,\; +2,\; +5,\; -1,\\
&+4,\; -3,\; +2,\; +5,\; -4,\; +1,\; +3,\; -2,\; +5,\; -2,\\
&+3,\; +4,\; -1,\; +2,\; -3,\; +5,\; +1,\; -4,\; +3,\; -2,\\
&+2,\; +4,\; -3,\; +5,\; -1,\; +3,\; -2,\; +4,\; +1,\; -3
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Stylus Profilometer**

**Block Diagram (described):**

```
Workpiece → Stylus (diamond tip) → LVDT transducer
                                       ↓
              Display ← Microprocessor ← λc Filter ← Amplifier
                ↓
            Profile plotter
```

**Working Principle:**

1. The **diamond-tipped stylus** (radius 2 μm or 10 μm) is dragged across the workpiece surface under a controlled force of **0.7 mN (ISO 3274)**.
2. As the stylus traverses peaks and valleys, it moves **vertically**.
3. The vertical displacement is converted to an **electrical signal** by an **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)**.
4. The signal is **amplified** and then passed through the **λc (cutoff) filter**.
5. The **microprocessor** computes Ra, Rq, Rz, Rt and displays them.
6. A **chart recorder** traces the filtered profile.

**Role of the Cutoff Filter:**

The cutoff filter **separates roughness from waviness**. The measured profile contains:
- Long-wavelength components (form, waviness)
- Short-wavelength components (roughness, noise)

The cutoff λc **passes only wavelengths shorter than λc** (i.e., the rough peaks) and **rejects longer wavelengths** (waviness). The choice of λc depends on the **expected Ra** of the surface (see Section 2.2 table).

If λc is **too small** → important roughness components are filtered out → Ra is underestimated.
If λc is **too large** → waviness contaminates the roughness reading → Ra is overestimated.

**[7 Marks: 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for working, 2 marks for cutoff filter role]**

---

#### **Part (b) — Computation of Ra and Rq**

**Step 1 — Absolute Values:**

List all 50 absolute deviations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&3,5,2,4,1,6,3,2,0,4,\\
&5,2,3,5,1,4,3,2,5,1,\\
&4,3,2,5,4,1,3,2,5,2,\\
&3,4,1,2,3,5,1,4,3,2,\\
&2,4,3,5,1,3,2,4,1,3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of Absolute Deviations (per row of 10):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 1:}\;& 3+5+2+4+1+6+3+2+0+4 = 30 \\
\text{Row 2:}\;& 5+2+3+5+1+4+3+2+5+1 = 31 \\
\text{Row 3:}\;& 4+3+2+5+4+1+3+2+5+2 = 31 \\
\text{Row 4:}\;& 3+4+1+2+3+5+1+4+3+2 = 28 \\
\text{Row 5:}\;& 2+4+3+5+1+3+2+4+1+3 = 28
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Total sum} \;=\; 30 + 31 + 31 + 28 + 28 \;=\; 148
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Ra:**

$$
R_a \;=\; \frac{148}{50} \;=\; 2.96 \,\mu m
$$

**Step 4 — Sum of Squares (per row of 10):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 1:}\;& 9+25+4+16+1+36+9+4+0+16 = 120 \\
\text{Row 2:}\;& 25+4+9+25+1+16+9+4+25+1 = 119 \\
\text{Row 3:}\;& 16+9+4+25+16+1+9+4+25+4 = 113 \\
\text{Row 4:}\;& 9+16+1+4+9+25+1+16+9+4 = 94 \\
\text{Row 5:}\;& 4+16+9+25+1+9+4+16+1+9 = 94
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Total sum of squares} \;=\; 120 + 119 + 113 + 94 + 94 \;=\; 540
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Rq:**

$$
R_q \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{540}{50}} \;=\; \sqrt{10.8} \;\approx\; 3.286 \,\mu m
$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**

$$
R_q / R_a \;=\; 3.286 / 2.96 \;\approx\; 1.11 \quad \checkmark \text{ (matches Gaussian expectation)}
$$

**Final Answer Box:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Ra** | **2.96 μm** |
| **Rq** | **3.29 μm** |

**[7 Marks: 2 marks for Ra setup, 2 marks for correct Ra, 2 marks for Rq setup, 1 mark for Rq value]**

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **various methods of measuring surface finish**. Compare contact-type and non-contact-type methods.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **standard surface roughness symbol** as per ISO 1302. Explain the meaning of each element of the symbol.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Methods of Surface Finish Measurement**

**1. Stylus-type Profilometer (Contact Method)**

The most common industrial instrument. A diamond stylus (radius 2–10 μm) is drawn across the surface at constant speed. Vertical displacement of the stylus is converted to an electrical signal by an LVDT.

*Advantages:* Quantitative, widely accepted, reproducible, ISO-compliant.
*Limitations:* Contact force may scratch soft materials, slow speed, limited lateral resolution.

**2. Optical Profilometer (Non-contact)**

Uses **white-light interferometry** or **laser confocal microscopy**. The surface is illuminated with a focused beam, and the reflected light intensity is analyzed to map the surface.

*Advantages:* No contact (no scratching), nanometer resolution, fast scanning.
*Limitations:* Expensive, sensitive to surface reflectivity, requires calibration on reference artefacts.

**3. Pneumatic Method (Non-contact)**

Compressed air is forced through a small nozzle directed at the surface. The **back-pressure** varies with the **gap between nozzle and surface**, giving a measure of roughness.

*Advantages:* Simple, robust, suitable for in-process measurement.
*Limitations:* Qualitative only, requires calibration, sensitive to nozzle wear.

**4. Capacitive / Inductive Probe (Non-contact)**

The surface forms one plate of a capacitor; distance variations change the capacitance. Inductive probes use eddy currents.

*Advantages:* High sensitivity, fast.
*Limitations:* Sensitive to material properties, requires metallic surface.

**5. Comparison / Visual Method**

The inspector visually or by touch compares the surface with a set of **standard reference specimens** of known Ra. Roughness "comparison blocks" are widely used in shop-floor inspection.

*Advantages:* Quick, cheap, no instrument.
*Limitations:* Highly subjective, only qualitative, operator-dependent.

**6. Replica Method**

A thin film of **cellulose acetate** or **silicone rubber** is pressed against the surface. The negative replica is then measured under a microscope or profilometer.

*Advantages:* Enables measurement of inaccessible surfaces (e.g., cylinder bores).
*Limitations:* Resolution limited by replica material.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Contact (Stylus) | Non-Contact (Optical) |
|---------|------------------|------------------------|
| Resolution | 0.01 μm | 0.001 μm |
| Speed | Slow (mm/s) | Fast (mm/s to cm/s) |
| Cost | Moderate | High |
| Soft materials | Limited | Suitable |
| Standardization | Fully ISO-compliant | Less standardized |
| Operator skill | Low | Moderate |

**[7 Marks: 3 marks enumeration, 2 marks contact type details, 2 marks non-contact details, 1 mark conclusion]**

---

#### **Part (b) — ISO 1302 Surface Roughness Symbol**

**Sketch (described in words):**

```
              ⊙   ← Lay symbol (multidirectional)
              |
        ___________
       /  Ra 0.8   \    ← Ra value in μm
      V___________ /
       \___________/    
        |   λc 2.5    ← Cutoff in mm
        |
────────────────────  ← Surface line (horizontal extension)
```

**Element-by-element Meaning:**

| Element | Meaning |
|---------|---------|
| **Open V (60° tick)** | Base symbol for surface roughness |
| **Single horizontal bar above the V** | Material removal required (machined surface) |
| **Circle inside the apex** | Material removal prohibited (as-molded, forged, cast) |
| **Number inside V (e.g., Ra 0.8)** | The maximum permissible Ra value in micrometers |
| **Value below extension (e.g., λc 2.5)** | Cutoff filter wavelength in mm |
| **Small circle on extension (⊙, //, ⊥)** | Direction of lay (⊥ = perpendicular, // = parallel, M = multidirectional) |
| **Numerical value on extension** | Sampling length in mm |
| **Letter inside V** | Machining method (e.g., G = grinding, T = turning) |

**Example interpretation:**

`V with Ra 0.8 inside, λc 2.5 below, ⊥ on extension`

→ "The surface must be machined to **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm**, measured with a **cutoff filter of 2.5 mm**, and the lay (dominant pattern) must be **perpendicular** to the reference direction shown."

**[7 Marks: 2 marks for sketch, 3 marks for element meaning, 2 marks for example interpretation]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> **Pitfall 1 — Mean Line Misuse:** Many students compute Ra without first verifying that the data is referenced to the **mean line**. If the given data has a non-zero sum, the calculated Ra will be incorrect. *(Lose up to 2 marks)*
>
> **Pitfall 2 — Confusing Sampling Length and Evaluation Length:** The Rz calculation uses only **one sampling length**, while Rt uses the **full evaluation length (5 × sampling length)**. Mixing these up gives wrong answers. *(Lose up to 2 marks)*
>
> **Pitfall 3 — Forgetting the Filter:** Students describe a stylus profilometer but **omit the role of the λc cutoff filter**. This is a mandatory part of any working explanation. *(Lose 2 marks per occurrence)*
>
> **Pitfall 4 — Surface Symbol Confusion:** Students confuse the **V symbol** with a generic "tick mark." Always use the **60° V** with optional horizontal bar and circle in apex. *(Lose 1 mark)*
>
> **Pitfall 5 — Units:** Always express Ra, Rz, Rt in **μm** and λc in **mm**. KTU examiners mark strict unit adherence. *(Lose 1 mark per missing unit)*
>
> **Pitfall 6 — Wrong Formula for Rz:** Some students use $R_z = R_p(\text{max}) + R_v(\text{max})$. This is **Rt**, not Rz. Rz is the **average of five peaks + five valleys**, divided by 5. *(Lose 2–3 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and paste it on your study wall one day before the exam.**

**✅ Definitions to Memorize:**
- [ ] Surface finish = micro-geometry of a real surface.
- [ ] Three components: **Roughness, Waviness, Lay**.
- [ ] Sampling length (lr) = single measurement reference length.
- [ ] Evaluation length (ln) = 5 × lr per ISO 4287.
- [ ] Mean line = least-squares line with $\sum y_i = 0$.

**✅ Formulas to Memorize:**
- [ ] $R_a = (1/n)\sum \left\vert y_i \right\vert$
- [ ] $R_q = \sqrt{(1/n)\sum y_i^{2}}$
- [ ] $R_z = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5}(R_{pi} + R_{vi})$
- [ ] $R_t = R_p^{max} + R_v^{max}$ (over evaluation length)
- [ ] $R_q \approx 1.11 \, R_a$ (Gaussian profile)

**✅ Standard λc Values (mm):**
- [ ] **0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0** (five ISO standard cutoffs)

**✅ Surface Roughness Symbol (ISO 1302):**
- [ ] V symbol = 60° open tick.
- [ ] Horizontal bar = material removal required.
- [ ] Circle in apex = material removal NOT permitted.
- [ ] Number inside = Ra in μm.
- [ ] Below = λc in mm.
- [ ] Above = lay direction (⊙, //, ⊥, M).

**✅ Instruments to Know:**
- [ ] **Stylus profilometer** (contact, LVDT, diamond tip 2–10 μm, force 0.7 mN).
- [ ] **Optical profilometer** (non-contact, interferometry).
- [ ] **Pneumatic gauge** (non-contact, back-pressure).
- [ ] **Replica method** (cellulose acetate, for inaccessible surfaces).
- [ ] **Comparison blocks** (qualitative, shop-floor).

**✅ Typical Ra Values for Common Surfaces:**
- [ ] Bearing journal: 0.1 – 0.4 μm.
- [ ] Hydraulic bore: 0.2 – 0.8 μm.
- [ ] General machined: 0.8 – 3.2 μm.
- [ ] Rough cast/forged: > 6.3 μm.

**✅ KTU Numerical Workflow:**
1. Verify mean line (sum of deviations ≈ 0).
2. Compute absolute values → $\sum \left\vert y_i \right\vert$ → Ra.
3. Compute squares → $\sum y_i^{2}$ → Rq.
4. Sort and find 5 peaks + 5 valleys → Rz.
5. Find max peak + max valley → Rt (over evaluation length).
6. **Always include units (μm).**

**✅ Examiners' Favourite Questions:**
- [ ] Define Ra, Rz, Rt with formulae.
- [ ] Sketch and explain stylus profilometer with cutoff filter.
- [ ] Compute Ra, Rz, Rq from a 40 or 50 ordinate dataset.
- [ ] Draw and interpret ISO 1302 surface symbol.
- [ ] Compare contact vs non-contact methods.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Measurement of Surface Finish**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain Measurement: Theory of Strain Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strain Measurement: Theory of Strain Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain (ε):** Strain is defined as the ratio of change in dimension (length) to the original dimension of a body when subjected to an external force. It is a dimensionless quantity, typically expressed in microstrains (µε).

Mathematically, for a uniaxial loading condition:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

Where:
- $\Delta L$ = Change in length (m)
- $L$ = Original length (m)
- $\varepsilon$ = Strain (dimensionless, often expressed as $\mu\varepsilon$ where $1\,\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}\,\varepsilon$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Strain Gauge (Strain Gage):** A strain gauge is a passive transducer that converts mechanical deformation (strain) into a proportional change in electrical resistance. The underlying principle is the **piezoresistive effect**, wherein the electrical resistivity of a material changes when it is mechanically stressed.

**Types of Strain:**
- **Normal Strain (ε):** Linear deformation per unit length along the direction of applied force.
- **Shear Strain (γ):** Angular deformation between two originally perpendicular planes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a thin rubber band stretched between two posts. As you pull it, it becomes:
1. **Longer** (length increases)
2. **Thinner** (cross-section decreases)
3. **Its internal structure resists the pull** (atomic lattice distorts)

A strain gauge works on exactly this principle, but instead of rubber, it uses a **metallic foil or semiconductor** whose **electrical resistance** changes predictably with these three deformations. We then measure this tiny resistance change using a **Wheatstone bridge circuit**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of strain gauges as "muscle sensors." Just as your muscles stretch and tense under load, the strain gauge's conductive grid stretches and its resistance changes — much like how a stretched copper wire becomes harder for electrons to flow through.

## 1.3 Classification of Strain Gauges

| Category | Type | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| **Mechanical** | Mechanical extensometer | Direct dial gauge reading |
| **Optical** | Moire fringe, photoelastic | Uses light interference patterns |
| **Electrical** | Resistive (bonded, unbonded) | Most widely used; based on piezoresistance |
| **Electrical** | Semiconductor | Uses silicon; gauge factor ~100× higher than metallic |
| **Electrical** | Capacitive/Inductive | Less common, niche applications |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examinations, the focus is overwhelmingly on **resistive (piezoresistive) strain gauges** and their associated bridge circuits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of Resistive Strain Gauges

The foundational principle is the relationship between the **resistance** of a conductor and its geometric/physical properties:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

Where:
- $R$ = Electrical resistance (Ω)
- $\rho$ = Resistivity of the material (Ω·m)
- $L$ = Length of the conductor (m)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area (m²)

When a strain gauge is bonded to a structure under load, **all three** of these parameters change simultaneously. Differentiating $R = \rho L / A$ using the product rule of partial differentiation gives:

$$dR = \frac{\rho}{A}dL + \frac{L}{A}d\rho - \frac{\rho L}{A^2}dA$$

Dividing throughout by $R = \rho L / A$:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{dL}{L} + \frac{d\rho}{\rho} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

## 2.2 Derivation of Gauge Factor (G.F.)

The **Gauge Factor** is the most critical parameter of any strain gauge. It quantifies the sensitivity — the ratio of fractional resistance change to fractional length change:

$$G_f = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L} = \frac{dR / R}{\varepsilon}$$

From the previous equation:

$$G_f = \frac{dL/L}{dL/L} - \frac{dA/A}{dL/L} + \frac{d\rho/\rho}{dL/L} = 1 - \frac{dA/A}{\varepsilon} + \frac{d\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}$$

For a conductor with **circular cross-section**, $A = \pi r^2$. Therefore $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = \frac{2\pi r \, dr}{\pi r^2} = \frac{2 \, dr}{r}$$

By Poisson's ratio definition: $\nu = -\frac{dr/r}{dL/L}$, so $\frac{dr}{r} = -\nu \, \varepsilon$.

Substituting:

$$G_f = 1 - \frac{2(-\nu\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon} + \frac{d\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{d\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Gauge Factor Equation:**
> $$G_f = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}$$
>
> The first two terms (1 + 2ν) account for geometric effects; the third term is the **piezoresistive effect**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Value |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Engineering strain | $\sim 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| 2 | $R = \rho L / A$ | Conductor resistance | 100 Ω to 1000 Ω |
| 3 | $G_f = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | Gauge factor definition | Metallic: 2.0; Semiconductor: 100–200 |
| 4 | $G_f = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$ | Theoretical G.F. | For Constantan: ~2.1 |
| 5 | $\Delta R = R \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | Resistance change | Micro-ohms typical |
| 6 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (stress-strain) | E = Young's modulus |
| 7 | $V_o = V_s \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon / 4$ | Quarter bridge output | For $R_1$ active gauge |
| 8 | $V_o = V_s \cdot G_f \cdot (\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2) / 4$ | Half bridge (adjacent arms) | Bending strain rejection |
| 9 | $V_o = V_s \cdot G_f \cdot (\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 - \varepsilon_4) / 4$ | Full bridge | Maximum sensitivity |
| 10 | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} / \varepsilon_{\text{axial}}$ | Poisson's ratio | Steel: 0.30; Aluminum: 0.33 |
| 11 | $V_o = V_s \cdot [R_1/(R_1+R_2) - R_3/(R_3+R_4)]$ | General Wheatstone bridge | General case |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Note for KTU Exams:** Most students forget the **$(\Delta\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$** term. For metallic gauges this is nearly zero, but for **semiconductor strain gauges**, this term dominates (it can be 100× larger than the geometric term). Always mention it in derivations.

## 2.4 Types of Bonded Strain Gauges (Detailed)

### A. Bonded Metallic Strain Gauges
- **Wire type:** Fine resistance wire (Constantan, Nichrome) zigzagged and bonded to a carrier.
- **Foil type:** Photo-etched from a thin metal foil. Better heat dissipation, more flexible.
- **Thin-film type:** Deposited by sputtering/vacuum evaporation. Used in harsh environments.

### B. Unbonded Strain Gauges
- Wires stretched between fixed posts; strain deflects the frame, changing wire tension.
- Used in load cells and pressure transducers.

### C. Semiconductor Strain Gauges
- Made from doped silicon (p-type or n-type).
- Gauge factor ~ 50 to 200 (much higher than metallic).
- More sensitive to temperature (require compensation).
- Used in micro-sensors and MEMS applications.

## 2.5 Wheatstone Bridge Configurations

The Wheatstone bridge converts the tiny resistance change into a measurable voltage.

### Quarter Bridge (Single Active Gauge)
$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$

### Half Bridge (Two Active Gauges — Adjacent Arms)
$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot (\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2)$$
- Naturally rejects temperature and rejects bending if gauges are on opposite sides.

### Half Bridge (Two Active Gauges — Opposite Arms)
$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot (\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2)$$

### Full Bridge (Four Active Gauges)
$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot (\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 - \varepsilon_4)$$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM):** Bridges, buildings, aircraft wings.
2. **Load Cells:** Weighing scales, force measurement in industrial presses.
3. **Pressure Transducers:** Converts pressure to strain on a diaphragm.
4. **Torque Measurement on Rotating Shafts:** Slip-ring or wireless telemetry.
5. **Biomedical Sensors:** Respiratory belts, prosthetic feedback.
6. **Automotive:** Crash test dummies, suspension testing.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Gauge Factor

### Starting Point
We begin with the fundamental equation of resistance for a uniform conductor:

$$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$

Where $\rho$, $L$, and $A$ are functions of mechanical strain. To find the total differential $dR$, we apply the product rule for partial differentiation.

### Step 1: Partial Differentiation of R

$$\frac{\partial R}{\partial \rho} = \frac{L}{A}$$

$$\frac{\partial R}{\partial L} = \frac{\rho}{A}$$

$$\frac{\partial R}{\partial A} = -\frac{\rho L}{A^2}$$

### Step 2: Total Differential

$$dR = \left(\frac{\partial R}{\partial \rho}\right) d\rho + \left(\frac{\partial R}{\partial L}\right) dL + \left(\frac{\partial R}{\partial A}\right) dA$$

Substituting the partial derivatives:

$$dR = \frac{L}{A} d\rho + \frac{\rho}{A} dL - \frac{\rho L}{A^2} dA$$

### Step 3: Normalize by Dividing by R

Since $R = \rho L / A$, dividing both sides by $R$:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{L \, d\rho}{A} \cdot \frac{A}{\rho L} + \frac{\rho \, dL}{A} \cdot \frac{A}{\rho L} - \frac{\rho L \, dA}{A^2} \cdot \frac{A}{\rho L}$$

Simplifying each term:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

### Step 4: Express dA in Terms of dL Using Poisson's Ratio

For a circular cross-section: $A = \pi r^2$.

Differentiating:

$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr$$

So:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = \frac{2\pi r \, dr}{\pi r^2} = 2 \cdot \frac{dr}{r}$$

By the definition of Poisson's ratio ($\nu$):

$$\nu = -\frac{\text{lateral strain}}{\text{axial strain}} = -\frac{dr/r}{dL/L}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dr}{r} = -\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon$$

Substituting back:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot (-\nu \cdot \varepsilon) = -2\nu\varepsilon$$

### Step 5: Assemble the Gauge Factor

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \varepsilon - (-2\nu\varepsilon) + \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \varepsilon + 2\nu\varepsilon + \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \varepsilon(1 + 2\nu) + \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

By definition of gauge factor $G_f = (\Delta R/R)/\varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{G_f = \frac{dR/R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}}$$

### Step 6: Numerical Verification (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A Constantan strain gauge has $\nu = 0.33$ and negligible piezoresistive effect. Find its gauge factor.

**Solution:**

$$G_f = 1 + 2(0.33) + 0 = 1 + 0.66 = \mathbf{1.66}$$

Practical constantan gauges report $G_f \approx 2.0$ to 2.1 because the piezoresistive contribution is not exactly zero.

---

## 3.2 Wheatstone Bridge Output Derivation

### General Bridge Equation

Consider a Wheatstone bridge with four arms $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$, excited by supply voltage $V_s$.

By voltage divider rule:

$$V_a = V_s \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_b = V_s \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}$$

The differential output voltage is:

$$V_o = V_a - V_b = V_s \left[\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}\right]$$

### Quarter Bridge Configuration

Assume $R_1 = R(1 + G_f \varepsilon)$ is the active strain gauge; $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$.

$$V_o = V_s\left[\frac{R(1+G_f\varepsilon)}{R(1+G_f\varepsilon)+R} - \frac{R}{2R}\right]$$

$$V_o = V_s\left[\frac{1+G_f\varepsilon}{2+G_f\varepsilon} - \frac{1}{2}\right]$$

Combining over common denominator:

$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{2(1+G_f\varepsilon) - (2+G_f\varepsilon)}{2(2+G_f\varepsilon)}$$

$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{2 + 2G_f\varepsilon - 2 - G_f\varepsilon}{2(2+G_f\varepsilon)}$$

$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{G_f\varepsilon}{2(2+G_f\varepsilon)}$$

Since $G_f\varepsilon \ll 1$, we approximate $2 + G_f\varepsilon \approx 2$:

$$\boxed{V_o \approx \frac{V_s \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon}{4}}$$

### Full Bridge Configuration (Maximum Sensitivity)

With four active gauges, $R_1 = R(1+G_f\varepsilon_1)$, $R_2 = R(1+G_f\varepsilon_2)$, $R_3 = R(1+G_f\varepsilon_3)$, $R_4 = R(1+G_f\varepsilon_4)$:

$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{4} G_f (\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 - \varepsilon_4)$$

> [!TIP]
> The **full bridge** provides 4× the sensitivity of a quarter bridge and **automatically rejects temperature** if all four gauges experience the same thermal strain.

---

## 3.3 Strain Rosette Analysis (Planar)

When strain at a point must be measured in multiple directions, a **strain rosette** is used.

### Rectangular Rosette (0°-45°-90°)

Three gauges measure strains $\varepsilon_a$ (0°), $\varepsilon_b$ (45°), $\varepsilon_c$ (90°).

The principal strains are:

$$\varepsilon_{1,2} = \frac{\varepsilon_a + \varepsilon_c}{2} \pm \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(\varepsilon_a - \varepsilon_c)^2 + (2\varepsilon_b - \varepsilon_a - \varepsilon_c)^2}$$

### Principal Strain Direction

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\varepsilon_b - \varepsilon_a - \varepsilon_c}{\varepsilon_a - \varepsilon_c}$$

### Maximum Shear Strain

$$\gamma_{\max} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \sqrt{(\varepsilon_a - \varepsilon_c)^2 + (2\varepsilon_b - \varepsilon_a - \varepsilon_c)^2}$$
