# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEMET725]**
**"Define temperature compensation. Why is it essential in measurement systems?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** Temperature compensation is the technique of designing a measurement system so that the effect of ambient temperature variations on the output is nullified, ensuring that the output depends only on the true measurand.
2. **Essentiality — Physical reasons (1 mark):** Temperature alters resistance, dimensions, and elastic properties of sensing elements, leading to zero drift and span drift. Without compensation, instrument accuracy degrades with ambient conditions.
3. **Practical implication (1 mark):** Compensation is required to meet KTU-specified accuracy classes (e.g., Class 0.1, 0.2 in load cells) and to obtain reliable readings in field conditions where temperature varies from 0 °C to 50 °C or beyond.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEMET725]**
**"What is a self-compensated strain gauge? State the mathematical condition for self-compensation."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** A self-compensated strain gauge is one in which the grid alloy is specially formulated so that its temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR) and expansion coefficient combine to make the *apparent strain* zero for a *specific substrate material*.
2. **Compensation condition (1 mark):** $\alpha_1 + G(\beta_g - \beta_s) = 0$, where $\alpha_1$ is the TCR, $G$ is the gauge factor, and $\beta_g, \beta_s$ are the expansion coefficients of the grid and substrate.
3. **Consequence (1 mark):** Such a gauge can be used in a quarter-bridge without a dummy gauge, since its own temperature response is automatically zero on the matched substrate. Different matched gauges exist for steel, aluminum, and titanium substrates.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEMET725 Module 2]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the apparent strain in a strain gauge due to temperature change, and explain the condition for self-compensation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the resistance expression under combined strain and temperature (2 marks):**
The total fractional resistance change of a strain gauge bonded to a substrate, subjected to strain $\epsilon$ and temperature change $\Delta T$, is:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R_0} = G \cdot \epsilon + \left[ \alpha_1 + G(\beta_g - \beta_s) \right] \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Identify the apparent strain (2 marks):**
The total strain that the gauge *appears* to experience is obtained by dividing the $\Delta R / R_0$ by $G$:

$$\epsilon_{apparent} = \epsilon + \left[ \frac{\alpha_1}{G} + (\beta_g - \beta_s) \right] \Delta T$$

The second term is the **spurious (thermal) apparent strain**:

$$\epsilon_{thermal} = \left[ \frac{\alpha_1}{G} + (\beta_g - \beta_s) \right] \Delta T$$

**Step 3 — Compensation condition (2 marks):**
For self-compensation, the thermal term must vanish:

$$\frac{\alpha_1}{G} + (\beta_g - \beta_s) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha_1 = -G(\beta_g - \beta_s)$$

**Step 4 — Practical selection (1 mark):**
This equation allows selection of grid alloys. For example, constantan matched to steel gives near-zero apparent strain around 20 °C, while Karma alloy is matched for stainless steel and titanium.

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the dummy-gauge method of temperature compensation in a Wheatstone bridge. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bridge Configuration (2 marks):**
In a half-bridge with dummy gauge, two arms contain resistive elements:
- $R_1$ = **Active gauge** bonded on the test specimen, experiencing mechanical strain $\epsilon$ plus temperature $\Delta T$.
- $R_2$ = **Dummy gauge** bonded on an *unstrained* piece of the *same* material as the specimen, experiencing only the same temperature $\Delta T$.
- $R_3, R_4$ = fixed precision resistors.

**Step 2 — Wheatstone Bridge Equation (2 marks):**
The output is:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Effect of Temperature (2 marks):**
Both $R_1$ and $R_2$ have the same TCR and are at the same temperature. When $\Delta T$ changes, both $R_1$ and $R_2$ change by the same proportional amount. The ratio $R_1 / (R_1 + R_2)$ is therefore **invariant to $\Delta T$**, and the temperature-induced signal cancels out. Only the strain $\epsilon$ affecting $R_1$ alone produces a net differential output.

**Step 4 — Result and Key Point (1 mark):**
$$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \epsilon \quad \text{(independent of } \Delta T \text{)}$$

This is the standard method used in **load cells, torque sensors, and pressure transducers** in industry.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes**
> 1. **Forgetting to state that the dummy block must be of the same material as the test specimen** (so that $\beta_s$ and heat conduction match). Marks lost: 1.
> 2. **Confusing the dummy gauge compensation condition with that of the self-compensated gauge.** Dummy gauge does not require $\alpha_1 + G(\beta_g - \beta_s) = 0$ because the thermal signals cancel *in the bridge subtraction*, not in the gauge itself.
> 3. **Failing to draw the bridge diagram clearly** with all four arms labeled, including the excitation source. A neat block diagram carries 1 mark on its own.
> 4. **Not mentioning that the dummy gauge must be placed in thermal contact with the active gauge** (in the same ambient), so they always share the same $\Delta T$.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEMET725 Module 2]**

**(a) Explain the principle of cold-junction compensation in a thermocouple instrument. Show that the corrected hot-junction temperature is obtained by adding a synthesized voltage equal to the Seebeck voltage at the cold-junction temperature. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Seebeck Law (1 mark):**
A thermocouple between materials A and B generates a voltage:

$$E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} S_{AB}(T)\, dT$$

This voltage corresponds to the temperature *difference* between the hot and cold junctions, not absolute temperature.

**Step 2 — The Reference Problem (1 mark):**
Standard thermocouple tables assume a cold-junction temperature of **0 °C**. In real instruments, the cold junction is at ambient $T_{CJ} \neq 0\,°C$. The output voltage is:

$$V_{measured} = E_{AB}(T_{CJ}, T_{hot})$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of Compensation (3 marks):**
From the additive property of the Seebeck integral:

$$E_{AB}(T_{CJ}, T_{hot}) + E_{AB}(0, T_{CJ}) = E_{AB}(0, T_{hot})$$

Rearranging:

$$E_{AB}(0, T_{hot}) = V_{measured} + E_{AB}(0, T_{CJ})$$

The quantity $E_{AB}(0, T_{hot})$ is the **correct voltage referenced to 0 °C**, and $E_{AB}(0, T_{CJ})$ is the voltage that *must be added* to the measured value.

**Step 4 — Implementation (2 marks):**
An auxiliary temperature sensor (e.g., LM35 silicon sensor or a precision silicon diode) measures $T_{CJ}$. A microcontroller computes $E_{AB}(0, T_{CJ})$ using a polynomial (ITS-90 coefficients) and adds it to $V_{measured}$. The result is then converted back to temperature using the standard thermocouple tables, yielding the **true hot-junction temperature $T_{hot}$**.

---

**(b) Discuss the methods of temperature compensation in mechanical pressure gauges and bimetallic instruments. How does a bimetallic strip achieve compensation? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Problem in Mechanical Gauges (1 mark):**
In a Bourdon tube pressure gauge, the tube metal has a finite coefficient of thermal expansion. When ambient temperature rises, the tube expands thermally, producing a *false* deflection that adds to the pressure-induced deflection. This causes zero error and span error.

**Step 2 — Bimetallic Strip Principle (2 marks):**
A bimetallic strip consists of two metals with **different** linear expansion coefficients ($\beta_1 > \beta_2$) bonded together. When heated, the metal with higher $\beta$ expands more, causing the strip to bend toward the metal with lower $\beta$. The deflection is approximately:

$$\delta \approx \frac{3(\beta_1 - \beta_2) \Delta T \cdot L^2}{2t}$$

where $L$ is the length and $t$ is the thickness.

**Step 3 — Compensation Strategy (2 marks):**
A second bimetallic strip is incorporated into the linkage between the Bourdon tube tip and the pointer gear. This strip is arranged so that its thermal deflection *opposes* the thermal deflection of the Bourdon tube. With proper sizing:

$$\delta_{bourdon, thermal} + \delta_{bimetal, thermal} = 0$$

The net pointer movement is therefore due **only to pressure**, not to temperature.

**Step 4 — Application Range and Limitations (2 marks):**
- Bimetallic compensation is inexpensive, robust, and used widely in industrial pressure gauges, thermometers, and HVAC thermostats.
- Limitation: Compensation is approximate over a narrow range (~±20 °C around design point). For higher precision, electronic compensation (thermistor in the linkage) or full thermal isolation (oven-controlled gauges) is used.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes**
> 1. **Conflating "cold-junction compensation" with "ambient temperature compensation of a strain gauge"** — these are different topics; CJC is *additive*, while bridge compensation is *subtractive* / *canceling*.
> 2. **Omitting the sign convention** in the Seebeck integral: write $E_{AB}(0, T_{CJ})$ with the correct limits, not as a generic symbol.
> 3. **Not specifying the bimetallic deflection equation** — marks are reserved for the formula.
> 4. **Failing to mention that compensation is approximate** and valid only over a limited temperature band. Without this qualifier, the answer is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final-minute KTU prep)**

- **Definition:** Temperature compensation = cancelling or correcting the effect of ambient temperature on a measurement system's output.
- **Three error pathways:** (1) Resistance drift, (2) Dimensional expansion, (3) Elastic modulus change.
- **Master equation for strain gauge:**
  $$\frac{\Delta R}{R_0} = G\epsilon + \left[ \alpha_1 + G(\beta_g - \beta_s) \right] \Delta T$$
- **Self-compensation condition:** $\alpha_1 = -G(\beta_g - \beta_s)$.
- **Wheatstone bridge output (full bridge):** $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} G \epsilon$, **independent of uniform $\Delta T$**.
- **Dummy gauge principle:** Mount an identical gauge on an unstrained dummy block in the *adjacent* arm of the bridge; thermal signals cancel.
- **Cold-junction compensation equation:** $E(0, T_{hot}) = V_{measured} + E(0, T_{CJ})$.
- **Bimetallic strip:** Differential thermal expansion creates counter-deflection used in mechanical gauges.
- **TCR for common alloys:**
  - Constantan: $\approx 0$ /°C → ideal for strain gauges.
  - Nichrome: $0.00017$ /°C.
  - Platinum: $0.00385$ /°C → for RTDs, not gauges.
- **Standard reference temperature:** 20 °C (ISO/BIS).
- **Compensation is approximate** — always valid over a limited bandwidth (e.g., ±20 °C around design point).
- **For KTU 2024 exam:** Always draw a labeled diagram, write the condition, and conclude with the final numerical or symbolic result.
- **Active vs. Passive compensation:** Passive = inherent design (dummy gauge, alloy choice). Active = external circuit or controller (thermistor bridge, software polynomial, oven).
- **Best for laboratory use:** Dummy-gauge half-bridge.
- **Best for OEM sensor production:** Self-compensated gauge alloy.
- **Best for thermocouples:** Software CJC using onboard temperature sensor.
- **Always remember:** A full Wheatstone bridge gives **4× the sensitivity** and **4× the temperature rejection** of a quarter-bridge.
- **Practice deriving** the apparent-strain expression; it is a **favourite 7-mark question** in KTU Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bridge Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bridge Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Bridge Circuit?

A **Bridge Circuit** is a null-deflection type electrical network consisting of four arms, a source of excitation, and a sensitive null-detector (galvanometer for DC / detector for AC). It operates on the principle of *comparative measurement* — the unknown quantity is balanced against known standard quantities until the detector reads zero current. The condition of zero detector current is called the **null-balance condition**, and the relationship among the bridge components at null gives the value of the unknown.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *A bridge circuit is a null-type measuring network in which the unknown electrical quantity (resistance, inductance, capacitance, or frequency) is determined by balancing one or more known standards against it, such that the potential difference across the detector is zero. The accuracy of measurement depends only on the precision of the standards and the sensitivity of the null detector — NOT on the absolute value of the source voltage.*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Mechanical Balance Scale

Imagine a **two-pan balance scale** in a vegetable market. The vendor places the unknown weight of grains on one pan and slides known standard weights (1 kg, 500 g, 200 g…) on the other pan until the pointer comes to the centre (null position). The vendor does **not** need to know the spring constant of the balance or the exact gravitational force — he only needs accurate standard weights.

The bridge circuit is the **electrical twin** of this balance scale:
| Mechanical Balance | Electrical Bridge |
|---|---|
| Two pans (load + standard) | Four arms (unknown + known) |
| Pointer at centre = null | Galvanometer reads zero = null |
| Standard weights | Precision resistors / capacitors / inductors |
| Hand of vendor | Adjustable decade resistance box |

Once null is achieved, the **law of the lever** gives the unknown weight. Similarly, the **bridge balance equation** gives the unknown electrical quantity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why null-balance methods are superior:** All active transducers (ammeters, voltmeters) draw current and disturb the very circuit they are measuring (loading error). The null detector in a bridge ideally draws **zero current** at balance, so it **does not load** the source. This makes bridge methods one of the most accurate techniques in instrumentation — accuracy of **0.1 % to 0.01 %** is routinely achievable.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Bridge Circuits

Bridge circuits are broadly classified based on the **type of excitation** and the **type of unknown quantity** being measured.

> [!NOTE]
> **DC Bridges** use a DC source + DC galvanometer (for pure resistance).
> **AC Bridges** use an AC source + AC detector (headphones, vibration galvanometer, tuned amplifier) for resistance, inductance, capacitance, and frequency.

### A. DC Bridges
1. **Wheatstone Bridge** — measurement of medium resistances (**1 Ω to 1 MΩ**).
2. **Kelvin Double Bridge** — measurement of low resistances (**below 1 Ω**).

### B. AC Bridges
3. **Maxwell Bridge** — measurement of medium-Q inductance (Q between 1 and 10).
4. **Hay Bridge** — measurement of high-Q inductance (Q > 10).
5. **Anderson Bridge** — precise self-inductance measurement (modified Maxwell).
6. **Owen Bridge** — measurement of inductance over a wide range.
7. **De Sauty Bridge** — comparison of two capacitances.
8. **Schering Bridge** — measurement of capacitance, dissipation factor, and insulation testing.
9. **Wien Bridge** — measurement of frequency and in harmonic distortion analysers.
10. **Universal Bridge** — combined measurement of R, L, and C using one instrument.

---

## 1.4 General Two-Port Network Representation

Every bridge can be represented as a **two-port network** with arms $Z_1, Z_2, Z_3, Z_4$, source between nodes A and C, and detector between nodes B and D.

```
       A ───────[ Z1 ]──────── B
       │                       │
     [ Source ]            [ Detector ]
       │                       │
       C ───────[ Z3 ]──────── D
              └── [ Z4 ] ──┘
```

- $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ form the **ratio arms**.
- $Z_3$ and $Z_4$ form the **product (or balancing) arms**, one of which is usually the unknown.

The general balance condition derived via **Thevenin equivalent** is:

$$Z_1 \cdot Z_4 = Z_2 \cdot Z_3$$

This is the **universal bridge balance equation** — the heart of every bridge circuit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step by Step

1. **Excitation** is applied across one diagonal (A–C) of the bridge.
2. Currents flow through the four arms. The potentials at nodes B and D depend on the impedance ratios.
3. The **detector** (galvanometer / headphones / amplifier) connected across the other diagonal (B–D) reads the **potential difference** between B and D.
4. One (or more) arms is varied (decade box, standard capacitor, etc.) until the detector reads **zero (null)**.
5. At null: $V_B = V_D$, hence no current flows through the detector. This eliminates loading error.
6. The **balance equation** relating the four arms is solved for the unknown.

---

## 2.2 General Balance Equation — Proof Sketch

Let the four arms be $Z_1$ (A→B), $Z_2$ (B→C), $Z_3$ (A→D), $Z_4$ (D→C). At null, $V_B = V_D$.

Taking the potential divider, $V_B = V_A - I_1 Z_1$ and $V_D = V_A - I_3 Z_3$. Since detector current is zero, the same current flows through $Z_1, Z_2$ ($I_1$) and $Z_3, Z_4$ ($I_3$).

Equating $V_B = V_D$ and using $I_1 Z_1 = I_3 Z_3$ and $I_1 Z_2 = I_3 Z_4$:

$$\boxed{\,Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For AC bridges**, the balance equation is a **complex phasor equation**. Equating real and imaginary parts independently gives **TWO independent balance conditions** — meaning **TWO adjustable elements** are required in any AC bridge to achieve complete balance.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Bridge Type | Unknown Measured | Balance Equations | Range / Best Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wheatstone** (DC) | $R_x$ | $R_x = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$ | 1 Ω to 1 MΩ, medium resistance |
| 2 | **Kelvin Double** (DC) | $R_x$ (low) | $R_x = R_s \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}$ (with auxiliary ratio $r_1/r_2 = R_1/R_2$) | 1 µΩ to 1 Ω, low resistance |
| 3 | **Maxwell** (AC, for L) | $L_x, R_x$ | $L_x = R_2 R_3 C_1$ ; $R_x = \dfrac{R_2 R_3}{R_1}$ | Medium-Q coils (1 < Q < 10) |
| 4 | **Hay** (AC, for L) | $L_x, R_x$ | $L_x = \dfrac{R_2 R_3 C_1}{1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2}$ ; $R_x = \dfrac{\omega^2 R_1 R_2 R_3 C_1^2}{1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2}$ | High-Q coils (Q > 10) |
| 5 | **Anderson** (AC, for L) | $L_x, R_x$ | $L_x = \dfrac{R_2 R_3 C}{1 + (R_2/R_1)^2} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$ — see derivation | High-precision inductance |
| 6 | **Schering** (AC, for C) | $C_x, R_x$, tan δ | $C_x = C_1 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}$ ; $R_x = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$ ; $D = \omega C_x R_x = \omega R_4 C_1$ | Capacitance, dissipation factor, high-voltage insulation |
| 7 | **De Sauty** (AC, for C) | $C_x$ (pure) | $C_x = C_s \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$ | Comparison of two lossless capacitors |
| 8 | **Wien** (AC, freq) | $f$ | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ (when $R_1 = R_2 = R, C_1 = C_2 = C$: $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$) | Frequency measurement, audio oscillators |
| 9 | **Owen** (AC, for L) | $L_x, R_x$ | $L_x = R_2 R_3 C_1$ ; $R_x = \dfrac{C_1 R_3}{C_2}$ | Wide-range inductance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Q-Factor of a coil:** $Q = \dfrac{\omega L_x}{R_x} = \dfrac{1}{\tan \delta}$ where δ is the loss angle of the coil.

---

## 2.4 Sensitivity of Bridge Circuits

Bridge sensitivity $S_b$ is defined as the **deflection per unit change in the unknown**:

$$S_b = \dfrac{d\theta}{dR_x} = S_g \cdot \dfrac{dI_g}{dR_x}$$

where $S_g$ is the galvanometer sensitivity and $I_g$ is the galvanometer current under slight unbalance. For the Wheatstone bridge:

$$I_g = \dfrac{E \, (R_2 R_3 - R_1 R_4)}{R_g (R_1 + R_2)(R_3 + R_4) + R_1 R_2 (R_3 + R_4) + R_3 R_4 (R_1 + R_2)}$$

**Practical tips for maximum sensitivity:**
- All four arms should be of **equal order of magnitude** (within 1:10 ratio).
- Source voltage $E$ should be high (but limited by heating of the unknown resistor).
- Galvanometer resistance $R_g$ should be chosen to match the **equivalent Thevenin resistance** seen by the detector.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application | Bridge Used |
|---|---|
| Strain gauges in load cells | Wheatstone bridge |
| RTD / Thermistor temperature sensors | Wheatstone bridge |
| Shunt resistors in ammeters, winding resistances of motors | Kelvin double bridge |
| Inductance of chokes, transformer windings | Maxwell / Hay / Anderson |
| Capacitance & dissipation factor of cables, bushings | Schering |
| Frequency of audio signals, oscillators (Hewlett-Packard) | Wien |
| Cable fault location, electrolytic capacitors | Special AC bridges |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are shown in **exhaustive algebraic detail** with every transition explained, in line with the KTU Premium Engine V10 protocol.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Wheatstone Bridge Balance Equation

**Circuit description:** Four resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ form the four arms. A DC source of EMF $E$ (internal resistance negligible) is connected between nodes A and C. A galvanometer of resistance $R_g$ is connected between nodes B and D.

```
   A ────[ R1 ]──── B
   │                │
  ( E )           ( G )
   │                │
   C ────[ R3 ]──── D
          └──[ R4 ]──┘
```

**Step 1 — Identify the two loops.**
Loop 1: $A \to R_1 \to B \to G \to D \to R_4 \to C \to$ back to $A$.
Loop 2: $A \to R_1 \to B \to G \to D \to R_3 \to A$ (this is the upper loop with $R_2$ in the source branch — note the convention: the *opposite* arm pairs are $R_1$ with $R_3$, and $R_2$ with $R_4$ across the galvanometer).

Let the standard textbook layout be:

```
        A
       / \
     R1   R2
     /     \
    B ---[ G ]--- B'   (Detector between B and B')
     \     /
     R3   R4
       \ /
        C
```

So the **two pairs of opposite arms** are $(R_1, R_3)$ and $(R_2, R_4)$. The source is between A and C; the detector is between B and B'.

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) at null.**

At null, the galvanometer current $I_g = 0$. The current through $R_1$ and $R_2$ is the same (call it $I_1$). The current through $R_3$ and $R_4$ is the same (call it $I_2$).

The potential at node B with respect to node A is $-I_1 R_1$. The potential at node B' with respect to node A is $-I_2 R_3$.

At null, $V_B = V_{B'}$:

$$I_1 R_1 = I_2 R_3 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KVL to the loop containing the source.**

The total source voltage $E$ equals the sum of drops in the series path $A \to R_1 \to B \to R_2 \to C$, and also equals the sum in $A \to R_3 \to B' \to R_4 \to C$:

$$E = I_1(R_1 + R_2) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$
$$E = I_2(R_3 + R_4) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 4 — Divide equation (ii) by (iii).**

$$\dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{R_3 + R_4}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 5 — Divide equation (i) by $I_2$.**

$$\dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_1}$$

**Step 6 — Equate the two expressions for $I_1/I_2$.**

$$\dfrac{R_3}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_3 + R_4}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$R_3(R_1 + R_2) = R_1(R_3 + R_4)$$

$$R_1 R_3 + R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_3 + R_1 R_4$$

$$\boxed{\,R_2 R_3 = R_1 R_4\,} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}$$

This is the famous **Wheatstone bridge balance equation**.

If $R_4 = R_x$ (the unknown) and $R_1, R_2, R_3$ are the known ratio and standard arms, then:

$$R_x = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kelvin Double Bridge Balance Equation

**Why a Wheatstone bridge fails for low resistance:**
The lead resistances connecting the unknown low resistor to the bridge arms (typically a few milliohms) become comparable to, or even larger than, the unknown itself — producing gross errors.

**Kelvin's idea:** Add a **second pair of ratio arms** ($r_1, r_2$) — a "double bridge" — that carry the lead-resistance drop and feed it back into the balance so that it cancels out.

**Thevenin equivalent of the bridge network:**

Using Y-Δ transformation on the lower star of $r_1, r_2$ and the galvanometer resistance $R_g$, the balance equation reduces to:

$$\boxed{\,R_x = R_s \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} + \dfrac{r \cdot R_s}{r_1 + r_2 + r} \cdot \left( \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} - \dfrac{r_1}{r_2} \right)\,}$$

where $r$ is the resistance of the **yoke** (the low-resistance link between $R_x$ and $R_s$). If the auxiliary ratio is exactly matched to the main ratio:

$$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{r_1}{r_2}$$

then the **second term vanishes**, leaving:

$$R_x = R_s \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}$$

This is the **Kelvin balance equation**. In practice, the two ratios are kept equal to within 1 part in $10^4$, reducing the error term to a negligible value.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Maxwell Bridge Balance Equation

**Aim:** Measure an unknown inductance $L_x$ in series with its resistance $R_x$.

**Bridge layout (Maxwell-Wien bridge):**

```
        A
       / \
     R1   Lx ── Rx
     /         \
    B ─[G]──── B'
     \         /
     R3      R2
      \       /
       C
        └── C1 ── R1 (parallel) ──┘
```

Arms:
- Arm AB: $Z_1 = R_1$ (pure resistance, non-inductive).
- Arm BC: $Z_2 = R_2$ (non-inductive).
- Arm AD: $Z_3 = R_3$ (non-inductive).
- Arm DC: $Z_4 = R_x + j \omega L_x$ (unknown coil).

The unknown is balanced by placing a **standard capacitor $C_1$ in parallel with $R_1$**. The impedance of this arm is:

$$Z_1 = \dfrac{R_1 \cdot \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_1}}{R_1 + \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_1}} = \dfrac{R_1}{1 + j \omega R_1 C_1}$$

**Balance condition:** $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$ (with $Z_2 = R_2, Z_3 = R_3$):

$$\dfrac{R_1}{1 + j \omega R_1 C_1} \cdot (R_x + j \omega L_x) = R_2 R_3$$

Multiply numerator and denominator of the LHS by the conjugate $(1 - j \omega R_1 C_1)$:

$$\dfrac{R_1 (1 - j \omega R_1 C_1)}{1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2} \cdot (R_x + j \omega L_x) = R_2 R_3$$

Expand the numerator:

$$\dfrac{R_1 [R_x + j \omega L_x - j \omega R_1 R_x C_1 + \omega^2 R_1 L_x C_1]}{1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2} = R_2 R_3$$

**Equate the real part:**

$$\dfrac{R_1 R_x + \omega^2 R_1^2 L_x C_1}{1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2} = R_2 R_3$$

**Equate the imaginary part** (coefficient of $j$):

$$\dfrac{\omega R_1 L_x - \omega R_1^2 R_x C_1}{1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2} = 0$$

From the imaginary part: $\omega R_1 L_x = \omega R_1^2 R_x C_1$, hence:

$$L_x = R_1 R_x C_1 \quad \text{...(A)}$$

**Substitute (A) into the real-part equation:**

$$\dfrac{R_1 R_x + \omega^2 R_1^2 \cdot R_1 R_x C_1 \cdot C_1}{1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2} = R_2 R_3$$

$$\dfrac{R_1 R_x [1 + \omega^2 R_1^2 C_1^2]}{1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2} = R_2 R_3$$

The bracketed terms cancel:

$$R_1 R_x = R_2 R_3$$

$$\boxed{\,R_x = \dfrac{R_2 R_3}{R_1}\,} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{\,L_x = R_2 R_3 C_1\,}$$

**Q-factor of the unknown coil:**

$$Q = \dfrac{\omega L_x}{R_x} = \omega R_1 C_1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** The Maxwell bridge balance is **independent of frequency** ($\omega$ cancels out for $L_x$). This is its biggest advantage. However, the equation for $R_x$ *also* becomes independent of frequency, so the bridge is unsuitable for very high Q (where small $\omega R_1 C_1$ makes the balance sluggish) — for that, the **Hay bridge** is preferred.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Schering Bridge Balance Equation

**Aim:** Measure unknown capacitance $C_x$ in series with its equivalent series resistance $R_x$, and determine the **dissipation factor** $D = \omega R_x C_x = \tan \delta$.

**Bridge layout:**

```
        A
       / \
     C1   Cx ── Rx
     /         \
    B ─[G]──── B'
     \         /
     R3      C2 ── R4
      \      (parallel)
       C
```

- Arm AB: $Z_1 = \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_1}$ (standard lossless capacitor).
- Arm BC: $Z_2 = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{R_4} + j \omega C_2} = \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j \omega R_4 C_2}$ (standard $R_4$ in parallel with $C_2$).
- Arm AD: $Z_3 = R_3$ (non-inductive).
- Arm DC: $Z_4 = R_x + \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_x} = \dfrac{1 + j \omega R_x C_x}{j \omega C_x}$.

**Balance condition:** $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$.

$$\dfrac{1}{j \omega C_1} \cdot \dfrac{1 + j \omega R_x C_x}{j \omega C_x} = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j \omega R_4 C_2}$$

Cross-multiply:

$$(1 + j \omega R_x C_x)(1 + j \omega R_4 C_2) = j \omega C_1 \cdot j \omega C_x R_3 R_4$$

Note: $j \omega C_1 \cdot j \omega C_x = -\omega^2 C_1 C_x$.

**Left side expansion:**

$$1 + j \omega R_4 C_2 + j \omega R_x C_x - \omega^2 R_x R_4 C_x C_2$$

**Right side:**

$$-\omega^2 C_1 C_x R_3 R_4$$

**Equate real parts:**

$$1 - \omega^2 R_x R_4 C_x C_2 = -\omega^2 C_1 C_x R_3 R_4$$

This is messy; instead, use the **direct ratio approach** for clarity (this is what examiners expect).

**Alternative clean derivation** — use the impedance-admittance substitution by noting that arms 1 and 2 contain pure reactances in their **series-equivalent** form:

Using the **product of opposite arms = product of the other pair** form with $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$:

$$\left(\dfrac{1}{j \omega C_1}\right)\left(R_x + \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_x}\right) = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j \omega R_4 C_2}$$

Multiplying both sides by $j \omega C_1$:

$$R_x + \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_x} = \dfrac{j \omega C_1 R_3 R_4}{1 + j \omega R_4 C_2}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator of RHS by the conjugate $(1 - j \omega R_4 C_2)$:

$$RHS = \dfrac{j \omega C_1 R_3 R_4 (1 - j \omega R_4 C_2)}{1 + (\omega R_4 C_2)^2} = \dfrac{j \omega C_1 R_3 R_4 + \omega^2 C_1 R_3 R_4^2 C_2}{1 + (\omega R_4 C_2)^2}$$

**Equate real parts** of $RHS = LHS$:

$$R_x = \dfrac{\omega^2 C_1 R_3 R_4^2 C_2}{1 + (\omega R_4 C_2)^2}$$

For the standard Schering bridge, $C_2$ is chosen such that $\omega R_4 C_2 \ll 1$, so the denominator $\approx 1$, and:

$$\boxed{\,R_x \approx \omega^2 C_1 C_2 R_3 R_4^2\,}$$

A more useful and commonly tabulated form (obtained by using $Z_2$ as a series $R_4$–$C_2$ combination and rearranging) is:

$$\boxed{\,C_x = C_1 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}\,} \quad ; \quad \boxed{\,R_x = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}\,} \quad ; \quad \boxed{\,D = \omega C_x R_x = \omega R_4 C_1\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The dissipation factor $D = \omega R_4 C_1$ depends ONLY on standard values**, not on the unknown. This is why the Schering bridge is so widely used in **insulation testing** of cables, transformer bushings, and capacitor dielectrics.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of the Wien Bridge Frequency Equation

**Aim:** Measure the frequency of an unknown AC signal.

**Bridge layout:**

```
        A
       / \
     R1   R2
     |     |
     C1    C2
     \     /
      B ─[G]─ B'
     \     /
     R3   R4
      \  /
       C
```

- Arm AB: $Z_1 = R_1 + \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_1} = R_1 - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_1}$
- Arm BC: $Z_2 = R_2 + \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_2} = R_2 - \dfrac{j}{\omega C_2}$
- Arm AD: $Z_3 = R_3$ (pure resistance)
- Arm DC: $Z_4 = R_4$ (pure resistance)

**Balance condition:** $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$, so $Z_1 / Z_2 = R_3 / R_4$ (real). This means the **phase angles** of $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ must be equal (and the magnitudes in the given ratio).

**Phase angle of $Z_1$:**

$$\phi_1 = \arctan\left(\dfrac{-1/(\omega C_1)}{R_1}\right) = -\arctan\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega R_1 C_1}\right)$$

**Phase angle of $Z_2$:**

$$\phi_2 = -\arctan\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega R_2 C_2}\right)$$

Equating $\phi_1 = \phi_2$:

$$\arctan\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega R_1 C_1}\right) = \arctan\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega R_2 C_2}\right)$$

$$\dfrac{1}{\omega R_1 C_1} = \dfrac{1}{\omega R_2 C_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 C_1 = R_2 C_2$$

**Magnitude condition** ($|Z_1| / |Z_2| = R_3 / R_4$):

$$\dfrac{\sqrt{R_1^2 + 1/(\omega C_1)^2}}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + 1/(\omega C_2)^2}} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$$

Using $R_1 C_1 = R_2 C_2$:

$$\dfrac{1}{(\omega C_1)^2} = R_1^2 \cdot \dfrac{C_1^2}{C_2^2} = \left(\dfrac{R_1 C_1}{C_2}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{R_2 C_2}{C_2}\right)^2 = R_2^2$$

Therefore $R_1^2 + R_2^2 = R_1^2 (1 + C_1^2/C_2^2)$, and after simplification:

$$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4} - \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$$

This gives the **general Wien bridge balance**. For the common case $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$:

$$\dfrac{R}{R} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4} - 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_3 = 2 R_4$$

The **frequency** is obtained from the phase condition with $R_1 = R_2, C_1 = C_2$:

$$\omega = \dfrac{1}{RC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}\,}$$

---

## 3.6 Anderson Bridge — Modification of Maxwell Bridge

The Anderson bridge is essentially a **Maxwell bridge with an additional resistor $r$ and a fixed capacitor $C$** in a fifth branch. It eliminates the cumulative errors that plague the Maxwell bridge at low frequencies and provides better convergence.

The balance equations (after similar algebraic work) yield:

$$L_x = \dfrac{R \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_4} \quad ; \quad R_x = \dfrac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_4}$$

The Anderson bridge is more accurate than the Maxwell bridge for measuring iron-core inductors and is the standard method for inductors in the **millihenry to henry** range.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Viva / Theory Favourite)

| Property | Wheatstone | Kelvin Double | Maxwell | Hay | Schering | Wien |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Excitation | DC | DC | AC | AC | AC | AC |
| Quantity Measured | R | R (low) | L, R | L, R | C, R, D | f |
| Range | 1 Ω – 1 MΩ | < 1 Ω | Medium Q | High Q | All C | Audio |
| Frequency dependent? | No | No | No | **Yes** | Yes | N/A (output) |
| Source sensitivity to error | Low | Very Low | Low | High | Low | Medium |
| Detector | Galvanometer | Galvanometer | Vibration galv. | Vibration galv. | Vibration galv. | Headphones / amp |
| Adjustable elements | 1 (R₃) | 2 (R₁, R₂) | 2 (R₁, R₃) | 2 (R₁, R₃) | 2 (R₃, C₂) | 2 (C₁, R₃) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

flowchart TD

```

```

## 4.5 Bridge Balance State Machine (Null-Detection Process)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    ExciteBridge --> MeasureDetector
    MeasureDetector --> IsNull
    IsNull --> AdjustArms: NOT NULL
    AdjustArms --> MeasureDetector
    IsNull --> LockBalance: NULL ACHIEVED
    LockBalance --> ComputeUnknown
    ComputeUnknown --> DisplayValue
    DisplayValue --> [*]
```

## 4.6 Comparison of Bridge Types — Functional Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ{WHAT QUANTITY TO MEASURE}
    nodeQ --> nodeR{IS IT RESISTANCE}

    nodeRmag --> nodeRmed{1 OHM TO 1 MOHM}
    nodeRlow --> nodeKEL[KELVIN DOUBLE BRIDGE]
    nodeRmed --> nodeWHT[WHEATSTONE BRIDGE]
    nodeL --> nodeQval{Q FACTOR VALUE}
    nodeQval --> nodeQmed{MEDIUM Q 1 TO 10}
    nodeQval --> nodeQhigh{HIGH Q ABOVE 10}
    nodeQmed --> nodeMAX[MAXWELL BRIDGE]
    nodeQhigh --> nodeHAY[HAY BRIDGE]
    nodeC --> nodeCtest{IS IT FOR INSULATION}
    nodeCtest --> nodeCins{YES HIGH VOLTAGE}
    nodeCtest --> nodeCcmp{NO COMPARISON}

    nodeF --> nodeWIE[WIEN BRIDGE]
```
