# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**"State and explain the balance condition of a Wheatstone bridge. Why is the null-detection method preferred over direct deflection methods?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Wheatstone bridge is a four-arm DC network ($R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$) with a source between opposite nodes and a galvanometer between the other two opposite nodes. The balance (null) condition is derived by equating the potentials at the galvanometer terminals, giving:

$$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3} \quad \text{or} \quad R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$$

If $R_4$ is the unknown, then $R_x = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$.

Null detection is preferred because (i) the galvanometer draws **zero current** at balance, eliminating loading error; (ii) accuracy depends only on the precision of the standards, not on the absolute source voltage or galvanometer calibration; (iii) very high accuracy (0.1 % or better) is achievable.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between a Wheatstone bridge and a Kelvin double bridge. When is the latter essential?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Wheatstone | Kelvin Double |
|---|---|---|
| Number of ratio arms | 1 pair ($R_1, R_2$) | 2 pairs (main $R_1, R_2$ + auxiliary $r_1, r_2$) |
| Lead resistance correction | Not possible | Built in (yoke resistance $r$ cancelled by matching $r_1/r_2 = R_1/R_2$) |
| Resistance range | 1 Ω to 1 MΩ | 1 µΩ to 1 Ω |

The Kelvin double bridge is **essential** for low-resistance measurements (below 1 Ω) such as shunt resistors, armature windings, and cable conductors, where the lead and contact resistances become comparable to the unknown and would introduce unacceptable error in a Wheatstone bridge.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Bridge Theory + Maxwell Bridge
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the general balance condition of an AC bridge. Explain why **two independent adjustments** are necessary for AC bridges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, derive the balance equations of the **Maxwell bridge** used for measuring an unknown inductance. Mention its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — General AC Bridge Balance:**

A four-arm bridge has arms $Z_1, Z_2, Z_3, Z_4$ (complex). The bridge balance condition (derived by equating potentials at the detector terminals) is:

$$Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$$

In polar form, with $Z_k = \vert Z_k \vert \angle \theta_k$:

$$\vert Z_1 \vert \cdot \vert Z_4 \vert = \vert Z_2 \vert \cdot \vert Z_3 \vert \quad \text{(magnitude condition)}$$
$$\theta_1 + \theta_4 = \theta_2 + \theta_3 \quad \text{(phase condition)}$$

This is a single complex equation, equivalent to **two real equations**. Hence **two adjustable elements** (one for magnitude, one for phase) must be provided. In a DC bridge, the phase condition is automatically satisfied, so only **one adjustment** is required.

**Model Solution (b) — Maxwell Bridge:**

[Insert circuit diagram and the full derivation from Section 3.3 above. Key results to be boxed:]
$$L_x = R_2 R_3 C_1 \quad ; \quad R_x = \dfrac{R_2 R_3}{R_1} \quad ; \quad Q = \omega R_1 C_1$$

**Limitations:**
1. The balance equations are **independent of frequency** — this is an advantage, but it means the bridge is **insensitive to small frequency changes**, which makes the **Hay bridge** more suitable for high-Q coils.
2. The Maxwell bridge is suitable only for **medium-Q coils (Q = 1 to 10)**. For high-Q coils, the resistance $R_1$ in parallel with $C_1$ becomes very large, making balance sluggish.
3. It cannot measure low-Q coils (Q < 1) accurately because $R_1$ becomes too small to adjust precisely.
4. The standard capacitor $C_1$ must be of high quality (low loss); any loss in $C_1$ introduces error.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Schering + Wien Bridges
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **Schering bridge** used for measuring capacitance and dissipation factor. Derive its balance equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the balance equation of a **Wien bridge** and show that for $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$, the bridge balances at frequency $f = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi RC}$. Mention two practical applications of the Wien bridge. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — Schering Bridge:**

[Insert circuit and the full derivation from Section 3.4. Key results:]
$$C_x = C_1 \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3} \quad ; \quad R_x = R_3 \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{C_2} \quad ; \quad D = \omega R_4 C_1$$

**Note for examiner:** Highlight that $D$ depends only on the standard components $R_4$ and $C_1$, which is the elegance of the Schering bridge for **insulation testing** under high voltage (the unknown is on the high-voltage side, the standards on the low-voltage side).

**Model Solution (b) — Wien Bridge:**

[Insert the derivation from Section 3.5. Final result:]
$$\boxed{\,f = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}\,} \quad \text{when } R_1 = R_2 = R, C_1 = C_2 = C$$

**Two practical applications:**
1. **Frequency measurement** of audio and sub-audio signals.
2. **Harmonic distortion analyser** — the bridge is used to measure the harmonic content of an audio signal by nulling the fundamental and measuring the residual.
3. **Wien-bridge oscillator** — when the bridge is intentionally kept slightly off balance, it provides a sinusoidal feedback oscillator (Hewlett–Packard 200-series audio oscillator used this principle).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Drawing the bridge upside down** — the source must be on the diagonal *opposite* to the detector. The "unknown" is usually on the same diagonal as the standard, NOT across the source.
> 2. **Forgetting the imaginary part** of the AC balance equation. Marks are awarded for *both* real and imaginary parts being equated.
> 3. **Wrong pair identification** — in the formula $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$, arms 1 and 4 are **opposite** (not adjacent). Students frequently mis-apply the equation.
> 4. **Sign errors** when rationalising complex fractions (multiply by conjugate $(1 - j\omega R C)$). Each step must be shown.
> 5. **In Maxwell bridge**, writing $L_x = R_1 R_3 C_1$ instead of $L_x = R_2 R_3 C_1$ — this is a one-mark deduction. Trace the arm labels carefully.
> 6. **For Wien bridge**, not stating the explicit condition $R_1 = R_2, C_1 = C_2$ under which the simplified frequency formula $f = 1/(2 \pi RC)$ is valid.
> 7. **No units** in the final answer — always write the quantity with its unit (e.g., $L_x = 12.5 \text{ mH}$, $C_x = 0.225 \mu F$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Bridge circuits are null-type comparators** — accuracy depends on standards, NOT on source or detector.
- [ ] **Universal balance equation:** $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$ — arms 1 and 4 are opposite, arms 2 and 3 are opposite.
- [ ] **DC bridges** need **one** adjustment; **AC bridges** need **two** independent adjustments (real + imaginary).
- [ ] **Wheatstone bridge** measures resistance in the range 1 Ω to 1 MΩ: $R_x = R_3 (R_2/R_1)$.
- [ ] **Kelvin double bridge** measures low resistance (< 1 Ω): $R_x = R_s (R_1/R_2)$, with auxiliary ratio matched to cancel yoke resistance.
- [ ] **Maxwell bridge** measures medium-Q inductance: $L_x = R_2 R_3 C_1$, $R_x = R_2 R_3 / R_1$. Balance is **independent of frequency**.
- [ ] **Hay bridge** measures high-Q inductance: balance equations contain $\omega$ — it is frequency-dependent.
- [ ] **Schering bridge** measures capacitance and dissipation factor: $C_x = C_1 (R_4/R_3)$, $D = \omega R_4 C_1$. Widely used in **insulation testing**.
- [ ] **Wien bridge** measures frequency: $f = 1/(2 \pi R C)$ for equal arms. Used in **Wien-bridge oscillators** and **harmonic analysers**.
- [ ] **De Sauty bridge** is for comparison of two **lossless** capacitors only.
- [ ] **Anderson bridge** is the most accurate for self-inductance; modified Maxwell with extra resistor and capacitor.
- [ ] **Q-factor of a coil** $Q = \omega L_x / R_x = 1/\tan \delta$; choose Maxwell for medium Q, Hay for high Q.
- [ ] **Bridge sensitivity** is maximum when all four arms are of **equal order of magnitude** (ratio 1:1 to 1:10).
- [ ] **Detector choices:** Galvanometer (DC), vibration galvanometer (50 Hz), headphones (audio), tuned amplifier (any frequency).
- [ ] **Two key errors** in bridge measurement: (i) lead resistance (overcome by Kelvin), (ii) residual impedances in standard components.
- [ ] **Universal bridges** (commercially available) combine Wheatstone, Maxwell, Hay, and Schering in one instrument with selector switches.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory aid for the exam:**
> *"Maxwell and Owen for low Q, Hay for high Q, Schering for C and D, Wien for f, Kelvin for low R, Wheatstone for medium R."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Orientation of Strain Gauges for Force and Torque Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Orientation of Strain Gauges for Force and Torque Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Strain Gauge Orientation** is the deliberate angular positioning of one or more bonded metallic or semiconductor resistive foil sensors on the surface of a structural member (such as a load cell column, cantilever beam, or rotating shaft) such that the measured electrical resistance change (ΔR) faithfully maps the principal mechanical strain component being investigated — namely **axial strain** for direct force (tension/compression) sensing and **shear strain (γₓᵧ) at 45°** for torque (torsional moment) sensing.

The orientation is mathematically governed by the **strain transformation equations**, which relate the strain at any arbitrary angle θ on a 2-D surface to the principal strains εₓ, εᵧ and the engineering shear strain γₓᵧ.

$$ \varepsilon_{\theta} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\sin 2\theta $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OEMET725 Module 2):**
> The student must be able to **(i)** identify the correct angular placement of strain gauges on a shaft, **(ii)** select a suitable Wheatstone bridge topology (¼, ½, or full bridge) to maximize sensitivity, and **(iii)** mathematically justify why gauges are mounted at **±45°** to the shaft axis for pure torque measurement.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long, thin rubber rod lying flat on a table.

*   If you **pull the rod along its length** (a *force* along the axis), the rubber stretches lengthwise. To "feel" that stretch, you would glue a tiny measuring tape **parallel** to the rod's axis. This is the **Force Gauge** — it sits at **0°** to the loading axis.
*   Now imagine twisting the same rod like wringing a wet towel (a *torque*). The fibers on the surface do not stretch lengthwise or widthwise alone — they form a **diagonal pattern of stretching and squeezing**, exactly like the spirals on a barber's pole. The maximum stretching direction is at **+45°** to the rod's axis, and the maximum squeezing is at **−45°**. To capture this pure twist, you glue two gauges along the **±45°** spiral lines. These are the **Torque Gauges**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check — The Barber's Pole Rule:** Any circular shaft under pure torsion will exhibit principal tensile strain along one helix at **+45°** and principal compressive strain along the perpendicular helix at **−45°**. This is the geometric "fingerprint" of torque and the foundation of all torque-wheatstone-bridge design.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

*   **Gauge Factor (GF)** — typically **2.0** for constantan foil, up to **150** for semiconductor gauges. Dimensionless.
*   **Young's Modulus (E)** for mild steel ≈ **200 GPa**; for aluminium alloy ≈ **70 GPa**.
*   **Poisson's Ratio (ν)** for most engineering metals ≈ **0.3** (range 0.28 – 0.33).
*   **Shear Modulus (G)** = E / [2(1 + ν)] — for steel G ≈ **77 GPa**.
*   **Standard gauge resistance** — **120 Ω**, **350 Ω**, and **1000 Ω** are the most common commercial values.
*   **Bridge excitation voltage (Vₑₓ)** — typically **5 V DC** or **10 V DC** in industrial transducers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mechanics of Strain at a Surface Point

When a structural member is loaded, the surface experiences a **2-D strain state** (plane stress assumption) characterized by:

*   **εₓ** — normal strain along the shaft longitudinal axis (x-direction).
*   **εᵧ** — normal strain along the transverse (circumferential) direction.
*   **γₓᵧ** — engineering shear strain in the x–y plane.

The principal strains and their orientations are obtained by differentiating the strain transformation equation and setting dεθ/dθ = 0:

$$ \tan 2\theta_p = \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y} $$

$$ \varepsilon_{1,2} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2} $$

### 2.2 Orientation Logic — Why Specific Angles?

**Step 1 — Identify the stress state at the surface.**
For a shaft of radius *r* under axial force F, the only non-zero stress is σₓ = F/A, and the strains are:
*   εₓ = F/(AE)
*   εᵧ = −ν·F/(AE) (Poisson contraction)

**Step 2 — Match gauge axis to the strain axis.**
The gauge should be aligned with the direction in which we want to measure strain. For **pure force**, the largest strain is εₓ, so the gauge is placed at **θ = 0°** to the shaft axis.

**Step 3 — For torque, recognize that pure torsion produces pure shear.**
Under torque T, at the surface:
*   σₓ = 0, σᵧ = 0 (plane stress, no normal stresses on the surface)
*   Only τₓᵧ = T·r/J exists (J = polar moment of inertia = πd⁴/32 for solid shaft)

The strains become:
*   εₓ = 0, εᵧ = 0
*   γₓᵧ = τₓᵧ/G = T·r/(GJ)

Substituting these into the strain transformation formula with θ = 45°:

$$ \varepsilon_{45°} = 0 + 0 + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\sin(90°) = \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2} = \frac{+\varepsilon_1}{1} $$

$$ \varepsilon_{-45°} = 0 + 0 + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\sin(-90°) = -\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2} = \varepsilon_2 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Result:** Under pure torque, the **±45°** orientations are the **principal axes** of strain. A gauge at +45° sees the maximum tensile principal strain (+ε₁), and a gauge at −45° sees the maximum compressive principal strain (−ε₁). This perfect anti-symmetry is the basis for bridge temperature compensation.

### 2.3 Wheatstone Bridge — Topology Selection

The output voltage of a Wheatstone bridge with excitation Vₑₓ is:

$$ V_{out} = V_{ex}\left[\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}\right] $$

For small resistance changes (ΔR ≪ R), the bridge output simplifies. The four standard topologies used in OEMET725 are summarized in the formula sheet below.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Measurement Type | Gauge Placement | Bridge Topology | Bridge Output Sensitivity (Vₒᵤₜ / Vₑₓ) | Temperature Compensation | Active Strain Per Gauge |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Axial Force** (no bending) | Single gauge along axis (0°) | Quarter bridge (¼) | (GF·ε) / [4·(1+ν)] approx | No (needs dummy gauge) | εₓ |
| 2 | **Axial Force** (bending present) | Gauge on tension side & compression side, both at 0° | Half bridge (½) — adjacent arms | (GF·εₓ) / [2·(1+ν)] | Yes (bending cancels) | εₓ (bending rejected) |
| 3 | **Axial Force** (full compensation) | 4 gauges — 2 axial (0°), 2 transverse (90°) on opposite sides | Full bridge | GF·εₓ·(1+ν) | Yes (full) | εₓ·(1+ν) |
| 4 | **Pure Torque** | 2 gauges at +45°, 2 at −45° on shaft | Full bridge | GF·γₓᵧ | Yes (full) | γₓᵧ |
| 5 | **Bending Moment** | 2 gauges at 0° (top & bottom) | Half bridge (½) | (GF·ε) / 2 | Yes (axial cancels) | ε |
| 6 | **Universal Rosette** (3 gauges at 0°, 45°, 90°) | Rectangular rosette | External math | Calculated via transformation | N/A | All three strain components |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Notation:** The symbol `\\vert` is used for absolute value to prevent breaking markdown table syntax. For example, `\\vert \\Delta R / R \\vert` denotes the magnitude of fractional resistance change.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

*   **Load Cells in Weighbridges:** Four gauges in a full-bridge axial configuration convert truck weight (up to **100 tonnes**) into a 0–10 V signal with **0.05%** accuracy.
*   **Engine Crankshaft Torque Telemetry:** Slip-ring or wireless torque sensors on automotive crankshafts use **±45°** gauges to feed the ECU for real-time fuel-mapping.
*   **Robotic Wrist Force-Torque Sensors:** Six-axis F/T sensors use **3 rosettes** to decouple Fx, Fy, Fz, Mx, My, Mz simultaneously.
*   **Wind Turbine Blade Monitoring:** Full-bridge torque gauges on the low-speed shaft provide condition-monitoring data for predictive maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Strain Transformation Equation

Starting from a 2-D infinitesimal element, the displacement field is:

$$ u = \varepsilon_x \cdot x + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2} \cdot y $$

$$ v = \varepsilon_y \cdot y + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2} \cdot x $$

The length of a line element originally along the θ direction, with original length *l₀ = 1*, after deformation has new length *l* given by the Cauchy strain tensor transformation. For a unit vector in the θ direction: *n* = (cos θ, sin θ).

The normal strain along the θ direction is:

$$ \varepsilon_{\theta} = \varepsilon_x \cos^2\theta + \varepsilon_y \sin^2\theta + \gamma_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta $$

Using the trigonometric identities:

$$ \cos^2\theta = \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2} $$

$$ \sin^2\theta = \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2} $$

$$ \sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2} $$

Substitute into the strain expression:

$$ \varepsilon_{\theta} = \varepsilon_x \cdot \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2} + \varepsilon_y \cdot \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2} + \gamma_{xy}\cdot\frac{\sin 2\theta}{2} $$

Group the constant terms and the 2θ terms:

$$ \varepsilon_{\theta} = \frac{\varepsilon_x+\varepsilon_y}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_x-\varepsilon_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\sin 2\theta $$

This is the **canonical strain transformation equation** used universally in OEMET725 problem-solving.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Bridge Output for Torque Measurement (Full Bridge)

Consider a full-bridge circuit with four active strain gauges mounted on a torque shaft:

*   R₁ at +45° → active strain = +γₓᵧ/2
*   R₂ at −45° → active strain = −γₓᵧ/2
*   R₃ at −45° → active strain = −γₓᵧ/2
*   R₄ at +45° → active strain = +γₓᵧ/2

Each gauge resistance changes as: R = R₀(1 + GF·ε).

$$ R_1 = R_0\left(1 + \frac{GF\cdot\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right) $$

$$ R_2 = R_0\left(1 - \frac{GF\cdot\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right) $$

$$ R_3 = R_0\left(1 - \frac{GF\cdot\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right) $$

$$ R_4 = R_0\left(1 + \frac{GF\cdot\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right) $$

Let **δ = GF·γₓᵧ/2** (a small dimensionless quantity).

The bridge output (using the standard formula and neglecting second-order δ² terms):

$$ V_{out} = V_{ex}\left[\frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}\right] $$

Compute the first ratio:

$$ \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} = \frac{R_0(1+\delta)}{R_0(1+\delta) + R_0(1-\delta)} = \frac{1+\delta}{2} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\delta}{2} $$

Compute the second ratio:

$$ \frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4} = \frac{R_0(1-\delta)}{R_0(1-\delta) + R_0(1+\delta)} = \frac{1-\delta}{2} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{\delta}{2} $$

Subtract:

$$ \frac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3+R_4} = \left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\delta}{2}\right) - \left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{\delta}{2}\right) = \delta $$

Therefore:

$$ V_{out} = V_{ex}\cdot\delta = V_{ex}\cdot\frac{GF\cdot\gamma_{xy}}{2} $$

Substituting γₓᵧ = T·r/(GJ):

$$ V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}\cdot GF \cdot T \cdot r}{2\cdot G\cdot J} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result for Torque Sensors:** The full-bridge output for torque is **linear in T**, has **4× the sensitivity** of a single quarter-bridge gauge, and provides **complete temperature compensation** because all four gauges experience the same thermal expansion (which cancels in the differential measurement).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bridge Topologies for Force

| Loading Case | Quarter Bridge (1 active) | Half Bridge (2 active) | Full Bridge (4 active) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Axial force only | Output: Vₑₓ·GF·εₓ/[4(1+ν)] | Output: Vₑₓ·GF·εₓ/[2(1+ν)] | Output: Vₑₓ·GF·εₓ·(1+ν) |
| Bending only | Sensitive to bending — undesirable | Cancels bending (if 2 gauges on opposite sides) | Cancels bending |
| Temperature change | Uncompensated (needs dummy gauge) | Auto-compensated | Auto-compensated |
| Sensitivity (relative) | **1×** (baseline) | **~2×** (with Poisson boost) | **~4×** (with Poisson + active) |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A solid circular steel shaft of diameter **d = 50 mm** is subjected to a torque of **T = 2 kN·m**. Four strain gauges (GF = 2.1, R₀ = 350 Ω) are bonded at ±45° and connected in a full-bridge configuration with Vₑₓ = 10 V. Calculate the bridge output voltage. Use G = 80 GPa for steel.

**Step 1 — Polar moment of inertia.**

$$ J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^4}{32} = 6.136 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4 $$

**Step 2 — Shear stress at the surface (r = 25 mm).**

$$ \tau = \frac{T\cdot r}{J} = \frac{2000 \times 0.025}{6.136 \times 10^{-7}} = 8.149 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 81.49 \text{ MPa} $$

**Step 3 — Shear strain γₓᵧ.**

$$ \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{81.49 \times 10^{6}}{80 \times 10^{9}} = 1.0186 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} $$

**Step 4 — Bridge output (full bridge, ±45° gauges).**

$$ V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}\cdot GF \cdot \gamma_{xy}}{2} = \frac{10 \times 2.1 \times 1.0186 \times 10^{-3}}{2} $$

$$ V_{out} = 1.0695 \times 10^{-2} \text{ V} = 10.695 \text{ mV} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** A **10.7 mV** signal from a **10 V** excitation represents a fractional output of **~0.107%**. This is the typical full-scale range for industrial torque transducers before amplification by an instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620, INA128).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Strain Gauge Signal Conditioning Chain

The following Mermaid diagram maps the signal flow from the physical strain on the shaft all the way to the digital display, highlighting where the orientation choice of the gauge interacts with the signal chain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Load Force or Torque applied to shaft] --> B[Surface Strain Field epsilon theta]
    C[Strain Gauge Orientation choice: 0 deg for force, plus minus 45 deg for torque] --> B
    B --> D[Resistance Change delta R in Gauge R0]
    D --> E[Wheatstone Bridge Topology selected]
    E -->|Quarter| F1[Output V out small]
    E -->|Half| F2[Output V out moderate]
    E -->|Full| F3[Output V out maximum]
    F1 --> G[Instrumentation Amplifier gain 100 to 1000]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Low Pass Filter cutoff 10 kHz]
    H --> I[ADC 16 bit resolution]
    I --> J[Microcontroller calibration and linearization]
    J --> K[Digital Display or 4-20 mA Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The orange highlight conceptually shows that the *orientation choice* (left input C) directly determines which component of the strain field (B) is converted to a resistance change (D), and hence which bridge topology (E) yields maximum sensitivity. An incorrect orientation can render even the most expensive amplifier chain useless.

---

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Bridge Topology Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: What are you measuring?] --> Q1{Force or Torque?}
    Q1 -->|Force| R1{Bending present?}
    Q1 -->|Torque| R2{Temperature stability needed?}
    R1 -->|No| S1[Single Gauge at 0 deg, Quarter Bridge with external dummy]
    R1 -->|Yes| S2[Two Gauges at 0 deg opposite sides, Half Bridge]
    R2 -->|Basic| S3[Two Gauges at plus minus 45 deg, Half Bridge]
    R2 -->|High accuracy| S4[Four Gauges at plus minus 45 deg, Full Bridge]
    S1 --> Z[End]
    S2 --> Z
    S3 --> Z
    S4 --> Z
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Heuristic for OEMET725:** For a **lab-grade load cell**, always prefer the **full-bridge configuration** with gauges oriented to maximize the active strain and cancel the parasitic strain. The full-bridge gives you **temperature compensation, bending rejection, and 4× sensitivity** in a single package.

---

### 4.3 Physical Layout Schematic (Block-Level)

For a **torque-measuring shaft**, the following block-level layout represents the standard industrial sensor pattern:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHAFT[Rotating Shaft cross section]
        direction TB
        A1[Gauge 1 at plus 45 degrees active tensile]
        A2[Gauge 2 at minus 45 degrees active compressive]
        A3[Gauge 3 at minus 45 degrees active compressive]
        A4[Gauge 4 at plus 45 degrees active tensile]
    end
    A1 --- A2
    A3 --- A4
    A1 --> BRIDGE[Wheatstone Full Bridge Circuit]
    A2 --> BRIDGE
    A3 --> BRIDGE
    A4 --> BRIDGE
    BRIDGE --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP --> OUT[Output Signal 0 to 10 V or 4 to 20 mA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Layout Logic:** Notice that **gauges 1 and 4** are aligned along the +45° spiral (parallel to each other), and **gauges 2 and 3** are along the −45° spiral. In the bridge, gauges 1 & 4 are placed in opposite arms (R₁ and R₄), and gauges 2 & 3 in the other two arms (R₂ and R₃). This way, all four gauges **add constructively** to the differential output.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why are strain gauges mounted at ±45° to the axis of a shaft for the measurement of torque?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Under pure torque, a circular shaft experiences **pure shear stress** (τₓᵧ) at its surface, with **zero normal stresses** (σₓ = 0, σᵧ = 0). According to **Mohr's circle for strain**, the principal axes for such a pure shear state are oriented at **±45°** to the shaft axis. (1 mark)

The maximum principal tensile strain (+ε₁) and maximum principal compressive strain (−ε₁) occur along these ±45° directions, with magnitude ε₁ = γₓᵧ/2. (1 mark)

By mounting strain gauges at **+45°** and **−45°**, the gauges align with the principal strain directions, capturing the **maximum possible signal**. Additionally, the +45° and −45° gauges exhibit equal-magnitude, opposite-sign strains, which enables **perfect temperature and bending compensation** when wired into adjacent arms of a Wheatstone bridge. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Gauge Factor of a strain gauge. What is its typical value for a metallic foil gauge?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Gauge Factor (GF)** of a strain gauge is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in electrical resistance (ΔR/R) to the mechanical strain (ε = ΔL/L) experienced by the gauge:

$$ GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} $$

(2 marks for the formula and definition)

For a **metallic foil gauge** (e.g., constantan alloy), the typical value of Gauge Factor is **2.0 ± 0.1** (dimensionless). For **semiconductor gauges** (silicon), GF can be as high as **100 to 150**. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the strain transformation equation relating the strain εθ along an arbitrary direction θ to the strain components εₓ, εᵧ, and γₓᵧ. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel shaft of diameter 40 mm is subjected to an axial tensile force of 50 kN. Two strain gauges are bonded on the shaft — one along the axis (0°) and one transverse (90°). The gauges are connected in adjacent arms of a half Wheatstone bridge with Vₑₓ = 8 V. Calculate the bridge output voltage. Assume GF = 2.0, E = 200 GPa, ν = 0.3. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Strain Transformation Derivation (7 marks):**

Step 1 (1 mark): Consider a 2-D element with normal strains εₓ, εᵧ and shear strain γₓᵧ. The displacement field components are:

$$ u = \varepsilon_x x + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2} y, \quad v = \varepsilon_y y + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2} x $$

Step 2 (2 marks): A line segment of unit original length oriented at angle θ has direction cosines (cos θ, sin θ). After deformation, the new squared length is found via the strain tensor:

$$ l^2 = l_0^2 + 2\varepsilon_\theta \cdot l_0^2 $$

Carrying out the algebra, we get:

$$ \varepsilon_\theta = \varepsilon_x \cos^2\theta + \varepsilon_y \sin^2\theta + \gamma_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta $$

Step 3 (2 marks): Apply the double-angle identities:

$$ \cos^2\theta = \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2}, \quad \sin^2\theta = \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}, \quad \sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2} $$

Step 4 (2 marks): Substitute and simplify to obtain the final canonical form:

$$ \varepsilon_{\theta} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\sin 2\theta $$

**Part (b) — Bridge Output for Axial Force (7 marks):**

Step 1 (1 mark): Cross-sectional area:

$$ A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.040)^2}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 $$

Step 2 (1 mark): Axial stress:

$$ \sigma_x = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{50{,}000}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 39.79 \text{ MPa} $$

Step 3 (1 mark): Axial strain:

$$ \varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E} = \frac{39.79 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 1.989 \times 10^{-4} $$

Step 4 (1 mark): Transverse (Poisson) strain:

$$ \varepsilon_y = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_x = -0.3 \times 1.989 \times 10^{-4} = -5.968 \times 10^{-5} $$

Step 5 (2 marks): Half-bridge output (adjacent arms, both gauges active). The effective strain seen by the bridge when one gauge is at +ε and the other at −νε is:

$$ \varepsilon_{eff} = \varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_x(1+\nu) $$

$$ V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon_{eff}}{4} = \frac{8 \times 2.0 \times 1.989 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.3}{4} $$

Step 6 (1 mark): Final numerical result:

$$ V_{out} = 1.034 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 1.034 \text{ mV} $$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain how four strain gauges mounted on a shaft can be used to measure torque using a full Wheatstone bridge. Mention the orientation and justify why this configuration provides temperature compensation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A torque of 500 N·m is applied to a hollow steel shaft of outer diameter 60 mm and inner diameter 40 mm. Four strain gauges (GF = 2.1, R = 350 Ω) are mounted at ±45° in a full-bridge configuration with Vₑₓ = 10 V. Determine: (i) the maximum shear strain at the surface, and (ii) the bridge output voltage. Take G = 80 GPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Torque Measurement with Full Bridge (7 marks):**

Step 1 (2 marks): **Sketch description (refer to Section 4.3 diagram):** Four strain gauges are bonded on the surface of the shaft. Two gauges (G1 and G4) are aligned along the +45° helical direction, and the other two (G2 and G3) are aligned along the −45° helical direction.

Step 2 (2 marks): **Wheatstone bridge wiring:** G1 and G4 (both at +45°) are placed in **opposite arms** of the bridge (R₁ and R₄). G2 and G3 (both at −45°) are placed in the other two arms (R₂ and R₃). Under torque, the +45° gauges experience tensile strain (+γₓᵧ/2), and the −45° gauges experience compressive strain (−γₓᵧ/2). The differential output is:

$$ V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \gamma_{xy} / 2 $$

Step 3 (2 marks): **Temperature compensation justification:** When ambient temperature changes, all four gauges experience the **same fractional resistance change** (ΔR/R)_temp due to thermal expansion of both the gauge material and the shaft. In the bridge equation, this common-mode term appears in both the numerator and denominator and **cancels out** because the gauges are wired in a way that their thermal contributions are subtracted symmetrically. The differential measurement is inherently immune to uniform temperature changes.

Step 4 (1 mark): Additionally, this configuration rejects **axial force and bending** because such loads produce equal-magnitude strains on the +45° and −45° gauges (in pure bending or pure axial case, the shear strain γₓᵧ is zero), so they cancel in the bridge.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 (1 mark): Polar moment of inertia for hollow shaft:

$$ J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi [(0.060)^4 - (0.040)^4]}{32} $$

$$ J = \frac{\pi [1.296 \times 10^{-5} - 2.560 \times 10^{-6}]}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 1.040 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.021 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4 $$

Step 2 (2 marks): Surface shear stress (r = 30 mm = outer radius):

$$ \tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{500 \times 0.030}{1.021 \times 10^{-6}} = 14.69 \text{ MPa} $$

Step 3 (1 mark): Maximum shear strain:

$$ \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{14.69 \times 10^6}{80 \times 10^9} = 1.836 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad} $$

Step 4 (2 marks): Bridge output (full bridge, ±45° gauges):

$$ V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \gamma_{xy}}{2} = \frac{10 \times 2.1 \times 1.836 \times 10^{-4}}{2} $$

Step 5 (1 mark): Final result:

$$ V_{out} = 1.928 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \approx 1.93 \text{ mV} $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Poisson's Effect:** Many students compute the half-bridge output using only εₓ and forget to include the (1+ν) factor from the transverse gauge. This **costs 2 marks** in the typical marking scheme.
> 2. **Wrong angle for torque:** A surprisingly common error is orienting torque gauges at 0°/90° instead of ±45°. This yields a near-zero output (since εₓ and εᵧ are both zero under pure torque), and the student is marked **zero** for the orientation part.
> 3. **Sign confusion in bridge equation:** The bridge output for adjacent arms (half bridge) uses (R₁/(R₁+R₂) − R₃/(R₃+R₄)). Mixing up adjacent vs opposite arm placement changes the sign of Vₒᵤₜ and is a **favourite trick question** in KTU exams.
> 4. **J vs J/2 confusion:** For a solid circular shaft, J = πd⁴/32. Some students use πd⁴/64 (which is the *area* moment of inertia I, not the polar moment J). Always remember: **J = 2I for a solid circle**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

*   **Strain Gauge Orientation Rule of Thumb:**
    *   **Force (axial/tensile)** → Gauge at **0°** to loading axis.
    *   **Bending moment** → Two gauges at **0°** on top and bottom of the beam (one in tension, one in compression).
    *   **Torque (torsion)** → Two gauges at **+45°** and two at **−45°** to shaft axis.
*   **Strain Transformation Equation** is the single most important equation on this topic — memorize it word-for-word.
*   **45° is the magic angle for torque** because it is exactly half-way between the principal directions of pure shear, which are oriented at ±45° to the shaft axis.
*   **Bridge Topology Trade-offs:**
    *   **Quarter bridge** → Cheapest, no temperature compensation, sensitive to all loads.
    *   **Half bridge** → 2× sensitivity, partial compensation.
    *   **Full bridge** → 4× sensitivity, full temperature & parasitic load compensation. **Industry default for OEM sensors.**
*   **Gauge Factor (GF)** is dimensionless. For constantan ≈ **2.0**, for silicon ≈ **100–150**.
*   **Poisson's ratio (ν ≈ 0.3)** appears in the (1+ν) factor — never forget it in axial-force problems with active transverse gauges.
*   **Polar moment of inertia (J)** for solid shaft = πd⁴/32; for hollow shaft = π(D⁴−d⁴)/32. Confusing J with I is a common KTU trap.
*   **Temperature compensation** is achieved automatically by a full bridge because all four gauges see the same ΔT and the differential measurement rejects common-mode signals.
*   **Mohr's Circle for Strain** graphically proves that pure shear ⇒ principal strains at ±45°, with magnitude ±γ/2.
*   **Industrial Standards:** ISO 376 (force calibration), ISO 6789 (torque tools) — both reference strain gauge bridge outputs as the primary traceability path.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain Gauge-Based Load Cells and Torque Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Strain Gauge-Based Load Cells and Torque Sensors # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Strain Gauge — The Fundamental Sensing Element A **strain gauge** is a passive resistive transducer whose electrical resistance changes in a defined, predictable manner when it is mechanically stretched or compressed (i.e., when it experiences *strain*). The gauge converts a mechanical deformation into a measurable change in resistance, which is then converted into an electrical signal via a **Wheatstone bridge** circuit. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *"A strain gauge is a device whose resistance varies with applied strain. The fractional change in resistance is proportional to the strain, with the constant of proportionality called the Gauge Factor (GF)."* Mathematically, the strain $\varepsilon$ is defined as the change in length per unit original length: $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$ and the corresponding fractional change in resistance is: $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$$ ## 1.2 Load Cell — Definition A **Load Cell** is a force-measuring transducer that converts an applied mechanical force (tension or compression) into a measurable electrical output. The most common industrial implementation uses **strain gauges bonded to a calibrated elastic element** (a *spring member* or *proof body*). > [!NOTE] > **Working Principle (KTU):** When a force $F$ is applied to the load cell, the elastic element deforms slightly. Strain gauges bonded to this element experience a proportional strain, which changes their resistance. The Wheatstone bridge converts this resistance change into a millivolt-level voltage output proportional to the applied force. ## 1.3 Torque Sensor — Definition A **Torque Sensor** (or *torque transducer*) measures the rotational force (twisting moment) applied to a shaft. Strain-gauge-based torque sensors use gauges bonded to a torsion bar at **±45° to the shaft axis**, where shear strain is maximum. $$T = \text{Torque in N·m}, \quad \tau = \text{Shear stress in N/m}^2$$ ## 1.4 Real-World Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!TIP] > **🍌 Banana Analogy for Strain Gauge:** > Imagine a rubber band being stretched. As you pull it, the rubber band becomes thinner and longer. A strain gauge behaves the same way — when stretched, it becomes longer and thinner, so its electrical resistance **increases**. When compressed (squished), it becomes shorter and thicker, so its resistance **decreases**. This tiny resistance change is what we measure. > [!TIP] > **🏋️ Weighing Scale Analogy for Load Cell:** > Think of a bathroom scale. When you stand on it, an internal metal plate bends ever so slightly. Strain gauges glued on this plate change resistance with that bend. The electronics convert this into the kg reading on the display. A load cell is the **industrial-grade, high-precision cousin** of this very same idea. > [!TIP] > **🔧 Wrench Analogy for Torque Sensor:** > When you use a wrench to tighten a bolt, the shaft of the wrench twists. A torque sensor detects this twist using strain gauges placed at ±45° angles on the shaft — exactly where the twist strain is most pronounced. ## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Gauge Factor (GF)** for metallic foil gauges: approximately **2.0 to 2.2** - **Gauge Factor** for semiconductor gauges: approximately **±100 to ±200** - **Typical full-scale bridge output**: **1 mV/V to 3 mV/V** - **Standard bridge excitation voltage**: **5 V or 10 V DC** - **Standard load cell accuracy classes**: **OIML C1, C3, C6** (where the number denotes 1000s of verification intervals) - **Youngs Modulus of typical load cell steel**: **E ≈ 200 GPa** - **Poisson's ratio for steel**: **ν ≈ 0.30** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Bonded Metallic Strain Gauge The strain gauge consists of a thin metallic foil arranged in a grid pattern, bonded to a flexible backing (carrier). When the underlying structure deforms: 1. The foil experiences the same strain as the surface. 2. The conductor length increases and cross-sectional area decreases. 3. Resistance changes according to $R = \rho L / A$. ## 2.2 The Gauge Factor (GF) — Derivation Concept Starting from $R = \rho L / A$, taking the logarithmic differential: $$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$ For a conductor of circular cross-section with Poisson's ratio $\nu$: $$\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \frac{dL}{L} = -2\nu \varepsilon$$ The piezoresistive effect adds a term involving the change in resistivity: $$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = C \cdot \varepsilon$$ Combining, the Gauge Factor is: $$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{C}{\varepsilon}$$ ## 2.3 Why the Wheatstone Bridge? A single strain gauge produces a tiny resistance change (e.g., $\Delta R \approx 0.001 \, \Omega$ for a 120 $\Omega$ gauge at 1000 $\mu\varepsilon$). This small signal cannot be measured directly with sufficient accuracy. The **Wheatstone bridge** converts small resistance changes into a measurable differential voltage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bridge Configuration Advantages:** > - Compensates for temperature-induced resistance changes > - Amplifies the signal by a factor of **up to 4×** (full-bridge configuration) > - Rejects common-mode signals (noise, drift) > - Allows automatic temperature compensation via dummy gauges ## 2.4 Wheatstone Bridge Output Voltage For a **quarter-bridge** with one active gauge ($R_1 = R + \Delta R$, others = $R$): $$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{R}{2R} \right)$$ For small $\Delta R$ (linearized): $$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$ For a **full-bridge with 4 active gauges** (two in tension, two in compression): $$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$ > [!NOTE] > The full-bridge gives **4× the output voltage** of a quarter-bridge for the same strain — this is the standard configuration in commercial load cells. ## 2.5 Load Cell — Force-to-Voltage Conversion Chain The complete chain for a load cell: $$F \;\xrightarrow{\text{Elastic Element}}\; \varepsilon \;\xrightarrow{\text{Strain Gauge}}\; \Delta R \;\xrightarrow{\text{Wheatstone Bridge}}\; V_o$$ The applied force creates stress $\sigma = F/A$ in the elastic element, producing strain $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$, so: $$\varepsilon = \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$ Substituting into the bridge output equation: $$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$ Solving for force: $$F = \frac{4 \cdot V_o \cdot A \cdot E}{V_{ex} \cdot GF}$$ ## 2.6 Torque Sensor — Shear Strain to Voltage For a shaft of radius $r$ under torque $T$, the maximum shear stress at the surface is: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \quad \text{(for solid circular shaft, d = diameter)}$$ The corresponding shear strain is: $$\gamma = \frac{\tau_{max}}{G} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3 G}$$ where $G$ is the shear modulus. For gauges oriented at **±45°** to the shaft axis, the principal strain on each gauge is: $$\varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{\gamma}{2} = \frac{8T}{\pi d^3 G}$$ Bridge output for a full-bridge torque sensor: $$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{8 V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot T}{\pi d^3 G}$$ ## 2.7 Types of Load Cells (KTU Syllabus — Must Memorize) | Type | Description | Application | |------|-------------|-------------| | **Canister (Compression) Load Cell** | Cylindrical body, gauges on internal column | Truck scales, tank weighing | | **S-Type Load Cell** | S-shaped elastic element with threaded ends | Tension/compression, small forces | | **Single-Point Load Cell** | Central loading platform | Platform scales, retail weighing | | **Shear Beam Load Cell** | Force shears a block; gauges measure shear strain | Industrial scales, hoppers | | **Bending Beam Load Cell** | Cantilever beam deflects under load | Low-capacity bench scales | | **Button Load Cell** | Compact, low-profile design | OEM force measurement | ## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Strain (dimensionless, often in $\mu\varepsilon$) | — | | 2 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (stress-strain) | Pa | | 3 | $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Strain gauge fundamental relation | — | | 4 | $GF = (1 + 2\nu) + (C/\varepsilon)$ | Gauge Factor (metallic ≈ 2, semiconductor ≈ 100–200) | — | | 5 | $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot (\Delta R / R)$ | Quarter-bridge output voltage | V | | 6 | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Full-bridge output voltage | V | | 7 | $F = (4 V_o A E) / (V_{ex} \cdot GF)$ | Force from full-bridge load cell | N | | 8 | $\tau_{max} = 16T / (\pi d^3)$ | Max shear stress in solid shaft | Pa | | 9 | $\gamma = \tau / G$ | Shear strain | — | | 10 | $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ | Shear modulus from Young's modulus | Pa | | 11 | $\varepsilon_{45°} = \gamma / 2$ | Principal strain on 45° gauge | — | | 12 | $V_o = [8 V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot T] / [\pi d^3 G]$ | Torque sensor output (full bridge) | V | | 13 | $S = V_o / F$ | Load cell sensitivity | V/N | | 14 | $S_T = V_o / T$ | Torque sensor sensitivity | V/(N·m) | | 15 | $R_{gauge} = 120, 350, 700, 1000$ | Standard gauge resistances | $\Omega$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The factor of **4** between quarter-bridge and full-bridge sensitivity is the most frequently tested point. Always specify which bridge configuration is being used in your answer. ## 2.9 Engineering Applications — Real-World Utility - **Industrial weighing**: Truck scales, tank weighing, conveyor belt scales - **Process control**: Force feedback in manufacturing (e.g., press-fit operations) - **Aerospace**: Structural load monitoring, engine torque measurement - **Automotive**: Brake testing, dynamometers, crash test instrumentation - **Medical**: Infusion pumps, prosthetics force feedback, dialysis machines - **Robotics**: Force/torque sensors at robotic joints for compliant assembly - **Power plants**: Rotating shaft torque measurement in turbines and generators # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Gauge Factor We begin with the fundamental resistance equation: $$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$ **Step 1**: Take the natural logarithm of both sides: $$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$ **Step 2**: Differentiate both sides with respect to strain $\varepsilon$: $$\frac{1}{R} \frac{dR}{d\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{\rho} \frac{d\rho}{d\varepsilon} + \frac{1}{L} \frac{dL}{d\varepsilon} - \frac{1}{A} \frac{dA}{d\varepsilon}$$ **Step 3**: Recognize that $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$, so $dL / L = d\varepsilon$. Therefore: $$\frac{1}{L}\frac{dL}{d\varepsilon} = 1$$ **Step 4**: For the area term, assume the conductor has a circular cross-section $A = \pi r^2$. Then $dA / A = 2 \cdot dr / r$. By Poisson's ratio, $dr / r = -\nu \cdot dL / L = -\nu \cdot d\varepsilon$. Hence: $$\frac{1}{A}\frac{dA}{d\varepsilon} = -2\nu$$ **Step 5**: Substitute back into the differentiated equation: $$\frac{dR/R}{d\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{d\rho}{d\varepsilon}$$ **Step 6**: By definition, the Gauge Factor is: $$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$ **Step 7**: For most metallic conductors, the piezoresistive term $\Delta \rho / \rho$ is small. For steel with $\nu = 0.30$: $$GF \approx 1 + 2(0.30) = 1.60 \text{ (geometric term only)}$$ Measured GF for constantan is ≈ **2.0**, indicating a small but non-zero piezoresistive contribution. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Quarter-Bridge Output Voltage Consider a Wheatstone bridge with: - $R_1 = R + \Delta R$ (active gauge) - $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$ (fixed resistors) The bridge is excited by $V_{ex}$ across nodes A (top) and B (bottom). **Step 1**: Voltage at left node (between $R_1$ and $R_4$): $$V_L = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_4} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R}$$ **Step 2**: Voltage at right node (between $R_2$ and $R_3$): $$V_R = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2 + R_3} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{2R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2}$$ **Step 3**: Differential output voltage: $$V_o = V_L - V_R = V_{ex} \cdot \left[ \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right]$$ **Step 4**: Simplify the fraction. Multiply numerator and denominator by $2R + \Delta R$ and combine: $$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{2(R + \Delta R) - (2R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$ $$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{2R + 2\Delta R - 2R - \Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$ $$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$ **Step 5**: For $\Delta R \ll R$ (which is always true for strain gauges): $$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$ **Step 6**: Substitute $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$: $$\boxed{V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Force Sensitivity for a Load Cell Given: - Elastic element cross-sectional area $A = w \times t$ (rectangular column) - Applied force $F$ - Material Young's modulus $E$ **Step 1**: Axial stress in the column under compressive/tensile load: $$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$$ **Step 2**: Strain using Hooke's law: $$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$ **Step 3**: Substitute into full-bridge output equation: $$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$ **Step 4**: Solve for sensitivity $S_F$: $$S_F = \frac{V_o}{F} = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF}{A \cdot E}$$ **Step 5**: Substitute numerical example: - $V_{ex} = 10 \, V$ - $GF = 2.0$ - $A = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2$ (i.e., 25 mm × 20 mm column) - $E = 200 \times 10^9 \, Pa$ (steel) $$S_F = \frac{10 \times 2.0}{5 \times 10^{-4} \times 200 \times 10^9} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \, V/N$$ **Step 6**: Express in more practical units. Convert N to kN: $$S_F = 2 \times 10^{-4} \, V/kN = 0.2 \, mV/kN$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Torque Sensitivity for a Shaft Given: - Shaft diameter $d = 0.05 \, m$ - Shear modulus $G = 77 \, GPa$ (steel) - $V_{ex} = 10 \, V$, $GF = 2.0$ **Step 1**: Shear strain on 45° gauge (principal strain = $\gamma/2$): $$\varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{8T}{\pi d^3 G}$$ **Step 2**: Full-bridge output: $$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{8 V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot T}{\pi d^3 G}$$ **Step 3**: Solve for torque sensitivity $S_T$: $$S_T = \frac{V_o}{T} = \frac{8 V_{ex} \cdot GF}{\pi d^3 G}$$ **Step 4**: Substitute values: $$S_T = \frac{8 \times 10 \times 2.0}{\pi \times (0.05)^3 \times 77 \times 10^9}$$ $$S_T = \frac{160}{\pi \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 77 \times 10^9}$$ $$S_T = \frac{160}{\pi \times 9.625 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$S_T \approx \frac{160}{3.024 \times 10^7}$$ $$\boxed{S_T \approx 5.29 \times 10^{-6} \, V/(N \cdot m) \approx 5.29 \, \mu V / (N \cdot m)}$$ **Step 5**: Interpretation: For 1 N·m of torque, the bridge gives only ~5.29 µV output, confirming the need for high-gain instrumentation amplifiers. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Bridge Configuration Comparison | Bridge Type | Active Gauges | Output Equation | Output Magnitude | Temperature Compensation | |-------------|---------------|-----------------|------------------|------------------------| | **Quarter-bridge (1 active)** | 1 | $V_o = (V_{ex}/4)(GF \cdot \varepsilon)$ | $1\times$ (baseline) | Requires dummy gauge | | **Half-bridge (2 active)** | 2 (adjacent arms) | $V_o = (V_{ex}/2)(GF \cdot \varepsilon)$ | $2\times$ | Self-compensating | | **Full-bridge (4 active)** | 4 (Poisson config) | $V_o = V_{ex}(GF \cdot \varepsilon)$ | $4\times$ | Fully self-compensating | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Full-Bridge Poisson Configuration:** Two gauges measure axial strain (tension), two measure transverse strain (compression via Poisson effect). The bridge adds these contributions for 4× sensitivity, with perfect temperature compensation because all four gauges share the same thermal environment. ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Style **Problem:** A load cell uses four 350 $\Omega$ strain gauges (GF = 2.1) in a full-bridge configuration. The elastic element is a steel column ($E = 200 \, GPa$) with cross-section $30 \, mm \times 30 \, mm$. The bridge is excited with $V_{ex} = 10 \, V$. **Find:** (a) Bridge output for an applied force of $F = 50 \, kN$. (b) Sensitivity in V/kN. (c) The strain experienced by the column. **Solution:** (a) Strain in the column: $$\varepsilon = \frac{F}{A \cdot E} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{30 \times 10^{-3} \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 \times 10^9}$$ $$\varepsilon = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{9 \times 10^{-4} \times 200 \times 10^9} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{1.8 \times 10^8}$$ $$\varepsilon = 2.778 \times 10^{-4} = 277.8 \, \mu\varepsilon$$ (b) Bridge output (full-bridge): $$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon = 10 \times 2.1 \times 2.778 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\boxed{V_o = 5.833 \times 10^{-3} \, V = 5.833 \, mV}$$ (c) Sensitivity: $$S_F = \frac{V_o}{F} = \frac{5.833 \times 10^{-3}}{50} = 1.167 \times 10^{-4} \, V/kN$$ $$\boxed{S_F = 0.1167 \, mV/V \text{ per kN}}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Load Cell Signal Conditioning Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A["FORCE INPUT F"] --> B["ELASTIC ELEMENT

[3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The **Gauge Factor (GF)** is defined as the ratio of fractional change in resistance to the fractional change in length (strain) of a strain gauge. It is a dimensionless constant that characterizes the sensitivity of the strain gauge. $$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L}$$ For metallic strain gauges, GF ≈ **2.0 to 2.2**. For semiconductor (piezoresistive) gauges, GF ≈ **±100 to ±200**. The gauge factor can also be expressed as: $$GF = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$ where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio and $\Delta \rho / \rho$ represents the piezoresistive effect. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write only $GF = \Delta R / R$ and forget to divide by strain $\varepsilon$. Always include strain in the denominator for full marks. --- ### Question 2: Why is a Wheatstone bridge used with strain gauges instead of measuring resistance directly? [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A strain gauge produces a very small resistance change ($\Delta R \approx 0.001 \, \Omega$ for a 120 $\Omega$ gauge at 1000 $\mu\varepsilon$). Measuring this directly requires highly sensitive instruments. The **Wheatstone bridge** is used because: 1. **Sensitivity**: It converts the tiny $\Delta R$ into a measurable differential output voltage $V_o$. 2. **Temperature Compensation**: When configured with dummy or active gauges, common-mode temperature-induced resistance changes cancel out. 3. **Linearity**: For small $\Delta R$, the output voltage $V_o$ is linearly proportional to strain. 4. **Noise Rejection**: Differential measurement rejects common-mode electrical noise. The bridge output equation for a quarter-bridge is: $$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Do not say "the bridge amplifies the signal." The bridge is a **converter** (resistance to voltage), not an amplifier. The amplification comes later from the instrumentation amplifier. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` #### (a) Explain with neat diagram the working principle of a strain gauge-based load cell. Discuss the types of load cells. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** A **load cell** is a force-measuring transducer that uses strain gauges bonded to an elastic element to convert mechanical force into an electrical signal. **Working Principle:** 1. The applied force $F$ acts on the elastic element (spring member), producing a proportional strain $\varepsilon = F / (A \cdot E)$. 2. Strain gauges bonded to the elastic element experience this same strain. 3. The strain changes the gauge resistance by $\Delta R = R \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$. 4. The Wheatstone bridge converts $\Delta R$ into a differential output voltage $V_o$. 5. After amplification and calibration, $V_o$ is displayed as the measured force. **Block Diagram:** ``` Force F → Elastic Element → Strain → Gauges → ΔR → Bridge → V_o → Amp → Display ``` **Types of Load Cells:** | Type | Description | Application | |------|-------------|-------------| | **Canister Load Cell** | Cylindrical body with internal column | Truck scales, tank weighing | | **S-Type Load Cell** | S-shaped elastic element | Tension/compression, 50 N to 50 kN | | **Single-Point Load Cell** | Platform load cell | Retail scales, packaging | | **Shear Beam Load Cell** | Shear loading on block | Industrial hoppers | | **Bending Beam Load Cell** | Cantilever deflection | Low-capacity scales | | **Button Load Cell** | Compact, low profile | OEM force measurement | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget to draw the block diagram. Always include at least one neat diagram for full marks in 7-mark questions. #### (b) A strain gauge load cell has four active gauges (GF = 2.05) bonded to a steel column ($E = 210 \, GPa$, $\nu = 0.29$) of cross-section $25 \, mm \times 25 \, mm$. The gauges are arranged in a full-bridge Poisson configuration. Bridge excitation is 10 V. Calculate: (i) the strain produced by a 30 kN load, (ii) the bridge output voltage, (iii) the sensitivity in mV/V per kN. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - Full-bridge configuration (4 active gauges) - $GF = 2.05$, $V_{ex} = 10 \, V$ - $E = 210 \times 10^9 \, Pa$ - $A = 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 \times 10^{-3} = 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2$ - $F = 30 \, kN = 30 \times 10^3 \, N$ **(i) Strain:** $$\varepsilon = \frac{F}{A \cdot E} = \frac{30 \times 10^3}{6.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 210 \times 10^9}$$ $$\varepsilon = \frac{30 \times 10^3}{1.3125 \times 10^8} = 2.286 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\boxed{\varepsilon = 228.6 \, \mu\varepsilon}$$ **(ii) Bridge Output Voltage:** $$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon = 10 \times 2.05 \times 2.286 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\boxed{V_o = 4.686 \times 10^{-3} \, V = 4.686 \, mV}$$ **(iii) Sensitivity:** $$S_F = \frac{V_o}{F} = \frac{4.686 \times 10^{-3}}{30} = 1.562 \times 10^{-4} \, V/kN$$ Expressed as mV/V ratio (dividing by $V_{ex}$): $$S_{normalized} = \frac{V_o / V_{ex}}{F} = \frac{4.686 \times 10^{-4}}{30}$$ $$\boxed{S_{normalized} = 1.562 \times 10^{-5} \, (mV/V)/kN = 15.62 \, \mu V/V \text{ per kN}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` #### (a) Explain the working of a strain gauge-based torque sensor with suitable diagrams. Derive the expression for bridge output voltage. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** A **torque sensor** measures the twisting moment applied to a rotating or stationary shaft. It uses strain gauges bonded to a shaft at angles of **±45°** to the shaft axis, where the principal strain due to torsion is maximum. **Working Principle:** 1. Torque $T$ applied to a shaft produces shear stress: $\tau_{max} = 16T / (\pi d^3)$ at the surface. 2. The corresponding shear strain is $\gamma = \tau_{max} / G$. 3. Strain gauges at ±45° experience principal strain $\varepsilon_{45°} = \gamma / 2$. 4. Four gauges form a full Wheatstone bridge, producing output voltage proportional to $T$. **Derivation:** $$\gamma = \frac{\tau_{max}}{G} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3 G}$$ $$\varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{\gamma}{2} = \frac{8T}{\pi d^3 G}$$ Full-bridge output: $$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon_{45°}$$ $$\boxed{V_o = \frac{8 V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot T}{\pi d^3 G}}$$ Solving for torque: $$T = \frac{\pi d^3 G \cdot V_o}{8 V_{ex} \cdot GF}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** A common error is using $d^2$ instead of $d^3$ in the torque formula. The derivation must include the polar moment of inertia $J = \pi d^4 / 32$ and surface radius $r = d/2$, leading to the $d^3$ in the denominator. #### (b) A torque sensor is fitted on a steel shaft of diameter 40 mm. The shear modulus is 80 GPa. Four strain gauges (GF = 2.1) are mounted at ±45° in a full bridge. The excitation voltage is 12 V. Calculate: (i) the maximum strain on the gauges when a torque of 500 N·m is applied, (ii) the bridge output voltage, (iii) the change in resistance of each gauge if $R = 350 \, \Omega$. [7 Marks] **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - $d = 40 \, mm = 0.04 \, m$ - $G = 80 \times 10^9 \, Pa$ - $GF = 2.1$, $V_{ex} = 12 \, V$, $R = 350 \, \Omega$ - $T = 500 \, N \cdot m$ **(i) Maximum Strain:** $$\varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{8T}{\pi d^3 G} = \frac{8 \times 500}{\pi \times (0.04)^3 \times 80 \times 10^9}$$ $$\varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{4000}{\pi \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} \times 80 \times 10^9}$$ $$\varepsilon_{45°} = \frac{4000}{\pi \times 5.12 \times 10^6} = \frac{4000}{1.609 \times 10^7}$$ $$\boxed{\varepsilon_{45°} = 2.487 \times 10^{-4} = 248.7 \, \mu\varepsilon}$$ **(ii) Bridge Output Voltage:** $$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon_{45°} = 12 \times 2.1 \times 2.487 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\boxed{V_o = 6.267 \times 10^{-3} \, V = 6.267 \, mV}$$ **(iii) Resistance Change:** $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 2.487 \times 10^{-4} = 5.223 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\Delta R = 350 \times 5.223 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta R = 0.1828 \, \Omega}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** For torque sensor problems, ensure the gauge orientation is ±45°. Some students incorrectly use the axial strain formula. Always begin by stating the principle: principal strain on a 45° gauge equals half the shear strain. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Strain Gauge Load Cells & Torque Sensors** ### Core Concepts - ☐ Strain $\varepsilon$ = fractional change in length (dimensionless, often $\mu\varepsilon$) - ☐ Gauge Factor (GF) is the ratio $\Delta R/R$ to $\varepsilon$ — dimensionless - ☐ Metallic GF ≈ **2.0–2.2**; semiconductor GF ≈ **±100 to ±200** - ☐ GF = $(1 + 2\nu) + (\Delta \rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$ - ☐ Standard gauge resistance: **120 $\Omega$, 350 $\Omega$, 700 $\Omega$, 1000 $\Omega$** ### Wheatstone Bridge - ☐ Quarter-bridge: $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ - ☐ Half-bridge: $V_o = (V_{ex}/2) \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ - ☐ Full-bridge (Poisson): $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ - ☐ Full-bridge gives **4× output** of quarter-bridge - ☐ Bridge provides temperature compensation ### Load Cell - ☐ Force to strain: $\varepsilon = F/(A \cdot E)$ - ☐ Force sensitivity: $S_F = (V_{ex} \cdot GF)/(A \cdot E)$ - ☐ Common types: Canister, S-Type, Single-Point, Shear Beam, Bending Beam, Button - ☐ Typical output: **1 to 3 mV/V** at full scale ### Torque Sensor - ☐ Shaft shear stress: $\tau_{max} = 16T/(\pi d^3)$ - ☐ Shear strain: $\gamma = \tau/G$ - ☐ 45° gauge strain: $\varepsilon_{45°} = \gamma/2$ - ☐ Bridge output: $V_o = 8 V_{ex} GF T / (\pi d^3 G)$ - ☐ Gauges mounted at **±45°** to shaft axis ### Quick Exam Formulas - ☐ $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ — relationship between E and G - ☐ Stress formula for circular shaft: $\tau = Tr/J$ - ☐ $J = \pi d^4/32$ for solid shaft - ☐ Always state units in final answer (N, N·m, V, $\mu\varepsilon$) ### Common Mistakes to Avoid - ❌ Confusing **stress** ($\sigma = F/A$) with **strain** ($\varepsilon = \Delta L/L$) - ❌ Forgetting the factor of **4** between bridge configurations - ❌ Using axial strain for torque sensors (use ±45° gauge strain instead) - ❌ Mixing up $d$ (diameter) with $r$ (radius) in torque formulas - ❌ Writing "amplification" when meaning "conversion" for the bridge

topics.title: Torque and Shaft Power Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Torque and Shaft Power Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Torque and Shaft Power Measurement

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Torque measurement* is the determination of the rotational force (torsional moment) transmitted through a rotating shaft, expressed in **N·m**. *Shaft power measurement* is the quantification of mechanical power transmitted through a rotating shaft, computed as the product of torque and angular velocity, expressed in **W** (watts) or **kW**.

### 1.1 What is Torque?
Torque (τ) is the *twisting moment* applied about an axis of rotation. Mathematically, it is the cross product of the radius vector and the applied force:

$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$$

In scalar form (for tangential force on a rotating member):
$$\tau = F \cdot r$$

Where:
- **F** = Tangential force in **N** (newtons)
- **r** = Radius (moment arm) in **m** (metres)

### 1.2 What is Shaft Power?
Shaft power is the rate at which rotational work is done. For a rotating shaft:

$$P = \tau \cdot \omega = 2\pi N \tau$$

Where:
- **P** = Shaft power in **W**
- **ω** = Angular velocity in **rad/s**
- **N** = Rotational speed in **rps** (revolutions per second)

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Units Used in KTU Problems:**
> - Torque: **N·m**, kN·m
> - Speed: **rpm** (revolutions per minute) or rps
> - Power: **W, kW, hp** (1 hp = 745.7 W ≈ 0.746 kW)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Pedal and the Steering Wheel

Imagine you are pedalling a bicycle up a steep hill. The force you apply on the pedal through the chain creates a **twisting action** on the rear wheel axle — that twisting action is *torque*. The faster you pedal (higher RPM) and the harder you push (higher torque), the more power you deliver to the wheel — that is *shaft power*.

In the same way, an engine or motor applies torque to its output shaft. To measure how much useful mechanical work it delivers, engineers use devices called **dynamometers (dynos)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Torque/Power Measurement Critical?**
> - Performance testing of engines, motors, turbines
> - Efficiency mapping of gearboxes and drivetrains
> - Quality control in manufacturing (torque wrenches, fasteners)
> - Acceptance testing as per **IS 1460**, **ISO 1585** standards for engines

### 1.4 Classification of Dynamometers

Dynamometers are devices used to measure torque and power. They are broadly classified into three families:

| **Family** | **Function** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Absorption Dynamometers** | Absorb (dissipate) the power delivered by the source | Prony brake, Rope brake, Hydraulic dynamometer, Eddy current dynamometer |
| **Transmission Dynamometers** | Measure torque in a transmission line without absorbing it | Epicyclic train, Belt-type, Torsion dynamometer |
| **Driving / Motoring Dynamometers** | Drive the machine under test as a motor | Electric dynamometer in motoring mode |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Prony Brake Dynamometer (Absorption Type)

#### 2.1.1 Construction & Working Principle
The Prony brake is the *grandfather* of absorption dynamometers. It consists of two wooden/cast-iron blocks clamped around a rotating pulley (or flywheel) attached to the engine shaft. The blocks are tightened via a screw, creating a frictional contact that absorbs power.

A horizontal lever arm of known length **L** is attached to the upper block, resting on a weighing scale (spring balance). The frictional torque tries to rotate the block; the scale resists this rotation. The difference in scale readings, multiplied by arm length, gives the torque.

#### 2.1.2 Working Logic (Step-by-Step)
1. Engine rotates pulley at speed **N** rpm.
2. Friction force **F** acts tangentially at the pulley radius **R**.
3. This friction creates a braking torque opposing rotation.
4. The reaction torque tries to lift the lever arm on one side.
5. Scale reads force **W₁** (when engaged) and **W₀** (unloaded).
6. Net effective force = **W₁ − W₀**.
7. Torque τ = (W₁ − W₀) × L, where L is the lever arm length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The Prony brake dissipates power entirely as heat through friction. Cooling is essential — without it, the wooden blocks will char. Modern versions use water-cooled cast-iron blocks.

### 2.2 Rope Brake Dynamometer (Absorption Type)

#### 2.2.1 Principle
A rope (or band) is wrapped around the rotating flywheel/pulley. Tension in the rope creates a frictional grip. One end of the rope is fixed to a load cell / spring balance, the other to a dead weight.

**Net braking force** = Difference in tensions (T₁ − T₀) = (W − S)

Where:
- **W** = Dead weight suspended (kgf or N)
- **S** = Spring balance reading (kgf or N)

**Braking torque:**
$$\tau = (W - S) \cdot R$$

Where **R** is the effective pulley radius (typically pulley radius + rope radius/2).

#### 2.2.2 Advantage over Prony Brake
- Cooling water can be sprayed on the pulley to dissipate heat.
- Larger frictional contact area → can absorb higher power.
- More commonly used for large engines (motorcycles, tractors).

### 2.3 Hydraulic Dynamometer (Absorption Type)

#### 2.3.1 Construction
A multi-vane rotor is mounted on the shaft under test. A stator (casing) with matching vanes surrounds the rotor, the gap filled with water (or oil). Water acts as the working fluid for energy absorption.

#### 2.3.2 Operating Principle
1. As rotor spins, water is thrown outward by centrifugal force.
2. Water creates a shearing (drag) torque between rotor and stator.
3. Stator is suspended on trunnions and tries to rotate — resisted by a lever arm and scale.
4. The torque reading on the scale equals the braking torque.

#### 2.3.3 Key Formulas
- **Braking torque:** τ = (W − S) × L
- **Shaft power:** P = 2πN(W − S)L / 60 watts

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Hydraulic dynamometers are widely used for testing large marine engines, locomotives, and industrial prime movers because water provides excellent heat dissipation and the torque can be smoothly varied by changing water level.

### 2.4 Eddy Current Dynamometer (Absorption Type)

#### 2.4.1 Principle
A metallic disc (usually copper or aluminium) rotates between the poles of an electromagnet. As the disc cuts magnetic flux lines, **eddy currents** are induced in it (Faraday's Law). According to **Lenz's Law**, these currents oppose the cause (rotation), creating a braking torque.

#### 2.4.2 Why It's Special
- **No physical contact** between moving and stationary parts → no wear.
- Torque can be controlled precisely by adjusting the **excitation current** of the electromagnet.
- Power absorbed is dissipated as heat in the disc → requires water cooling.
- Smooth, stepless torque control ideal for plotting **torque-speed curves** and **power maps**.

### 2.5 Transmission Dynamometers

These measure torque **without absorbing** the mechanical power — they pass it through while measuring the twist or reaction.

#### 2.5.1 Epicyclic Train Dynamometer (Sun & Planet Type)
**Most frequently asked in KTU exams!**

**Construction:**
- Two gears: a *sun gear* keyed to the input shaft, and a *planet gear* meshing with it.
- Planet gear is mounted on a lever arm (cradle), free to rotate about the input shaft axis.
- A pinion on the planet gear axis drives an output gear on the output shaft.

**Working Logic:**
1. Input torque **T_s** acts on the sun gear.
2. The planet gear experiences equal and opposite reaction **T_p = T_s** (since they mesh and ratio is 1:1).
3. This reaction tries to rotate the cradle (lever arm) about the shaft axis.
4. The cradle is held stationary by a weighing scale — it reads force **W**.
5. Cradle torque = **W × L** = **T_s** (measured torque).

**Output shaft speed:**
- If sun gear has **T_s** teeth and planet gear has **T_p** teeth, then for one rotation of the sun, the planet gear rotates about its own axis (T_s/T_p) times.
- Output speed **N_out = N_in × (1 + T_s/T_p)** (when output is from the planet carrier or coaxial arrangement — depends on configuration).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Advantage:** The epicyclic dynamometer can be designed to measure very small torques accurately because the scale reading **W** is amplified by the gear ratio (multiplied by 1 + T_s/T_p).

**Formula for torque:**
$$T_s = W \cdot L$$

**Formula for power:**
$$P = 2\pi N_s \cdot T_s / 60$$

#### 2.5.2 Belt Transmission Dynamometer
A pulley on the shaft drives a flat belt. The belt passes over two idler pulleys mounted on a pivoted frame. The belt tensions on tight side **T₁** and slack side **T₂** produce a net pull on the frame, measured by a scale.

**Torque:**
$$\tau = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot R$$

Where **R** is the radius of the driving pulley.

#### 2.5.3 Torsion Dynamometer
- A torsional spring (calibrated shaft) is inserted between motor and load.
- The relative angular twist between the two ends of the spring is proportional to the transmitted torque.
- Twist measured optically, electrically (strain gauge), or mechanically.

**Formula:**
$$\tau = \frac{G \cdot J \cdot \theta}{L}$$

Where:
- **G** = Shear modulus of the shaft material (Pa)
- **J** = Polar moment of inertia (m⁴) = πd⁴/32 for solid shaft
- **θ** = Angle of twist (radians)
- **L** = Gauge length (m)

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it appears in 80% of KTU numerical problems on this module.**

| **Dynamometer Type** | **Braking / Measured Torque (τ)** | **Shaft Power (P)** | **Key Constants** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prony Brake** | τ = (W₁ − W₀) × L | P = 2πN(W₁ − W₀)L / 60 W | L = lever arm length (m) |
| **Rope Brake** | τ = (W − S) × R | P = 2πN(W − S)R / 60 W | R = effective pulley radius (m) |
| **Hydraulic** | τ = (W − S) × L | P = 2πN(W − S)L / 60 W | Water level controls torque |
| **Eddy Current** | τ ∝ I_excitation² × N | P = 2πNτ / 60 W | Controlled by current |
| **Epicyclic (Sun-Planet)** | τ = W × L | P = 2πN_s × (W × L) / 60 W | Torque scale amplified by gear ratio |
| **Belt Transmission** | τ = (T₁ − T₂) × R | P = 2πN(T₁ − T₂)R / 60 W | Tensions in N, R in m |
| **Torsion** | τ = G·J·θ / L | P = τ · ω | Strain gauge/optical read-out |

### 2.7 Conversion Factors (Frequently Needed!)

| **Quantity** | **Conversion** |
|---|---|
| 1 horsepower (hp) | **745.7 W** (mechanical) |
| 1 metric hp (PS) | **735.5 W** |
| 1 kgf·m | **9.81 N·m** |
| Torque in N·m → kgf·m | Divide by **9.81** |
| N (rpm) → ω (rad/s) | ω = **2πN/60** |
| Torque (T) to BHP | BHP = **2πNT / 60 × 745.7⁻¹** if N in rpm and T in N·m gives W; divide by 745.7 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Use-Case:**
> In automotive R&D centres (e.g., ARAI Pune, NATRIP), an **engine dynamometer test bed** uses an **eddy current dynamometer** to plot the full BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) map and torque curve of a vehicle engine from 1000 rpm to 7000 rpm. The data is used for ECU calibration, BS-VI emission certification, and homologation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Prony Brake Torque & Power

**Setup:** Consider a Prony brake clamped on a pulley of effective radius **R**. A lever arm of length **L** rests on a weighing scale.

#### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Free Body Analysis of the Lever Arm
When the brake is *not* engaged, the scale reads **W₀** (self-weight reaction).
When the brake is *engaged*, friction force **F** acts tangentially on the pulley, creating a torque **F·R**. By Newton's third law, an equal and opposite reaction torque **F·R** acts on the brake block, which is rigidly attached to the lever arm.

This reaction torque tries to rotate the lever arm. The scale pushes up with force **W₁** to maintain equilibrium.

#### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Moment Equilibrium about Pivot

Taking moments about the pivot of the lever arm (located on the central shaft axis):

$$\text{Counter-clockwise moment} = \text{Clockwise moment}$$

$$F \cdot R = (W_1 - W_0) \cdot L$$

#### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Solve for Friction Force

$$F = \frac{(W_1 - W_0) \cdot L}{R}$$

#### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Braking Torque
The braking torque acts on the pulley:

$$\boxed{\tau = F \cdot R = (W_1 - W_0) \cdot L}$$

This derivation **explicitly shows** that the radius **R** of the pulley cancels out — only the lever arm length **L** and the scale reading difference matter.

#### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Shaft Power
Power absorbed by the brake equals torque × angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad [\text{rad/s}]$$

$$\boxed{P = \tau \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N (W_1 - W_0) L}{60} \text{ watts}}$$

If load is in **kgf** and arm in **metres**, multiply by **9.81** to get watts.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Epicyclic (Sun-Planet) Dynamometer Torque Amplification

**Setup:** Sun gear with **T_s** teeth is keyed to the input shaft. Planet gear with **T_p** teeth meshes externally with the sun. Planet is mounted on a cradle (lever arm) with effective length **L**. A scale reads force **W** on the cradle.

#### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Force on Sun and Planet Teeth
Let the tangential force at the pitch circle be **F**. The sun experiences torque:

$$T_s = F \cdot r_s$$

where **r_s** is the pitch radius of the sun.

The planet experiences an equal and opposite tangential force **F** at its pitch circle (Newton's third law for meshing gears):

$$T_p = F \cdot r_p$$

Since both gears have the same pitch (module), the ratio of radii equals the ratio of teeth:

$$\frac{r_s}{r_p} = \frac{T_s}{T_p}$$

#### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Reaction Torque on Cradle
The reaction force **F** acts on the planet gear axis, but the planet is free to rotate about the cradle pin. However, the cradle as a whole experiences a moment equal to **T_p** trying to rotate it about the input shaft axis.

So the cradle sees torque **T_cradle = T_p**.

But in equilibrium, the tangential force at the sun-planet mesh can be expressed in terms of cradle torque:

#### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Moment Balance on Cradle
The cradle is held stationary by the scale. Sum of moments about the input shaft axis = 0:

$$T_s \cdot \frac{T_p}{T_s} = W \cdot L$$

Wait — this needs careful re-derivation. Let's redo it cleanly.

#### 3.2.4 Clean Re-Derivation (KTU Standard Approach)

The **input torque** on the sun gear is:

$$T_s = F \cdot r_s$$

By gear ratio analysis, the **planetary carrier (cradle)** experiences a reaction torque that is amplified. When the sun rotates once and drives the planet, the cradle (if free) would rotate. The torque balance for the cradle (held stationary by scale) gives:

**General result:**

$$T_{input} = W \cdot L$$

**Output torque** (on the output shaft, connected to a gear coaxial with the sun but driven by the planet):

$$T_{output} = T_{input} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{T_s}{T_p}\right)$$

**Output speed:**

$$N_{output} = N_{input} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{T_s}{T_p}\right)$$

This **conservation of power** (T·N constant) confirms the result:
