$$T_{input} \cdot N_{input} = T_{output} \cdot N_{output}$$

#### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Why This Matters
The scale force **W** directly measures the input torque, while the output torque is amplified. This makes the device excellent for measuring **small torques accurately**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Torsion Dynamometer Strain-Gauge Bridge Output

For a shaft of diameter **d** and gauge length **L** under torque **τ**, shear strain at the surface:

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau \cdot r}{J \cdot G} = \frac{16 \tau}{\pi d^3 G}$$

A strain gauge bonded at 45° to the shaft axis experiences axial strain:

$$\epsilon = \frac{\gamma}{2} = \frac{8 \tau}{\pi d^3 G}$$

Bridge output voltage (with excitation **V_ex** and gauge factor **G_f**):

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot \epsilon = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot 8 \tau}{4 \pi d^3 G}$$

Solving for torque:

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{\pi d^3 G \cdot V_{out}}{2 V_{ex} G_f}}$$

This is the basis of modern **digital shaft power analysers** used in wind turbine test beds.

---

### 3.4 Solved Numerical — KTU Board Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2022, Model Question]**
> A Prony brake is applied to a pulley of effective radius 200 mm attached to a motor shaft running at 1440 rpm. The lever arm length is 500 mm, and the scale reads 60 N when the brake is engaged and 10 N when disengaged. Calculate the braking torque and the shaft power in kW.

#### Solution:

**Given:**
- R = 200 mm = 0.2 m
- L = 500 mm = 0.5 m
- N = 1440 rpm
- W₁ = 60 N, W₀ = 10 N

**Step 1 — Net effective force:**
$$W_1 - W_0 = 60 - 10 = 50 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Braking torque:**
$$\tau = (W_1 - W_0) \cdot L = 50 \times 0.5 = 25 \text{ N·m}$$

(Note: Notice R does not appear in this formula.)

**Step 3 — Angular velocity:**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1440}{60} = 48\pi \approx 150.796 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Shaft power:**
$$P = \tau \cdot \omega = 25 \times 150.796 = 3769.9 \text{ W} \approx 3.77 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall 1:** Many students incorrectly include the pulley radius **R** in the torque formula for a Prony brake — they write τ = F·R. This is wrong because **R cancels out** as shown in derivation 3.1.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall 2:** Forgetting to convert **rpm → rad/s**. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if you write ω = N directly without conversion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dynamometer Test Bed

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENG["Prime Mover / Engine Under Test"]
        ENG1[Engine / Motor]
        ENG2[Output Shaft rotating at N rpm]
        ENG1 --> ENG2
    end

    subgraph COUP["Coupling Section"]
        C1[Torsion Coupling / Shaft]
        ENG2 --> C1
    end

    subgraph DYNO["Dynamometer Unit"]
        D1{Rotor Element}
        D2[Absorption / Transmission Mechanism]
        D3[Torque Sensor]
        D4[Speed Sensor Tachogenerator]
        D1 --> D2
        D2 --> D3
        D1 --> D4
    end

    C1 --> D1

    subgraph READ["Readout & Control"]
        R1[Load Cell / Scale W]
        R2[Strain Gauge Bridge]
        R3[Tachometer Display]
        R4[Data Acquisition DAQ]
        R5[Power Computer P = 2πNT/60]
        D3 --> R2 --> R4
        D4 --> R3 --> R4
        R1 --> R4
        R4 --> R5
    end

    subgraph COOL["Cooling Subsystem"]
        K1[Water / Oil / Air Cooling]
        K2[Heat Exchanger]
        K1 --> K2
        D2 -.Heat dissipated.-> K1
    end

    style ENG fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style DYNO fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px
    style READ fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
    style COOL fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px
    style R5 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Prony Brake Dynamometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Shaft rotates] --> B[Pulley Spins at N rpm]
    B --> C[Friction F at contact surface]
    C --> D[Frictional Torque FxR opposes rotation]
    D --> E[Reaction torque on Brake Block]
    E --> F[Lever arm tries to lift]
    F --> G[Scale reads W1 minus W0]
    G --> H[Effective force Fscale]
    H --> I[Torque tau = Fscale x L]
    I --> J[Power P = 2piNtau / 60]
    J --> K[Heat dissipated to atmosphere]
    style A fill:#FFD700
    style D fill:#FF6347
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Epicyclic (Sun-Planet) Dynamometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Input Shaft] --> S2[Sun Gear T_s teeth]
    S2 --> S3{Force F at mesh}
    S3 --> S4[Planet Gear T_p teeth]
    S4 --> S5[Planet Pin on Cradle]
    S5 --> S6[Reaction Torque on Cradle]
    S6 --> S7[Scale reads W at arm L]
    S7 --> S8[Input Torque T_in = W x L]
    S4 --> S9[Output Gear coaxial with sun]
    S9 --> S10[Output Shaft]
    S8 --> S11[Output Torque T_out = T_in x 1+T_s/T_p]
    S10 --> S12[Output Speed N_out = N_in x 1+T_s/T_p]
    S11 --> S13[Power P = T_in x omega_in = T_out x omega_out]
    style S1 fill:#FFD700
    style S8 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style S13 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Functional Flow — Eddy Current Dynamometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Engine Shaft] --> E2[Conductive Disc rotates]
    E2 --> E3[Disc cuts magnetic flux]
    E3 --> E4[Eddy currents induced]
    E4 --> E5[Lenzs Law opposing motion]
    E5 --> E6[Braking Torque on Disc]
    E6 --> E7[Stator rotates by reaction]
    E7 --> E8[Scale / Load Cell reads W]
    E8 --> E9[Torque tau proportional to I_excitation squared]
    E9 --> E10[Heat in Disc]
    E10 --> E11[Water Cooling Jacket]
    style E1 fill:#FFD700
    style E6 fill:#FF6347
    style E11 fill:#B0E0E6
```

### 4.5 Comparative Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Need to Measure Shaft Power?] --> Q1{Power to Absorb?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Application Type?}
    Q1 -->|No - just measure| TRANSMISSION[Transmission Type]
    Q2 -->|Small engine| PRONY[Prony Brake]
    Q2 -->|Medium - test bed| ROPE[Rope Brake]
    Q2 -->|Large - marine| HYD[Hydraulic]
    Q2 -->|Automotive R&D| EDDY[Eddy Current]
    Q2 -->|Smooth control needed| EDDY
    TRANSMISSION --> Q3{Range of Torque?}
    Q3 -->|Small torque accurate| EPI[Epicyclic Sun-Planet]
    Q3 -->|In-line continuous| BELT[Belt Transmission]
    Q3 -->|Strain measurement| TORSION[Torsion Shaft Strain Gauge]
    style START fill:#FFD700
    style EDDY fill:#90EE90
    style EPI fill:#87CEEB
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a dynamometer. List the two main categories of dynamometers with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
A dynamometer is a device used for measuring the torque and/or power transmitted by a rotating shaft.

Two main categories:
1. **Absorption dynamometer** — absorbs the mechanical power delivered by the source (e.g., Prony brake, rope brake, hydraulic, eddy current).
2. **Transmission dynamometer** — measures torque in a transmission line without absorbing the power (e.g., epicyclic train, belt-type, torsion dynamometer).

*(Word limit: 80–100 words. Award 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Explain why an eddy current dynamometer is preferred over a Prony brake for modern engine testing.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **No physical contact** → no wear of friction surfaces → long life, low maintenance.
2. **Stepless torque control** via excitation current → easy plotting of torque-speed curves.
3. **High accuracy and reproducibility** of readings.
4. **Easy absorption of large powers** since heat is dissipated through water-cooled disc.
5. **Suitable for automation** — can be interfaced directly with data acquisition systems.
6. **Lower operating cost** compared to frequent replacement of Prony brake blocks.

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Prony Brake + Eddy Current Combination

**[KTU University Exam — May 2019 / Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a Prony brake dynamometer. Derive the expression for braking torque and shaft power. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rope brake dynamometer has a rope wound 1.5 times around a brake drum of 600 mm diameter. The dead weight on one side is 400 N and the spring balance on the other reads 100 N. If the shaft rotates at 240 rpm, calculate: (i) Net braking force, (ii) Braking torque, (iii) Shaft power absorbed in kW, (iv) Frictional force on the drum surface, assuming uniform pressure. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Prony Brake — Construction & Working:**

The Prony brake consists of two rigid blocks (wooden or cast iron) hinged together at one end and clamped around a pulley by a tightening screw. The upper block carries a horizontal lever arm resting on a platform scale (spring balance).

*Working:* When the engine rotates the pulley, friction between the block and pulley creates a tangential force **F**. This generates a braking torque on the pulley and an equal & opposite reaction torque on the block. The reaction torque is balanced by the scale force on the lever arm.

**Derivation:**
Taking moments about the pivot:
$$\tau = (W_1 - W_0) \cdot L \quad \text{[3 marks for derivation]}$$

Shaft power:
$$P = \frac{2\pi N (W_1 - W_0) L}{60} \text{ watts} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

Sketch and explanation: **(2 marks)**

---

**(b) Rope Brake Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Number of turns, n = 1.5
- Drum diameter D = 600 mm → R = 0.3 m
- Dead weight W = 400 N
- Spring balance S = 100 N
- N = 240 rpm

**(i) Net braking force:**
$$F_{net} = W - S = 400 - 100 = \boxed{300 \text{ N}} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**(ii) Braking torque:**
For uniform pressure, the effective radius is the drum radius plus half the rope thickness (negligible here) → R_eff = R = 0.3 m
$$\tau = F_{net} \times R = 300 \times 0.3 = \boxed{90 \text{ N·m}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iii) Shaft power:**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 8\pi = 25.133 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$P = \tau \cdot \omega = 90 \times 25.133 = 2261.9 \text{ W} = \boxed{2.262 \text{ kW}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iv) Frictional force per unit arc length:**
Total angle of wrap θ = 1.5 × 2π = 3π rad = 540°
Arc length L = R × θ = 0.3 × 3π = 0.9π ≈ 2.827 m

Assuming uniform pressure distribution (capstan / Euler's belt friction NOT considered here for uniform pressure case):
$$f = \frac{F_{net}}{L} = \frac{300}{2.827} = \boxed{106.1 \text{ N/m}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Epicyclic Dynamometer Deep Dive

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of an epicyratic (sun and planet) train dynamometer. Show that the input torque is given by T = W × L. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An epicyclic train dynamometer has a sun gear of 30 teeth and a planet gear of 20 teeth. The cradle arm is 400 mm long and the scale reads 200 N when the input shaft rotates at 900 rpm. Calculate: (i) Input torque, (ii) Output torque, (iii) Output speed, (iv) Power transmitted. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Epicyclic Sun-Planet Dynamometer:**

**Construction:** It consists of a sun gear keyed to the input shaft (T_s teeth). A planet gear meshes externally with the sun and is mounted on a pin attached to a cradle (lever arm) which can rotate freely about the input shaft axis. The planet gear drives a coaxial output gear on the output shaft via a pinion. The cradle is held stationary by a scale at distance **L**. **[2 marks]**

**Working:** The torque on the sun gear **T_s** causes a tangential force at the mesh. This force creates an equal and opposite torque on the planet, which the cradle experiences as a reaction. The scale reads **W**, balancing the cradle.

**Derivation:**
Let F = tangential force at mesh, r_s = sun pitch radius, r_p = planet pitch radius.

Input torque:
$$T_{input} = F \cdot r_s$$

For the cradle (held stationary by scale), sum of moments = 0:
$$F \cdot r_s \cdot \frac{r_p}{r_s} = W \cdot L$$

Since the gear ratio r_p/r_s = T_p/T_s, and reaction torque on cradle = F·r_p, the scale force W satisfies:

$$T_{input} = W \cdot L \quad \text{[3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for working explanation]}$$

---

**(b) Epicyclic Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- T_s = 30, T_p = 20
- L = 400 mm = 0.4 m
- W = 200 N
- N_in = 900 rpm

**(i) Input torque:**
$$T_{input} = W \cdot L = 200 \times 0.4 = \boxed{80 \text{ N·m}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(ii) Output torque:**
Gear ratio of amplification = 1 + T_s/T_p = 1 + 30/20 = 1 + 1.5 = 2.5
$$T_{output} = T_{input} \times \left(1 + \frac{T_s}{T_p}\right) = 80 \times 2.5 = \boxed{200 \text{ N·m}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iii) Output speed:**
By power conservation (T·N constant):
$$N_{out} = N_{in} \times \frac{T_{input}}{T_{output}} = 900 \times \frac{80}{200} = 900 \times 0.4 = \boxed{360 \text{ rpm}} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Alternatively: N_out = N_in / (1 + T_s/T_p) = 900 / 2.5 = 360 rpm ✓

**(iv) Power transmitted:**
$$\omega_{in} = \frac{2\pi \times 900}{60} = 94.248 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$P = T_{input} \times \omega_{in} = 80 \times 94.248 = 7539.8 \text{ W} = \boxed{7.54 \text{ kW}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in Module 3:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm → rad/s** (loses 1 mark per numerical).
> 2. **Mixing up kgf and N** — never write "kgf·m" in SI-based answers without converting.
> 3. **Prony brake formula τ = (W₁ − W₀)L does NOT depend on pulley radius R** — students erroneously include R and lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Epicyclic dynamometer torque amplification factor (1 + T_s/T_p)** is often written backwards as (1 + T_p/T_s) — check the direction of sun/planet ratio.
> 5. **Not drawing the diagram** in long-answer questions — typically 2 marks are reserved for a labelled sketch in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 6. **Hydraulic dynamometer confusion** — students write water pressure formulas instead of the standard τ = (W − S)L formula used uniformly.
> 7. **In rope brake**, the *effective* radius is **pulley R + rope thickness/2**, not just R — KTU examiners may ask this in tricky follow-up questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist for Torque & Shaft Power Measurement:**

- ☐ **Torque** = Force × perpendicular distance (units: N·m). For rotating shafts: τ = F·r where r is pulley radius.
- ☐ **Shaft Power** = 2πNT/60 watts (when N in rpm, T in N·m). Or P = τω.
- ☐ **Prony Brake** formula: τ = (W₁ − W₀)L — independent of pulley radius.
- ☐ **Rope Brake** formula: τ = (W − S)R — radius of drum **does** matter here.
- ☐ **Hydraulic Dynamometer**: Water as working fluid; rotor drags water against stator.
- ☐ **Eddy Current Dynamometer**: Magnetic field + rotating disc → Lenz's law braking. Excitation current controls torque.
- ☐ **Epicyclic Dynamometer**: Sun-planet gear with cradle. T_in = W·L; output torque **amplified** by (1 + T_s/T_p); output speed **reduced** by same factor.
- ☐ **Belt Transmission**: τ = (T₁ − T₂)R; used for low-speed, large-horsepower applications.
- ☐ **Torsion Dynamometer**: τ = GJθ/L; modern variants use strain gauges for digital readout.
- ☐ **Transmission dynos** measure torque without absorbing power (useful for in-line monitoring).
- ☐ **Absorption dynos** convert mechanical energy into heat (Prony → friction; Hydraulic → fluid shear; Eddy → eddy current heat).
- ☐ **Cooling** is critical for all absorption dynos — otherwise friction/shearing surfaces overheat.
- ☐ **1 hp = 745.7 W**; **1 kgf·m = 9.81 N·m** — know these conversions by heart.
- ☐ **Industrial application**: Engine test beds (automotive), turbine acceptance testing (power plants), motor testing (manufacturing QA).

> **🎯 Examiner's Mantra:** Always state the formula first, show the unit conversion next, and finish with a boxed final answer. This 3-step structure guarantees you don't lose marks for "method" even if you make an arithmetic slip in the final step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Method of Force Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Method of Force Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Force Measurement** is defined as the quantitative determination of the magnitude, direction, and nature of a mechanical force (tension, compression, shear, or torque) acting on a body, using standardized instruments and techniques that convert the applied force into a measurable physical quantity such as displacement, strain, pressure, or electrical signal.

In instrumentation engineering, force measurement forms the cornerstone of **metrology**, **process control**, **materials testing**, and **structural health monitoring**. The **SI unit of force** is the **Newton (N)**, where $1\ \text{N}$ is defined as the force required to accelerate a mass of $1\ \text{kg}$ at $1\ \text{m/s}^2$.

### Fundamental Categories of Force

| Force Category | Description | Typical Range (Engineering) |
|---|---|---|
| **Tensile Force** | Pulling/stretching action | $10^{-1}\ \text{N}$ to $10^{7}\ \text{N}$ |
| **Compressive Force** | Pushing/squeezing action | $10^{-1}\ \text{N}$ to $10^{7}\ \text{N}$ |
| **Shear Force** | Tangential sliding action | $1\ \text{N}$ to $10^{6}\ \text{N}$ |
| **Torsional Force** | Rotational/twisting action | $10^{-2}\ \text{N·m}$ to $10^{5}\ \text{N·m}$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Spring Scale Story

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Check:** Imagine you are at a vegetable market. The vendor hangs a bag of potatoes on a hanging spring scale. As the weight (a force due to gravity) pulls the spring downward, the spring stretches. The amount of stretch is directly proportional to the weight. The vendor reads the displacement on a calibrated scale. **This is the most fundamental principle of force measurement** — converting force into a measurable deformation or displacement of an elastic element.

In professional force measurement:
- The **spring** is replaced by a precisely machined **elastic member** (load cell, proving ring, or diaphragm).
- The **displacement** is measured using **strain gauges**, **LVDTs**, **piezoelectric crystals**, or **hydraulic pressure**.
- The **calibrated reading** gives the force value with high accuracy.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Force Measurement

Every force measuring system, regardless of complexity, is built upon three fundamental principles:

1. **Force Balance Principle** — Comparing an unknown force against a known reference force (gravity, hydraulic pressure, calibrated weights).
2. **Elastic Deformation Principle** — Using **Hooke's Law** ($F = k \cdot x$) where force produces measurable displacement in an elastic body.
3. **Force-to-Electrical Transduction** — Converting mechanical force into proportional electrical signals using sensors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Module 3 syllabus explicitly requires students to understand *basic methods* including elastic force measuring devices, load cells (hydraulic, pneumatic, strain gauge, piezoelectric), torque measurement on rotating shafts, and dynamometers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Principle: Hooke's Law Foundation

The entire domain of elastic force measurement rests on **Robert Hooke's Law (1660)**:

> **Within the elastic limit, the stress developed in a body is directly proportional to the strain produced.**

Mathematically expressed as:

$$F = k \cdot x$$

Where:
- $F$ = Applied force (in **N**)
- $k$ = Spring constant / stiffness of the elastic element (in **N/m**)
- $x$ = Displacement / deflection (in **m**)

### Step-by-Step Logical Framework

1. **Step 1 — Force Application:** An unknown force $F$ is applied to an elastic transducer element.
2. **Step 2 — Elastic Deformation:** The element deforms proportionally by amount $x$.
3. **Step 3 — Sensing:** A transducer converts this deformation into a measurable signal.
4. **Step 4 — Calibration:** The output signal is calibrated against known forces to produce the final reading.
5. **Step 5 — Display:** The force value is displayed as a digital/analog output.

## 2.2 Classification of Basic Methods of Force Measurement

### A. Direct Methods (Comparison Methods)

In direct methods, the unknown force is **directly balanced** against a known standard force.

#### (i) Dead Weight Tester / Hydraulic Force Standard

- A known mass $m$ is placed on a piston of known cross-sectional area $A$.
- Hydraulic fluid transmits pressure to the test piston.
- The reference force is: $F = m \cdot g$, where $g = 9.80665\ \text{m/s}^2$.

#### (ii) Proving Ring

- A high-grade steel ring with a calibrated deflection characteristic.
- The applied force deflects the ring by a measurable amount.
- Force-deflection relationship: $F = k \cdot \delta$, where $\delta$ is the diametrical deflection.

### B. Indirect Methods (Elastic Transducer Methods)

The unknown force produces an elastic deformation that is measured using a secondary transducer.

#### (i) Hydraulic Load Cell
- Force applied to a piston creates fluid pressure.
- Pressure measured by a Bourdon tube or pressure gauge.
- $F = P \cdot A$, where $P$ is pressure and $A$ is piston area.

#### (ii) Pneumatic Load Cell
- Similar to hydraulic, but uses air/gas.
- Lower force ranges, suitable for delicate measurements.

#### (iii) Strain Gauge Load Cell
- Strain gauges bonded to an elastic element.
- Resistance change $\Delta R$ measured using Wheatstone bridge.
- Gauge factor: $G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$

#### (iv) Piezoelectric Load Cell
- Certain crystals (quartz, PZT) generate charge when stressed.
- Charge output: $Q = d \cdot F$, where $d$ is the piezoelectric charge constant.

#### (v) Capacitive & Inductive Methods
- Force changes capacitance/inductance of a sensing element.
- Measured using AC bridge circuits.

### C. Force Balance Methods (Gravitational Methods)

- Unknown force is balanced against the gravitational force of known masses.
- Used in **analytical balances** and **force calibration laboratories**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for KTU examinations on this topic. Memorize every entry.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = k \cdot x$ | Hooke's Law (Linear Elastic) | N | Elastic force transducers |
| 2 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Hydraulic/Pneumatic Force | N | Hydraulic & Pneumatic load cells |
| 3 | $F = m \cdot g$ | Gravitational Force | N | Dead weight testers |
| 4 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Mechanical Stress | $\text{N/m}^2$ (Pa) | Stress analysis in load cells |
| 5 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ | Strain (Dimensionless) | — | Strain gauge principle |
| 6 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Young's Modulus Relation | Pa | Elastic deformation |
| 7 | $G_F = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | Gauge Factor | — | Strain gauge calibration |
| 8 | $Q = d \cdot F$ | Piezoelectric Charge Equation | C | Piezoelectric sensors |
| 9 | $\delta = \dfrac{F \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | Cantilever Beam Deflection | m | Beam-type load cells |
| 10 | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ | Wheatstone Bridge Output | V | Strain gauge circuits |
| 11 | $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ | Torsional Shear Stress | Pa | Torque measurement |
| 12 | $\theta = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$ | Angle of Twist | rad | Shaft torque measurement |

**Boundary Conditions & Key Constants:**
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.80665\ \text{m/s}^2$ (standard)
- Young's Modulus of Steel: $E \approx 200\ \text{GPa}$
- Young's Modulus of Aluminum: $E \approx 70\ \text{GPa}$
- Gauge factor of typical foil strain gauge: $G_F \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$
- Piezoelectric constant of quartz: $d_{11} \approx 2.3\ \text{pC/N}$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Force Measurement Application | Instrument Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Engine thrust, wing load testing | Strain gauge load cells |
| **Automotive** | Crash testing, brake force | Piezoelectric & strain gauge |
| **Civil Engineering** | Cable tension, pile load testing | Hydraulic load cells, proving rings |
| **Manufacturing** | Press force, rolling mill tension | Hydraulic dynamometers |
| **Medical** | Prosthetic testing, infusion pump force | Miniature strain gauge sensors |
| **Robotics** | Gripper force feedback | Tactile piezoelectric sensors |

## 2.5 Selection Criteria for a Force Measuring Device

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common 7-mark question asks to compare methods or justify selection. Use the criteria below.

1. **Magnitude of force** (N to MN range)
2. **Accuracy required** (±0.1% to ±5%)
3. **Dynamic vs. Static measurement** (frequency response)
4. **Environmental conditions** (temperature, humidity, vibration)
5. **Output signal type** (analog, digital, pneumatic)
6. **Calibration traceability** (NIST, NPL standards)
7. **Size and mounting constraints**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydraulic Load Cell — Force from Pressure

### Problem Setup
A hydraulic load cell has a piston of diameter $D = 50\ \text{mm}$. A force $F$ is applied to the piston, creating pressure in the hydraulic fluid, which is read by a Bourdon gauge as $P = 4\ \text{MPa}$. Find the applied force.

### Given
- Piston diameter: $D = 50\ \text{mm} = 0.05\ \text{m}$
- Measured pressure: $P = 4\ \text{MPa} = 4 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa}$
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.80665\ \text{m/s}^2$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the cross-sectional area of the piston.

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.05)^2}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.0025}{4}$$

$$A = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the fundamental relationship between force and pressure.

$$F = P \cdot A$$

$$F = (4 \times 10^6) \cdot (1.9635 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F = 7853.98\ \text{N}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to more practical engineering units (kN).

$$F \approx 7.854\ \text{kN}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Pressure is force per unit area, so multiplying pressure by the effective piston area yields the total force exerted on the piston. This force equals the unknown applied force by **Newton's Third Law** of equilibrium.

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{F \approx 7.854\ \text{kN}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Strain Gauge Load Cell — Bridge Output Voltage

### Problem Setup
A strain gauge load cell uses a Wheatstone bridge with four active strain gauges (each with $G_F = 2.1$, $R = 350\ \Omega$). The excitation voltage is $V_{ex} = 10\ \text{V}$. If the strain experienced by the gauges is $\varepsilon = 1000\ \mu\varepsilon$ (microstrain), calculate the output voltage of the bridge.

### Given
- Gauge factor: $G_F = 2.1$
- Gauge resistance: $R = 350\ \Omega$
- Excitation voltage: $V_{ex} = 10\ \text{V}$
- Strain: $\varepsilon = 1000\ \mu\varepsilon = 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3}$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the fractional change in resistance.

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = 2.1 \cdot 10^{-3}$$

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = 2.1 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2:** For a full-bridge configuration (4 active gauges), the output voltage formula is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

$$V_{out} = 10 \cdot (2.1 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$V_{out} = 21 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to millivolts for practical reading.

$$V_{out} = 21\ \text{mV}$$

**Conversion Logic:** In a full Wheatstone bridge with four active gauges (two in tension, two in compression), the output is amplified by a factor of $V_{ex}$. Each gauge experiences the same strain but with opposite signs, leading to additive bridge output.

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{V_{out} = 21\ \text{mV}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Proving Ring — Deflection to Force Conversion

### Problem Setup
A proving ring has a stiffness constant $k = 1.2 \times 10^{6}\ \text{N/m}$. A diametrical deflection of $\delta = 0.085\ \text{mm}$ is observed when an unknown force is applied. Determine the applied force.

### Given
- Stiffness constant: $k = 1.2 \times 10^{6}\ \text{N/m}$
- Diametrical deflection: $\delta = 0.085\ \text{mm} = 0.085 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} = 8.5 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply Hooke's Law for the elastic deformation of the proving ring.

$$F = k \cdot \delta$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$F = (1.2 \times 10^{6}) \cdot (8.5 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$F = 1.2 \times 8.5 \times 10^{6-5}$$

**Step 3:** Perform the multiplication.

$$F = 10.2 \times 10^{1}$$

$$F = 102\ \text{N}$$

**Conversion Logic:** A proving ring acts as a precision-calibrated elastic spring. Its stiffness $k$ is determined during manufacture through calibration against dead-weight standards. Therefore, any measured deflection directly converts to force using the linear Hooke's relationship.

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{F = 102\ \text{N}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Dead Weight Tester — Calibration Force Generation

### Problem Setup
A dead weight tester uses a piston of effective area $A = 80\ \text{mm}^2$. What total mass (including the piston weight) must be loaded to generate a calibration force equivalent to $F = 500\ \text{N}$?

### Given
- Required force: $F = 500\ \text{N}$
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the gravitational force equation.

$$F = m \cdot g$$

**Step 2:** Solve for mass $m$.

$$m = \frac{F}{g}$$

$$m = \frac{500}{9.81}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the numerical value.

$$m = 50.968\ \text{kg}$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{m \approx 50.97\ \text{kg}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Cantilever Beam Load Cell — Deflection Formula

### Problem Setup
A cantilever-type load cell has the following specifications:
- Length: $L = 100\ \text{mm} = 0.1\ \text{m}$
- Young's Modulus: $E = 200\ \text{GPa} = 200 \times 10^{9}\ \text{Pa}$
- Moment of Inertia: $I = 5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^4$
- Applied force at free end: $F = 200\ \text{N}$

Calculate the deflection at the free end.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the standard cantilever beam deflection formula for a point load at the free end.

$$\delta = \frac{F \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$\delta = \frac{200 \cdot (0.1)^3}{3 \cdot (200 \times 10^{9}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-9})}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator.

$$\text{Numerator} = 200 \cdot 0.001 = 0.2\ \text{N·m}^3$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator.

$$\text{Denominator} = 3 \cdot 200 \times 10^{9} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 3 \cdot 1000 = 3000$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the deflection.

$$\delta = \frac{0.2}{3000}$$

$$\delta = 6.667 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$$

$$\delta = 66.67\ \mu\text{m}$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{\delta \approx 66.67\ \mu\text{m}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Force Measuring Methods

| Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Strain Gauge | Piezoelectric | Proving Ring |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | $F = P \cdot A$ | $F = P \cdot A$ | $F \to \varepsilon \to \Delta R$ | $F \to Q$ (charge) | $F = k \cdot \delta$ |
| **Range** | High (kN–MN) | Low–Medium (N–kN) | Wide (N–MN) | Dynamic only (N–kN) | Medium (kN) |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.1% | ±0.5% | ±0.05% | ±1% | ±0.2% |
| **Frequency Response** | Low (static) | Low | Medium (kHz) | Very High (MHz) | Very Low |
| **Output Signal** | Pressure | Pressure | Voltage (mV/V) | Charge (pC) | Displacement |
| **Cost** | Medium | Low | Medium | High | High |
| **Best Application** | Heavy industrial | Low-force precision | Universal (industry standard) | Dynamic shock/vibration | Calibration labs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Generic Force Measurement System

The following block diagram illustrates the standard signal flow in **any** force measurement instrument, from physical input to final display.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["UNKNOWN FORCE INPUT"] --> B["ELASTIC TRANSDUCER ELEMENT"]
    B --> C["MECHANICAL DEFORMATION"]
    C --> D["PRIMARY SENSING STAGE"]
    D --> E["SIGNAL CONDITIONING UNIT"]
    E --> F["AMPLIFIER AND FILTER"]
    F --> G["ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER"]
    G --> H["MICROPROCESSOR / DSP UNIT"]
    H --> I["CALIBRATION AND LINEARIZATION"]
    I --> J["DIGITAL DISPLAY OR DATA LOGGER"]
    J --> K["FINAL FORCE READING NEWTON"]

    subgraph S1["MECHANICAL DOMAIN"]
        A
        B
        C
    end

    subgraph S2["TRANSDUCTION DOMAIN"]
        D
        E
        F
    end

    subgraph S3["ELECTRONIC DOMAIN"]
        G
        H
        I
    end

    subgraph S4["OUTPUT DOMAIN"]
        J
        K
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This block diagram illustrates the universal signal chain in force measurement. The **Mechanical Domain** contains the physical input and elastic deformation. The **Transduction Domain** converts the deformation into an electrical signal. The **Electronic Domain** processes and linearizes the signal. The **Output Domain** displays the final calibrated force value.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Hydraulic Load Cell System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["APPLIED FORCE F NEWTON"] --> B["PISTON OF AREA A"]
    B --> C["HYDRAULIC FLUID PRESSURE P"]
    C --> D["BOURDON TUBE PRESSURE GAUGE"]
    D --> E["DIAL INDICATOR CALIBRATED IN NEWTON"]
    E --> F["FORCE READOUT"]

    G["CALIBRATION MASS M"] --> H["GRAVITATIONAL REFERENCE MG"]
    H --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In a hydraulic load cell, the applied force on the piston generates fluid pressure, which is read by a Bourdon tube gauge. The system is calibrated against known gravitational forces from standard masses to ensure traceability.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Strain Gauge Load Cell with Wheatstone Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["APPLIED FORCE F"] --> B["ELASTIC BEAM ELEMENT"]
    B --> C["STRAIN EPSILON AT GAUGE LOCATIONS"]
    C --> D["RESISTANCE CHANGE DELTA R"]
    D --> E["WHEATSTONE BRIDGE NETWORK"]
    F["EXCITATION VOLTAGE VEX"] --> E
    E --> G["DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUT VOUT"]
    G --> H["INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER"]
    H --> I["ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER"]
    I --> J["DIGITAL FORCE DISPLAY"]

    subgraph S1["FULL BRIDGE FOUR ACTIVE GAUGES"]
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph S2["SIGNAL PROCESSING CHAIN"]
        H
        I
        J
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This diagram shows the signal flow in a strain gauge-based load cell. The applied force produces strain in the elastic beam, which changes the resistance of bonded strain gauges. These gauges are connected in a Wheatstone bridge with an excitation voltage. The differential output voltage is proportional to the applied force and is processed through amplification and digitization.

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — Comparative Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FORCE MEASUREMENT REQUIREMENT"] --> B{"DYNAMIC OR STATIC?"}
    B -->|"STATIC"| C{"FORCE MAGNITUDE?"}
    B -->|"DYNAMIC"| D["PIEZOELECTRIC LOAD CELL"]

    C -->|"HIGH MN RANGE"| E["HYDRAULIC LOAD CELL"]
    C -->|"MEDIUM KN RANGE"| F["STRAIN GAUGE LOAD CELL"]
    C -->|"LOW N RANGE"| G["PNEUMATIC LOAD CELL"]

    E --> H["CALIBRATED FORCE OUTPUT"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    D --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This decision-tree diagram guides the selection of an appropriate force measurement method based on whether the application is static or dynamic and the magnitude of the force to be measured. Each path leads to a specific load cell technology that is optimally suited for those conditions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

Define the term **gauge factor** of a strain gauge and state its typical value for a metallic foil gauge.

**Model Answer:**

The **gauge factor** ($G_F$) of a strain gauge is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in electrical resistance ($\Delta R / R$) to the mechanical strain ($\varepsilon$) experienced by the gauge:

$$G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity. For typical **metallic foil strain gauges**, the gauge factor ranges from approximately **2.0 to 2.2**. A higher gauge factor indicates greater sensitivity of the strain gauge to mechanical deformation.

**[3 Marks: Definition (1) + Formula (1) + Typical value (1)]**

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

Distinguish between **static** and **dynamic** force measurement with **one example** each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Static Force Measurement | Dynamic Force Measurement |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Force measurement where the load is constant or slowly varying with time | Force measurement where the load changes rapidly with time |
| **Frequency Response** | Not critical | Must be high |
| **Example** | Weighing scale using strain gauge load cell | Impact hammer testing using piezoelectric load cell |
| **Sensor Type** | Strain gauge, hydraulic | Piezoelectric |

**[3 Marks: Static definition (1) + Dynamic definition (1) + Example (1)]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **hydraulic load cell** with a neat functional block diagram. Derive the relationship between applied force and output pressure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic load cell has a piston diameter of $40\ \text{mm}$. The Bourdon gauge reads a pressure of $5\ \text{MPa}$. Calculate the force being measured. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Working Principle of Hydraulic Load Cell:**

A hydraulic load cell converts an applied force into a proportional hydraulic pressure, which is then displayed on a calibrated pressure gauge.

**Functional Block Diagram:**

$$\text{Applied Force } F \rightarrow \text{Piston} \rightarrow \text{Hydraulic Fluid Pressure } P \rightarrow \text{Bourdon Gauge} \rightarrow \text{Force Reading}$$

**Step-by-Step Operation:**

1. The unknown force $F$ is applied on the piston of known effective area $A$.
2. The piston transmits this force to the hydraulic fluid (typically oil) enclosed in the cylinder.
3. By **Pascal's Law**, the pressure generated in the fluid is uniform in all directions:

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

4. This pressure acts on a **Bourdon tube pressure gauge**.
5. The Bourdon tube deflects proportionally to the pressure, and the gauge is **calibrated directly in force units (N or kN)**.

**Derivation:**

Consider a piston of cross-sectional area $A$ subjected to an applied force $F$. The pressure developed in the hydraulic fluid directly beneath the piston is:

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{F = P \cdot A}$$

Where $A$ is the effective piston area calculated as:

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$$

for a circular piston of diameter $D$.

**Advantages:**
- Self-contained, no external power required.
- High reliability in harsh environments.
- Suitable for very high force measurements (up to several MN).

**Disadvantages:**
- Limited frequency response (suitable mainly for static loads).
- Fluid leakage and temperature effects on accuracy.

**[7 Marks: Working principle (3) + Diagram (1) + Derivation (2) + Advantages/Disadvantages (1)]**

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Piston diameter: $D = 40\ \text{mm} = 0.04\ \text{m}$
- Measured pressure: $P = 5\ \text{MPa} = 5 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa}$

**Step 1:** Calculate the piston area.

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.04)^2}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.0016}{4}$$

$$A = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the force-pressure relationship.

$$F = P \cdot A$$

$$F = (5 \times 10^6) \cdot (1.2566 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F = 6283.19\ \text{N}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to kN.

$$F \approx 6.283\ \text{kN}$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{F \approx 6.283\ \text{kN}}$$

**[7 Marks: Area calculation (2) + Formula application (2) + Numerical computation (2) + Final answer with units (1)]**

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a **Wheatstone bridge** in a strain gauge-based load cell. Explain how the output voltage is related to the applied force. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A strain gauge load cell uses four active strain gauges ($G_F = 2.0$, $R = 350\ \Omega$) in a full bridge configuration with an excitation voltage of $V_{ex} = 12\ \text{V}$. If the output voltage is $V_{out} = 24\ \text{mV}$, determine the strain experienced by the gauges. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Wheatstone Bridge in Strain Gauge Load Cell:**

The **Wheatstone bridge** is the fundamental circuit used to detect the small resistance changes produced by strain gauges when subjected to mechanical strain.

**Circuit Description:**

A Wheatstone bridge consists of four resistive arms $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$ arranged in a diamond configuration. An **excitation voltage** $V_{ex}$ is applied across two opposite nodes (A and C), and the **output voltage** $V_{out}$ is measured across the other two nodes (B and D).

In a strain gauge load cell:
- All four arms contain **active strain gauges** (full bridge configuration).
- Two gauges are oriented along the direction of principal tensile strain.
- Two gauges are oriented along the direction of principal compressive strain.

**Bridge Output Equation:**

For a balanced bridge, $V_{out} = 0$. When strain is applied:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

For a full bridge with four active gauges (two in tension +, two in compression −):

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Relationship to Applied Force:**

The strain $\varepsilon$ in the elastic element is related to the applied force $F$ through the mechanical geometry of the load cell:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$

for an axially loaded member, where $A$ is the cross-sectional area and $E$ is Young's Modulus.

Therefore:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$

$$\boxed{F = \frac{V_{out} \cdot A \cdot E}{V_{ex} \cdot G_F}}$$

**Key Points:**
- The output voltage is **directly proportional** to the applied force.
- Higher excitation voltage increases sensitivity.
- Higher gauge factor increases sensitivity.

**[7 Marks: Circuit description (2) + Bridge equation (2) + Force relationship (2) + Key points (1)]**

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Gauge factor: $G_F = 2.0$
- Gauge resistance: $R = 350\ \Omega$ (not directly needed for strain calculation)
- Excitation voltage: $V_{ex} = 12\ \text{V}$
- Output voltage: $V_{out} = 24\ \text{mV} = 24 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}$

**Step 1:** Write the full-bridge output equation.

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 2:** Solve for strain $\varepsilon$.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ex} \cdot G_F}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{24 \times 10^{-3}}{12 \cdot 2.0}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{24 \times 10^{-3}}{24}$$

$$\varepsilon = 1 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4:** Express in microstrain for engineering convention.

$$\varepsilon = 1000\ \mu\varepsilon$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 1000\ \mu\varepsilon = 10^{-3}}$$

**[7 Marks: Formula (2) + Rearrangement (2) + Numerical substitution (2) + Final answer with units (1)]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Failing to convert $\text{mm}$ to $\text{m}$ and $\text{MPa}$ to $\text{Pa}$ before calculation. This single mistake costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing Gauge Factor with Strain:** Gauge factor $G_F$ is dimensionless, but strain $\varepsilon$ is also dimensionless — students often mistakenly multiply or divide by 10.
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert mV to V:** When substituting $V_{out}$ into equations, students often forget that $1\ \text{mV} = 10^{-3}\ \text{V}$.
> 4. **Missing the Factor of 4 in Full Bridge:** Some students use half-bridge formulas (factor of 2) when the question specifies a full bridge (factor of 4 for two active gauges in tension/compression).
> 5. **Not Drawing the Functional Diagram:** In 7-mark theory questions, **always include a labeled block diagram or circuit diagram**. Examiners explicitly allocate 1–2 marks for this.
> 6. **Writing Only Final Answers Without Steps:** KTU valuation requires step-by-step working. Skipping intermediate steps can cost up to **50% of the marks** for numerical problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This Before Every Exam**

### 🔑 Core Concepts to Master
- **Hooke's Law:** $F = k \cdot x$ is the foundation of all elastic force measurement.
- **Hydraulic Load Cell:** Uses $F = P \cdot A$ principle; best for heavy static loads.
- **Strain Gauge Load Cell:** Uses $\Delta R / R = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$; industry standard for general-purpose measurement.
- **Piezoelectric Load Cell:** Uses $Q = d \cdot F$; only for **dynamic** measurements (no static response).
- **Proving Ring:** Uses $F = k \cdot \delta$; used as a **calibration standard** in metrology labs.
- **Dead Weight Tester:** Uses $F = m \cdot g$; provides **primary standard** force traceability.

### 📐 Must-Know Numerical Conversions
- $1\ \text{kN} = 10^3\ \text{N}$
- $1\ \text{MN} = 10^6\ \text{N}$
- $1\ \text{MPa} = 10^6\ \text{Pa} = 1\ \text{N/mm}^2$
- $1\ \mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$ strain

### ⚡ Quick-Recall Method Selection Table
- **Static + Heavy** → Hydraulic Load Cell
- **Static + Medium** → Strain Gauge Load Cell
- **Static + Light + Precise** → Pneumatic Load Cell
- **Dynamic (any range)** → Piezoelectric Load Cell
- **Calibration Reference** → Proving Ring or Dead Weight Tester

### 🎯 KTU-Specific Exam Tips
1. Always state the **principle** before the **formula** in descriptive answers.
2. In numerical problems, **show all three steps**: Given → Formula → Calculation → Answer.
3. For 14-mark questions, **subdivide clearly into (a) and (b)** as instructed.
4. Draw **labeled diagrams** even if not explicitly asked — they guarantee 1–2 bonus marks.
5. Memorize the **gauge factor** of typical strain gauges ($G_F \approx 2.0$–$2.2$) and **Young's Modulus of steel** ($E \approx 200\ \text{GPa}$).

### 🧠 Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- Do not use **piezoelectric sensors for static measurements** — they have zero DC response.
- Do not confuse **stress** ($\sigma$, in Pa) with **strain** ($\varepsilon$, dimensionless).
- Do not forget that **Poisson's effect** causes transverse strain in strain gauge measurements.
- Always specify **units** in the final numerical answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note:** This topic carries **high weightage** in KTU 2024 OEMET725 examinations. Mastering the derivation of $F = P \cdot A$, $F = k \cdot \delta$, and $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ will secure the majority of marks in any Module 3 question on force measurement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elastic Force Transducers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Elastic Force Transducers ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Elastic Force Transducers** are devices that convert an applied **force** (input mechanical quantity) into a measurable **electrical signal** (output) by exploiting the **elastic deformation** of a sensing element. The transducer operates strictly within the **elastic limit** (Hooke's region) of the material so that the deformation is fully recoverable, linear, and proportional to the applied force. The general measurement chain is: $$\text{Applied Force} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Elastic Element (Deformation)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Displacement / Strain} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Signal Conditioning} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Output Voltage / Current}$$ The key relationship that governs all elastic force transducers is **Hooke's Law**: $$F = k \cdot x$$ where: - $F$ = Applied force (N) - $k$ = Spring constant / stiffness of the elastic element (N/m) - $x$ = Deflection / displacement (m) ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Think of a bathroom weighing scale.** When you stand on it, a metal spring or a flexure element inside gets slightly compressed or bent. That tiny mechanical deformation is read by a sensor (a strain gauge glued to the flexure), which then converts it into a digital reading. The scale works because the metal bends proportionally to your weight and returns to its original shape when you step off — this is the essence of an elastic force transducer. A second analogy: imagine pushing down on a **metallic ruler** clamped to the edge of a table. The ruler bends slightly. If you could measure that bend electronically (which strain gauges do), you'd have a force sensor. ### 1.3 Why Elastic Force Transducers Are Important - They are the **backbone of industrial weighing systems** (truck weighbridges, hopper scales, tank weighing). - They form the **primary element in load cells**. - They measure **force, weight, torque, and pressure** (when a pressure acts on a diaphragm). - They are used in **materials testing machines** (UTM), **rocket thrust stands**, and **process control**. ### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Material Properties > [!IMPORTANT] > The fundamental material property that determines how an elastic element behaves is the **Young's Modulus (E)**, measured in **GPa**. > > Standard reference values: > - Steel: $E \approx \mathbf{200 \; GPa}$ > - Aluminium: $E \approx \mathbf{70 \; GPa}$ > - Phosphor Bronze: $E \approx \mathbf{120 \; GPa}$ > - Constantan (strain gauge alloy): $E \approx \mathbf{162 \; GPa}$ > > The **Poisson's ratio ($\nu$)** for most metals is between **0.25 and 0.35**. For steel, $\nu \approx \mathbf{0.30}$. ### 1.5 Classification Snapshot Elastic force transducers are classified by the **geometry of the elastic element** and the **type of secondary transduction**: | Classification Basis | Categories | |---|---| | Geometry of Elastic Element | Cantilever beam, Simply-supported beam, Ring, Diaphragm, Proving ring, Helical spring | | Secondary Transducer | Strain gauge (bonded/unbonded), LVDT, Capacitive, Piezoelectric, Piezoresistive | | Output Form | Active (self-generating) vs Passive (requires excitation) | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operational Concept — Step-by-Step The working of an elastic force transducer can be broken into **four sequential stages**: 1. **Force Application Stage** — The unknown force $F$ is applied to the elastic element (beam, ring, diaphragm). 2. **Elastic Deformation Stage** — The element deforms by an amount $x$ (deflection) or $\varepsilon$ (strain), governed by Hooke's Law: $F = kx$ or $\sigma = E\varepsilon$. 3. **Secondary Transduction Stage** — A secondary sensor (most commonly a **strain gauge**) bonded to the elastic element converts the mechanical deformation into an electrical resistance change $\Delta R$. 4. **Signal Conditioning Stage** — The tiny resistance change is detected using a **Wheatstone bridge**, amplified, and displayed as a force reading. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why an elastic element?** — To provide a *predictable, linear, and reversible* mechanical response. - **Why a strain gauge?** — It is the most accurate, mature, and commercially available secondary transducer. - **Why a Wheatstone bridge?** — A single strain gauge gives a tiny $\Delta R$ (typically $\sim 0.1\%$); the bridge amplifies this and also provides **temperature compensation**. ### 2.3 Strain Gauge — The Heart of Modern Elastic Force Transducers A strain gauge is a thin metallic foil (or semiconductor) patterned in a grid. When stretched or compressed, its resistance changes as: $$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$ A change in geometry (length $L$ and area $A$) and piezoresistivity ($\rho$) gives: $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$$ where: - $G$ = **Gauge Factor** (dimensionless, typically $2.0$ for metallic, $100+$ for semiconductors) - $\varepsilon$ = Strain = $\frac{\Delta L}{L}$ (dimensionless, often expressed in microstrain $\mu\varepsilon$) ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU university exam numericals on this topic. Memorize the units and constraints. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F = k \cdot x$ | Hooke's Law (linear elastic) | N, N/m, m | | 2 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Young's Modulus relation | Pa, Pa, dimensionless | | 3 | $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Strain gauge fundamental equation | dimensionless | | 4 | $G = 1 + 2\nu$ | Gauge factor for metallic foil (approx.) | dimensionless | | 5 | $x_{cantilever} = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}$ | Tip deflection of cantilever beam | m | | 6 | $x_{ssb} = \frac{F L^{3}}{48 E I}$ | Centre deflection of simply supported beam | m | | 7 | $x_{ring} = \frac{1.09 \, F R^{3}}{E b t^{3}}$ | Proving ring diametrical deflection | m | | 8 | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{G \varepsilon}{4}$ (approx.) | Quarter-bridge output | V | | 9 | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{G \varepsilon}{2}$ (approx.) | Half-bridge output | V | | 10 | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \varepsilon$ | Full-bridge output | V | | 11 | $\varepsilon_{cantilever,max} = \frac{6 F L}{E b t^{2}}$ | Surface strain on cantilever (top/bottom fibre) | dimensionless | | 12 | $I = \frac{b t^{3}}{12}$ | Second moment of area (rectangular cross-section) | m$^{4}$ | **Where:** - $L$ = length of beam (m) - $b$ = width of beam (m) - $t$ = thickness of beam (m) - $R$ = mean radius of ring (m) - $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa) - $V_{ex}$ = Bridge excitation voltage (V) - $I$ = Area moment of inertia (m$^{4}$) ### 2.5 Practical Engineering Utility Elastic force transducers are used in: - **Load cells** for weighing systems (platform scales, truck weighbridges, tank weighing) - **Force testing machines** (Universal Testing Machines — UTM) - **Press-fit and torque monitoring** in assembly lines - **Aircraft weight and balance systems** - **Rocket engine thrust measurement** (load cells in test stands) - **Robotic end-effectors** and force feedback systems - **Civil engineering** for monitoring structural loads in bridges and buildings > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Hint:** The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically lists **strain gauge type force measurement** and **proving rings** as high-priority topics. Numerical problems almost always come from cantilever beams, simply-supported beams, or proving rings — focus on those geometries. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Strain Gauge Resistance Change to Voltage Output **Given:** A single strain gauge mounted on a beam, connected in a quarter Wheatstone bridge. **To Derive:** The output voltage $V_{out}$ in terms of strain $\varepsilon$ and excitation voltage $V_{ex}$. **Step 1 — Write the basic strain gauge equation.** The fractional change in resistance of a strain gauge is proportional to strain: $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$$ **Step 2 — Write the Wheatstone bridge output equation.** For a bridge with four arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ and excitation voltage $V_{ex}$: $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_4 + R_3} \right)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the strained gauge.** Let the gauge have unstrained resistance $R$ and strained resistance $R + \Delta R$. Then in a quarter-bridge configuration ($R_1 = R + \Delta R$, $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$): $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{R + \Delta R + R} - \frac{R}{R + R} \right)$$ $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right)$$ **Step 4 — Apply small-signal approximation.** Since $\Delta R \ll R$ (typically $\sim 0.1\%$ of $R$), we ignore $\Delta R$ in the denominator: $$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R} - \frac{1}{2} \right)$$ $$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\Delta R}{2R} - \frac{1}{2} \right)$$ $$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2R}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the strain gauge relation.** Using $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \varepsilon$: $$\boxed{V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon}{2} \quad \text{(Quarter-Bridge)}}$$ For a full bridge (all four arms active), the output is doubled again: $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon \quad \text{(Full-Bridge)}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cantilever Beam Tip Deflection **Given:** A cantilever beam of length $L$, rectangular cross-section $b \times t$, Young's modulus $E$, with a point load $F$ at the free tip. **To Derive:** The tip deflection $x$. **Step 1 — Bending moment at a section distance $u$ from the fixed end.** The bending moment at a section located at distance $u$ from the fixed end is due to the load $F$ acting at distance $(L - u)$: $$M(u) = F \cdot (L - u)$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Euler-Bernoulli beam equation.** $$\frac{d^{2}x}{du^{2}} = \frac{M(u)}{E I} = \frac{F(L - u)}{E I}$$ **Step 3 — Integrate once to get slope.** $$\frac{dx}{du} = \int \frac{F(L - u)}{E I} \, du = \frac{F}{EI} \left( L u - \frac{u^{2}}{2} \right) + C_1$$ Boundary condition: at fixed end ($u = 0$), slope = 0, so $C_1 = 0$. $$\frac{dx}{du} = \frac{F}{EI} \left( L u - \frac{u^{2}}{2} \right)$$ **Step 4 — Integrate again to get deflection.** $$x(u) = \frac{F}{EI} \left( \frac{L u^{2}}{2} - \frac{u^{3}}{6} \right) + C_2$$ Boundary condition: at fixed end ($u = 0$), deflection = 0, so $C_2 = 0$. **Step 5 — Evaluate at the free end ($u = L$).** $$x(L) = \frac{F}{EI} \left( \frac{L \cdot L^{2}}{2} - \frac{L^{3}}{6} \right)$$ $$x(L) = \frac{F L^{3}}{EI} \left( \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{6} \right) = \frac{F L^{3}}{EI} \cdot \frac{1}{3}$$ $$\boxed{x_{tip} = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}}$$ Substituting $I = \frac{b t^{3}}{12}$ for a rectangular cross-section: $$x_{tip} = \frac{4 F L^{3}}{E b t^{3}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Surface Strain in a Cantilever Beam The maximum bending stress at the surface of a beam is: $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$$ where $c = t/2$ is the distance from neutral axis to outer fibre, and $M = F \cdot L$ at the fixed end. $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{F L \cdot (t/2)}{I} = \frac{F L \cdot t}{2 I} = \frac{F L \cdot t}{2 \cdot (b t^{3}/12)} = \frac{6 F L}{b t^{2}}$$ Using Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E \varepsilon$): $$\boxed{\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{6 F L}{E b t^{2}}}$$ This is the strain that a strain gauge bonded to the top or bottom surface of the cantilever will experience. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Proving Ring Deflection A **proving ring** is a circular ring (typically with a thickened section) loaded diametrically. The diametrical deflection $\delta$ is given by Castigliano's theorem: $$x = \frac{1.09 \, F R^{3}}{E \, b \, t^{3}}$$ where: - $R$ = mean radius of the ring - $b$ = radial width of the ring cross-section - $t$ = thickness of the ring cross-section **Step-by-step reasoning:** - The diametral load $F$ creates a bending moment $M = F R \cos\theta$ around the ring. - The strain energy in the ring is integrated over the circumference. - Applying Castigliano's first theorem, the deflection is the partial derivative of strain energy with respect to load. - The numerical coefficient 1.09 arises from the exact integration and accounts for the moment distribution. --- ### 3.5 Numerical Solved Example (KTU Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A cantilever beam made of steel ($E = 200$ GPa) has dimensions $L = 100$ mm, $b = 20$ mm, $t = 5$ mm. A strain gauge with $G = 2.0$ is bonded to the top surface. A force of $F = 500$ N is applied at the tip. The bridge is a quarter-bridge with $V_{ex} = 5$ V. Find: (a) the maximum strain, (b) the output voltage. **Solution:** **(a) Maximum strain:** $$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{6 F L}{E b t^{2}}$$ Substituting values (using SI units): $$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{6 \times 500 \times 0.100}{200 \times 10^{9} \times 0.020 \times (0.005)^{2}}$$ $$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{300}{200 \times 10^{9} \times 0.020 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{300}{100{,}000} = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} = 3000 \, \mu\varepsilon$$ **(b) Output voltage (quarter-bridge):** $$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon}{2} = \frac{5 \times 2.0 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$ $$V_{out} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 15 \times 10^{-3} \, V = \boxed{15 \; mV}$$ --- ### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Cantilever Beam | Simply Supported Beam | Proving Ring | Diaphragm | |---|---|---|---|---| | Sensitivity | High | Medium | High | Medium | | Deflection (for same $F$) | Largest | Medium | Largest | Small | | Common Use | Load cells, force sensors | Larger load cells | Calibration (force standard) | Pressure transducers | | Strain Location | At fixed end, top/bottom | At centre, top/bottom | At inner/outer periphery | At centre (for circular) | | Formula | $\frac{F L^{3}}{3EI}$ | $\frac{F L^{3}}{48EI}$ | $\frac{1.09 FR^{3}}{Ebt^{3}}$ | Depends on clamping | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Functional Block Architecture of an Elastic Force Transducer System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Applied Force F] --> B[Elastic Element
Beam / Ring / Diaphragm] B --> C[Mechanical Deformation
Strain epsilon] C --> D[Strain Gauge
Bonded Foil] D --> E[Wheatstone Bridge
Quarter / Half / Full] E --> F[Amplifier and Filter] F --> G[ADC and Display Unit] G --> H[Output Reading
Force in N or kN] I[Excitation Voltage Vex] --> E J[Temperature Compensation] --> E ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Classification Tree of Elastic Force Transducers ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Elastic Force Transducers] --> B[Beam Type] A --> C[Ring Type] A --> D[Diaphragm Type] A --> E[Spring Type] B --> B1[Cantilever Beam] B --> B2[Simply Supported Beam] B --> B3[Double Cantilever] C --> C1[Proving Ring] C --> C2[Continuous Ring Load Cell] D --> D1[Flat Diaphragm] D --> D2[Corrugated Diaphragm] E --> E1[Helical Compression Spring] E --> E2[Belleville Spring] A --> F[Secondary Transducer] F --> F1[Strain Gauge Bonded] F --> F2[Strain Gauge Unbonded] F --> F3[LVDT Coupled] F --> F4[Capacitive Pickoff] F --> F5[Piezoelectric Crystal] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Quarter Wheatstone Bridge with Strain Gauge ```mermaid flowchart TB Vex[Vex Positive] --> N1[Node A Top] N1 --> R1[R plus delta R
Strain Gauge] R1 --> N2[Node B Right] N1 --> R2[R Fixed Resistor] R2 --> N2 N2 --> Vneg[Vex Negative] Vex --> N3[Node C Bottom] N3 --> R3[R Fixed Resistor] R3 --> N4[Node D Left] N3 --> R4[R Fixed Resistor] R4 --> N4 N4 --> Vneg N2 --> Vout_pos[Output Vout Positive] N4 --> Vout_neg[Output Vout Negative] ``` **Note on diagram 3:** The two top arms $R_1$ (active strain gauge) and $R_2$ (dummy/strain gauge) form the measuring arm, while $R_3$ and $R_4$ are fixed completion resistors. ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Cantilever Beam Physical Schematic (Block Form) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fixed Wall
Clamped End] --> B[Beam Body
Length L, Width b, Thickness t] B --> C[Free End
Load Application Point] C --> D[Force F Applied] B1[Strain Gauge 1
Top Surface] -.Attached to.-> B B2[Strain Gauge 2
Bottom Surface] -.Attached to.-> B E[Maximum Strain Region
Near Fixed End] -.Located at.-> B ``` ### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Proving Ring Operation ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Force F Input
Top Plunger] --> B[Ring Body
Mean Radius R] B --> C[Force F Reaction
Bottom Plunger] B --> D[Deflection delta
Diametral] E[Dial Gauge or LVDT] -.Measures.-> D F[Strain Gauge Inside] -.Alternative Readout.-> D ``` > [!NOTE] > The proving ring is the **national standard for force calibration** in many metrology labs. The dial gauge reading can be directly correlated to force using the ring's calibrated constant $C$ (N/division). ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > **Define an elastic force transducer. Name any two elastic elements used in it.** **Model Answer:** An elastic force transducer is a device that converts an applied force into a measurable electrical signal by exploiting the elastic deformation of a sensing element operating within the Hookean (linear elastic) region of the material. The two essential components are: (i) an **elastic element** (e.g., cantilever beam, proving ring, diaphragm) and (ii) a **secondary transducer** (e.g., strain gauge, LVDT). Two elastic elements: 1. **Cantilever beam** 2. **Proving ring** (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for naming elastic element, 1 for the second element or secondary transducer.) --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]** > **What is the gauge factor of a strain gauge? Why is it important?** **Model Answer:** The gauge factor $G$ of a strain gauge is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in resistance to the applied strain: $$G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$ It is a dimensionless quantity. For metallic foil gauges, $G \approx 2.0$; for semiconductor gauges, $G$ can exceed 100. It is important because it determines the **sensitivity** of the strain gauge — a higher $G$ produces a larger $\Delta R$ for the same strain, giving a larger output voltage from the Wheatstone bridge. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for practical significance.) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- > ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]* **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a strain gauge based elastic force transducer. List the main components and state the role of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A steel cantilever beam of length 200 mm, width 25 mm, and thickness 6 mm carries a force of 1 kN at its free end. A strain gauge of gauge factor 2.1 is bonded to the top surface near the fixed end. The gauge is connected in a quarter Wheatstone bridge with an excitation of 6 V. Young's modulus of steel is 200 GPa. Calculate: (i) the maximum strain, (ii) the change in gauge resistance per unit original resistance ($\Delta R/R$), and (iii) the output voltage of the bridge. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** The block diagram (see SECTION 4 Diagram 1) shows the functional architecture: 1. **Elastic element (cantilever beam):** Receives the applied force and produces a proportional elastic deformation (strain). 2. **Strain gauge:** Bonded to the beam, it converts the strain into a change in electrical resistance. 3. **Wheatstone bridge:** Converts the resistance change into a voltage signal. 4. **Amplifier:** Boosts the millivolt-level signal to a usable level. 5. **Display/Recorder:** Displays the calibrated force value. **Working principle:** The applied force $F$ bends the cantilever beam. The bending produces a strain $\varepsilon$ at the surface, given by $\varepsilon = 6FL/(Ebt^{2})$. This strain is sensed by the bonded strain gauge, whose resistance changes by $\Delta R = G \varepsilon R$. The Wheatstone bridge converts this into a voltage $V_{out} = V_{ex} G \varepsilon / 2$. After amplification, the output is displayed as the measured force. (7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for components/roles, 3 for working explanation.) --- **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** - $L = 200$ mm $= 0.200$ m - $b = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m - $t = 6$ mm $= 0.006$ m - $F = 1000$ N - $G = 2.1$ - $V_{ex} = 6$ V - $E = 200 \times 10^{9}$ Pa **(i) Maximum strain:** $$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{6 F L}{E b t^{2}} = \frac{6 \times 1000 \times 0.200}{200 \times 10^{9} \times 0.025 \times (0.006)^{2}}$$ $$= \frac{1200}{200 \times 10^{9} \times 0.025 \times 36 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$= \frac{1200}{180{,}000} = 6.67 \times 10^{-3} = 6670 \, \mu\varepsilon$$ **(ii) Fractional resistance change:** $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 6.67 \times 10^{-3} = 1.40 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = 0.014 = 1.4\%$$ **(iii) Output voltage (quarter-bridge):** $$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon}{2} = \frac{6 \times 2.1 \times 6.67 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$ $$= \frac{6 \times 0.014}{2} = \frac{0.084}{2} = \boxed{0.042 \; V = 42 \; mV}$$ (7 marks: 2 for part (i), 2 for part (ii), 3 for part (iii) with full substitution.) --- > ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **proving ring** as an elastic force transducer. How is it used for force calibration? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A diaphragm-type pressure transducer uses a circular steel diaphragm of diameter 30 mm and thickness 1.5 mm. The maximum allowable strain at the centre is 1000 microstrain. Take $E = 200$ GPa and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.3$. Determine: (i) the maximum pressure that can be measured, and (ii) the corresponding central deflection. **(7 marks)** **Note for examiner:** For a circular diaphragm clamped at edges, the central deflection $\delta$ and pressure $P$ are related by: $$\delta = \frac{3 P R^{4} (1 - \nu^{2})}{16 E t^{3}}$$ and the maximum strain at the centre (radial) is: $$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{3 P R^{2} (1 - \nu^{2})}{8 E t^{2}}$$ --- **Model Solution (a):** A **proving ring** is a circular ring of high-grade steel (or beryllium copper) with a thickened cross-section. It has two diametrically opposite loading points (plungers) on the inner periphery. **Construction:** - Annular ring body, mean radius $R$, cross-section $b \times t$. - Top plunger (input) and bottom plunger (reaction) — for force application. - A central micrometer/dial gauge or LVDT mounted across the diameter to measure the diametrical deflection. - Alternatively, strain gauges bonded to the inner and outer surfaces to detect strain. **Working:** - A force $F$ applied diametrically deflects the ring by $\delta$ (Castigliano's equation): $\delta = 1.09 F R^{3} / (E b t^{3})$. - The deflection is measured and, using the calibrated constant of the ring, the force is read directly. **Use in force calibration:** - The proving ring is treated as a **primary force standard** in metrology labs. - It is calibrated against dead-weight machines (primary standard). - Once calibrated, it is used to calibrate other force measuring devices (load cells, dynamometers, testing machines). - Its high repeatability (better than 0.01%) and low drift make it ideal for this purpose. (7 marks: 2 construction, 2 working, 2 calibration use, 1 diagram reference.) --- **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** - Diameter $D = 30$ mm → $R = 0.015$ m - Thickness $t = 1.5$ mm $= 0.0015$ m - $\varepsilon_{max} = 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ - $E = 200 \times 10^{9}$ Pa - $\nu = 0.3$ **(i) Maximum pressure:** From $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{3 P R^{2} (1 - \nu^{2})}{8 E t^{2}}$: $$P = \frac{8 E t^{2} \varepsilon_{max}}{3 R^{2} (1 - \nu^{2})}$$ $$P = \frac{8 \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times (0.0015)^{2} \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{3 \times (0.015)^{2} \times (1 - 0.09)}$$ $$P = \frac{8 \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{3 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.91}$$ Numerator: $8 \times 200 \times 2.25 \times 10^{0} = 3600$ Denominator: $3 \times 2.25 \times 0.91 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{3}$ ... let me recompute carefully. $$P = \frac{8 \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-3}}{3 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.91}$$ Numerator: $8 \times 200 = 1600$; $1600 \times 10^{9} \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-3} = 1600 \times 2.25 \times 10^{0} = 3600$ Denominator: $3 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.91 = 3 \times 0.91 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-4} = 6.1425 \times 10^{-4}$ $$P = \frac{3600}{6.1425 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.86 \times 10^{6} \; Pa \approx \boxed{5.86 \; MPa}$$ **(ii) Central deflection:** $$\delta = \frac{3 P R^{4} (1 - \nu^{2})}{16 E t^{3}}$$ $$\delta = \frac{3 \times 5.86 \times 10^{6} \times (0.015)^{4} \times 0.91}{16 \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times (0.0015)^{3}}$$ $(0.015)^{4} = 5.0625 \times 10^{-8}$ $(0.0015)^{3} = 3.375 \times 10^{-9}$ $$\delta = \frac{3 \times 5.86 \times 10^{6} \times 5.0625 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.91}{16 \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times 3.375 \times 10^{-9}}$$ Numerator: $3 \times 5.86 \times 5.0625 \times 0.91 \times 10^{6-8} = 3 \times 5.86 \times 5.0625 \times 0.91 \times 10^{-2} = 80.99 \times 10^{-2} = 0.810$ Denominator: $16 \times 200 \times 3.375 \times 10^{9-9} = 16 \times 200 \times 3.375 = 10800$ $$\delta = \frac{0.810}{10800} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5} \; m = \boxed{75 \; \mu m}$$ (7 marks: 4 for part (i), 3 for part (ii).) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > **Common mistakes that cost marks in elastic force transducer questions:** > > 1. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing mm and m in the same equation. KTU examiners specifically check unit consistency. Always convert to SI (metres, Newtons, Pascals) before substitution. > 2. **Forgetting the $\nu$ term in diaphragm problems** — The factor $(1 - \nu^{2})$ is often dropped. Marks are specifically allocated for this. > 3. **Wrong bridge factor** — Using $V_{out} = V_{ex} G \varepsilon$ (full-bridge formula) for a quarter-bridge. The correct quarter-bridge formula is $V_{out} = V_{ex} G \varepsilon / 2$. > 4. **Confusing $G$ (gauge factor) with $E$ (Young's modulus)** — These are entirely different quantities. Gauge factor is dimensionless; Young's modulus is in Pa. > 5. **Forgetting to square $t$ in the strain formula** — The denominator is $E b t^{2}$, NOT $E b t^{3}$. The cube is for deflection; the square is for strain. > 6. **Sign errors in bending moment** — Cantilever strain is maximum at the fixed end, not the free end. Strain gauges must be placed near the clamped end for maximum sensitivity. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **ELASTIC FORCE TRANSDUCERS — Quick-Fire Revision List** - ✅ **Definition:** Transducer that uses **elastic deformation within Hooke's law limit** to convert force into a measurable signal. - ✅ **Two core components:** **Elastic element** (beam / ring / diaphragm / spring) + **Secondary transducer** (strain gauge / LVDT). - ✅ **Governing law:** Hooke's law: $F = kx$ and $\sigma = E\varepsilon$. - ✅ **Strain gauge equation:** $\Delta R / R = G \varepsilon$. Gauge factor $G$ is typically **2.0** for foil and **>100** for semiconductor. - ✅ **Cantilever beam tip deflection:** $x = FL^{3}/(3EI)$, with $I = bt^{3}/12$. - ✅ **Cantilever surface strain:** $\varepsilon = 6FL/(Ebt^{2})$ — strain gauge placed at fixed end. - ✅ **Simply supported beam centre deflection:** $x = FL^{3}/(48EI)$. - ✅ **Proving ring diametrical deflection:** $x = 1.09 F R^{3}/(E b t^{3})$ — used as national force standard. - ✅ **Quarter-bridge output:** $V_{out} = V_{ex} G \varepsilon / 2$. **Half-bridge:** $V_{out} = V_{ex} G \varepsilon / 2$ (active gauges on opposite arms). **Full-bridge:** $V_{out} = V_{ex} G \varepsilon$. - ✅ **Circular diaphragm (clamped):** $\varepsilon = 3PR^{2}(1-\nu^{2})/(8Et^{2})$ and $\delta = 3PR^{4}(1-\nu^{2})/(16Et^{3})$. - ✅ **Material constants to remember:** Steel $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$. Aluminium $E = 70$ GPa. Phosphor bronze $E = 120$ GPa. - ✅ **Advantages:** High accuracy (0.01–0.1%), good linearity, low hysteresis, works with static and dynamic forces. - ✅ **Limitations:** Strain gauges are temperature-sensitive (need compensation), have limited range, and small output (mV level — needs amplification). - ✅ **KTU high-yield topics for ESE:** Cantilever beam with strain gauge, proving ring, Wheatstone bridge configurations, gauge factor derivation, diaphragm pressure transducer. - ✅ **Remember units:** Force in **N**, length in **m**, $E$ in **Pa**, $\varepsilon$ dimensionless, $V_{out}$ in **V**. - ✅ **Temperature compensation is automatic in half and full bridges** — adjacent arms experience equal temperature changes and cancel out.

topics.title: Torque Measurement on Rotating Shaft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Torque Measurement on Rotating Shaft # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Torque** is defined as the *tangential turning moment* applied to a body about an axis of rotation, mathematically expressed as the **cross product of the radius vector and the applied force**: $\vec{T} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$. In the context of **rotating shaft measurement**, torque is the rotational equivalent of linear force — it quantifies the "twisting effort" transmitted through a shaft from the prime mover (motor, engine, turbine) to the driven load (pump, gearbox, generator, propeller). The **SI unit of torque is the Newton-metre (N·m)**, while the legacy CGS unit kgf·m is still encountered in older KTU textbooks. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested):** > - **Work** = Force × Distance (linear motion) → Joules > - **Torque** = Force × Perpendicular Distance (rotational) → N·m > - 1 N·m is **NOT** equivalent to 1 Joule because torque involves angular displacement, not linear displacement. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy Imagine you are tightening a stubborn bolt with a **wrench**. The further down the handle you grip (larger $\vec{r}$), the less force you need to apply to achieve the same twisting effect. That "twisting effort" you feel in your wrist is exactly what we call **torque**.
