| Knudsen Number | Regime | Pressure | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Kn \ll 1$ | Continuum (Viscous) | High pressure | Heat conduction $\propto k$ (constant) |
| $Kn \approx 1$ | Transition | Intermediate | Non-linear behavior |
| $Kn \gg 1$ | Molecular (Free-Molecular) | Low pressure | Heat conduction $\propto P$ ✓ (Pirani operating regime) |

#### Step 5: Numerical Verification for Air

For air at $T = 300$ K, $d \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-10}$ m:

$$ \begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{\sqrt{2} \pi (3.7 \times 10^{-10})^2 \cdot P} \\
\lambda &\approx \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-3}}{P \text{ (in Pa)}} \text{ meters}
\end{aligned} $$

For a typical gauge with $L = 1$ cm = 0.01 m, the condition $Kn \gg 1$ requires $\lambda \gg 0.01$ m, meaning $P \ll 0.66$ Pa $\approx 5 \times 10^{-3}$ mbar — confirming the operational upper limit of the Pirani gauge.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A Pirani gauge with a platinum filament ($R_0 = 100\ \Omega$, $\beta = 0.0039\ /\text{°C}$) is operated in a bridge with $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 100\ \Omega$ and $V_s = 6$ V. At pressure $P_1$, the filament temperature is 150°C. When pressure drops to $P_2$, the filament temperature rises to 180°C. Calculate the bridge output voltage.

**Solution:**

#### Step 1: Compute Resistance Change

$$ \begin{aligned}
R_{f1} &= 100 \times [1 + 0.0039 \times (150 - 0)] \\
&= 100 \times [1 + 0.585] = 158.5\ \Omega
\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}
R_{f2} &= 100 \times [1 + 0.0039 \times (180 - 0)] \\
&= 100 \times [1 + 0.702] = 170.2\ \Omega
\end{aligned} $$

#### Step 2: Compute $\Delta R_f$

$$\Delta R_f = 170.2 - 158.5 = 11.7\ \Omega$$

#### Step 3: Apply Bridge Output Formula

Using $R_f \approx 158.5\ \Omega$ as the operating point:

$$ \begin{aligned}
V_{out} &\approx \left| -\frac{V_s}{4R} \cdot \Delta R_f \right| \\
&= \left| -\frac{6}{4 \times 158.5} \times 11.7 \right| \\
&= \frac{6 \times 11.7}{634} \\
&\approx 0.111 \text{ V} = 111 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $V_{out} \approx 111$ mV. This positive voltage indicates a pressure decrease (filament became hotter), consistent with the physical principle of the Pirani gauge.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Question:** Define a Thermal Conductivity Gauge (Pirani Gauge). State its principle of operation and operating pressure range.

**Model Answer:**

A **Thermal Conductivity Gauge**, also known as a **Pirani Gauge**, is an indirect-reading type of vacuum pressure measuring instrument that determines the pressure of a gas by measuring the rate at which the gas conducts heat away from a heated filament.

**Principle of Operation:** The gauge operates on the principle that the thermal conductivity of a gas at low pressures is directly proportional to its pressure (number density of molecules). A heated filament is suspended in the gas; as pressure changes, the heat loss from the filament to the cooler surroundings changes proportionally. This heat loss is detected as a change in filament temperature or resistance, which is then correlated to gas pressure.

**Operating Pressure Range:** The Pirani gauge operates in the **medium vacuum range** from approximately **10⁻¹ mbar (0.1 mbar) to 10⁻³ mbar (0.001 mbar)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3-Mark Allocation Guide:** Definition (1 mark) + Principle (1 mark) + Operating Range (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Question:** Why is a Thermal Conductivity Gauge unsuitable for measuring atmospheric pressure? Explain briefly.

**Model Answer:**

A Thermal Conductivity Gauge is unsuitable for atmospheric pressure measurement for the following reasons:

1. **Saturation Effect:** At atmospheric pressure (or high pressures in general), the mean free path of gas molecules becomes very small (much less than the chamber dimension). The thermal conductivity of the gas becomes **independent of pressure** in this regime (continuum flow regime), so the gauge loses its sensitivity.

2. **Non-Linear Region:** The fundamental linear relationship $Q_{gas} \propto P$ holds only in the **molecular flow regime** where $Kn \gg 1$, which corresponds to medium vacuum conditions.

3. **Heat Conduction Saturation:** At high pressures, the gas acts as a continuous medium with a fixed thermal conductivity $k$, regardless of further pressure increases.

Therefore, the gauge is functional only in the range where gas pressure influences thermal conductivity, i.e., the medium vacuum regime (**10⁻¹ to 10⁻³ mbar**).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Question:** 
**(a)** Describe with a neat diagram the construction and working of a Thermal Conductivity Gauge. Explain its principle of operation in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between the heat transfer from the filament and the gas pressure in a Pirani gauge. Show that at low pressures, heat loss is directly proportional to pressure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of Thermal Conductivity Gauge:**

**Construction:**
The Thermal Conductivity Gauge consists of the following main components:

1. **Filament (Sensing Element):** A thin wire of platinum or tungsten (typically ~25 μm diameter) is stretched along the axis of a glass or metal tube. Platinum is preferred due to its high temperature coefficient of resistance ($\beta = 0.0039/°C$).

2. **Connecting End-Wires:** The filament is supported and electrically connected through two lead wires (typically platinum-iridium alloy to minimize conduction losses).

3. **Gas Envelope Tube:** The filament is enclosed in a tube connected to the vacuum system via a port.

4. **Compensating Tube (Optional):** A second identical tube sealed at a reference pressure of ~10⁻⁴ mbar is included in some designs to compensate for ambient temperature variations.

5. **Wheatstone Bridge Circuit:** The filament forms one arm of the bridge along with three precision resistors.

6. **Display Unit:** Analog meter or digital readout displays the pressure value.

**Working Principle:**

- A constant voltage (typically 6 V) is applied across the Wheatstone bridge.
- Current flows through the filament, heating it to approximately 100–200°C above ambient.
- At equilibrium, the electrical power supplied equals the total heat losses (gas conduction + lead conduction + radiation).
- When the pressure of the surrounding gas changes:
  - **Lower pressure** → fewer gas molecules → reduced heat removal → filament becomes **hotter** → resistance **increases**.
  - **Higher pressure** → more gas molecules → enhanced heat removal → filament becomes **cooler** → resistance **decreases**.
- The resulting resistance change unbalances the Wheatstone bridge, producing an output voltage proportional to the pressure change.
- This output voltage is calibrated and displayed as a pressure reading.

**(b) Derivation of Heat Transfer vs. Pressure Relationship:**

Consider a hot filament at temperature $T_f$ suspended in a gas at temperature $T_w$.

**Step 1:** In the low-pressure regime (Knudsen regime where $\lambda \gg D$), gas molecules travel ballistically from the filament to the walls without intermolecular collisions.

**Step 2:** The number of molecules striking unit area of the filament per unit time (molecular flux) by kinetic theory is:

$$\phi = \frac{1}{4} n \bar{c}$$

Where $n$ is the number density and $\bar{c}$ is the mean molecular speed.

**Step 3:** Using $\bar{c} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}}$ and the ideal gas relation $P = n k_B T$:

$$\phi = \frac{P}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$$

**Step 4:** Each molecule striking the filament at $T_f$ leaves at $T_w$ (after thermal accommodation), carrying away energy:

$$\Delta E = c_v (T_f - T_w)$$

**Step 5:** The total heat conducted away by the gas per unit time per unit area is:

$$\frac{Q_{gas}}{A_f} = \phi \cdot \Delta E = \frac{P \cdot c_v \cdot (T_f - T_w)}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$$

**Step 6:** Defining the gauge constant $K = \dfrac{c_v \cdot A_f}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$:

$$Q_{gas} = K \cdot P \cdot (T_f - T_w)$$

Therefore, $Q_{gas} \propto P$, confirming that heat loss is **directly proportional to pressure** in the molecular flow regime.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Question:**

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain how a Pirani gauge is connected in a Wheatstone bridge configuration. Derive the expression for the output voltage when the filament resistance changes due to pressure variation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Pirani gauge uses a platinum filament with $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at 0°C and temperature coefficient $\beta = 0.0039/°C$. The filament operates at 152°C at pressure $P_1$ and at 178°C at pressure $P_2$. The gauge is connected in a balanced Wheatstone bridge with three equal arms of 100 Ω each and supply voltage 6 V. Calculate the bridge output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Wheatstone Bridge with Pirani Gauge:**

**Circuit Description:**

```
        Vs (+)
         |
    +----+----+
    |         |
    Rf        R3
    |         |
    +---Va----+---Vb
    |         |
    R2        R4
    |         |
    +----+----+
         |
        Vs (-)
```

The Pirani filament $R_f$ forms one arm, while precision resistors $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$ form the other arms. At the reference (balance) pressure, the bridge is balanced so that $V_A = V_B$ and $V_{out} = 0$.

**Derivation:**

Voltage at node A (between $R_f$ and $R_2$):

$$V_A = V_s \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_f + R_2}$$

Voltage at node B (between $R_3$ and $R_4$):

$$V_B = V_s \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

Output voltage (assuming $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$):

$$V_{out} = V_s \left[ \frac{R}{R_f + R} - \frac{R}{R + R} \right] = V_s \left[ \frac{R}{R_f + R} - \frac{1}{2} \right]$$

For small perturbation $\Delta R_f$ around the balance point $R_f = R$:

$$V_{out} \approx -\frac{V_s}{4R} \cdot \Delta R_f$$

Since $\Delta R_f$ is proportional to pressure change via the resistance-temperature characteristic, the output voltage becomes a measure of pressure change.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate filament resistance at $P_1$ ($T_f = 152°C$):**

$$R_{f1} = R_0 [1 + \beta (T_f - T_0)] = 100 [1 + 0.0039 \times 152] = 100 \times 1.5928 = 159.28\ \Omega$$

**Step 2: Calculate filament resistance at $P_2$ ($T_f = 178°C$):**

$$R_{f2} = 100 [1 + 0.0039 \times 178] = 100 \times 1.6942 = 169.42\ \Omega$$

**Step 3: Resistance change:**

$$\Delta R_f = R_{f2} - R_{f1} = 169.42 - 159.28 = 10.14\ \Omega$$

**Step 4: Apply bridge output formula:**

Using $R \approx 159.28\ \Omega$ as the operating resistance:

$$V_{out} = \left| -\frac{V_s \cdot \Delta R_f}{4R} \right| = \frac{6 \times 10.14}{4 \times 159.28} = \frac{60.84}{637.12} \approx 0.0955 \text{ V}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_{out} \approx 95.5$ mV (positive, indicating pressure decrease)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use absolute temperature in resistance formula:** Always use $(T_f - T_0)$ where both are in °C for platinum's $\beta$ value, not Kelvin.
> 2. **Sign convention errors:** Filament getting hotter (pressure decreasing) gives POSITIVE output voltage. Mixing signs loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Misapplying the linear approximation:** The formula $V_{out} \approx -V_s \Delta R_f / 4R$ is valid ONLY for small perturbations around balance. For large changes, full voltage divider equation must be used.
> 4. **Confusing the operating range:** Students often incorrectly cite "1 mbar to 10⁻⁶ mbar" — the correct range is **10⁻¹ mbar to 10⁻³ mbar**. This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Omitting the Wheatstone bridge diagram:** In Part B (a) questions, a circuit diagram is mandatory. Skipping it typically costs 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Full Name:** Thermal Conductivity Gauge (Pirani Gauge)
- [ ] **Type:** Indirect / Secondary Vacuum Gauge
- [ ] **Operating Range:** **10⁻¹ mbar to 10⁻³ mbar** (medium vacuum only)
- [ ] **Sensing Element:** Thin platinum or tungsten filament (~25 μm diameter)
- [ ] **Core Principle:** Gas heat conduction $\propto$ gas pressure (in Knudsen regime)
- [ ] **Key Condition:** $Kn = \lambda / L \gg 1$ (molecular flow regime)
- [ ] **Bridge Configuration:** Filament forms one arm of a Wheatstone bridge
- [ ] **Two Operating Modes:** Constant Voltage mode and Constant Temperature mode
- [ ] **Constant Temperature Mode** is preferred in modern instruments
- [ ] **Platinum $\beta = 0.0039/°C$** — must be memorized
- [ ] **Radiation Loss Formula:** $Q_{rad} = \sigma \epsilon A (T_f^4 - T_w^4)$ — Stefan-Boltzmann law
- [ ] **Energy Balance:** $I^2 R_f = Q_{gas} + Q_{leads} + Q_{rad}$
- [ ] **At low pressures** (high vacuum), gauge output saturates and reading becomes unreliable
- [ ] **At atmospheric pressure** (or high pressures), gauge cannot operate — thermal conductivity becomes pressure-independent
- [ ] **Major Industrial Applications:** Semiconductor vacuum chambers, refrigeration leak testing, vacuum metallurgy
- [ ] **Advantage:** No high-voltage requirement; mechanically robust; long operational life
- [ ] **Disadvantage:** Gas-type dependent (requires calibration for different gases); limited range
- [ ] **Common Exam Trap:** Don't confuse with thermocouple gauge (which also uses thermal principle but operates via thermoelectric EMF, not resistance change)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pirani Gauge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pirani Gauge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pirani Gauge** (also called a *thermal conductivity gauge* or *Pirani vacuum gauge*) is a pressure-measuring instrument used to measure **low absolute pressures** in vacuum systems, operating on the principle that the **thermal conductivity of a gas is pressure-dependent** in the low-pressure regime (where the mean free path of gas molecules is comparable to or greater than the physical dimensions of the gauge chamber).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):** *"The Pirani gauge is a thermal-type vacuum gauge in which a heated filament loses heat to the surrounding gas at a rate dependent on gas pressure. The change in filament temperature (and hence electrical resistance) is measured and correlated to the absolute pressure of the system."*

The standard measurable range of a conventional Pirani gauge is from approximately **100 Pa (≈ 1 torr)** down to about **10⁻² Pa (≈ 10⁻⁴ torr)**. Modern wide-range Pirani designs can extend measurement up to atmospheric pressure (10⁵ Pa).

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Hot Cup of Tea in a Cold Room"**
> Imagine you have a freshly brewed cup of hot tea sitting on a table in a closed, cold room.
> - If the room is **filled with air molecules (high pressure)**, these molecules constantly bump into the hot cup and carry heat away — the tea cools **quickly**.
> - If you **pump out most of the air (low pressure / vacuum)**, there are very few molecules around to bump into the cup — the tea cools **very slowly**.
> - By simply **measuring how fast the tea is cooling**, you can deduce **how many air molecules (pressure) are in the room**.
>
> The **Pirani gauge works identically**: the hot "cup of tea" is a **heated electrical filament**, and the "rate of cooling" is detected by measuring the **change in filament temperature/resistance**. Faster cooling = more gas molecules = higher pressure.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Principle — Pressure-Dependent Thermal Conductivity

The key physical behavior exploited is the relationship between **thermal conductivity (k)** of a low-pressure gas and its **pressure (P)**:

| Pressure Regime | Mean Free Path (λ) vs. Chamber Size (d) | Thermal Conductivity Behavior |
|:---|:---|:---|
| High (near atmospheric) | λ ≪ d (molecule-molecule collisions dominate) | k ≈ constant (independent of P) |
| **Intermediate/Low (Pirani range)** | **λ ≥ d (molecule-wall collisions dominate)** | **k ∝ P (linear with pressure)** |
| Ultra-high vacuum (UHV) | λ ≫ d | k → 0 (too few molecules to conduct) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Pirani gauge **only functions** in the *molecular flow regime* (Knudsen number **Kn = λ/d > 1**), where the number of gas molecules — and hence the heat conduction — is directly proportional to pressure. This is why the gauge is restricted to vacuum measurement.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Standard operating range:** **1 torr to 10⁻⁴ torr** (≈ **133 Pa to 0.013 Pa**)
- **Filament material:** Tungsten or Platinum (high temperature coefficient of resistance)
- **Reference standard pressure:** **10⁻⁶ torr** (sealed reference tube at high vacuum)
- **Typical filament temperature:** **100 °C to 150 °C** above ambient
- **Bridge supply voltage:** typically **5 V to 10 V DC**
- **Sensitivity:** typically **0.1 mV per 10⁻³ torr** change

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The Pirani gauge operates through the following **causal chain**:

1. **Electric current is passed** through a fine filament (tungsten/platinum) mounted inside a chamber connected to the vacuum system being measured.
2. The **filament heats up** due to **Joule heating (I²R losses)**, reaching an equilibrium temperature $T_f$ above the chamber wall temperature $T_w$.
3. At equilibrium, **electrical input power = total heat loss from filament**:
   $$\text{Electrical Input Power} = \text{Heat Loss by Conduction through gas} + \text{Heat Loss by Conduction through leads} + \text{Heat Loss by Radiation}$$

4. The dominant pressure-dependent term is the **gas-conduction heat loss**, $Q_{gas}$, which is proportional to pressure **P** in the molecular flow regime:
   $$Q_{gas} = \alpha \cdot P \cdot (T_f - T_w)$$
   where $\alpha$ is a **gauge constant** (depends on gas type, geometry, and surface temperatures).

5. As **P changes**, $Q_{gas}$ changes, causing the equilibrium $T_f$ to shift.
6. Since filament resistance $R_f$ depends on temperature via the **temperature coefficient of resistance**:
   $$R_f = R_0 \left[1 + \beta (T_f - T_0)\right]$$
   a shift in $T_f$ produces a measurable shift in $R_f$.
7. The change in $R_f$ is detected by a **Wheatstone bridge** and is **calibrated directly in pressure units**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
> - **Step 1–2:** Create a temperature differential between filament and walls.
> - **Step 3:** Energy balance is the universal equilibrium condition.
> - **Step 4:** This is the *only* term that varies meaningfully with P — leads/radiation losses are (nearly) pressure-independent and are treated as offsets.
> - **Step 5–6:** Convert the *thermal* change into a *measurable electrical* signal.
> - **Step 7:** Make the abstract quantity (resistance change) directly readable as a physical quantity (pressure).

## 2.2 Modes of Operation (KTU High-Yield)

| Mode | What's Held Constant | What's Measured | Sensitivity | KTU Exam Tip |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Constant Voltage (CV)** | Bridge voltage V | Bridge output current I or unbalance voltage | Low (non-linear) | Simple, but suffers drift |
| **Constant Current (CC)** | Filament current I | Filament voltage V (proportional to $R_f$ ∝ $T_f$ ∝ P) | Medium | Better linearity, common in industrial gauges |
| **Constant Temperature (CT)** | Filament resistance $R_f$ (i.e., $T_f$) | Bridge voltage V needed to maintain $R_f$ | **High (most accurate)** | Modern Pirani gauges; preferred for KTU numericals |
| **Constant Resistance (CR)** | Filament resistance (alternate form of CT) | Heating power required | High | Synonym for CT in some texts |

## 2.3 The Wheatstone Bridge Configuration

A **compensated twin-filament Pirani gauge** uses **two identical Pirani tubes**:
- **Tube 1 (Measuring)**: Open to the vacuum system under test.
- **Tube 2 (Reference)**: Sealed at a known reference pressure (typically **< 10⁻⁶ torr**).

Both tubes are placed in adjacent arms of a **Wheatstone bridge**. This compensates for:
- **Ambient temperature fluctuations**
- **Common-mode drift** in bridge supply
- **Aging of filament material**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark-Winning Point:** The reference tube at high vacuum is sealed once-and-for-all at a pressure **so low** that its thermal conductivity contribution is essentially **zero**. Hence, any bridge unbalance is due **solely to the pressure in the measuring tube**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Validity / Boundary Conditions |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $Q_{gas} = \alpha P (T_f - T_w)$ | Heat loss to gas (linear regime) | **W** | Valid when $Kn = \lambda / d > 1$ (molecular flow) |
| 2 | $I^2 R_f = Q_{gas} + Q_{leads} + Q_{rad}$ | Energy balance at equilibrium | **W** | General equation for any mode |
| 3 | $R_f = R_0 \left[1 + \beta (T_f - T_0)\right]$ | Resistance vs. temperature of filament | **Ω** | $\beta$ ≈ 0.004 to 0.006 /°C for Pt, ≈ 0.0045 /°C for W |
| 4 | $P \propto (T_f - T_w)$ | At equilibrium (constant input power) | **Pa** or **torr** | Holds in linear regime |
| 5 | $\Delta V_{bridge} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R_f}{R_f}$ | Bridge unbalance voltage (small-signal) | **V** | $V_{ex}$ = excitation voltage, valid for $\Delta R_f \ll R_f$ |
| 6 | $Kn = \dfrac{\lambda}{d} = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 P}$ | Knudsen number criterion | dimensionless | Pirani works when $Kn \geq 0.01$ (approx.) |
| 7 | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi \sigma^2 P}$ | Mean free path of gas molecule | **m** | $\sigma$ = molecular diameter, $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant |
| 8 | $P_{min} \approx \dfrac{2 \sigma \epsilon A T_w^3}{k_{gas} A_f}$ | Theoretical lower pressure limit | **Pa** | Limited by radiation heat transfer |
| 9 | $P_{max} \approx 100$ to $1000$ Pa | Practical upper pressure limit | **Pa** | Above this, convection dominates, $k \neq f(P)$ |

**Key Material Constants Used in KTU Problems:**
- Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
- Molecular diameter of air: $\sigma \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-10}$ **m**
- Temperature coefficient (Tungsten): $\beta \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ **/°C**
- Stefan-Boltzmann constant: $\sigma_{SB} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ **W/(m²·K⁴)**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Industry | Use of Pirani Gauge |
|:---|:---|
| **Semiconductor fabrication** | Process chamber vacuum monitoring during deposition, etching, ion implantation |
| **Pharmaceutical freeze-drying (lyophilization)** | Vacuum level control in drying chambers |
| **Space simulation chambers** | Low-pressure measurement in thermal-vacuum test facilities |
| **Incandescent / halogen lamp manufacturing** | Vacuum/inert gas backfill pressure verification |
| **Mass spectrometer inlet systems** | Fore-vacuum pressure indication |
| **Nuclear reactor coolant systems** | Leak detection and primary loop vacuum monitoring |
| **Particle accelerators** | Beam pipe vacuum monitoring (often as part of a wider gauge controller) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Heat Loss Equation in the Molecular Flow Regime

**Starting Point:** In the molecular flow regime (low pressure, $Kn \gg 1$), gas molecules travel from the hot filament to the cold wall without intermolecular collisions. Each molecule carries an energy proportional to the filament temperature and is replaced by a molecule from the wall at wall temperature.

**Energy carried per molecule leaving the filament:**
$$E_{out} = c_v (T_f - T_w) \quad \text{(per molecule, per traversal)}$$

**Number of molecular impacts per unit time on the filament of area $A_f$:**
$$\dot{N} = \dfrac{1}{4} n \bar{c} A_f$$

where $n$ is the number density (molecules/m³) and $\bar{c}$ is the **mean molecular speed**:
$$\bar{c} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}}$$

**Total heat loss per unit time:**
$$Q_{gas} = \dot{N} \cdot c_v (T_f - T_w) = \dfrac{1}{4} n \bar{c} A_f c_v (T_f - T_w)$$

**Convert number density to pressure using ideal gas law:** $P = n k_B T$, so $n = P / (k_B T)$.

$$Q_{gas} = \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \dfrac{P}{k_B T} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}} \cdot A_f \cdot c_v (T_f - T_w)$$

**Simplify by grouping all geometric/physical constants into a single gauge constant $\alpha$:**

$$\boxed{Q_{gas} = \alpha \, P \, (T_f - T_w)}$$

where 
$$\alpha = \dfrac{A_f c_v}{4 k_B T} \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is the *master equation* of the Pirani gauge. The linear dependence on **P** in the molecular regime is the entire reason the gauge works as a pressure transducer. At higher pressures where $Kn \to 0$, the collisions between molecules *decouple* the gas temperature from the wall — and $Q_{gas}$ saturates, no longer tracking P.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Bridge Output Voltage and Sensitivity

Consider the **Wheatstone bridge** with the Pirani filament $R_f$ in one arm and three fixed resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ in the other arms, all nominally equal to $R_0$.

**Initial Balance:** At the *reference* pressure, $R_f = R_0$, and the bridge is balanced, so $V_{out} = 0$.

**When pressure changes by $\Delta P$:** The filament temperature changes by $\Delta T_f$, causing a resistance change:
$$\Delta R_f = R_0 \, \beta \, \Delta T_f$$

**Bridge Output (small-signal approximation):** For a bridge with all four arms ≈ $R_0$ and excitation $V_{ex}$, the differential output is:
$$V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R_f}{R_0} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \beta \, \Delta T_f$$

**Substitute $\Delta T_f$ from the heat balance:** From $I^2 R_f = \alpha P (T_f - T_w) + \text{const}$, differentiating at constant I:
$$0 = \alpha (T_f - T_w) \Delta P + \alpha P \, \Delta T_f$$
$$\Delta T_f = -\dfrac{(T_f - T_w)}{P} \Delta P$$

**Therefore:**
$$\boxed{V_{out} = -\dfrac{V_{ex} \, \beta \, (T_f - T_w)}{4 P} \, \Delta P}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result for KTU:** The **sensitivity** $S = \vert \partial V_{out} / \partial P \vert = \dfrac{V_{ex} \, \beta \, (T_f - T_w)}{4 P}$.
> - Sensitivity is **inversely proportional to P** — so the gauge is **most sensitive at LOW pressures** (which is exactly the regime where it's used).
> - Sensitivity is **proportional to $(T_f - T_w)$** — higher filament temperature gives better sensitivity but reduces filament life.
> - Sensitivity is **proportional to $V_{ex}$** — but $V_{ex}$ cannot be increased indefinitely without burning out the filament.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Mean Free Path and the Lower Pressure Limit

The Pirani gauge fails at very low pressures because **radiation losses** become comparable to gas-conduction losses, masking the pressure-dependent signal.

**Gas conduction loss (proportional to P):**
$$Q_{gas} = \alpha P (T_f - T_w)$$

**Radiation loss (independent of P):**
$$Q_{rad} = \sigma_{SB} A_f \epsilon (T_f^4 - T_w^4)$$

**Lower limit criterion:** $Q_{gas} \approx Q_{rad}$, solving for the minimum detectable pressure:

$$P_{min} = \dfrac{\sigma_{SB} \epsilon (T_f^4 - T_w^4)}{\alpha (T_f - T_w)}$$

**Approximating $T_f^4 - T_w^4 \approx 4 T_w^3 (T_f - T_w)$ for small $\Delta T$:**

$$\boxed{P_{min} \approx \dfrac{4 \, \sigma_{SB} \, \epsilon \, T_w^3}{\alpha}}$$

For typical $T_w = 300$ K, $\epsilon \approx 0.3$, $\alpha \approx 10^{-4}$ W/(Pa·K):

$$P_{min} \approx \dfrac{4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.3 \times (300)^3}{10^{-4}} \approx 0.06 \text{ Pa} \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ torr}$$

This is consistent with the **typical lower limit of ~10⁻⁴ torr** observed in practice.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Examination Style)

> **Problem:** A Pirani gauge has a tungsten filament with $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at $T_0 = 20\ ^\circ C$ and temperature coefficient $\beta = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}\ /\ ^\circ C$. At a measured pressure, the filament operates at $T_f = 120\ ^\circ C$ with $T_w = 25\ ^\circ C$. If the excitation voltage is $V_{ex} = 8\ \text{V}$ and the gauge constant is $\alpha = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{W/(Pa·K)}$, calculate:
> (a) The filament resistance at operating temperature.
> (b) The heat loss to the gas at $P = 10\ \text{Pa}$.
> (c) The bridge unbalance voltage per 1 Pa change in pressure.

### Solution

**Part (a):** Apply the linear resistance-temperature relation:
$$R_f = R_0 \left[1 + \beta (T_f - T_0)\right]$$

$$R_f = 100 \left[1 + 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times (120 - 20)\right]$$

$$R_f = 100 \left[1 + 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 100\right] = 100 \left[1 + 0.45\right] = \mathbf{145\ \Omega}$$

**Part (b):** Apply the gas heat-loss equation:
$$Q_{gas} = \alpha \, P \, (T_f - T_w)$$

$$Q_{gas} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 \times (120 - 25)$$

$$Q_{gas} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 \times 95 = \mathbf{0.475\ W}$$

**Part (c):** Bridge sensitivity formula:
$$S = \dfrac{V_{ex} \, \beta \, (T_f - T_w)}{4 P}$$

$$S = \dfrac{8 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 95}{4 \times 10}$$

$$S = \dfrac{3.42}{40} = \mathbf{0.0855\ \text{V/Pa} = 85.5\ \text{mV/Pa}}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Q: Define Pirani gauge. State the principle on which it works and mention its operating range.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Pirani gauge** is a **thermal conductivity type vacuum gauge** used for the measurement of low absolute pressures.

**Principle:** It works on the principle that the **thermal conductivity of a gas is directly proportional to its pressure** in the low-pressure regime (molecular flow regime, where the mean free path of gas molecules is comparable to or greater than the dimensions of the gauge tube). A heated filament inside the gauge loses heat to the surrounding gas at a rate dependent on the gas pressure, and the resulting change in filament temperature (and hence its electrical resistance) is measured using a **Wheatstone bridge circuit** and calibrated to indicate pressure.

**Operating Range:** Approximately **1 torr to 10⁻⁴ torr** (133 Pa to 0.013 Pa).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Q: Why is a reference tube used in a Pirani gauge? What should be the pressure inside the reference tube?**

**Model Answer:**

A **reference tube** is used in a Pirani gauge to **compensate for ambient temperature variations and zero-drift errors** in the bridge circuit. It is a second, identical Pirani tube placed in an adjacent arm of the Wheatstone bridge, but it is **sealed off** (not connected to the system under test). Since both tubes experience the same ambient temperature, any thermal disturbance affects both arms equally and produces no net bridge unbalance — only the **pressure difference** between the two tubes produces a true output signal.

**Pressure inside the reference tube:** The reference tube is sealed at a **very high vacuum** — typically at a pressure **less than 10⁻⁶ torr** — so that its gas-conduction heat loss is essentially **zero**. This ensures that the bridge output is determined solely by the pressure being measured in the active tube.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Pirani gauge** used for low-pressure measurement. Discuss how the bridge unbalance is related to pressure. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A Pirani gauge uses a **tungsten filament** with cold resistance $R_0 = 80\ \Omega$ at $20\ ^\circ C$ and temperature coefficient $\beta = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}\ /\ ^\circ C$. When connected to a vacuum of $P = 5$ Pa, the filament temperature rises to $100\ ^\circ C$ while the wall temperature is $30\ ^\circ C$. The Wheatstone bridge excitation is $V_{ex} = 10\ \text{V}$ and the gauge constant is $\alpha = 6 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{W/(Pa·K)}$. Calculate:
   1. The hot resistance of the filament. **[2 Marks]**
   2. The heat loss to the gas per second. **[2 Marks]**
   3. The bridge sensitivity (V/Pa) at this operating point. **[3 Marks]**

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### (a) Construction and Working — **[7 Marks]**

**Construction:**

A Pirani gauge consists of a **glass or metal envelope** with a port connected to the vacuum system to be measured. Inside the envelope, a **fine filament of tungsten or platinum wire** is mounted coaxially between two electrical lead-throughs. The filament is typically **5–10 cm long** with a diameter of about **10–25 µm** and is electrically insulated from the envelope.

The gauge is connected into a **Wheatstone bridge** as follows:
- **Arm 1 (active):** Pirani filament in the measuring tube, exposed to the unknown pressure $P_x$.
- **Arm 2 (reference):** Identical Pirani filament sealed at high vacuum ($< 10^{-6}$ torr).
- **Arms 3 & 4:** Two precision fixed resistors.

**Working:**

1. The bridge is excited by a stable DC voltage $V_{ex}$.
2. Initially, with both tubes at the same (reference) vacuum, the bridge is balanced — $V_{out} = 0$.
3. As gas at pressure $P_x$ enters the measuring tube, the gas molecules **conduct heat away** from the filament at a rate $Q_{gas} = \alpha P_x (T_f - T_w)$.
4. This causes the filament temperature (and hence resistance $R_f$) to change.
5. The reference tube, still at near-zero pressure, suffers **no significant gas-conduction heat loss**, so its filament resistance remains unchanged.
6. The **resulting bridge unbalance voltage** $V_{out}$ is proportional to the change in filament resistance and, by calibration, **directly indicates the pressure $P_x$**.

The bridge can be operated in:
- **Constant Voltage** mode (output current indicates pressure)
- **Constant Temperature** mode (output voltage to maintain $R_f$ indicates pressure — most sensitive and most common in modern instruments)

**Block Diagram (Schematic):**

```
                Pirani Tube (Measuring)
                       |
              ┌────────┴────────┐
   Vex + ─────┤                  ├──── Vout ─► Amplifier ─► Display
              │   Wheatstone    │
              │     Bridge      │
              │                  │
   Vex - ─────┤                  ├────
              └────────┬────────┘
                       |
                Pirani Tube (Reference - Sealed at HV)
```

#### (b) Numerical Solution — **[7 Marks]**

**Given:** $R_0 = 80\ \Omega$, $T_0 = 20\ ^\circ C$, $\beta = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}\ /\ ^\circ C$, $P = 5$ Pa, $T_f = 100\ ^\circ C$, $T_w = 30\ ^\circ C$, $V_{ex} = 10$ V, $\alpha = 6 \times 10^{-4}$ W/(Pa·K).

**Step 1 — Hot resistance of filament:**

$$R_f = R_0 \left[1 + \beta (T_f - T_0)\right]$$

$$R_f = 80 \left[1 + 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times (100 - 20)\right]$$

$$R_f = 80 \left[1 + 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 80\right] = 80 \left[1 + 0.36\right]$$

$$\boxed{R_f = 108.8\ \Omega}$$

**Step 2 — Heat loss to the gas per second:**

$$Q_{gas} = \alpha \, P \, (T_f - T_w)$$

$$Q_{gas} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times (100 - 30)$$

$$Q_{gas} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 70 = 0.21\ \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{gas} = 0.21\ \text{W} = 210\ \text{mW}}$$

**Step 3 — Bridge sensitivity (V/Pa):**

$$S = \dfrac{V_{ex} \, \beta \, (T_f - T_w)}{4 P}$$

$$S = \dfrac{10 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 70}{4 \times 5}$$

$$S = \dfrac{3.15}{20} = 0.1575\ \text{V/Pa}$$

$$\boxed{S = 157.5\ \text{mV/Pa}}$$

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **limitations and sources of error** in a Pirani gauge. How can the upper and lower pressure limits be extended? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between **bridge output voltage and pressure change** for a Pirani gauge operated in **constant-current mode**, clearly stating all assumptions. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### (a) Limitations and Error Sources — **[7 Marks]**

**Major Limitations:**

1. **Limited Pressure Range (1 torr to 10⁻⁴ torr):**
   - **Upper limit (~1 torr):** Above this, **natural convection** currents in the gas become significant, breaking the linear relationship between thermal conductivity and pressure.
   - **Lower limit (~10⁻⁴ torr):** Below this, the **radiation heat loss** from the filament becomes comparable to (and eventually masks) the gas-conduction loss. Since radiation is pressure-independent, the gauge loses sensitivity.

2. **Gas-Type Dependence:** The thermal conductivity $k$ varies with the **type of gas** — hydrogen and helium have much higher $k$ than air. Hence, the gauge must be **recalibrated** for each gas (or a correction factor applied).

3. **Ambient Temperature Sensitivity:** Even with a reference tube, large ambient temperature swings cause errors because:
   - The temperature coefficient $\beta$ of the filament is temperature-dependent.
   - The reference tube wall and measuring tube wall may not be at exactly the same temperature.

4. **Contamination and Filament Aging:** 
   - Deposition of oil/condensable vapors on the filament alters its emissivity and effective $\beta$.
   - Oxidation (if the gauge is accidentally exposed to air while hot) changes filament resistance permanently.

5. **Non-linearity:** The relationship between bridge output and pressure is **non-linear** outside the central operating range — requires lookup tables or polynomial fitting.

**Extension of Range:**
- **Lower limit extension:** Use a **smaller filament area** (reduces radiation) and a **higher filament temperature** (increases gas-conduction signal) — but reduces filament life. Also use **low-emissivity coatings** on the filament.
- **Upper limit extension:** Use **forced convection suppression** (vertical orientation, small tube diameter) and **gas-specific calibration** to push the upper limit toward atmospheric pressure (modern "wide-range Pirani" gauges).

#### (b) Derivation of Bridge Output in Constant-Current Mode — **[7 Marks]**

**Assumptions:**
1. Thermal conductivity of the gas is **linearly proportional to pressure** (molecular flow regime, $Kn \geq 1$).
2. The filament is in **thermal equilibrium** (steady-state).
3. Heat loss through the **end-leads is constant** and is lumped into an offset $C_0$.
4. **Radiation losses** are small compared to gas-conduction losses (in the operating range).
5. Bridge unbalance is small: $\Delta R_f \ll R_0$.

**Derivation:**

At thermal equilibrium, the **electrical power supplied** to the filament equals the **total heat lost**:

$$I^2 R_f = \alpha P (T_f - T_w) + C_0$$

**Equation (1):** Energy balance.

Substitute the resistance-temperature relation:

$$R_f = R_0 \left[1 + \beta (T_f - T_0)\right] \Rightarrow T_f - T_0 = \dfrac{R_f - R_0}{R_0 \beta}$$

**Substitute into energy balance:**

$$I^2 R_f = \alpha P \left[\dfrac{R_f - R_0}{R_0 \beta} + (T_0 - T_w)\right] + C_0$$

**At the reference (calibration) pressure $P_0$**, the filament resistance is $R_{f,0}$. Differentiating with respect to $P$:

$$0 = \alpha (T_f - T_w) + \alpha P \cdot \dfrac{1}{R_0 \beta} \cdot \dfrac{dR_f}{dP}$$

Solving for $\dfrac{dR_f}{dP}$:

$$\dfrac{dR_f}{dP} = -\dfrac{\beta R_0 (T_f - T_w)}{P}$$

**Bridge output voltage** (small-signal approximation):

$$V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R_f}{R_0} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R_0} \cdot \dfrac{dR_f}{dP} \cdot \Delta P$$

**Substituting:**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -\dfrac{V_{ex} \, \beta \, (T_f - T_w)}{4 P} \, \Delta P}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that as pressure increases, filament temperature (and resistance) **decreases** — making the bridge output **negative-going** for positive pressure changes. The **magnitude** of the sensitivity is:

$$\vert S \vert = \dfrac{V_{ex} \, \beta \, (T_f - T_w)}{4 P}$$

**Key Observations:**
- $S \propto V_{ex}$ → higher excitation gives higher sensitivity (limited by filament burnout).
- $S \propto (T_f - T_w)$ → higher over-temperature gives better sensitivity.
- $S \propto 1/P$ → sensitivity **increases as pressure decreases** — the gauge is naturally most sensitive in its intended low-pressure regime.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Pirani Gauge Questions (Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the reference tube role:** Students often describe the Pirani gauge as using *only one* filament. In KTU board exams, you lose **1–2 marks** if you do not mention the **compensating reference tube** sealed at high vacuum.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong heat-loss equation:** The equation $Q = \alpha P (T_f - T_w)$ is valid **only in the molecular regime** ($Kn \geq 1$). Writing it without this condition loses **1 mark** in numericals.
>
> 3. **Sign errors in bridge sensitivity:** Many students miss the negative sign in $V_{out}$ and lose 1 mark. Always state **"the bridge output is negative-going for positive pressure change"** for full credit.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the radiation term at the lower limit:** When asked "what limits the lower range of a Pirani gauge?", the answer is **NOT** "filament burnout" — it is **radiation heat loss masking the gas-conduction signal**. This distinction is worth 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing the modes of operation:** Constant Voltage ≠ Constant Current ≠ Constant Temperature. Examiners frequently test this. Use a clear **comparison table** in your answer.
>
> 6. **No unit analysis:** In numericals, writing $S = 0.1575$ without units is penalized. Always write **"V/Pa"** or **"mV/Pa"**.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to mention the temperature coefficient β:** The change in resistance is meaningless without specifying the **material-dependent** temperature coefficient. State **$\beta$ for tungsten ($4.5 \times 10^{-3}\ /\ ^\circ C$) or platinum ($3.9 \times 10^{-3}\ /\ ^\circ C$)**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Pirani gauge** = **thermal conductivity vacuum gauge** for the low-pressure range **1 torr to 10⁻⁴ torr**.
- [x] Operates on the principle: **$k_{gas} \propto P$** in the **molecular flow regime** ($Kn \geq 1$).
- [x] **Construction:** Tungsten or platinum filament + reference tube + Wheatstone bridge.
- [x] **Reference tube** is sealed at $< 10^{-6}$ torr for **temperature compensation**.
- [x] **Master equation:** $Q_{gas} = \alpha P (T_f - T_w)$ — the entire operation rests on this.
- [x] **Resistance-temperature relation:** $R_f = R_0 [1 + \beta (T_f - T_0)]$.
- [x] **Bridge sensitivity:** $S = \dfrac{V_{ex} \, \beta \, (T_f - T_w)}{4 P}$ — **inversely proportional to P**.
- [x] **Three modes of operation:** Constant Voltage, Constant Current, **Constant Temperature (most sensitive)**.
- [x] **Upper limit (~1 torr):** Set by onset of **natural convection** in the gas.
- [x] **Lower limit (~10⁻⁴ torr):** Set by **radiation heat loss** becoming comparable to gas-conduction loss.
- [x] **Sources of error:** Gas-type dependence, ambient temperature, contamination/oxidation of filament, non-linearity.
- [x] **Filament materials:** Tungsten ($\beta \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-3}\ /\ ^\circ C$) and Platinum ($\beta \approx 3.9 \times 10^{-3}\ /\ ^\circ C$) — chosen for high $\beta$ and high melting point.
- [x] **Sensitivity ∝ excitation voltage** → use highest safe $V_{ex}$ for best signal.
- [x] **Sensitivity ∝ over-temperature** $(T_f - T_w)$ → higher filament temperature gives better sensitivity but shorter filament life.
- [x] **Applications:** Semiconductor fabrication, mass spectrometer fore-vacuum, freeze-drying, lamp manufacturing, space simulation chambers.
- [x] **KTU One-liner:** *"The Pirani gauge is essentially a **'thermal analog of an ohmmeter for vacuum'** — it reads pressure by reading how fast a hot wire cools."*

| **"C-L-R-G"** | **C**onvection (upper limit), **L**inear regime (operation), **R**adiation (lower limit), **G**as type (error source) |
