h &= \dfrac{k}{D}\left[A' + B'\left(\dfrac{\rho U D}{\mu}\right)^n\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Insert into the energy balance.**

Because the wire resistance $R_w$ depends linearly on temperature, $R_w = R_0(1+\alpha(T_w - T_f))$. After substitution and rearrangement:

$$\begin{aligned}
\underbrace{I^2 R_w}_{\text{(const)}}\,\propto\, A + B\,U^n
\end{aligned}$$

In a **constant-temperature anemometer (CTA)**, the voltage $E$ is held proportional to $I$, yielding the famous empirical **King's Law**:

$$\boxed{\,E^2 = A + B\,U^n\,}$$

Typical values for air: $n \approx 0.45 - 0.50$. The constants $A, B, n$ are found by calibration with known velocities.

**Velocity from a single hot-wire reading:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U = \left(\dfrac{E^2 - A}{B}\right)^{1/n}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Hot-Wire Anemometer)

**Problem:** A hot-wire anemometer was calibrated in air and found to obey King's Law:
$E^2 = 0.6 + 0.4\,U^{0.5}$
where $E$ is in volts and $U$ is in m/s. Find the velocity when the output voltage is **$E = 1.2$ V**.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E^2 &= A + B\,U^n
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.2)^2 &= 0.6 + 0.4\,U^{0.5}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
1.44 &= 0.6 + 0.4\,U^{0.5}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
0.4\,U^{0.5} &= 0.84
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
U^{0.5} &= 2.1
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= (2.1)^2 = 4.41
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,U \approx 4.41 \text{ m/s}\,}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: LDA Doppler Frequency

**Setup:** Two coherent laser beams of wavelength $\lambda$ cross at angle $\theta$. A seed particle moving with velocity $U_\perp$ (perpendicular to the bisector) crosses the interference fringes.

**Step 1 — Fringe spacing.** From two-beam interference geometry:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_f &= \dfrac{\lambda}{2\sin(\theta/2)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Doppler burst frequency.** A particle crossing one fringe produces one cycle of scattered light. Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_D &= \dfrac{U_\perp}{d_f}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two results.**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_D &= \dfrac{U_\perp}{\lambda/(2\sin(\theta/2))}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_D = \dfrac{2\,U_\perp \sin(\theta/2)}{\lambda}\,}$$

This is the **principal LDA equation** tested in KTU examinations.

**Velocity recovery:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U_\perp &= \dfrac{f_D \lambda}{2\sin(\theta/2)}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Pitot-Static | Hot-Wire | LDA | Ultrasonic Doppler | Vane / Multi-hole |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Measures** | Magnitude | Magnitude | 1-D component (× 2 → 2-D) | 1-D component | Magnitude + direction |
| **Invasiveness** | Slightly invasive | Invasive | **Non-invasive** | Non-invasive | Invasive |
| **Bandwidth** | ~ Hz | ~ 1 MHz | ~ 10 kHz | ~ kHz | ~ Hz |
| **Spatial resolution** | ~ mm | ~ 100 µm | ~ 10 µm | ~ cm | ~ cm |
| **Fluid needed** | Liquid or gas | Gas or liquid | Needs seed particles | Needs scatterers | Liquid or gas |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | **High** | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Calibration** | Once (Bernoulli) | Frequent (drift) | Not required (absolute) | Once | Once |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Local Flow Velocity Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Flowing Fluid at Point] --> B[Sensor Probe]
    B --> C{Sensor Type}
    C -->|Pressure| D[Pitot / Cobra Probe]
    C -->|Thermal| E[Hot-Wire Anemometer]
    C -->|Optical| F[Laser Beams and Particle]
    C -->|Acoustic| G[Ultrasonic Transducer]
    D --> H[Pressure Transducer]
    E --> I[Bridge and Amplifier]
    F --> J[Photodetector]
    G --> K[Signal Processor]
    H --> L[Signal Conditioning]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Display or DAQ]
    M --> N[Local Velocity U and Direction]
```

## 4.2 Pitot-Static Tube — Internal Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Flow[Incompressible Flow U, Ps] -->|kinetic energy| Nose[Stagnation Nose]
    Nose -->|captures| Pt[Total Pressure Pt]
    Flow -->|side holes| Side[Static Taps]
    Side -->|senses| Ps[Static Pressure Ps]
    Pt --> Diff[Differential Pressure Sensor]
    Ps --> Diff
    Diff -->|delta P| Calc[Apply Bernoulli: U = sqrt 2 delta P over rho]
    Calc --> Out[Local Velocity U m per s]
```

## 4.3 Hot-Wire Anemometer — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heated Tungsten Wire] --> B[Resistance R depends on T]
    B --> C[Bridge Circuit]
    C --> D{Operating Mode}
    D -->|Constant Current| E[CCA Amplifier]
    D -->|Constant Temperature| F[CTA Feedback Loop]
    E --> G[Output Voltage E]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply King Law: E squared equals A plus B U to the n]
    H --> I[Display Local U]
```

## 4.4 LDA Optical Bench — Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[He-Ne Laser 632.8 nm] --> BS[Beam Splitter]
    BS --> M1[Mirror 1]
    BS --> M2[Mirror 2]
    M1 --> X[Crossing Point and Probe Volume]
    M2 --> X
    Flow[Flow with seed particles] --> X
    X --> PD[Photodetector]
    PD --> F[Burst Frequency fD]
    F --> Calc[U perpendicular equals fD lambda over 2 sin theta over 2]
    Calc --> Out[Local Velocity U]
```

## 4.5 Direction-Meter Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Flow[Local 3D Flow Vector] --> Yaw[Yaw Vane aligns in x-y plane]
    Flow --> Pitch[Pitch Vane aligns in x-z plane]
    Yaw --> Y1[Yaw Angle beta]
    Pitch --> P1[Pitch Angle alpha]
    Y1 --> Vec[Reconstruct Vector]
    P1 --> Vec
    Vec --> Mag[Compute Magnitude U]
    Vec --> Dir[Compute Direction unit vector]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Physical schematics of the probes are drawn to scale in standard KTU textbooks; the diagrams above represent the *information flow* rather than the geometric layout, in compliance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE schematic safeguard.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a "local flow velocity meter." Give two examples and state one difference between a local velocity meter and a flow rate meter.*

**Model Answer:**
A local flow velocity meter is an instrument that measures the velocity of a fluid at a *single point* in the flow field, as opposed to the average flow rate over a cross-section.  
**Examples:** (i) Pitot-static tube, (ii) hot-wire anemometer (or LDA).  
**Difference:** A local velocity meter measures at a *point* with very small spatial averaging volume (mm³), while a flow rate meter (orifice, rotameter) integrates the velocity profile to give the *total volumetric or mass flow rate* through a pipe.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State King's Law as used in hot-wire anemometry. What is the typical value of the velocity exponent $n$ for air?*

**Model Answer:**
King's Law relates the bridge output voltage of a hot-wire anemometer to the local fluid velocity:

$$E^2 = A + B\,U^n$$

For air, the velocity exponent $n$ typically lies in the range **0.45 to 0.50** (often taken as $n = 0.5$).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**A(a)** [7 marks] *Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a Pitot-static tube. Derive the expression for the local velocity of the fluid in terms of the differential pressure measured.*

**A(b)** [7 marks] *A Pitot-static tube is used to measure the local velocity of water in a pipe. The differential manometer reads 250 mm of mercury. Calculate the local velocity. Take $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $C_p = 0.98$.*

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

A Pitot-static tube consists of two concentric tubes: an inner impact tube with its opening facing the flow (captures the **stagnation / total pressure $P_t$**) and an outer tube with side holes perpendicular to the flow (captures the **static pressure $P_s$**). The two pressures are led to a differential manometer or transducer.

Applying Bernoulli's equation along a streamline from the undisturbed flow (point 1) to the stagnation point at the nose (point 2), with $U_2 = 0$ and $z_1 = z_2$:

$$P_s + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 = P_t$$

Rearranging:

$$U = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_t - P_s)}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho}}$$

A real probe has the corrected form $U = C_p\sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}$ with $C_p \approx 0.98$.

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

Differential pressure corresponding to a 250 mm Hg column:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{water})\,g\,h \\
        &= (13600 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.250 \\
        &= 12600 \times 9.81 \times 0.250 \\
        &= 30\,901.5 \text{ N/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Apply Pitot-static equation with probe coefficient:

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= C_p \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho_{water}}} \\
  &= 0.98 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 30\,901.5}{1000}} \\
  &= 0.98 \times \sqrt{61.803} \\
  &= 0.98 \times 7.861 \\
  &\approx 7.704 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,U \approx 7.70 \text{ m/s}\,}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**B(a)** [7 marks] *Describe the working principle of a Laser Doppler Anemometer (LDA). Derive the expression relating the Doppler frequency to the local velocity.*

**B(b)** [7 marks] *A hot-wire anemometer in air obeys King's Law $E^2 = 0.45 + 0.35\,U^{0.5}$. The output voltage is $E = 1.5$ V. Determine the local air velocity.*

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

A Laser Doppler Anemometer is a non-intrusive optical instrument that measures the local velocity of a fluid by detecting the **Doppler shift** of laser light scattered from small seed particles carried in the flow. A single-frequency laser (typically He–Ne, $\lambda = 632.8$ nm) is split into two beams of equal intensity by a beam splitter. The two beams are focused so that they cross at the measurement volume. The crossing forms an **interference fringe pattern** of spacing:

$$d_f = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\sin(\theta/2)}$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between the two beams. A particle moving through the fringes with velocity $U_\perp$ produces a scattered-light burst whose **frequency** is:

$$f_D = \dfrac{U_\perp}{d_f} = \dfrac{2\,U_\perp \sin(\theta/2)}{\lambda}$$

The photodetector records $f_D$ from which $U_\perp$ is computed. Because LDA measures only one component, two perpendicular LDA systems are used to obtain 2-D velocity.

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

Apply King's Law:

$$\begin{aligned}
E^2 &= A + B\,U^n \\
(1.5)^2 &= 0.45 + 0.35\,U^{0.5} \\
2.25 &= 0.45 + 0.35\,U^{0.5} \\
0.35\,U^{0.5} &= 1.80 \\
U^{0.5} &= \dfrac{1.80}{0.35} = 5.1429 \\
U &= (5.1429)^2 = 26.45
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,U \approx 26.45 \text{ m/s}\,}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing **stagnation** ($P_t$) with **static** ($P_s$) pressure in Pitot-static derivations. Always state the *boundary condition* $U_2 = 0$ explicitly.
> 2. Forgetting the **probe coefficient $C_p$** in numerical problems. Examiners award 1 mark for stating the corrected form.
> 3. Using $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ for air — always use $\rho_{air} \approx 1.225$ kg/m³ unless told otherwise.
> 4. In King's Law problems, students often forget to **square-root** when $E$ is given (not $E^2$) — leading to wrong powers of 10.
> 5. In LDA problems, mixing $\theta$ (full angle) with $\theta/2$ (half-angle) — examiners check the geometry carefully.
> 6. In manometer-based Pitot problems, the differential pressure is $\Delta P = (\rho_{fluid,manometer} - \rho_{flow})\cdot g \cdot h$, **not** simply $\rho_{Hg}\,g\,h$ — losing a mark for fluids other than mercury.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Local velocity meter** = instrument measuring fluid speed at a *point* (Pitot, hot-wire, LDA, ultrasonic, vane).
- **Pitot-static tube (Prandtl tube):**
  - Captures $P_t$ at nose, $P_s$ at side holes.
  - Core equation: $U = C_p\sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}$ (incompressible).
  - Compressible form uses $\gamma$ and $P_t/P_s$ ratio.
- **Hot-wire anemometer:**
  - **King's Law:** $E^2 = A + B\,U^n$, with $n \approx 0.45$–$0.5$ in air.
  - Two modes: **CTA** (high bandwidth, common) and **CCA** (simpler).
  - Frequent calibration required because the wire ages and **contaminates**.
- **LDA / LDV:**
  - Non-invasive, absolute calibration (no need for reference velocity).
  - $f_D = \dfrac{2U_\perp \sin(\theta/2)}{\lambda}$; uses He–Ne 632.8 nm.
  - Requires **seed particles** in the flow.
- **Ultrasonic Doppler:** $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_0 U\cos\phi}{c}$.
- **Direction meters:**
  - **Yaw–pitch vane** for 2-D; **5-/7-hole Cobra probe** for 3-D.
  - Pressure coefficients $C_{p,i} = (P_i - P_s)/(P_t - P_s)$ reconstructed into a vector.
- **Numerical checklist for KTU:**
  - Manometer reading → $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f)\,g\,h$.
  - Use correct $\rho$ for the **flow fluid**, not the manometer fluid.
  - Include $C_p$ when given.
  - For hot-wire, always **square $E$** first.
- **Real-world signatures:**
  - Aircraft Pitot tubes (icing hazard).
  - Wind tunnels (hot-wire + LDA).
  - Cleanroom HVAC (hot-wire).
  - CFD validation (LDA benchmark).
- **Memorize the Formula Sheet (Section 2.7)** — at least 8 of 10 equations appear in every KTU paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hot Wire Anemometer Velometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hot Wire Anemometer Velometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hot Wire Anemometer (HWA)**
> A Hot Wire Anemometer is a thermal flow measurement instrument used to measure the instantaneous velocity and turbulence characteristics of a flowing fluid (typically a gas or air) by exploiting the convective heat loss from a small, electrically heated sensor exposed to the flow. The heat dissipated by the wire is functionally related to the local fluid velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hot Wire Velometer (HWV)**
> A Hot Wire Velometer is the field/industrial-portable implementation of the hot wire anemometer principle, packaged as a hand-held or panel-mounted instrument used for direct read-out of flow velocity in ducts, ventilation systems, and laboratory benches. It uses the same King's Law thermal principle as the laboratory-grade HWA but is optimised for spot measurements rather than turbulence spectral analysis.

**Symbol Nomenclature used in this module:**

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit |
|--------|----------|---------|
| $U$ | Fluid velocity (free stream) | **m/s** |
| $E$ | Voltage across hot wire | **V** |
| $I$ | Current through hot wire | **A** |
| $R_w$ | Hot wire resistance | **Ω** |
| $T_w$ | Hot wire temperature | **K** |
| $T_f$ | Fluid (free stream) temperature | **K** |
| $h$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | **W/(m²·K)** |
| $A$ | Surface area of hot wire | **m²** |
| $D$ | Diameter of hot wire | **m** |
| $L$ | Length of hot wire | **m** |
| $k$ | Thermal conductivity of fluid | **W/(m·K)** |
| $\rho$ | Density of fluid | **kg/m³** |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity of fluid | **N·s/m²** |
| $Nu$ | Nusselt number | dimensionless |
| $Re$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Cold-Wind-on-Skin" Analogy

Imagine stepping out of a hot shower into a breezy bathroom. The stronger the breeze, the colder you feel. That is **convective cooling**: moving fluid carries heat away from your warm skin faster than still air does.

A Hot Wire Anemometer works on **exactly the same physical idea**, but in reverse and with ruthless precision:

1. A hair-thin wire (typically **5 µm diameter tungsten** or **platinum**) is electrically heated to a temperature of about **150 °C to 300 °C** above the surrounding fluid.
2. When the fluid flows past it, the moving fluid "sweeps heat away" — the same way the bathroom breeze cooled you.
3. The faster the flow, the more heat is carried away.
4. The instrument measures **how much electrical energy is needed to keep the wire at a fixed temperature** (or measures the **temperature drop if current is held constant**).
5. From that electrical measurement, the fluid velocity is mathematically recovered.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Velometer"?** The suffix *-meter* denotes a measuring device, while *-vane* / *-anemo* refers to wind. A *velometer* is essentially a *velocity meter* — a portable instrument whose purpose is to *directly display fluid velocity*, as opposed to a research-grade anemometer which may output raw voltage for spectral analysis of turbulence.

---

## 1.3 Classification by Operating Mode

A Hot Wire Anemometer can be operated in three distinct electrical modes. KTU frequently tests this classification.

| Mode | What's held CONSTANT | What's MEASURED | Output relationship |
|------|----------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **CCA — Constant Current Anemometer** | Current $I$ | Wire temperature drop → change in $R_w$ → voltage $E$ changes | $E \propto \sqrt{U}$ |
| **CVA — Constant Voltage Anemometer** | Voltage $E$ | Wire temperature (via $R_w$) varies with flow | $E \propto (T_w - T_f)^{1/2}$ implicitly |
| **CTA — Constant Temperature Anemometer** | Wire temperature $T_w$ (i.e., $R_w$) | Current / voltage required to maintain $T_w$ | $E^{2} = A + B \cdot U^{n}$ (King's Law) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The **Constant Temperature Anemometer (CTA)** is the most widely used mode in practice because it offers the highest frequency response (up to **500 kHz**), best stability against ambient temperature drift, and is best suited for turbulence measurements. Always state CTA when asked about "the most common" mode.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Convective Heat Loss

The hot wire loses heat to the surrounding fluid by **forced convection**. The rate of heat loss $Q$ from the wire is governed by Newton's law of cooling:

$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_f)$$

where:
- $Q$ = heat loss rate from wire (**W**)
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²·K**)
- $A$ = lateral surface area of the wire = $\pi D L$ (**m²**)
- $T_w - T_f$ = temperature difference between hot wire and free stream (**K**)

The electrical power supplied to the wire equals the heat loss rate (under steady state):

$$P_{elec} = I^{2} R_w = \frac{E^{2}}{R_w} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_f)$$

This is the **master energy balance equation** of the HWA.

---

## 2.2 Why Heat Transfer Coefficient $h$ Depends on Velocity

For forced convection over a thin cylinder (the hot wire) immersed in cross-flow, empirical correlations give the Nusselt number as a function of Reynolds number:

$$Nu = \frac{h D}{k} = C \cdot Re^{n}$$

where $n \approx 0.5$ for the relevant range of Re. Substituting $Re = \dfrac{\rho U D}{\mu}$:

$$\frac{h D}{k} = C \cdot \left(\frac{\rho U D}{\mu}\right)^{n}$$

Solving for $h$:

$$h = \frac{k \cdot C}{D} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho U D}{\mu}\right)^{n}$$

$$h = C_{1} \cdot U^{n}$$

where $C_{1} = \dfrac{k \cdot C}{D} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho D}{\mu}\right)^{n}$ is a lumped constant for a given fluid and wire geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The heat transfer coefficient is **not constant** — it scales as a power law of velocity. Higher velocity → more vigorous fluid mixing around the wire → more efficient heat removal → higher $h$ → more heat loss → more electrical power required to maintain $T_w$.

---

## 2.3 King's Law — The Cornerstone Equation

Substituting $h = C_{1} U^{n}$ into the master energy balance for a **Constant Temperature** (constant $T_w$, hence constant $R_w$ since $R_w \propto T_w$) anemometer:

$$E^{2} = R_{w}^{2} \cdot C_{1} \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_f) \cdot U^{n} + E_{0}^{2}$$

To account for the small natural-convection heat loss even at zero velocity (the "free convection" offset), an empirical offset term $A$ is added. The result is **King's Law (1914)**:

$$\boxed{E^{2} = A + B \cdot U^{n}}$$

where:
- $E$ = output voltage of the anemometer (V)
- $A$ = zero-velocity offset (V²), typically small and positive
- $B$ = sensitivity coefficient (V² · s/m)
- $n$ = velocity exponent, typically **0.45 to 0.50** for air (often approximated as $n = 0.5$)
- $U$ = fluid velocity (m/s)

A very common engineering simplification used in KTU problems takes $n = 1/2$:

$$\boxed{E^{2} = A + B \sqrt{U}}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations — Memorise for KTU University Exam**

| # | Equation | Meaning / When to use |
|---|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | $E^{2} = A + B \cdot U^{n}$ | King's Law (general form) |
| 2 | $E^{2} = A + B \sqrt{U}$ | King's Law with $n = 0.5$ (most common textbook form) |
| 3 | $P = I^{2} R_w = h A (T_w - T_f)$ | Master energy balance — always start here |
| 4 | $h = C_{1} U^{n}$ | Convective coefficient as velocity function |
| 5 | $Nu = C \cdot Re^{n}$ | Empirical Nusselt–Reynolds correlation |
| 6 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho U D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number of flow over wire |
| 7 | $R_w = R_{0} \left[1 + \alpha (T_w - T_{0})\right]$ | Hot wire resistance with temperature coefficient $\alpha$ |
| 8 | $A = \pi D L$ | Lateral surface area of cylindrical wire |
| 9 | $E^{2} - A = B U^{n}$ | Rearranged form to solve for $U$ |
| 10 | $U = \left[\dfrac{E^{2} - A}{B}\right]^{1/n}$ | Velocity recovered from measured voltage |
| 11 | $\tau \approx \dfrac{\rho_{w} c_{w} D^{2}}{k_{f}}$ | Thermal time constant — sets frequency response |
| 12 | $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau}$ | Cut-off frequency of HWA |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why HWA / Velometer is chosen |
|--------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Wind tunnel aerodynamics** | Measures turbulent fluctuations up to 500 kHz — pitot-static tubes cannot resolve turbulence |
| **HVAC & Building Services** | Hot Wire Velometer directly displays air velocity at diffusers/grilles in m/s or fpm |
| **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)** | Detects drafts, measures face velocity at fume hoods (occupational safety) |
| **Combustion & Engine Research** | Measures fluctuating velocity inside cylinder/jet flows |
| **Process Industry** | Clean-room air flow, laminar flow cabinets, semiconductor fabs |
| **Environmental Monitoring** | Atmospheric boundary-layer studies, pollution dispersion |
| **Medical / Pharmaceutical** | Sterile laminar flow validation in operating theatres and isolators |

> [!TIP]
> **Why not use a Pitot-static tube for these?** Pitot tubes give only **mean velocity** integrated over a finite volume; they have **slow response** and **inadequate spatial resolution** for thin boundary layers. HWA measures at a **point**, with **microsecond response**, in volumes as small as a few cubic millimetres.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of King's Law (Step-by-Step, KTU Valuation Style)

**Starting Point:** Energy balance — electrical power dissipated in the wire equals convective heat loss to the fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption Set for King's Law derivation:**
> 1. Wire is long, thin, and operates in cross-flow.
> 2. Constant temperature mode (CTA): $T_w = $ constant, therefore $R_w = $ constant.
> 3. Wire is in thermal equilibrium — no net heat storage in the wire.
> 4. Radiation and end-conduction losses are lumped into the offset constant $A$.
> 5. Forced convection dominates over natural convection.

---

### STEP 1 — Write the master energy balance

The electrical power supplied to the wire is $P_{elec} = \dfrac{E^{2}}{R_w}$, and this equals the convective heat loss $Q$ from the wire surface to the fluid.

$$\frac{E^{2}}{R_w} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_f)$$

---

### STEP 2 — Express the convective heat transfer coefficient $h$ via Nusselt number

By definition of Nusselt number, $Nu = \dfrac{h D}{k}$, where $D$ is wire diameter and $k$ is fluid thermal conductivity.

$$h = \frac{Nu \cdot k}{D}$$

---

### STEP 3 — Use the empirical Nusselt–Reynolds correlation for cross-flow over a cylinder

For a thin cylinder in cross-flow at low-to-moderate Reynolds numbers, the standard empirical correlation is:

$$Nu = C \cdot Re^{n}$$

where $C$ and $n$ are empirical constants (for air, $C \approx 0.683$ and $n \approx 0.466$ for $Re$ in the range 0.02 to 44).

---

### STEP 4 — Substitute $Re = \dfrac{\rho U D}{\mu}$

$$Nu = C \cdot \left(\frac{\rho U D}{\mu}\right)^{n}$$

---

### STEP 5 — Substitute back into $h$

$$h = \frac{k}{D} \cdot C \cdot \left(\frac{\rho U D}{\mu}\right)^{n}$$

Collecting the velocity-independent terms into a single constant $C_{1}$:

$$h = C_{1} \cdot U^{n}$$

where

$$C_{1} = \frac{k \cdot C}{D} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho D}{\mu}\right)^{n}$$

---

### STEP 6 — Substitute $h$ back into the energy balance

$$\frac{E^{2}}{R_w} = C_{1} \cdot U^{n} \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_f)$$

Solving for $E^{2}$:

$$E^{2} = R_w \cdot C_{1} \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_f) \cdot U^{n}$$

---

### STEP 7 — Introduce the zero-velocity offset $A_{0}$ (King's empirical correction)

In practice, even at $U = 0$, there is a small heat loss due to natural convection and radiation. King (1914) introduced an empirical offset term $A_{0}$ so that the equation fits real data at low velocities.

Define:
- $A = E^{2}\big\vert_{U=0}$ — the squared output voltage at zero flow
- $B = R_w \cdot C_{1} \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_f)$ — the sensitivity coefficient

The final form of **King's Law** is:

$$\boxed{E^{2} = A + B \cdot U^{n}}$$

---

### STEP 8 — Simplification for KTU problems (assume $n = 0.5$)

For the typical KTU-level problem, $n$ is approximated as $1/2$:

$$E^{2} = A + B \sqrt{U}$$

The inverse form to recover velocity from a measured voltage is:

$$\boxed{U = \left(\frac{E^{2} - A}{B}\right)^{2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem (KTU Model):** A hot wire anemometer obeys King's Law in the form $E^{2} = A + B \sqrt{U}$. At zero velocity, the bridge output is $E_{0} = 1.20$ V. At a calibration velocity of $U_{1} = 4.0$ m/s, the output is $E_{1} = 4.50$ V. The wire is operated at a constant overheat ratio of 0.8.
>
> **(a)** Determine the constants $A$ and $B$.
> **(b)** Find the velocity $U_{2}$ when the measured output is $E_{2} = 3.00$ V.
> **(c)** Estimate the percentage change in output voltage when velocity changes from 4 m/s to 9 m/s.

---

### Solution — Part (a): Find $A$ and $B$

**Step 1:** At $U = 0$, the offset is:

$$A = E_{0}^{2} = (1.20)^{2} = 1.44 \; \text{V}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** At $U_{1} = 4.0$ m/s:

$$E_{1}^{2} = A + B \sqrt{U_{1}}$$

$$(4.50)^{2} = 1.44 + B \cdot \sqrt{4.0}$$

$$20.25 = 1.44 + B \cdot 2.0$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $B$:

$$B = \frac{20.25 - 1.44}{2.0} = \frac{18.81}{2.0} = 9.405 \; \text{V}^{2} \cdot \text{s/m}$$

**Final Answer (a):** $\; A = 1.44 \; \text{V}^{2}, \quad B = 9.405 \; \text{V}^{2} \cdot \text{s/m}$

---

### Solution — Part (b): Find $U_{2}$ at $E_{2} = 3.00$ V

**Step 1:** Square the measured voltage:

$$E_{2}^{2} = (3.00)^{2} = 9.00 \; \text{V}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into King's Law and solve for $\sqrt{U_{2}}$:

$$9.00 = 1.44 + 9.405 \cdot \sqrt{U_{2}}$$

$$\sqrt{U_{2}} = \frac{9.00 - 1.44}{9.405} = \frac{7.56}{9.405} = 0.8038 \; \sqrt{\text{m/s}}$$

**Step 3:** Square both sides to get $U_{2}$:

$$U_{2} = (0.8038)^{2} \approx 0.646 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Final Answer (b):** $\; U_{2} \approx 0.65 \; \text{m/s}$

---

### Solution — Part (c): Percentage change in $E$ for $U = 4 \to 9$ m/s

**Step 1:** Compute $E$ at $U = 4$ m/s (already given): $E_{4} = 4.50$ V.

**Step 2:** Compute $E$ at $U = 9$ m/s:

$$E_{9}^{2} = 1.44 + 9.405 \cdot \sqrt{9} = 1.44 + 9.405 \cdot 3 = 1.44 + 28.215 = 29.655$$

$$E_{9} = \sqrt{29.655} \approx 5.446 \; \text{V}$$

**Step 3:** Percentage change in $E$:

$$\% \Delta E = \frac{E_{9} - E_{4}}{E_{4}} \times 100 = \frac{5.446 - 4.50}{4.50} \times 100$$

$$\% \Delta E = \frac{0.946}{4.50} \times 100 \approx 21.0 \;\%$$

**Final Answer (c):** $\; \% \Delta E \approx +21.0\%$ (i.e., output voltage increases by ~21%)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Operating Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Table — KTU Board-Favourite Question**

| Parameter | CCA (Constant Current) | CVA (Constant Voltage) | CTA (Constant Temperature) |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Held constant | $I$ | $E$ | $T_w$ (and hence $R_w$) |
| Measured quantity | $E$ (or $R_w$) varies with $U$ | $T_w$ varies with $U$ | $E$ (or $I$) varies with $U$ |
| Frequency response | **Low** (thermal lag) | Lowest | **Highest** (up to 500 kHz) |
| Sensitivity to ambient temp | High | High | **Low** (inherently compensated) |
| Calibration drift | Significant | Significant | **Minimal** |
| Best for | Mean velocity | Simple velometers | Turbulence & fluctuating flows |
| King's Law applicability | $E \propto U^{n/2}$ | $E \propto (T_w - T_f)^{1/2} U^{n/2}$ | **$E^{2} = A + B U^{n}$** (cleanest form) |
| Closed-loop feedback? | No | No | **Yes** (Wheatstone bridge feedback) |
| Typical application | Survey work | Cheap velometers | Research-grade anemometry |

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Thermal Time Constant (for Frequency Response)

The hot wire's response to a step change in flow is governed by its thermal time constant $\tau$. Using lumped capacitance:

$$\tau = \frac{m_{w} \cdot c_{w}}{h \cdot A} = \frac{\rho_{w} c_{w} \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L}{h \cdot \pi D L} = \frac{\rho_{w} c_{w} D}{4 h}$$

Using $h = C_{1} U^{n}$:

$$\tau = \frac{\rho_{w} c_{w} D}{4 C_{1} U^{n}}$$

The cut-off frequency of the HWA is:

$$f_{c} = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau} = \frac{2 C_{1} U^{n}}{\pi \rho_{w} c_{w} D}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why $D$ matters:** Reducing the wire diameter (e.g., from 25 µm to 5 µm) **quadratically reduces** the mass and hence the thermal lag, dramatically improving frequency response. This is why research-grade HWAs use **5 µm** wires.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Constant Temperature Hot Wire Anemometer (CTA)

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Architecture:** The diagram below maps the closed-loop signal path of a CTA system. Each block performs a single, well-defined role.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[DC Power Supply] --> WHB[Wheatstone Bridge with Hot Wire Sensor]
    WHB --> DIFF[Error Detector / Differential Amplifier]
    DIFF --> CP[Compensation Network Lead Lag]
    CP --> PA[Power Amplifier]
    PA --> WHB
    PA --> VM[Voltmeter E for Output]
    VM --> DSP[Display Readout in m per s]
    FLOW[Flow U over Hot Wire] -.convective heat loss.-> WHB
    style PS fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD
    style WHB fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style DIFF fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style CP fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style PA fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C
    style VM fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F
    style DSP fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F
    style FLOW fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **DC Power Supply** | Provides excitation to the bridge; precision regulated to minimise drift. |
| **Wheatstone Bridge with Hot Wire** | The sensing element: $R_w$ forms one arm; small unbalance = error signal. |
| **Error Detector / Diff. Amplifier** | Amplifies bridge unbalance voltage proportional to $\Delta T$ between wire and setpoint. |
| **Compensation Network (Lead-Lag)** | Provides frequency compensation; ensures closed-loop stability, extends bandwidth. |
| **Power Amplifier** | Supplies heating current to maintain $T_w$ constant; output $E$ is the anemometer signal. |
| **Voltmeter (E)** | Measures the bridge excitation voltage $E$ — this is the linear output of the CTA. |
| **Display** | Computes $U = [(E^2 - A)/B]^{1/n}$ and displays in m/s, fpm, or other units. |
| **Flow over Wire** | The physical disturbance — convective heat loss changes $R_w$, bridge unbalances, feedback restores it. |

---

## 4.2 Sensor Probe Geometry — Side-View Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PRB[Support Prongs 2 mm apart] --> W[Hot Wire 5 micron diameter Tungsten]
    W -. length L 1 to 3 mm .-> PRB
    FLOW[Fluid Flow U] -->|cross flow| W
    style PRB fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B
    style W fill:#FF8A65,stroke:#BF360C
    style FLOW fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E
```

**Sensor Physical Description:**

| Component | Specification |
|-----------|---------------|
| Hot wire material | Tungsten (W) or Platinum (Pt) — high $\alpha$, high melting point |
| Wire diameter | 5 µm (research) to 25 µm (industrial) |
| Active length $L$ | 1 mm to 3 mm |
| Support prongs | Steel needle prongs; spacing 2 mm typical |
| Operating temperature | 150 °C to 300 °C above ambient |

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hot Wire Velometer (Field / Industrial Version)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SNS[Hot Wire Probe Mounted on Telescopic Rod] --> BRG[Bridge and Signal Conditioning]
    BRG --> AMP[Amplifier and Lineariser]
    AMP --> MC[Microcontroller with Kings Law Inverse]
    MC --> LCD[LCD Display showing U in m per s]
    KBD[Keyboard for Units and Calibration] --> MC
    style SNS fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57F17
    style BRG fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style AMP fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style MC fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B
    style LCD fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F
    style KBD fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C
```

**Interpretation:** The Hot Wire Velometer adds a **microcontroller** that numerically inverts King's Law so the user sees velocity directly on an LCD, and accepts **keyboard inputs** to switch between m/s, fpm, or knots and to enter field calibration constants.

---

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Feedback Concept (Textual Schematic)

> **Set Point:** Operator sets the desired wire overheat ratio (e.g., $T_w - T_f = 200$ °C).
> **Sensor:** $R_w$ rises if flow drops (less cooling) and falls if flow rises.
> **Bridge:** Converts $\Delta R_w$ into an error voltage $\varepsilon$.
> **Controller:** Drives power amplifier to inject more / less current.
> **Actuator:** Heating current $I$ adjusts to bring $T_w$ back to setpoint.
> **Result:** $T_w$ is held constant; the voltage $E$ required to do so is the **monotonic function of $U$** described by King's Law.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, May 2024]**

---

### Q1. (3 Marks)
**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Constant Temperature Hot Wire Anemometer. Why is this mode preferred over the constant current mode?**

**Model Answer:**

A Constant Temperature Anemometer (CTA) operates as a closed-loop thermal servo system.

**Block Diagram (textual):**

$$\text{Bridge with } R_w \rightarrow \text{Error Detector} \rightarrow \text{Amplifier} \rightarrow \text{Power Amp} \rightarrow \text{Heating Current} \rightarrow R_w$$

**Working principle:**
1. The hot wire $R_w$ forms one arm of a Wheatstone bridge.
2. A set-point resistor fixes the desired wire temperature (overheat ratio).
3. When fluid flow increases, convective cooling reduces $T_w$ and hence $R_w$, unbalancing the bridge.
4. The error voltage is amplified and fed back to a power amplifier that **increases** the heating current to restore $T_w$.
5. At steady state, the **voltage $E$ required to maintain $T_w$** is a unique, monotonic function of fluid velocity $U$, given by King's Law: $E^2 = A + B \cdot U^{n}$.

**Why CTA is preferred over CCA:**
- **Higher frequency response** (up to 500 kHz vs. < 1 kHz for CCA) — wire is held at constant $T_w$, eliminating thermal lag.
- **Lower calibration drift** — inherent compensation for ambient temperature changes.
- **Better linearity** over a wide velocity range.
- **Superior signal-to-noise ratio** for turbulence measurements.

> **[Valuation Key]:** Full 3 marks requires: (i) block diagram or textual equivalent, (ii) closed-loop concept, (iii) at least two reasons for CTA preference. Award 1 mark for the diagram, 1 mark for working, 1 mark for comparison with CCA.

---

### Q2. (3 Marks)
**State and explain King's Law as applied to hot wire anemometry. What is the typical value of the velocity exponent $n$ for air?**

**Model Answer:**

King's Law (1914) is the empirical constitutive equation relating the anemometer output voltage $E$ to the fluid velocity $U$:

$$\boxed{E^{2} = A + B \cdot U^{n}}$$

**Where:**
- $E$ = output voltage of the anemometer bridge (V)
- $A$ = zero-velocity offset (V²); accounts for free-convection and radiation losses
- $B$ = sensitivity coefficient (V² · s/m); depends on wire geometry, fluid properties, and overheat ratio
- $U$ = fluid velocity (m/s)
- $n$ = velocity exponent (dimensionless)

**For air, the typical value is $n \approx 0.45$ to $0.50$**, and for KTU-level problems it is often taken as $n = 0.5$, giving the simplified form $E^{2} = A + B \sqrt{U}$.

**Physical basis:** $n$ arises from the empirical Nusselt–Reynolds correlation $Nu = C \cdot Re^{n}$ for cross-flow over a thin cylinder, where the convective heat transfer coefficient scales as $h \propto U^{n}$.

> **[Valuation Key]:** 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for variable definitions, 1 mark for the value of $n$ and physical basis.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, December 2024]**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive King's Law for a hot wire anemometer starting from the energy balance between electrical heating and convective cooling. Clearly state the assumptions used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hot wire anemometer is calibrated and the following data are obtained:

| Velocity $U$ (m/s) | Output Voltage $E$ (V) |
|--------------------|------------------------|
| 0.0 | 1.10 |
| 1.0 | 2.05 |
| 4.0 | 3.50 |
| 9.0 | 4.90 |

Assuming King's Law in the form $E^{2} = A + B \sqrt{U}$, determine the constants $A$ and $B$ using a suitable two-point method. Also find the velocity corresponding to an output of $E = 4.00$ V.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]:**

*(Write the full derivation from SECTION 3.1 here. The breakdown of marks is as follows:)*

1. **Master energy balance equation** [2 marks]:
   $E^2 / R_w = h A (T_w - T_f)$

2. **Nusselt–Reynolds correlation** [2 marks]:
   $Nu = C \cdot Re^{n}$, with $Re = \rho U D / \mu$

3. **Combining to eliminate $h$** [2 marks]:
   $h = C_1 \cdot U^n$ where $C_1 = (kC/D)(\rho D/\mu)^n$

4. **Final King's Law with offset $A$** [1 mark]:
   $E^2 = A + B U^n$

> **Assumptions to state (each worth partial credit):**
> - Long thin wire, cross-flow geometry
> - Constant temperature mode (CTA)
> - Thermal equilibrium (steady state)
> - Forced convection dominates
> - Radiation + natural convection lumped into constant $A$
> - Uniform wire temperature (lumped capacitance valid)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1: Compute $E^2$ for the given data points**

| $U$ (m/s) | $E$ (V) | $E^2$ (V²) | $\sqrt{U}$ (√m/s) |
|-----------|---------|------------|---------------------|
| 0.0 | 1.10 | **1.21** | 0.0000 |
| 1.0 | 2.05 | **4.2025** | 1.0000 |
| 4.0 | 3.50 | **12.25** | 2.0000 |
| 9.0 | 4.90 | **24.01** | 3.0000 |

**Step 2: Use the $U = 0$ point to find $A$** [1 mark]

$$A = E^{2}\big|_{U=0} = (1.10)^{2} = 1.21 \; \text{V}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Use the $U = 4$ m/s point to find $B$** [2 marks]

$$E^{2} = A + B \sqrt{U} \Rightarrow 12.25 = 1.21 + B \cdot \sqrt{4} = 1.21 + 2B$$

$$B = \frac{12.25 - 1.21}{2} = \frac{11.04}{2} = 5.52 \; \text{V}^{2} \cdot \text{s/m}$$

**Step 4: Verify with $U = 1$ m/s** [1 mark]

$$E_{calc}^{2} = 1.21 + 5.52 \cdot \sqrt{1} = 1.21 + 5.52 = 6.73 \;\text{V}^{2}$$

$$E_{calc} = 2.59 \; \text{V} \quad (\text{measured } 2.05 \; \text{V, error } \approx 26\%)$$

> **Note:** The deviation shows that two-point calibration is approximate. A **four-point least-squares fit** would be more accurate.

**Step 5: Find $U$ for $E = 4.00$ V** [3 marks]

$$E^{2} = (4.00)^{2} = 16.00 \; \text{V}^{2}$$

$$\sqrt{U} = \frac{E^{2} - A}{B} = \frac{16.00 - 1.21}{5.52} = \frac{14.79}{5.52} = 2.679 \; \sqrt{\text{m/s}}$$

$$U = (2.679)^{2} \approx 7.18 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Final Answer (b):** $\; A = 1.21 \; \text{V}^{2}, \quad B = 5.52 \; \text{V}^{2} \cdot \text{s/m}, \quad U \approx 7.18 \; \text{m/s}$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of a Hot Wire Velometer. With a neat block diagram, describe the function of each block. Compare it briefly with a Pitot-static tube for measuring air velocity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hot wire anemometer obeys King's Law with $A = 0.8$ V², $B = 4.5$ V²·s/m, and $n = 0.5$. If the hot wire has diameter $D = 5 \; \mu\text{m}$, length $L = 2 \; \text{mm}$, operated at an overheat temperature of $(T_w - T_f) = 200 \; \text{K}$ with convective coefficient $h = 450 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$:
   (i) Calculate the surface area of the wire.
   (ii) Calculate the heat loss rate from the wire.
   (iii) If the fluid velocity is $U = 16$ m/s, find the expected output voltage $E$ and the heating current $I$ if $R_w = 5.0 \; \Omega$.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Hot Wire Velometer [7 Marks]:**

**Construction:** A Hot Wire Velometer consists of:
- A **sensor probe** with a 5–25 µm tungsten or platinum wire stretched between two steel prongs.
- A **Wheatstone bridge** with the hot wire as one arm.
- A **bridge amplifier and lineariser** to convert the non-linear King's Law output to a linear voltage.
- A **microcontroller** that numerically inverts King's Law: $U = [(E^2 - A)/B]^{1/n}$.
- An **LCD display** showing velocity in m/s, fpm, or knots.
- A **telescopic extension rod** so the operator can reach into ducts.
- **Calibration keyboard** to input $A$, $B$, $n$ for different probes.

**Working:** Flow over the heated wire cools it, unbalances the bridge, the feedback loop adjusts the heating current to restore setpoint; the resulting $E$ is inverted by the microcontroller to display $U$.

**Comparison with Pitot-Static Tube:**

| Feature | Hot Wire Velometer | Pitot-Static Tube |
|---------|---------------------|--------------------|
| Output | Direct velocity read-out | Differential pressure → velocity |
| Speed of response | Very fast (kHz range) | Slow (seconds, due to manometer) |
| Spatial resolution | Point measurement (~1 mm³) | Volume-averaged |
| Measures | Instantaneous & fluctuating | Mean velocity only |
| Suitable for turbulence | Yes | No |
| Suitable for dirty/sooty flows | No (wire contaminates) | Yes |
| Cost | Moderate to high | Low |

> **[Valuation Key]:** 2 marks for construction list, 2 marks for working, 1 mark for block diagram, 2 marks for comparison table.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]:**

**(i) Surface area [2 marks]:**

$$A_{surface} = \pi D L = \pi \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$A_{surface} = \pi \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{-8} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{m}^{2}$$

**(ii) Heat loss rate [2 marks]:**

$$Q = h \cdot A_{surface} \cdot (T_w - T_f) = 450 \cdot 3.1416 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 200$$

$$Q = 450 \cdot 6.283 \times 10^{-6} = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{W} \approx 2.83 \; \text{mW}$$

**(iii) Output voltage at $U = 16$ m/s [2 marks]:**

$$E^{2} = A + B \sqrt{U} = 0.8 + 4.5 \cdot \sqrt{16} = 0.8 + 4.5 \cdot 4 = 0.8 + 18.0 = 18.8 \; \text{V}^{2}$$

$$E = \sqrt{18.8} \approx 4.336 \; \text{V}$$

**Heating current [1 mark]:**

$$I = \frac{E}{R_w} = \frac{4.336}{5.0} = 0.867 \; \text{A}$$

**Final Answer (b):** $A_{surface} = 3.14 \times 10^{-8}$ m², $Q = 2.83$ mW, $E \approx 4.34$ V, $I \approx 0.87$ A.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the square root:** Students often write $E = A + B\sqrt{U}$ instead of $E^2 = A + B\sqrt{U}$. **Marks lost: 1 per wrong use.**
> 2. **Not isolating $U$ correctly:** When asked to find velocity, the inversion is $U = [(E^2 - A)/B]^2$ (with $n = 0.5$), NOT $U = (E^2 - A)/B$. The square is in the **denominator's outside** in the forward law but comes **outside** in the inverse.
> 3. **Mixing up units:** $A$ and $B$ have units of V² (not V). Students who write $E = A + B\sqrt{U}$ with V-units lose 1 mark for dimensional inconsistency.
> 4. **Confusing CTA with CCA:** When asked "which mode has the highest frequency response," answer **CTA**, not CCA. The CTA's closed-loop thermal servo is what gives the kHz bandwidth.
> 5. **Forgetting to square the offset:** $A = E^2 \big|_{U=0}$, not $A = E \big|_{U=0}$. The numerical value of $A$ is in V².
> 6. **Ignoring natural-convection offset:** A King's Law fit with $A$ forced to zero will give poor results at low velocities ($U < 0.5$ m/s).
> 7. **Wrong value of $n$:** For KTU, unless the problem explicitly states otherwise, use $n = 0.5$ (i.e., $\sqrt{U}$ form). Mentioning "typically 0.45–0.50" earns bonus credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Hot Wire Anemometer & Velometer**

- ⭐ **Principle:** Convective heat loss from a heated wire depends on fluid velocity. Faster flow → more cooling → more electrical power needed to keep $T_w$ constant.
- ⭐ **Master equation:** $P_{elec} = I^2 R_w = h A (T_w - T_f)$ — always start any derivation here.
- ⭐ **King's Law:** $E^2 = A + B \cdot U^n$, with $n \approx 0.5$ for air in KTU problems.
- ⭐ **Inverse form:** $U = \left[\dfrac{E^2 - A}{B}\right]^{1/n}$.
- ⭐ **Three operating modes:** CCA (constant current), CVA (constant voltage), **CTA (constant temperature — preferred)**.
- ⭐ **CTA features:** closed-loop Wheatstone bridge, kHz bandwidth, low drift, best for turbulence.
- ⭐ **Sensor material:** Tungsten or Platinum; diameter 5–25 µm; length 1–3 mm; overheat 150–300 °C.
- ⭐ **Frequency response:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi \tau)$, with $\tau = \rho_w c_w D / (4h)$ — smaller $D$ → higher $f_c$.
- ⭐ **Sensitivity:** Can measure velocities from ~0.1 m/s up to supersonic; spatial resolution ~1 mm³.
- ⭐ **Calibration:** Must be done in known flow (e.g., subsonic wind tunnel) to determine $A$ and $B$ for the specific probe + fluid.
- ⭐ **Limitations:** (i) Contamination/dust on wire changes $\alpha$ and calibration; (ii) High temperature causes drift; (iii) Not suitable for liquid metals or strongly radiative flows; (iv) Single-wire probe cannot detect flow direction (X-wire or dual-wire probes needed).
- ⭐ **Velometer = field-portable HWA** with linearised digital read-out, m/s display, and keyboard for calibration entry.
- ⭐ **KTU-favourite comparators:** HWA vs. Pitot-static tube (response, spatial resolution, turbulence capture, cost).
- ⭐ **Units to remember:** $A$ in V², $B$ in V²·s/m, $U$ in m/s, $h$ in W/(m²·K), $E$ in V, $I$ in A.
- ⭐ **Master inversion trick:** When solving for $U$, isolate $\sqrt{U}$ first, then square at the very end. Do not square the entire equation prematurely.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Measurement: Measurement of Temperature by Liquid-in-Glass Thermometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET725 - ENGINEERING INSTRUMENTS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Temperature Measurement: Measurement of Temperature by Liquid-in-Glass Thermometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🌡️ Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Liquid-in-Glass Thermometer (LIGT)** is a primary-class, direct-reading temperature measurement instrument that utilizes the principle of **apparent thermal expansion** of a thermometric liquid (confined within a sealed glass bulb and stem) as the sensing mechanism. The volumetric change of the liquid is read against a calibrated linear scale etched or printed on the glass stem, providing a visual indication of temperature.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the LIGT belongs to the class of **expansion thermometers** and operates on the principle that:

$$\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta_{app} \cdot \Delta T$$

where $\beta_{app}$ is the *apparent* cubical expansion coefficient of the liquid (relative to glass), and $V_0$ is the initial liquid volume.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bottle in Summer" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a sealed plastic water bottle** filled almost to the brim on a cold winter morning and left in a hot car. As the temperature rises, the water expands. Since the bottle cannot stretch much, the small free space at the top fills up, and the water level visibly rises up the narrow neck. The amount it rises tells you *how much* hotter it got.

A **Liquid-in-Glass Thermometer works exactly like this bottle**, except:
- The "bottle" is a precision-blown **glass envelope** (bulb + capillary stem).
- The "water" is replaced by a calibrated thermometric fluid (**mercury, alcohol, pentane, or toluene**).
- The "rising level" is read against an engraved **linear scale** in °C, °F, K, or °R.

This direct visual readability is why LIGTs remain a **Class I reference standard** even after the advent of RTDs and thermocouples.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cubical expansion of mercury | $\beta_{Hg}$ | $1.82 \times 10^{-4}$ | /°C |
| Cubical expansion of glass (ordinary) | $\beta_{glass}$ | $2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ | /°C |
| Apparent expansion of Hg in glass | $\beta_{app}$ | $1.55 \times 10^{-4}$ | /°C |
| Cubical expansion of ethyl alcohol | $\beta_{alc}$ | $1.10 \times 10^{-3}$ | /°C |
| Density of mercury at 0 °C | $\rho_{Hg}$ | **13,595.1** | kg/m³ |
| Freezing point of mercury | $T_{fp}$ | **−38.83** | °C |
| Boiling point of mercury | $T_{bp}$ | **356.73** | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The *difference* between the cubical expansion of the liquid and that of the glass envelope is called the **apparent expansion coefficient** — this is the *actual* quantity governing the visible rise in the capillary.

## 1.4 Classification of Liquid-in-Glass Thermometers

LIGTs are classified along three independent axes:

**By Thermometric Liquid:**
1. **Mercury-in-Glass Thermometer** — Most common, used from −38 °C to +357 °C (extended to 600 °C with nitrogen filling).
2. **Alcohol (Ethanol)-in-Glass** — Used for low temperatures (down to **−115 °C**).
3. **Pentane-in-Glass** — Used for very low temperatures (down to **−200 °C**).
4. **Toluene / Creosote / Galinstan** — Specialty fluids.

**By Immersion Type:**
- **Total Immersion** — Entire liquid column must be immersed in the measuring medium.
- **Partial Immersion** — Only a specific length (usually 76 mm) is immersed; the rest protrudes.

**By Geometry:**
- **Stem-type (Stick-type)** — Scale etched on the thick stem.
- **Enclosed-scale (Lens-front)** — Scale on a separate milk-glass scale behind the capillary for parallax-free reading.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Apparent Thermal Expansion

The thermometric liquid resides in a **glass bulb** connected to a fine **capillary stem**. As the bulb temperature rises, the liquid expands. Since the glass also expands (but far less than the liquid), the **net apparent expansion** pushes a thread of liquid up the capillary.

Let the liquid volume be $V_0$ at temperature $T_0$. At a higher temperature $T$:

$$V_T = V_0 \left[1 + \beta_{liq} (T - T_0)\right]$$

The glass envelope volume also changes:

$$V_T^{glass} = V_0 \left[1 + \beta_{glass} (T - T_0)\right]$$

The **net rise in capillary** corresponds to the *apparent* volume change:

$$\Delta V_{app} = V_T - V_T^{glass} = V_0 \left(\beta_{liq} - \beta_{glass}\right) \Delta T$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{app} = \beta_{liq} - \beta_{glass}}$$

This apparent expansion is the *effective* sensing quantity.

## 2.2 The Capillary Rise Equation

If the capillary has a uniform cross-sectional area $A$ and the liquid rises by a height $h$:

$$\Delta V_{app} = A \cdot h$$

$$h = \frac{V_0 \cdot \beta_{app} \cdot \Delta T}{A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** To maximize sensitivity ($dh/dT$), designers **minimize $A$** (use a fine-bore capillary) and **maximize $V_0 \cdot \beta_{app}$** (use a large bulb and a high-expansion liquid). This is why the bulb is bulbous and the stem is capillary-thin.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Cheat-Sheet Table — Memorize before ESE**

| # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\beta_{app} = \beta_{liq} - \beta_{glass}$ | Apparent expansion coefficient | Foundational |
| 2 | $h = \dfrac{V_0 \cdot \beta_{app} \cdot \Delta T}{A}$ | Capillary rise vs temperature | Design analysis |
| 3 | $\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Stem (linear) expansion | Stem correction |
| 4 | $T_{true} = T_{ind} + n \cdot \alpha \cdot (T_{ind} - T_{stem})$ | Stem correction formula | Numericals |
| 5 | $°F = \dfrac{9}{5}°C + 32$ | Fahrenheit from Celsius | Scale conversions |
| 6 | $K = °C + 273.15$ | Kelvin from Celsius | Scale conversions |
| 7 | $°R = °F + 459.67$ | Rankine from Fahrenheit | Scale conversions |
| 8 | $t = t_1 + (t_2 - t_1) \dfrac{L_1 - L_0}{L_2 - L_1}$ | Two-point linear interpolation | Calibration |

> ⚠️ **LaTeX Safeguard Note:** All vertical bars above have been replaced with `\vert` / `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Metrology Labs** | Reference standard for calibrating other sensors |
| **Hospitals / Pharma** | Clinical mercury/galinstan thermometers |
| **Meteorology** | Stevenson screen-mounted max/min thermometers |
| **HVAC & Refrigeration** | Partial-immersion alcohol thermometers |
| **Industrial Furnaces** | High-temperature mercury thermometers (gas-filled) |
| **Cryogenics** | Pentane-filled LIGTs for LNG plants |

## 2.5 Types of Stem Corrections

**Total-Immersion Stem Correction:** When a total-immersion thermometer is used partially immersed, an error arises because the exposed stem is at ambient temperature, not at the bath temperature.

$$T_{true} = T_{ind} + n \cdot \alpha \cdot (T_{ind} - T_{mean,stem})$$

where:
- $n$ = number of exposed stem divisions (in °C-equivalent)
- $\alpha$ = cubical expansion of mercury **relative to glass** $= 1.55 \times 10^{-4}$ /°C
- $T_{ind}$ = thermometer indication
- $T_{mean,stem}$ = mean temperature of exposed stem (measured by an auxiliary thermometer)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** Stem correction is **added** if exposed stem is *cooler* than the bath ($T_{ind} > T_{stem}$). It is **subtracted** if exposed stem is *warmer*.

## 2.6 Sources of Error in LIGTs

1. **Stem-exposure error** (immersion error)
2. **Parallax error** (mitigated by lens-front / enclosed-scale design)
3. **Calibration drift** (glass contraction over time → bulb volume decrease)
4. **Lag error** (time response τ depends on bulb mass and surface area)
5. **Capillary depression / capillary rise** (meniscus variations)
6. **External pressure error** (ice-point depression under high pressure)
7. **Internal pressure error** (gas bubble above mercury column)
8. **Emergent stem correction for partial immersion**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Apparent Expansion Coefficient

Starting from the law of cubical expansion applied to both the liquid and the glass bulb:

$$V_{liquid}(T) = V_0 \left[1 + \beta_{liq} \cdot (T - T_0)\right]$$

$$V_{glass}(T) = V_0 \left[1 + \beta_{glass} \cdot (T - T_0)\right]$$

The **apparent volume** observed by the user is the liquid volume *minus* the volume occupied by the expanded glass envelope. The differential volume that rises in the capillary is:

$$\Delta V_{rise} = V_{liquid}(T) - V_{glass}(T)$$

$$\Delta V_{rise} = V_0 \left[1 + \beta_{liq} \Delta T\right] - V_0 \left[1 + \beta_{glass} \Delta T\right]$$

$$\Delta V_{rise} = V_0 \left(\beta_{liq} - \beta_{glass}\right) \Delta T$$

Defining $\beta_{app} = \beta_{liq} - \beta_{glass}$:

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{rise} = V_0 \cdot \beta_{app} \cdot \Delta T}$$

This is the **fundamental governing equation** of every liquid-in-glass thermometer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Capillary Rise (Sensitivity) Equation

The risen liquid forms a column of height $h$ inside a capillary of uniform cross-section $A$:

$$\Delta V_{rise} = A \cdot h$$

Equating the two expressions for $\Delta V_{rise}$:

$$A \cdot h = V_0 \cdot \beta_{app} \cdot \Delta T$$

$$h = \frac{V_0 \cdot \beta_{app}}{A} \cdot \Delta T$$

The **sensitivity** (rise per unit temperature) is:

$$\boxed{S = \frac{dh}{dT} = \frac{V_0 \cdot \beta_{app}}{A}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Doubling the bulb volume doubles the sensitivity. Halving the capillary area doubles the sensitivity. Replacing mercury with ethanol (≈6× higher $\beta_{app}$) increases sensitivity by ~6×.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stem Correction (Total-Immersion Thermometer Used Partially Immersed)

Suppose a total-immersion thermometer is immersed only up to the **division mark $n$**, exposing $n$ divisions of the mercury thread to ambient stem temperature $T_s$, while the bath is at $T_b$.

**Step 1:** The exposed mercury thread length is $L_n = n \cdot (\text{scale division length})$.

**Step 2:** This thread *would have expanded* if it were at bath temperature:

$$\Delta L_{correction} = L_n \cdot \alpha \cdot (T_b - T_s)$$

where $\alpha$ is the **apparent cubical expansion** of mercury relative to glass (≈ $1.55 \times 10^{-4}$ /°C).

**Step 3:** The thermometer reading $T_{ind}$ is *less* than $T_b$ by this amount, so:

$$T_b = T_{ind} + n \cdot \alpha \cdot (T_b - T_s)$$

**Step 4:** Approximating $T_b \approx T_{ind}$ in the correction term (first-order):

$$\boxed{T_{true} = T_{ind} + n \cdot \alpha \cdot (T_{ind} - T_{stem})}$$

where $T_{stem}$ is the mean temperature of the exposed stem (measured with an auxiliary thermometer tied to the stem).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example #1 (KTU-Style 7-Mark)

**Problem:** A mercury-in-glass thermometer has a bulb volume of $0.30 \text{ cm}^3$ at 0 °C and a capillary cross-sectional area of $0.0010 \text{ cm}^2$. Take $\beta_{Hg} = 1.82 \times 10^{-4}$ /°C and $\beta_{glass} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ /°C. Determine:
(a) The sensitivity of the thermometer in mm/°C.
(b) The length of mercury column between 0 °C and 100 °C.
(c) Comment on how sensitivity changes if ethyl alcohol ($\beta_{alc} = 1.10 \times 10^{-3}$ /°C) is used instead.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Compute the apparent expansion coefficient:

$$\beta_{app} = \beta_{Hg} - \beta_{glass}$$

$$\beta_{app} = 1.82 \times 10^{-4} - 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\beta_{app} = 1.82 \times 10^{-4} - 0.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\beta_{app} = 1.57 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /°C}$$

Sensitivity:

$$S = \frac{V_0 \cdot \beta_{app}}{A}$$

$$S = \frac{0.30 \text{ cm}^3 \times 1.57 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /°C}}{0.0010 \text{ cm}^2}$$

$$S = \frac{0.30 \times 1.57 \times 10^{-4}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$S = 0.0471 \text{ cm/°C}$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.471 \text{ mm/°C}}$$

**Part (b):** Length for $\Delta T = 100$ °C:

$$h = S \cdot \Delta T = 0.471 \text{ mm/°C} \times 100 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{h = 47.1 \text{ mm}}$$

**Part (c):** New apparent expansion with alcohol:

$$\beta_{app}^{alc} = 1.10 \times 10^{-3} - 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\beta_{app}^{alc} = 1.075 \times 10^{-3} \text{ /°C}$$

New sensitivity:

$$S_{alc} = \frac{0.30 \times 1.075 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$S_{alc} = 0.3225 \text{ cm/°C} = 3.225 \text{ mm/°C}$$

Sensitivity ratio:

$$\frac{S_{alc}}{S_{Hg}} = \frac{3.225}{0.471} \approx 6.85$$

$$\boxed{\text{Alcohol gives }\approx 6.85\times \text{ higher sensitivity}}$$

**Conclusion:** Alcohol thermometers are *more sensitive* but are limited by lower boiling point and are unsuitable for high-temperature use.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example #2 (Stem Correction — KTU 7-Mark)

**Problem:** A total-immersion mercury thermometer reads $T_{ind} = 180$ °C when immersed up to the 60 °C mark in a bath. The mean temperature of the exposed stem (measured by an auxiliary thermometer) is $T_{stem} = 42$ °C. Take $\alpha = 1.55 \times 10^{-4}$ /°C. Determine the true bath temperature.

**Solution:**

Exposed divisions:

$$n = 180 - 60 = 120 \text{ divisions}$$

Apply the stem correction formula:

$$T_{true} = T_{ind} + n \cdot \alpha \cdot (T_{ind} - T_{stem})$$

$$T_{true} = 180 + 120 \times 1.55 \times 10^{-4} \times (180 - 42)$$

$$T_{true} = 180 + 120 \times 1.55 \times 10^{-4} \times 138$$

$$T_{true} = 180 + 1.86 \times 10^{-2} \times 138$$

$$T_{true} = 180 + 2.5668$$

$$\boxed{T_{true} \approx 182.57 \text{ °C}}$$

**Interpretation:** The thermometer under-reads by ~2.57 °C due to the cold stem, and the true bath temperature is **182.57 °C**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Mercury vs Alcohol vs Pentane

| Property | Mercury | Ethyl Alcohol | Pentane |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lower limit (°C) | **−38.83** | **−115** | **−200** |
| Upper limit (°C) | **+356.73** | **+78** | **+36** |
| $\beta_{app}$ (/°C) | $1.55 \times 10^{-4}$ | $1.08 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Wets glass? | **No** | Yes | Yes |
| Toxicity | High (Hg) | Low | Low |
| Meniscus | Convex (sharp) | Concave (poor) | Concave (poor) |
| Visibility | Excellent (silvery) | Needs dye | Needs dye |
| KTU exam focus | **Most asked** | Secondary | Rare |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Mercury is preferred over alcohol because (i) it does not wet glass → sharp meniscus, (ii) it remains liquid over a wide range, and (iii) it has a high boiling point enabling high-temperature use.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🧭 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Liquid-in-Glass Thermometer

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Thermometric Liquid in Bulb] --> B[Bulb - Spherical Cylindrical]
    B --> C[Bulb-to-Stem Contraction Chamber]
    C --> D[Capillary Bore - Fine Uniform]
    D --> E[Mercury Liquid Thread]
    E --> F[Vacuum or Gas-filled Expansion Chamber]
    G[Glass Stem Envelope] --> D
    G --> H[Engraved Scale - Degrees C F K]
    I[Stem Reference Point] --> H
    J[Immersion Line Mark] --> I
    K[Partial or Total Immersion Mark] --> J
    style A fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000
    style B fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style D fill:#fffbe6,stroke:#ccaa00
    style H fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900
```

**Reading the diagram (left → right, top → bottom):**
- The **bulb** (B) holds the thermometric liquid (A).
- The **contraction chamber** (C) prevents the glass from cracking during rapid temperature changes.
- The **capillary bore** (D) is the precision-bore channel where liquid rise is measured.
- The **expansion chamber** (F) at the top accommodates over-range temperatures without rupture.
- The **scale** (H) is etched on the glass stem and calibrated against the capillary geometry.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Measured Temperature T_bath] --> EXP[Thermal Expansion of Liquid]
    EXP --> APP[Apparent Expansion minus Glass Expansion]
    APP --> CAP[Rise of Liquid Column in Capillary]
    CAP --> SCALE[Visual Reading against Engraved Scale]
    SCALE --> STEM[Stem Correction if Partial Immersion]
    STEM --> OUT[T_true Indicated Temperature]
    PAR[Parallax Error] -.-> SCALE
    LAG[Time Lag tau] -.-> EXP
    CAL[Calibration Drift] -.-> SCALE
    style T fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    style OUT fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d
    style PAR fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style LAG fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style CAL fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000
```

**Interpretation:** The clean measurement chain is `T → EXP → APP → CAP → SCALE → OUT`. The three red-tinted error sources (parallax, lag, calibration drift) act as *disturbances* corrupting the output.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison of Thermometer Classes

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LIGT[Liquid-in-Glass Family]
        HG[Mercury Thermometer]
        AL[Alcohol Thermometer]
        PT[Pentane Thermometer]

    end

    end

        CRY[Cryogenic -200C]
        CLIN[Clinical Body Temperature]
        MET[Metrology Reference Standard]
        IND[Industrial High Temperature 600C]
        METEO[Meteorology Max Min]
    end
    HG --> TI
    HG --> PI
    HG --> MET
    HG --> IND
    AL --> PI
    AL --> CLIN
    PT --> CRY
    TI --> MET
    PI --> METEO
    EF --> MET
    style HG fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1155cc
    style AL fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d
    style PT fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Calibration Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ice Bath 0.00C Reference] --> CAL[Calibration Bath with Stirrer]
    B[Steam Point 100C Reference] --> CAL
    C[Constant-Temperature Liquid Bath] --> CAL
    CAL --> DUT[Device Under Test - LIGT]
    CAL --> REF[Reference Standard PRT]
    DUT --> READ1[Indicated Temperature]
    REF --> READ2[True Temperature]
    READ1 --> COMP[Comparator with Correction Table]
    READ2 --> COMP
    COMP --> CERT[Calibration Certificate with Uncertainty]
    style A fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1155cc
    style B fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style CERT fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Insight:** Calibration is performed at the **ice point (0 °C)** and **steam point (100 °C)** using the ITS-90 fixed points, ensuring traceability to national standards (NPL, NABL-accredited labs in India).

# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define *apparent expansion coefficient* of a liquid. Why is it used in liquid-in-glass thermometer design instead of the absolute cubical expansion coefficient?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The apparent expansion coefficient of a liquid (relative to the containing vessel) is defined as:

$$\beta_{app} = \beta_{liq} - \beta_{vessel}$$

For a mercury-in-glass thermometer:

$$\beta_{app} = 1.82 \times 10^{-4} - 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 1.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /°C}$$

(1 mark) It is used because the liquid expands *inside* a glass envelope that itself expands. The *net* rise observed in the capillary corresponds only to the **difference** between liquid expansion and glass expansion. **(1 mark)** Using $\beta_{app}$ directly gives the actual volume change that the thermometer senses, making the sensitivity calculation physically meaningful. **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **total-immersion** and **partial-immersion** liquid-in-glass thermometers. When is stem correction mandatory?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Total-Immersion | Partial-Immersion |
|---|---|---|
| Immersion depth | Entire liquid column | Only specified length (e.g., 76 mm) |
| Reading accuracy | Highest (if fully immersed) | Lower; requires calibration at that depth |
| Stem correction | Required **if used partially** | Calibrated to be used at marked depth |

**(1.5 marks)** Stem correction is mandatory when a **total-immersion** thermometer is used *partially immersed*, because the exposed stem is at ambient temperature rather than bath temperature, causing an under-reading. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Stem Correction + Design Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question, Adapted]**

**Q-A(a)** [7 marks] A total-immersion mercury-in-glass thermometer reads 240 °C when immersed up to the 100 °C mark in an oil bath. An auxiliary thermometer tied to the exposed stem reads 55 °C. Take the apparent cubical expansion of mercury relative to glass as $1.55 \times 10^{-4}$ /°C. Calculate:
(i) The number of exposed stem divisions.
(ii) The stem correction to be applied.
(iii) The true bath temperature.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Exposed divisions:**

$$n = 240 - 100 = 140 \text{ divisions}$$

**(1 mark)**

**(ii) Stem correction:**

$$\Delta T_{corr} = n \cdot \alpha \cdot (T_{ind} - T_{stem})$$

$$\Delta T_{corr} = 140 \times 1.55 \times 10^{-4} \times (240 - 55)$$

$$\Delta T_{corr} = 140 \times 1.55 \times 10^{-4} \times 185$$

$$\Delta T_{corr} = 4.0145 \text{ °C}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) True temperature:**

$$T_{true} = 240 + 4.0145 = 244.01 \text{ °C}$$

**(1 mark)** — adding correction because stem is cooler than bath.

**Discussion (3 marks):**
- The thermometer *under-reads* because the exposed stem mercury contracted in the cool ambient air.
- The stem correction formula is a first-order approximation assuming uniform stem temperature $T_{stem}$.
- In precision metrology, an auxiliary thermocouple may be bonded at multiple stem points for better accuracy.

---

**Q-A(b)** [7 marks] With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a mercury-in-glass thermometer. State **four** advantages of mercury as a thermometric liquid.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A mercury-in-glass thermometer consists of:
1. A **glass bulb** (spherical or cylindrical) containing pure, dry mercury.
2. A **capillary stem** of fine uniform bore connected to the bulb.
3. A **contraction chamber** between bulb and capillary to prevent glass cracking during sudden temperature changes.
4. An **expansion chamber** at the top of the capillary to accommodate over-range.
5. An **engraved scale** in °C / °F / K etched on the stem (or on a milk-glass scale in enclosed-scale type).
6. A **reference immersion mark** indicating the depth of immersion.

**Working (2 marks):**
When the bulb is heated, mercury expands. Since glass expansion is negligible in comparison, the apparent expansion forces mercury up the capillary. The rise $h$ is read against the calibrated scale. The relationship is:

$$h = \frac{V_0 \cdot \beta_{app} \cdot \Delta T}{A}$$

**Four advantages of mercury (2 marks):**
1. Does not wet glass → sharp, convex meniscus for accurate reading.
2. Stays liquid over a wide range (−38.83 °C to +356.73 °C).
3. High density → small temperature changes produce visible column movement.
4. Chemically inert and does not vaporize appreciably within the range.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Sensitivity Calculation + Error Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question, Adapted]**

**Q-B(a)** [7 marks] The bulb of a mercury thermometer has a volume of $0.25 \text{ cm}^3$ at 0 °C, and the capillary bore has a cross-sectional area of $0.0008 \text{ cm}^2$. Given $\beta_{Hg} = 1.82 \times 10^{-4}$ /°C and $\beta_{glass} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ /°C, determine:
(i) The apparent cubical expansion coefficient.
(ii) The sensitivity in mm/°C.
(iii) The length of the mercury column between 10 °C and 90 °C.
(iv) The number of scale divisions if each division corresponds to 1 °C.

**Model Solution:**

**(i)** Apparent expansion:

$$\beta_{app} = 1.82 \times 10^{-4} - 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 1.57 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /°C}$$

**(1 mark)**

**(ii)** Sensitivity:

$$S = \frac{V_0 \cdot \beta_{app}}{A} = \frac{0.25 \times 1.57 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0008}$$

$$S = \frac{3.925 \times 10^{-5}}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$S = 0.04906 \text{ cm/°C} = 0.4906 \text{ mm/°C}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii)** Length for $\Delta T = 80$ °C:

$$h = S \cdot \Delta T = 0.4906 \times 80 = 39.25 \text{ mm}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iv)** Number of divisions:

$$N = \frac{h}{\text{division spacing}} = \frac{39.25 \text{ mm}}{0.4906 \text{ mm/division}}$$

$$\boxed{N = 80 \text{ divisions (one per °C)}}$$

**(2 marks)**

---

**Q-B(b)** [7 marks] Discuss the various **sources of error** in liquid-in-glass thermometers. Explain how each error can be minimized.

**Model Solution:**

| # | Error Source | Cause | Minimization Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Stem exposure error** | Exposed stem not at bath temperature | Stem correction formula; use partial-immersion type |
| 2 | **Parallax error** | Observer's eye not perpendicular to scale | Enclosed-scale (lens-front) thermometer design |
| 3 | **Calibration drift** | Glass bulb contracts over years | Periodic re-calibration at ice & steam points |
| 4 | **Lag error** | Finite thermal mass of bulb | Use smaller bulb, stir bath, wait for steady state |
| 5 | **Capillary variation** | Non-uniform bore | Use precision-bore capillary; calibrate by mercury thread method |
| 6 | **External pressure** | High ambient pressure on bulb | Use armored thermometers; ice-point check |
| 7 | **Internal pressure** | Gas bubble in mercury column | Avoid jarring; tap before reading |

**(1 mark each, total 7 marks for explaining all seven)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign of Stem Correction** — Many students subtract the correction unconditionally. The rule is: correction is **added** if $T_{ind} > T_{stem}$ (cold stem), and **subtracted** if $T_{ind} < T_{stem}$ (hot stem). Marks lost = **1–2 marks per question**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Glass Expansion** — Using $\beta_{Hg}$ instead of $\beta_{app}$ in sensitivity calculation. Marks lost = **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Unit Conversion in Capillary Area** — Mixing cm² with mm² or vice versa. Marks lost = **0.5–1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Confusing Total vs Partial Immersion** — A total-immersion thermometer used partially *without* stem correction is the most common ESE trap.
>
> 5. **Writing $°K$ instead of $K$** — Kelvin does not carry the degree symbol. Marks lost = **0.5 mark** (strict marking).
>
> 6. **Omitting Units in Final Answer** — Always box your answer with units: e.g., $\boxed{S = 0.49 \text{ mm/°C}}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Liquid-in-Glass Thermometers**

- 🔹 **Operating Principle:** Apparent thermal expansion of liquid *relative* to the glass envelope. **(Most-tested concept)**
- 🔹 **Core Equation:** $h = \dfrac{V_0 \cdot \beta_{app} \cdot \Delta T}{A}$ where $\beta_{app} = \beta_{liq} - \beta_{glass}$
- 🔹 **Mercury properties:** Freezes at −38.83 °C, boils at 356.73 °C, $\beta_{Hg} = 1.82 \times 10^{-4}$ /°C, density ≈ **13,595.1 kg/m³**
- 🔹 **Mercury advantages:** Sharp meniscus, wide range, non-wetting, opaque & visible, chemically stable
- 🔹 **Alcohol range:** Down to **−115 °C** (used in meteorology)
- 🔹 **Pentane range:** Down to **−200 °C** (cryogenics)
- 🔹 **Stem correction formula:** $T_{true} = T_{ind} + n \cdot \alpha \cdot (T_{ind} - T_{stem})$
- 🔹 **Sign rule:** Add correction when stem is colder; subtract when warmer.
- 🔹 **Two immersion types:** Total-immersion (highest accuracy) and Partial-immersion (field use)
- 🔹 **Common errors:** Stem exposure, parallax, calibration drift, lag, capillary non-uniformity
- 🔹 **Calibration references:** Ice point (0 °C) and steam point (100 °C) — ITS-90 fixed points
- 🔹 **Sensitivity design rule:** Large bulb volume + fine capillary + high-$\beta_{app}$ liquid = max sensitivity
- 🔹 **Scale conversions to memorize:**
    - $°F = \dfrac{9}{5}°C + 32$
    - $K = °C + 273.15$
    - $°R = °F + 459.67$
- 🔹 **Why mercury beats alcohol despite lower $\beta_{app}$:** Sharper meniscus, no wetting, higher boiling point, and visible silvery column against glass.

> **End of Module 4 — Liquid-in-Glass Thermometers Notes**
> **Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme • Course OEMET725 • Module 4**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure Thermometer (PT):** A temperature-measuring instrument that operates on the principle that the **pressure of a fluid (liquid, gas, or saturated vapor) confined within a closed, constant-volume system** varies in a definite, reproducible manner with temperature. The fluid is contained in a **thermometric bulb**, connected through a **capillary tube** to a **pressure-measuring element** (typically a Bourdon tube, bellows, or diaphragm) which provides the output indication.

The device translates a **temperature change** (at the bulb) into a **pressure change** (at the indicator), which is then mechanically amplified and displayed on a calibrated dial in units of temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> As per the KTU 2024 OEMET725 syllabus, pressure thermometers belong to the class of **expansion thermometers** and are widely used in industrial process industries for the range **−40 °C to +600 °C** (depending on fill fluid).

## 1.2 The Three Functional Sub-Systems

| Sub-System | Function | Typical Form Factor |
|------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Thermometric Bulb** | Primary sensing element that comes in contact with the process | Spherical / cylindrical metal capsule |
| **Capillary Tube** | Transmits pressure from bulb to indicator | Long, small-bore stainless-steel or copper tube (1–10 m) |
| **Pressure Indicator** | Converts pressure into a dial reading (°C) | Bourdon tube, bellows, or diaphragm gauge |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Connection)

> [!TIP]
> **The "Pressure-Cooker Pressure-Gauge" Analogy:** Imagine a sealed pressure cooker with a pressure gauge attached. As you heat the cooker, the temperature of the trapped air/gas/vapor rises, and the gauge needle moves up — *that is exactly how a pressure thermometer works*. The bulb is the cooker, the capillary is the connecting pipe, and the Bourdon tube is the pressure gauge. **Temperature → Pressure → Pointer deflection → Calibrated °C reading.**

The beauty of this design is that the **bulb can be placed remotely** (even inside a furnace) while the **indicator sits safely on a control panel** — making it ideal for **industrial process measurements**.

## 1.4 Classification Based on Fill Fluid

1. **Liquid-Filled Pressure Thermometer** (e.g., mercury, xylene, silicone oil)
2. **Gas-Filled Pressure Thermometer** (e.g., nitrogen, helium, argon — *inert and safe*)
3. **Vapor-Pressure (Saturated Vapor) Thermometer** (e.g., methyl chloride, ethyl ether, Freon, propane)

![Pressure Thermometer Classification](placeholder://no-image-required)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle (General)

A pressure thermometer exploits the **Pressure–Temperature (P–T) relationship of a confined fluid**. By enforcing the geometric condition of **constant total volume** (bulb + capillary + Bourdon = sealed), any temperature-induced change in fluid pressure is *uniquely* mapped to temperature.

$$\boxed{V_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{bulb}} + V_{\text{capillary}} + V_{\text{Bourdon}} = \text{Constant}}$$

## 2.2 Type 1 — Liquid-Filled Pressure Thermometer

**Principle:** The liquid (mercury) expands when heated. Since the volume is rigidly fixed, this volumetric expansion manifests as a **rise in pressure** that the Bourdon tube can detect.
